content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτὴ_ἥδε_1">, implying the antithesis, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ δὲ ἔργῳ ἤδη ἐπιχειρητέον</quote>, which is virtually given by vv. 11 ff. For <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> thus deprived of its answering <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> by a change in the form of a long sentence, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1199"> <title>Ant.</title> 1199</ref> ff. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστιπτος_2"> is the form given by L here, which also has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στιπτή_2"> in v. 33. <quote xml:lang="grc">στιπτός</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">στειπτός</quote>, is also the best attested form in Aristophanes <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 180"> <title>Ach.</title> 180</ref>, and in Theophrastus <title>De Igne</title> § 37. See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_οἰκουμένη_2">. Aeschylus and Euripides had both written a <quote xml:lang="grc">Φιλοκτήτης</quote>, and each had composed his chorus of Lemnians,—thus making it seem strange that the sufferer should have been left so long without aid (Dion Chrysostom, or. 52). Sophocles wished to avoid that defect. Everything that is said of Lemnos throughout this play would naturally suggest a wholly uninhabited island. And the words ascribed to Philoctetes (vv. 220 f., 300 ff.) require us to suppose that he, at least, believed it to be so. The <title>Iliad</title>, however, represents <quote xml:lang="grc"> Εὔνηος</quote>, son of Jason and Hypsipylè, as reigning in Lemnos during the Trojan war (7. 467); and it was into ‘well-peopled Lemnos’ that Achilles sold Lycaon (21. 40). It is simplest to suppose that Sophocles, finding it convenient to have a desert island, ignored the Homeric notices. But it is also possible that he conceived the island as inhabited in some parts and desolate in others. This is the scholiast's view: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐρήμῳ γὰρ μέρει τῆς Λήμνου ἐξετέθη</quote>. The area of Lemnos is about 150 square miles, or more than thrice that of Jersey. Philoctetes could not crawl far from his sea-side cave (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 163">cp. 163</ref>, 291). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατίστου_τραφείς_3">: strictly, ‘bred from’ (not, ‘reared by’) ‘a sire who was the bravest of the Greeks.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_3"> is not a gen. of agency (like <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγεὶς θυγατρός</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 497"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 497</ref>), but a gen. of origin, as 1284 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρίστου πατρὸς αἴσχιστος γεγώς</quote>: cp. <title> O.T.</title> 1082 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς γὰρ πέφυκα μητρός</quote>, <title> O.C.</title> 1322 <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς λοχευθείς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τραφείς_3"> is more forcible than <quote xml:lang="grc">γεγώς</quote>, as suggesting, not birth merely, but the inborn qualities. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 556"> <title>Ai.</title> 556</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ δ’ ὅπως πατρὸς</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">δείξεις ἐν ἐχθροῖς οἷος ἐξ οἵου )τράφης</quote>, ‘thou must see that thou provest among thy father's foes of what mettle and <emph>what breed</emph> thou art.’ In <ref n="Aesch. Seven 792">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 792</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαρσεῖτε, παῖδες μητέρων τεθραμμέναι</quote>, the gen. seems again to be one of origin, ‘maidens <emph>who are true daughters of</emph> your mothers’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, who resemble them, rather than your intrepid fathers). Wakefield's conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ ὦ <’<emph>κ</emph>> κρατίστου</quote> was warranted by the commoner usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">τραφείς</quote> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 557">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 557</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 693">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 693</ref>; with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 262">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 262</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1229">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1229</ref>); but it was needless here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νεοπτόλεμε_4">, four syllables, the voice gliding so rapidly over the first <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> that, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote>, it gives the effect of only one syllable. So in 241, and <ref n="Eur. Andr. 14">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 14</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1126.">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1126.</ref>But the name is a word of five syllables in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1655">Eur. <title> Or.</title> 1655</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νεοπτόλεμος γαμεῖν νιν, οὐ γαμεῖ ποτε</quote>. Elmsley thought that verse corrupt; the same variation occurs, however, in <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεοκλύμενος</quote>, which is of four syllables in <title>Helen.</title> 9, but of five <emph> ib.</emph> 1168 and 1643.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_Μηλιᾶ_4">, belonging to Malis (‘the sheepcountry,’ from <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆλον</quote>, as the neighbouring Mount Oeta takes its name from <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶς</quote>),—a district almost enclosed by hills, at the head (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, west) of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μαλιακὸς κόλπος</quote>. That bay forms a deep recess in the south coast of Thessaly, just opposite the N.W. end of Euboea. Cp. n. on 490. The <title>Iliad</title> (2. 682) includes this region in the domain of Achilles, and assigns Philoctetes to the more northerly region of Thessaly, afterwards called Magnesia: see Introduction.—Her. , consistently Ionic, has, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Μηλὶς γῆ, ἡ Τρηχινίη</quote>: Attic writers always have <quote xml:lang="grc">Τραχίς</quote>: but Thuc. and Xen. say <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Μηλιεῖς</quote>, while Aeschines, like later writers, has <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Μαλιεῖς</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 725">Cp. 725</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Μηλιάδων νυμφᾶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέθηκ_5">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεβίβασα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aristot. Poet. 1459b">Arist. <title>Poet.</title> 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ περὶ τὴν ἔκθεσιν</quote>, the story of Odysseus being <emph>put ashore</emph> by the Phaeacians in Ithaca ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.116">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 13. 116</ref> ff.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταστάζοντα_7"> agrees with <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱόν</quote> (5): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόδα_7"> is acc. of respect: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 9"> <title>Ai.</title> 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στάζων ἱδρῶτι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαβόρῳ_7">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1084"> <title>Tr.</title> 1084</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τάλαινα διαβόρος νόσος</quote> (the venom of the hydra). So below, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 313">313</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βόσκων τὴν ἀδηφάγον νόσον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 745">745</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύκομαι</quote> </cit>. Aesch. fr. 253 (Philoctetes speaking) <quote xml:lang="grc">φαγέδαιν’ ἀεί μου σάρκας ἐσθίει ποδός</quote>: a v. which Euripides borrowed in his own <title>Philoctetes</title>, changing <quote xml:lang="grc">σάρκας ἐσθίει</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">σάρκα θοινᾶται</quote> ( <ref n="Aristot. Poet. 1458a">Arist. <title>Poet.</title> 22</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λοιβῆς_θυμάτων_8">. The sacrifice regularly preceded the libation (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.462"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 462</ref>); the order here is prompted by metrical convenience (as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.500"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 500</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λοιβῇ τε κνίσῃ τε</quote>), while the natural order is given below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1033">1033</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἴθειν ἱερά,...σπένδειν</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθιγεῖν_8">, fig., ‘engage in’; so the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνω</quote> (408, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 546"> <title>Ant.</title> 546</ref>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπτομαι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1005"> <title>Ant.</title> 1005</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπύρων ἐγευόμην.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσφημίαις_8">, cries of anguish, such as he utters below (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 743">743</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 785">785</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 1144">Eur. <title> Andr.</title> 1144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κραυγὴ δ’ ἐν εὐφήμοισι δύσφημος δόμοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτραισιν ἀντέκλαγξ’</quote> (cries of strife echoing in the Delphian temple from the rocks hard by). At a sacrifice, all present were first sprinkled with consecrated water, then silence was proclaimed, and then the offering began: Ar. <title>Av.</title> 958 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις σὺ περιχώρει λαβὼν τὴν χέρνιβα</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφημἴ ἔστω. ΧΡ. μὴ κατάρξῃ τοῦ τράγου</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκμὴ_λόγων_12">: cp. <title>El.</title> 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργων ἀκμή</quote>. Possibly a covert criticism on the length of the prologue in some previous <title>Philoctetes:</title> cp. <title>O. C.</title> 1116 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_καὶ_13">: this <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote>=‘e'en’ (not ‘both’): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 46">cp. 46</ref>, 534.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκχέω_13"> (aor. subj.), ‘waste’ ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1291"> <title>El.</title> 1291</ref>), which would properly be said of the labour bestowed on devising the scheme, is here applied, in the sense of ‘frustrate,’ to the <quote xml:lang="grc">σόφισμα</quote> itself: cp. Eur. fr. 789 <quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθων τῶν πρὶν ἐκχέαι χάριν</quote>. (Cp. <title>G.</title> 4. 491 <emph>ibi omnis</emph> | <title>Effusus labor.</title>)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_13"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 747"> <title>O. C.</title> 747</ref> n.— Aesch. and Eur. had both represented Odysseus as boldly confronting Philoctetes, who failed to recognise him; a marvel which Eur. excused by supposing that Athena had changed the aspect of Odysseus. These two verses remind us that dramatic probability required Odysseus to keep himself in the background. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 70">Cp. 70</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργον_σὸν_15">: a familiar Attic phrase, as appears from its frequency in Ar. , either (<emph>a</emph>) with inf., as <title>Nub.</title> 1343, <quote xml:lang="grc">σὸν ἔργον, ὦ πρεσβῦτα, φροντίζειν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: or (<emph>b</emph>) as a parenthesis before an imperat., as <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 862"> <title>Av.</title> 862</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερεῦ, σὸν ἔργον, θῦε</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1208"> <title>Th.</title> 1208</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">σὸν ἔργον, φεῦγε</quote>. It occurs more often without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> than with it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_στ_16">. Three modes of writing these words are possible: (1) as above, with prodelision of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔ</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 732"> <title>O. T.</title> 732</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ποῦ )σθ’ ὁ χῶρος</quote>...; <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 129">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ Ἀμφίθεός μοι ποῦ )στιν</quote>; So <ref n="Soph. OC 974">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 974</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐγὼ ’φάνην</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 457</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὅτου ’φάνη</quote>. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποὔστ’</quote>, with crasis, the mode followed by the scribe of L: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 812">cp. 812</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐ θέμις γ’ ἐμοὔστι</quote>. (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου ἔστι</quote>, with synizesis, the mode preferred by several recent edd. The fact that the 2nd syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου</quote> has ictus appears to render (1) or (2) slightly preferable to (3); and (1) seems recommended by the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">)φάνην, )φάνη</quote>, where, at the end of the verse, a synizesis would have had a very harsh effect.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_ἐνταῦθα_16">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, where (precisely) in this region. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 432">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοιτ’ ἂν οὖν φράσαι νῷν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Πλούτων’ ὅπου )νθάδ’ οἰκεῖ</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιάδ_ἵν_17">, ‘such that in it’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ᾗ</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιόσδε...ὅς</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. OC 1353"> <title>O. C.</title> 1353</ref>). Cp. Eur. fr. 183 <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμων τὸ πλεῖστον ἡμέρας</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ μέρος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ αὐτὸς αὑτοῦ τυγχάνει βέλτιστος ὤν</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ᾧ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡλίου_ἐνθάκησις_17">, lit., ‘a twofold means of sitting in the sun.’ Cp. <title>Probl.</title> 5 § 36 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστηκότες ἐν τῷ ἡλίῳ</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 16 § 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἐν ἡλίῳ τεθῶσι</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">θακεῖν ἐν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθακεῖν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡλίῳ</quote> could mean, ‘to sit in the sun’; and the genit. in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡλίου ἐνθάκησις</quote> is objective, corresponding to the dat. with the verb. This is better than to make it a gen. of quality, as if the phrase meant, ‘a sunny seat in (the cave).’ The morning sun could be enjoyed at the seaward mouth of the cave, which had a s. or S. E. aspect (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1457">cp. 1457</ref>); while the afternoon sun fell on the other entrance, looking N. or N. W.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιτρῆτος_17">, ‘pierced at both ends,’ ‘tunnelled’: perh. suggested by <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 707">Eur. <title> Cycl.</title> 707</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δἰ ἀμφιτρῆτος τῆσδε προσβαίνων πέτρας</quote> (so Kirchhoff for <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδί</quote>). This pass. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιτρής</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφοτέρωθεν τετρημένον</quote>, schol.), in which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίτρητος</quote> would be normal, cannot be illustrated by <quote xml:lang="grc">σιδηροκμής</quote> (‘slain with the sword,’ <ref n="Soph. Aj. 325">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 325</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">δορικμής</quote> <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 365">Aesch. <title> Ch.</title> 365</ref>), since those adjectives=‘succumbing to’ the sword, etc. (from the poet. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ καμόντες</quote>, etc.). But <quote xml:lang="grc">βοτοῖς σιδηροκμῆσιν</quote> in the former passage illustrates the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιτρής</quote>, properly masc. or fem., as a neuter adj.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐλίου_17">, as 954, 1087: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 30">cp. 30</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_σῶν_21">, a doubt the more natural since the island was volcanic (800). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_κυρεῖ_22">: ‘advance, I pray thee (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_22">), towards them’ [the cave and spring], ‘and sign (to me) whether he still occupies this same spot, or is elsewhere.’ The position of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_22_2"> indicates that it is the ethic dat. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1512"> <title>O. T.</title> 1512</ref>), rather than dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">σήμαινε</quote>, with which it can easily be understood.—In the Appendix reasons are given for the following views. (1) The words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σήμαιν_ἔχει_22"> break the metrical rule, since <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’</quote> must be considered as metrically belonging to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote> rather than to <quote xml:lang="grc">σήμαινε</quote>, and therefore the 5th foot ought to be an iambus. But nevertheless the words are sound, since the natural stress on the first syllable of the imperative <quote xml:lang="grc">σήμαιν’</quote>, coinciding with the rhythmical ictus, has the effect of making the next syllable (<quote xml:lang="grc"> αιν</quote>) seem relatively short to the ear. (2) In v. 23 the traditional <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον <emph>πρὸς</emph> αὐτὸν</quote> is untenable. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> with acc. could here mean only, ‘looking towards,’ ‘facing’; it could not mean merely, ‘in the neighbourhood of.’ And </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_22"> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον πρὸς αὐτὸν τόνδε</quote> could not mean either, ‘(the cave and spring) <emph>are situated</emph> facing just this spot’; nor, ‘he dwells facing this spot.’ We should read with Blaydes, <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον <emph>τὸν</emph> αὐτόν</quote>. (3) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδ_εἴτ_22"> is the best correction of L's <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’, ἤτ’</quote> in v. 23: and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’</quote> confirms the view that Philoctetes is the subject to the verbs. Odysseus is sure that the cave is somewhere near (16). His doubt is whether Philoctetes still lives in it, or has removed to some other part of the island. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπίλοιπα_λόγων_24">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐπιλοίπους</quote>, because the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote> are thought of collectively, not singly; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 131">cp. 131</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 499"> <title>Ant.</title> 499</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν σῶν λόγων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεστὸν οὐδέν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 682"> <title>Tr.</title> 682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμῶν οὐδέν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 352b">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 352 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ λοιπὰ τῆς ἑστιάσεως</quote> </cit>. The ref. is to the plan disclosed at <ref n="Soph. Phil. 50">50 ff.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινὰ_24">, subst., ‘joint action’ (not adv., ‘jointly,’ as though the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴῃ</quote> were ‘our plan,’ implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπίλοιπα τῶν λόγων</quote>): cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 8">Thuc. 1. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλωιμώτερα ἐγένετο παρ’ ἀλλήλους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἴῃ_24">, lit., ‘proceed from both’: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 294">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 294</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος γὰρ ἔκ τ’ ἀδοξούντων ἰὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ τῶν δοκούντων αὑτὸς οὐ ταὐτὸν σθένει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔργον_λέγεις_26">,=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔργον ὂ λέγεις οὐ μακράν ἐστι</quote>, ‘the task of which thou speakest is not far off,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> I can do thy bidding without going far. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔργον</quote> is the search for (and in) the cave. This seems simpler than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">μακράν</quote> as=‘<emph>to</emph> a distance’ (<title> O.T.</title> 16), and <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον</quote> as=‘mission.’ For the adverb as predicate, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 586"> <title>O. C.</title> 586</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν βραχεῖ δὴ τήνδε μ’ ἐξαιτεῖ χάριν</quote>, n.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 962"> <title>Tr.</title> 962</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχοῦ δ’ ἄρα κοὐ μακρὰν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">προὔκλαιον</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote>), ‘the sorrow foretold by my lament is near, and not afar’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1191"> <title>El.</title> 1191</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθεν τοῦτ’ ἐξεσήμηνας κακόν</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνωθεν_κάτωθεν_28">; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> above or below Neoptolemus, who is climbing the rocks. Odysseus is on the sea-shore. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1000">Cp. 1000</ref> ff. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κτύπος_29">, ‘and of foot-fall, at least, there is no sound.’ The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_29">, which has been suspected, is fitting; he is still a little below the cave, and cannot yet <emph>see</emph> whether it is empty. Seyffert's <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου <emph>δ’</emph> </quote> would be appropriate only if it followed the mention of some other sign that the cave was empty.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στίβου_29">, usu. ‘track (path),’ or ‘footprint,’ here, the act of treading: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 206">cp. 206</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στίβου κατ’ ἀνάγκαν</quote>, n. Remark how strongly </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτύπος_29"> (L's reading) is confirmed, as against </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύπος_29">, by v. 30, where Odysseus says (in effect), ‘perhaps the reason why you hear no sound is that he is asleep within.’—Other readings are <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου γ’ οὐδεὶς τύπος</quote> (Tricl. and Brunck): <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου )στ’ οὐχ ἑ=ς τύπος</quote> (Mudge): <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου )στ’ οὔδει τύπος</quote> (Bergk; though <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖδας</quote> is the only case of the noun found in Tragedy). These assume that there was sand or earth just in front of the cave on the side towards the sea. But vv. 1000 ff. imply that the cave's seaward mouth opened on steep rocks at some height above the beach. And if v. 29 referred to the presence or absence of foot-prints, v. 30 would lose its special point. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθ_ὕπνον_30">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 970"> <title>Tr.</title> 970</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθ’ ὕπνον ὄντα</quote>: but here <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote> need not be supplied; the phrase is adverbial, with <quote xml:lang="grc">καταυλισθεὶς κυρῇ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταυλισθεὶς_30">, ‘lodged’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 19">cp. 19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλίου</quote>, 153 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλάς</quote>), a word suitable to rough or temporary quarters, as to a bivouac: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 5. 15">Xen. <title> An.</title> 7. 5. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατηυλίσθησαν δ’ ἐν τῷ πεδίῳ</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. El. 304">Soph. <title>El.</title> 304</ref>(Electra speaking of her rustic cot tage) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵοις ἐν πέπλοις αὐλίζομαι</quote> (cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 168 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρότειραν αὐλάν</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατακλιθεὶς_30">, the weak reading of some later MSS., was prob. suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">καθ’ ὕπνον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρῇ_30"> is the reading of our MSS., and, though their authority on such a point is not great, the subjunct. seems here slightly better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖ_30">. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ...κυρεῖ</quote>, ‘see whether he is not,’ would imply that in the speaker's mind there was little doubt on the subject: cp. notes on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 278"> <title>Ant.</title> 278</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1253"> Ant. 1253</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Charm. 163a">Plat. <title>Charm.</title> 163 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅρα μὴ ἐκεῖνον κωλύει</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lach. 196c"> <title>Lach.</title> 196 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὁρῶμεν μὴ Νικίας οἴεταί τι λέγειν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Theaet. 145c"> <title>Theaet.</title> 145 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ παίζων ἔλεγεν</quote> </cit>. These are admonitions in the polite guise of suggestions. Now here we may, indeed, conceive Odysseus as saying <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...<emph> κυρεῖ</emph> </quote>: but, in the anxious uncertainty which he actually feels, it is more natural that he should say <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...<emph>κυρῇ</emph> </quote>. If it be said that general Attic usage rather favours the indic. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μή</quote>, we may refer to <ref n="Soph. El. 1003"> <title>El.</title> 1003</ref> and fr. 82 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅρα μὴ κρεῖσσον ᾖ</quote>) as a few places out of several where the subjunct. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μή</quote> is proved by metre. </gloss>

data.quote: ἀλλ’ ὁρῶμεν μὴ Νικίας οἴεταί τι λέγειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶ_31">. Neoptolemus, mounting the rocks, has now just reached the mouth of the cave. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κενὴν_31"> is made more explicit by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθρώπων_δίχα_31">: ‘empty,—yes, there is no man there.’ Such iteration is natural when the mind confirms itself in a first impression, or dwells on a striking thought; so <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 4.588">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 4. 588</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">vacuos sensit sine remige portus</quote> </cit> (‘empty,—no rower there’); <ref n="Soph. Aj. 464"> <title>Ai.</title> 464</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνὸν φανέντα τῶν ἀριστείων ἄτερ</quote> ‘(when I return) ungraced,—aye, without the meed of valour.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 487">Cp. 487</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 57"> <title>O. T.</title> 57</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 445"> <title>Ant.</title> 445</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκοποιός_τροφή_32">, ‘any comforts, such as make a human dwelling,’ in contradistinction to a wild beast's lair. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφή_32"> here=‘what sustains life,’—not only food and drink, but also provision for necessary repose and warmth: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 667b">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 667 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδωδῇ μὲν καὶ πόσει καὶ ξυμπάσῃ τροφῇ</quote> </cit>, ‘food and drink and the comforts of life generally.’ The question of Odysseus is comprehensive; in reply, Neopt. can only mention a bed; but that does not require us to assume that Od.used <quote xml:lang="grc"> τροφή</quote> in the specific sense of ‘furniture.’ The objection which has been made to <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφή</quote> here thus falls to the ground. Against Welcker's </gloss>

data.quote: ἐδωδῇ μὲν καὶ πόσει καὶ ξυμπάσῃ τροφῇ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρυφή_32">, remark:—(1) The irony would be misplaced here, where Od.is anxiously seeking information; it is otherwise in v. 37, where the slightly ironical tone of <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαύρισμα</quote> shows the first gleam of sinister joy. (2) The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc"> οἰκοποιὸς...τρυφή</quote> would be infelicitous. The adjective itself shows that the substantive ought to denote the rudiments, not the refinements, of a home. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στιπτή_γε_33"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘aye, a heap of leaves pressed down, as if for the use of one who sleeps in the place.’ Here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_33"> serves to correct the suggestion contained in the negative question: ‘There is nothing there?’ ‘Yes, there is something.’ In this use it may be compared with the <emph> Fr.si</emph>, since it is corrective without being emphatic. (‘Vous n'avez pas été là?’—‘Si.’) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 35">Cp. 35</ref>. For the spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">στιπτή</quote>, see v. 2. A bed of leaves (or rushes, etc.) was called <quote xml:lang="grc">στιβάς</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 507">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 507</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στιβάδα πρὸς χαμαιπετῆ</quote>). [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">λεῖπε χαμεύνας φυλλοστρώτους</quote> (of soldiers bivouacking). <quote xml:lang="grc">στιπτή</quote> means, pressed down by the body of the person who has slept on it. Some take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναυλίζοντίτῳ</quote> as dat. of agent with <quote xml:lang="grc">στιπτή</quote> (pressed down <emph>by</emph> some one lodging here); but the order of words renders it simpler to take the dat. as one of interest. Hartung, whom Nauck follows, changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στιπτή_33"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρωτή_33">, finding a hint of the latter in one of the two scholia on this v. in L, <quote xml:lang="grc">χαμαιστρωσία ἐκ φύλλων</quote>. But that may refer to the one word <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλλάς</quote>: while the other scholium unequivocally refers to <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>στιπτή</emph>, —ἡπλωμένη καὶ πατουμένη</quote>, (‘spread out, and <emph>pressed down</emph>,’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς κοιμωμένου ἐπ’ αὐτῇ τινος</quote>. If it be said that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡπλωμένη</quote> might refer to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρωτή_33_2">, we may reply that <quote xml:lang="grc">πατουμένη</quote> could refer only to <quote xml:lang="grc">στιπτή</quote>: and by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡπλωμένη</quote> the schol. meant (I think) to express that the leaves formed, not a soft heap, but only a shallow layer. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στιπτή_33_2"> is more graphic than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρωτή_33_3">: it suggests the recent impress of the body, and the cheerless discomfort of the couch.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_33"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναυλίζοντί τῳ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 203">cp. 203</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἄλλ_34">, all parts of the cave except that covered by the bed of leaves: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρημα_34">, ‘bare,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> without any sign of inhabitation. The second question, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐδέν_34"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, repeats the first in a more precise form. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόξυλον_35">, ‘of mere wood,’ means here, ‘of wood not artistically treated’; the piece of wood remained as nearly in its original state as was compatible with its serving for a cup. Cp. fr. com. 322 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόποκον ἱμάτιον</quote>, a cloak of rough wool: Alexis <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπριος</quote> 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ αὐτόπυρον ἄρτον</quote>, the loaf of unbolten wheat-flour: see <title>O.C.</title> 192 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοπέτρου βήματος</quote> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φλαυρουργοῦ_35">: seemingly the only extant instance of the form <quote xml:lang="grc">φλαῦρος</quote> in a compound adj. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεχνήματ_36">: the poet. plur. has a certain dignity, and there is possibly a shade of designed irony in its use here: <title>Scut.</title> 313 <quote xml:lang="grc">τρίπος, κλυτὰ ἔργα περίφρονος (Ηφαίστοιο</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1053">Eur. <title> Or.</title> 1053</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μνῆμα, ...κέδρου τεχνάσματα</quote> (a coffin,...finely wrought of cedar): <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 5.359">Virg. <title>Aen.</title> 5. 359</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">clypeum … Didymaonis artes.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρεῖα_36">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">igniaria</foreign>, ‘means of kindling a fire,’ the stones mentioned in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 296">296</ref>, and perhaps also bits of wood with which to catch the spark. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνου_37">, predicate, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Apol. 20E">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 20 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἐμὸν ἐρῶ τὸν λόγον</quote> </cit> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος, ὃν ἐρῶ, οὐκ ἐμὸς ἔσται</quote>).</gloss>

data.quote: οὐ γὰρ ἐμὸν ἐρῶ τὸν λόγον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θησαύρισμα_37">, ‘store’ (not so strong as ‘treasure’): the verb <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαυρίζω</quote> was used of ‘laying in’ supplies for household use ( <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 8. 2. 24">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8. 2. 24</ref>); cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 497">Soph. <title>El.</title> 497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαύρισμα Διονύσου</quote> (store of wine). Yet here the word is ironical, since the ‘store’ is so wretched. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰοὺ_ἰού_38">, a cry of surprise, with which the watcher greets the beacon in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 25">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 25</ref>,—where it is ‘extra metrum,’ as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 737."> Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 737.</ref>It stands within the verse, as here, in <ref n="Soph. OT 1071">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1071</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1182">Soph. O. T. 1182</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1143.">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1143.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_γ_38">. In v. 29, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου γ’, γε</quote> specially emphasises the word <quote xml:lang="grc">στίβου</quote>: here, it does not specially emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote>, but helps <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> to introduce the new fact; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it is not, ‘and <emph>here</emph> are rags,’ but rather, ‘<emph>yes</emph>, and here are rags.’ Wherever </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_γε_38"> occurs, it is well to note in which of these two ways it is used. Examples like <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτά γ’</quote> here are, below, 1296 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πέλας γ’</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1132"> <title>O. T.</title> 1132</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐδέν γε θαῦμα</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1319 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ θαῦμά γ’ οὐδέν</quote>. Examples like <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στίβου γ’</quote> are, below, <ref n="Soph. OT 674"> O. T. 674</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σέ γ’ εἰσάξω</quote>: 1277 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πέρα γ’ ἴσθ’ ἢ λέγω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλα_38">, ‘withal’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, besides the other objects already found): cp. <title> O.T.</title> 290 n.: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 424">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγας ὅδ’ ἄλλος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλπεται_38">, ‘are drying’ in the sun at the seaward mouth of the cave (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 17">cp. 17</ref>). Not, ‘are warm’ to the touch,—as if recently used. Cp. <title>Helen.</title> 181 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλίου πέπλους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐγαῖσιν ἐν ταῖς χρυσέαις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιθάλπουσ’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρείας_39">, ‘grievous,’ the epithet of the malady itself, as 1330 <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσου βαρείας</quote>. Not ‘fetid’ (like <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">gravis...hircus,</quote> <bibl n="Hor. Epod. 12.5">Hor. <title>Ep.</title> 12. 5</bibl> </cit>),—a sense in which <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρύς</quote> occurs only when it is the epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀσμή, ἀτμίς</quote> ( <title>Hist. An.</title> 9. 5), etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσηλείας_39"> (subst. from <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσηλός</quote>, ‘morbid,’) here=the matter discharged from the ulcer in the foot; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 824">cp. 824</ref>. Isocr. uses <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσηλεύω</quote> as=‘to tend the sick,’ and Plut. has <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσηλεία</quote> as either (i) ‘sickness,’ or (ii) ‘nursing of the sick.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέα_39">, tainted, stained with: cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 3. 5">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1. 3. 5</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ χεὶρ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέα σοι ἀπ’ αὐτῶν ἐγένετο</quote>, has been <emph>defiled</emph> by those things: so <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρεις</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1017.">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 1017.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_που_41">, as 163 <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας που</quote>, <title>O.T.</title> 1410 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω...που.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηρὶ_41">, ‘plague,’ as 1166 <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρα τάνδ’ ἀποφεύγειν</quote>,—but without ref. to the idea that the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> was ordained by fate (1326).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσβαίη_41">, in the sense of ‘advance,’ where we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">προβαίη</quote>, is certainly strange. It is partly excused, however, by the fact that the speaker is himself outside of the cave, and so can the more naturally place himself in imagination at the external point towards which the movement is made,—saying, ‘<emph>come</emph> far,’ instead of, ‘<emph>go</emph> far.’ I do not feel sure, then, that <quote xml:lang="grc">προσβαίη</quote> is corrupt, though it is suspicious. If corrupt, it probably conceals a compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρό</quote>. In the <title>Classical Review</title> (vol. II. p. 324, 1888) I have conjectured </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκάζοι_41">, ‘limp forth.’ Minuscule <quote xml:lang="grc">β</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κ</quote> often resemble each other (thus in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1094"> <title>Ant.</title> 1094</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λακεῖν</quote> is corrected from <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>). If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκάζοι_41_2"> had become <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσβάζοι</quote>, thelatter would easily have generated <quote xml:lang="grc">προσβαίη</quote>. A verb describing <emph>painful</emph> movement would be fitting here, after <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσῶν...κῶλον παλαιᾷ κηρί</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀγμεύει</quote> (163), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰλυόμην</quote> (291). It is immaterial that this particular compound of <quote xml:lang="grc">σκάζω</quote> does not occur elsewhere; many verbal compounds occur once only, as, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">προδείσας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 90"> <title>O. T.</title> 90</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">προκλίνας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 201"> <title>O. C.</title> 201</ref>). For other conjectures, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορβῆς_νόστον_43">. The defence of this much-impugned phrase depends on three points. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc"> νόστος</quote> is poetically used in the general sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδός</quote>: <title>I. A.</title> 1261 (speaking of the Greeks), <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς νόστος οὐκ ἔστ’ )Ιλίου πύργους ἔπι</quote>. (2) In <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβῆς‐νόστος</quote>, a food-journey, the gen. denotes the object of the <quote xml:lang="grc"> νόστος</quote>: the principle is the same as in <title>I. T.</title> 1066 <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς πατρῴας νόστος</quote>, ‘a fatherland-return,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> a return <emph> to</emph> it: <title>Orph. Argon.</title> 200 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ πλόον Ἀξείνοιο</quote>, on a voyage to the Euxine. (3) The poet has not said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξελήλυθε φορβῆς νόστον</quote> (‘cognate’ acc.), but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξελήλυθεν <emph>ἐπὶ</emph> φορβῆς νόστον</quote>, thus marking that <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστον</quote> denotes, not merely the act of going out, but the purpose of that act, viz., a <emph>quest.</emph> In other words, the presence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> before it already tinges <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστον</quote> with the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ζήτησιν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 4. 140">Her. 4. 140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέστρεφον ἐπὶ ζήτησιν τῶν Περσέων</quote>.—The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἢ )πὶ <emph>φορβὴν</emph> νόστον ἐξελήλυθεν</quote> seems, then, needless; but it is also open to a strong positive objection, viz. that <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστον</quote> then becomes a mere pleonasm. A cognate acc. added to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξελήλυθεν</quote> ought here to qualify it in some manner (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 287">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξόδους ἕρπειν κενάς</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_φύλλον_44"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The constr. is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ, εἰ φύλλον νώδυνόν τι κάτοιδέ που</quote>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτό</quote>): rather than, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλλον, εἴ τι νώδυνον</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> φύλλον</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτοιδέ που.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νώδυνον_44">, in active sense: <title>Anthol.</title> app. 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">φαρμάκοις ἀνωδύνοις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_παρόντα_45">,—‘thy attendant,’— the young chief's <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολος</quote>, who is called <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote> at v. 125. The phrase does not imply that he is actually at his master's side on the rocks. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_καὶ_46">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 13">cp. 13</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπεσών_46">, of sudden and unforeseen approach ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1157"> <title>O. C.</title> 1157</ref>): the same phrase below, <ref n="Soph. OC 156"> O. C. 156</ref>, and <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 338.">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 338.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕλοιτό_μ_47">. The enclitic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_47"> is warranted here (though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτ’ <emph>ἔμ’</emph> </quote> might seem more natural), since the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καὶ λάθῃ με προσπεσών</quote>, have already indicated Odysseus as the person chiefly menaced. It is as though he said: ‘We must take care that he does not surprise me; it would delight him more than to capture all the Greeks’; where the unemphatic ‘it’ would resemble the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> as merely referring back to a case already indicated. A similar instance (and one that is certified by metre) occurs below, 1049 ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ γὰρ τοιούτων δεῖ, τοιοῦτός εἰμ’ ἐγώ·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χὥπου δικαίων κἀγαθῶν ἀνδρῶν κρίσις</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν λάβοις <emph>μου</emph> μᾶλλον οὐδέν’ εὐσεβῆ</quote>: where the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote> in 1049 makes it needless to have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> in 1051. Such cases are distinct from those in which the enclitic form of the pers. pron. is justified by the fact that the chief emphasis is on a verbal notion (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, 958: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 546"> <title>Ant.</title> 546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι θάνῃς σὺ κοινά</quote>, ‘<emph>share</emph> not my death’).—The first hand in L seems to have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτε μ’</quote> (<emph>sic</emph>): the corrector changed the second <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote>, accenting the latter. If there had been reason to think that the first hand in L wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτ’ ἔμ’</quote>, then I should have taken that reading, not as better than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτό μ’</quote>, but as equally good and better attested.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_47">, ‘catch,’ ‘find in his power.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολεῖν_47"> in A was prob. a conjecture, or a mere error, rather than, as Boissonade supposed, a corruption of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_ἑλεῖν_47">. For the difference between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλεῖν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> λαβεῖν</quote> (in regard to warfare), see <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.144"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ ἕλεν Ἀστύνοον</quote> (‘slew’), and <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.159"> <emph>ib.</emph> 159</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ υἷας Πριάμοιο δύω λάβε Δαρδαvίδαο, | εἰν ἑνὶ δίφρῳ ἐόντας</quote> </cit> (‘caught’). Cp. below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101">101</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 103">103</ref>; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 266"> <title>O. T.</title> 266</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζητῶν τὸν αὐτόχειρα τοῦ φόνου λαβεῖν</quote> </cit> (‘find’).—Blaydes says that <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> is ‘clearly wrong,’ and reads </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔνθ’ υἷας Πριάμοιο δύω λάβε Δαρδαvίδαο, | εἰν
							ἑνὶ δίφρῳ ἐόντας
ζητῶν τὸν αὐτόχειρα τοῦ φόνου λαβεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαλεῖν_47"> (‘hit’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_48">, in assent, like ‘oh, well,’ —the implied adversative sense being, ‘nay, I have no objection’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 232">cp. 232</ref>, 336, 645, 647.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρχεται_48">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ παρών</quote> (45), ‘he goes,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘I send him’ (said as he makes a sign to the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολος</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1181">Cp. 1181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...ἔλθῃς</quote>, ‘<emph>depart</emph> not’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 99"> <title>Ant.</title> 99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνους μὲν ἔρχει</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 595"> <title>Tr.</title> 595</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεύσεται</quote> (‘depart’).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_καὶ_48"> marks the full assent to v. 45: he shall go, and for that purpose.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυλάξεται_48">, the fut. pass. in good prose also ( <ref n="Xen. Ec. 4. 9">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 4. 9</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλαχθήσομαι</quote> was late. For other such futures, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 303">cp. 303</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 93"> <title>Ant.</title> 93</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δευτέρῳ_λόγῳ_48">, ‘in <emph>further</emph> speech,’— continuing the former discourse. Cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 1"> Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 43</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δευτέρῳ χρόνῳ</quote> ,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑστέρῳ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_οἷς_50">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τούτοις ἐφ’ οἷς</quote>, ‘for’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘so as to aid’) ‘the objects for which,’ etc.; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1457"> <title>O. T.</title> 1457</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )πί τῳ δεινῷ κακῷ</quote>.—The sentence begins as if the form were to be, <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ...γενναῖον εἶναι, μὴ μόνον τῷ σώματι, ἀλλὰ καὶ τῇ γνώμῃ</quote>: he must show his true-bred spirit, not merely <emph> physically</emph>, but <emph>morally</emph>,—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, by bringing himself (<cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμα,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 82">82</bibl> </cit>) to aid plans which may be repugnant to him. Neopt. supposed that his task was to take Phil. by force (<cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς βίαν,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 90">90</bibl> </cit>). Odysseus seeks to prepare the disclosure very gently. Hence the hypothetical clause which takes the place of a simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ καὶ τῇ γνώμῃ</quote>, viz., <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀλλ’ ἤν τι καινόν</emph>, κ.τ.λ.</quote> After that clause, a modal partic., <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπουργοῦντα</quote> (‘by serving’), ought to have balanced the instrumental dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ σώματι</quote>. But, instead of it, we have a second infin., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπουργεῖν_50">, depending, like <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>, on <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote>: just as, in independent sentences, a new finite verb is often substituted for a second participial clause ( <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title> 351</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 256"> <title>Ant.</title> 256</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 816"> Ant. 816</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενναῖον_50">, ‘true-bred.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γενναῖον</quote> is, as Arist. defines it (<title>Hist. An.</title> 1. 1. 32), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ ἐξιστάμενον ἐκ τῆς αὑτοῦ φύσεως</quote>. Odysseus calls on Neopt. to prove himself a true son of his sire (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 3">cp. 3</ref>) by complete loyalty to his mission.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_σώματι_50">: cp. <title>Suppl.</title> 886 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποις τε χαίρων τόξα τ’ ἐντείνων χεροῖν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλει παρασχεῖν σῶμα χρήσιμον θέλων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινόν_50">, euphemistic, as oft.: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">α. δ.</quote> § 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">καινότατα γὰρ δή, εἰ χρὴ καινότατα μᾶλλον ἢ κακουργότατα εἰπεῖν, διαβάλλουσί με.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_50"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἃ</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_ἀκήκοας_50">, ‘(some novel thing), viz., one of those things which thou hast not heard before’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘a part of my plans which has not hitherto been disclosed to thee.’ Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 356">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 356</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τι δράσεις δεινόν, ὧν φόβος μ’ ἔχει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπηρέτης_53">, like <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑπηρετεῖν</quote> in 15, said of a friend and equal. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 821">Soph. <title>El.</title> 821</ref>(Orestes) <quote xml:lang="grc">Πυλάδην μὲν εἵλετ’ ἐν πόνοις ὑπηρέτην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δμῶας δ’ ἀπωθεῖ</quote>: and so even in good prose, as <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 9. 18.">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 9. 18.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἄνωγας_54">; The division of the verse between the speakers (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλαβή</quote>) serves at once to mark the surprise of Neopt. and to introduce the words of Od.with a certain abrupt force: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 722"> <title>O. C.</title> 722</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_δεῖ_54"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Two other examples of this constr. are extant: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 556"> <title>Ai.</title> 556</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ σ’ ὅπως πατρὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δείξεις ἐν ἐχθροῖς οἶος ἐξ ὅου )τράφης</quote>: Cratinus (the poet of the Old Comedy), <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέμεσις</quote> fr. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ σ’ ὅπως εὐσχήμονος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεκτρυόνος μηδὲν διοίσεις τοὺς τρόπους</quote>. In both these passages, as in this, the constr. is used by an elder, or superior, in giving a precept of conduct. The admonitory tone thus associated with the formula confirms the text, as against Matthiae's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">σε <emph> δεῖν</emph> ψυχὴν ὅπως λόγοισιν ἐκκλέψεις <emph>λέγω</emph> </quote>. The acc. of the object (<quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>) is like that in <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ σε τούτου</quote>: while the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> clause (answering to the genitive there) is like that in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδέοντο Βοιωτοὺς ὅπως παραδώσουσι</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 5. 36">Thuc. 5. 36</ref>§ 2). The partic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_54"> explains the instrum. dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισιν</quote> more clearly; it is not instrumental (‘<emph>by</emph> speaking’), but temporal; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, literally, ‘<emph>as you go on speaking.</emph>’ It indicates that Neopt. is to converse alone with Phil. (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 70">cp. 70</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμιλία</quote>), and is to deceive him in the course of their conversation. The next verse makes this still clearer:—‘<title>When he asks</title>, say,’ etc. A similar use of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγων</quote>, to denote the process of talk, is frequent in Herod. , when, after epitomising part of a speech, he gives the sequel in the speaker's own words; as 3. 156, ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν τε,’ ἔφη λέγων</quote>,... ‘And now,’ he <emph>went on</emph> to say,...(lit., said, as he went on speaking).—Other ways of taking </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_54_2">, which seem less good, are:—(1) As instrum. partic., with which <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτούς</quote> is to be supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισιν</quote>: ‘with words,...<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, by speaking them.’ For this view, Schneidewin cp. <title>Legg.</title> 885 B <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα λόγῳ καὶ ὅσα ἔργῳ περὶ θεοὺς ὑβρίζει τις λέγων ἢ πράττων</quote>. (2) As instrum. partic., used absolutely, to emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc"> λόγοισιν</quote>,—‘with words,—I repeat, by speaking.’ (3) As instrum. partic., to be taken closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισιν</quote>, in the sense, ‘speaking vain words.’ This is Seyffert's view, who explains <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις λέγειν</quote> as <emph>meris verbis dicere:</emph> a sense which the phrase could not bear.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκλέψεις_54">: here related to <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτειν</quote>, <emph>fallere</emph> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 243"> <title>Tr.</title> 243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ συμφοραὶ κλέπτουσί με</quote>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαπατᾶν</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπατᾶν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 968">Cp. 968</ref> . <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.217"> <title>Il.</title> 14. 217</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ τ’ ἔκλεψε νόον πύκα περ φρονεόντων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_57">, infin. for imper. (<ref n="Soph. OC 481"> <title>O. C.</title> 481</ref> n.); not depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 54">54</ref>.— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>Ἀχιλλέως</emph>, ˘¯¯</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> suffers synizesis again in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 364">364</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 582">582</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1066">1066</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1237">1237</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1298"> 1298</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1312">1312</ref>: though not in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 4">4</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 50">50</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 241">241</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 260">260</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1220">1220</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1433"> 1433.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδ_κλεπτέον_57">: lit., ‘this thing’ (his parentage) ‘must not be represented falsely,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the truth must not be hidden. <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτειν τι</quote> can mean, ‘to do (or speak) a thing fraudulently’: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 189"> <title>Ai.</title> 189</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτουσι μύθους</quote>, they speak false words. In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 437"> <title>Tr.</title> 437</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...ἐκκλέψῃς λόγον</quote>= ‘do not <emph>keep back</emph> the story’; but the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτειν</quote> could not literally express this.<emph> —<quote xml:lang="grc">κρυπτέον</quote> </emph> is a tame conjecture. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖς_58"> is more dramatic than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖν_58">, which would also be awkward after <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_οἶκον_58">. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> states the direction of the voyage: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> merely adds an indication of the voyager's purpose: ‘thou art homeward <emph> bound.</emph>’ (Not, ‘thou art sailing <emph>as if</emph> for home,’ with ref. to the story being untrue.) Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 44"> <title>Ai.</title> 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καὶ τὸ βούλευμ’ ὡς ἐπ’ Ἀργείοις τόδ’ ἦν</quote>; ‘was this plot, in his intention (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>), against the Greeks?’ (though the actual victims were the cattle). <ref n="Thuc. 4. 93">Thuc. 4. 93</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεσκευάζετο ὡς ἐς μάχην</quote>, made his dispositions with a view (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>) to fighting. <title> H.</title> 1. 1. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγεσθαι ἤδη αὐτοῦ μέλλοντος ὡς ἐπὶ ναυμαχίαν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχθος_μέγα_58">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1034"> <title>El.</title> 1034</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ αὖ τοσοῦτον ἔχθος ἐχθαίρω σ’ ἐγώ</quote>. For the aor. part. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 227">cp. 227</ref>, 309: <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title> N.</title> 7. 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλήσαντ’</quote> (having formed a friendship); <ref n="Soph. OT 11">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 11</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. OT 649">Soph. O. T. 649</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_60">, with causal force (Lat. <emph>qui</emph> with subjunct.): <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λιταῖς_60">, by means of prayers: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 102">cp. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δόλῳ...ἄγειν</quote>, 1393 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν λόγοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 764"> <title>Ant.</title> 764</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείλαντες_μολεῖν_60">: lit., having caused thee to set forth, so that thou shouldst come from home: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 164"> <title>Ant.</title> 164</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς δ’ ἐγὼ πομποῖσιν ἐκ πάντων δίκα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστειλ’ ἱκέσθαι</quote>. Odysseus and Phoenix were sent from Troy to bring the young Neoptolemus from Scyros: 343 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνην_61">. If L's <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνην <emph> δ’</emph> </quote> were sound, then <quote xml:lang="grc">στείλαντες</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντες δέ</quote> would express two reasons why the conduct of the Atreidae was bad:—‘when they had brought thee from home, and when that was their only way of taking Troy,’— the second clause implying that, as his presence was so momentous, his claim to good treatment was the stronger. But <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνην</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote>, is clearly right. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντες</quote> is causal, expressing the motive of <quote xml:lang="grc">στείλαντες</quote>,—‘having brought thee,... since they had no other way,’ etc. The insertion of <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote>, if not a mere error, may have been due to a corrector who, not perceiving the relation of the two participles, thought that they required a copula.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅλωσιν_61">, <emph>means of</emph> capture: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 75">Thuc. 2. 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶμα ἔχουν πρὸς τὴν πόλιν, νομίζοντες ταχίστην αἵρεσιν</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν αἵρεσιν</quote> Classen] <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσεσθαι αὐτῶν</quote> (the quickest <emph>way of taking</emph> the place). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ὅπλων_62">, gen. depending on the principal verb </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠξίωσαν_62">, instead of an acc., <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ Ἀχίλλεια ὅπλα</quote>, depending on the infin. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦναι_62">. This construction arises from eagerness for compact expression of the main idea,— as here the main idea is completely expressed by v. 62. The ‘epexegetic’ infin., like </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦναι_62_2">, is usu. the only word added: but here it is naturally supplemented by the words which denote the aggravating circumstances (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντι...κυρίως αἰτουμένῳ</quote>). <title>Legg.</title> 941 D <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκης οὖν οὐδέτερον οὐδετέρου ἐλάττονος...ὁ νόμος ἀξιοῖ ζημιοῦν</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιοῖ ζημιοῦν δίκῃ</quote>). <ref n="Thuc. 3. 6">Thuc. 3. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῆς μὲν θαλάσσης εἶργον μὴ χρῆσθαι τοὺς Μυτιληναίους</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1211">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1211</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρίως_62">, with good right (<emph>tuo iure</emph>), as heir of Achilles; cp. <ref n="Dem. 36.32">Dem. or. 36 § 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρίως δόντος τοῦ πατρὸς...κατὰ τοὺς νόμους αὐτὴν γεγαμῆσθαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέδοσαν_64">, handed over,—a word suggesting fraud or treachery, as oft.; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 399">cp. 399</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_64"> refers back to <quote xml:lang="grc"> λέγειν</quote> in 57 (with which, as infin. for imperat., the nomin. is rightly used in the 2nd pers., <ref n="Soph. OT 1529"> <title>O. T.</title> 1529</ref> n.). Odysseus leaves the available epithets to his young friend's imagination. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1287"> <title>O. T.</title> 1287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷ διοίγειν κλῇθρα καὶ δηλοῦν τινα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πᾶσι Καδμείοισι τὸν πατροκτόνον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μητρός, αὐδῶν ἀνόσἰ οὐδὲ ῥητά μοι</quote>. <title>I. T.</title> 16 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ λέγει Κάλχας τάδε·</quote> | ...‘<quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδ’ οὖν ἐν οἴκοις σὴ Κλυταιμνήστρα δάμαρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τίκτει’— τὸ καλλιστεῖον εἰς ἔμ’ ἀναφέρων</quote>— | ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν χρή σε θῦσαι.’</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθ_ἡμῶν_64">, in this context, seems best taken as=<quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἐμοῦ</quote>: for the sing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_64"> so closely following, see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734"> <title>Ant.</title> 734</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις γὰρ ἡμῖν ἁμὲ χρὴ τάσσειν ἐρεῖ;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχατ_ἐσχάτων_64">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 465"> <title>O. T.</title> 465</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρρητ’ ἀρρήτων</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτωνγὰρ_ἀλγυνεῖς_64"> is probably that which stood in L's archetype; for the inserted <quote xml:lang="grc">ν</quote>, by which <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέμ’</quote> has been made into <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδένμ’</quote>, is due to the first corrector of L, who revised the work of the scribe by comparing the copy with the original. The first question, then, is whether that reading can be kept. It is required to mean:—‘for <emph>in regard to no one of these things</emph>’ (viz., the <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote>, taunts) ‘wilt thou pain me.’ But it would properly mean:—‘for thou wilt not cause me any of these pains.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1021">Cp. 1021</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ἀλγύνομαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ αὔθ’ ὅτι ζῶ κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘I feel just this pain,—that I live,’ etc.: <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 2">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσθην δὲ βαιὰ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ δ’ ὠδυνήθην, κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 550">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 550</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ταῦτ’ ἀνιᾷς μ’</quote> (cause me this distress). Before <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων γὰρ οὐδέν μ’ ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> could be accepted, it would be needful to show that a cognate acc. (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote>) could thus replace an instrum. dat. The next question concerns its origin. It might be suggested that the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέμ’</quote> of the 1st hand in L came, not from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν μ’</quote>, but from <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὐδέν’</quote>, and that the sense is, ‘thou wilt pain no one of <emph> them</emph>’ (masc.),—so that <quote xml:lang="grc">καθ’ ἡμῶν</quote> in 65 should mean, Odysseus and the Atreidae. But this cannot be; for, here, there has been no direct mention of the Atreidae,—only of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιῶν</quote> generally (59); and so, for contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσιν Ἀργείοις</quote> (67), the pain denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> must be pain to Odysseus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_ἀλγυνεῖς_64"> being thus set aside, we have to weigh (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_μ_64">,— Dindorf's conjecture; and (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτῳγὰρ_ἀλγυνεῖς_64">,—Buttmann's. Both being possible, the question is, which of them is most likely to have generated <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων γὰρ οὐδέν μ’ ἀλγυνεῖς</quote>. The fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> precedes <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐργάσει</quote> diminishes the probability that <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> arose from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνεῖ μ’</quote> by assimilation of persons. Further, had <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν μ’ ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> come from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἀλγυνεῖ μ’</quote>, we might have expected to find a variant, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἀλγυνεῖς μ’</quote>. If, on the other hand, the words <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν μ’ ἀλγυνεῖς</quote> are genuine, we have only to suppose a change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτωι_64"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_64">. On these palaeographical grounds Buttmann's reading appears preferable to Dindorf's. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_67">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἐργάσει μή</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ἐργάσει</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 332">cp. 332</ref>, 653, <ref n="Soph. OT 328"> <title>O. T.</title> 328</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. El. 993"> <title>El.</title> 993</ref>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> as first word of a verse, when a word with which it is construed stands in the preceding verse, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1349"> <title>O. C.</title> 1349</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OT 348"> <title>O. T.</title> 348</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαλεῖς_67"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμβαλεῖς</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσβαλεῖς</quote>), ‘inflict’ on them: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 915"> <title>Tr.</title> 915</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">δεμνίοις</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">βάλλουσαν φάρη</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1534">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1534</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκότον ὄμμασι σοῖσι βαλών</quote>. In poetry the simple dat. (instead of dat. or accus. with a prep.) is sometimes thus used to denote the object to, or against, which an action is directed: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1232">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1232</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας προσώπῳ</quote>. Not, ‘launch against them,’ as though the <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπη</quote> were a missile; nor, ‘sow’ sorrow for them, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίας μοι κατασπείρας</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1005.">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1005.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_68">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἔστι_68"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> ‘if the bow is not to be taken, then it is impossible’ etc. Here the condition expressed by the fut. ind. in the protasis is really a present one; the meaning is, ‘if it is (now) settled that the bow is not to be taken.’ Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 4. 39">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 4. 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστι παρελθεῖν, εἰ μὴ τούτους ἀποκόψομεν</quote>: ‘it is an impossibility to advance, if we are not to dislodge these men’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> assuming that we do not mean to dislodge them). Practically, this is a more emphatic way of expressing the necessity of the act to which the protasis refers. Distinguish those cases in which the condition expressed by the fut. indic. is really future; as in 66 f., <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ἐργάσει</quote> (‘if thou fail to do this’), <quote xml:lang="grc">βαλεῖς</quote>: and in 75 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ με αἰσθήσεται</quote> (‘if he shall perceive me’), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλα</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοῦμαι</quote>): where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μὴ ἐργάσῃ, ἐάν με αἴσθῃ</quote> would differ from the fut. ind. with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> only as being somewhat less vivid.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_σοι_68">. The difference between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_68"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_68"> here resembles that between ‘thou canst <emph>never</emph> take’ and ‘<emph> thou</emph> canst never take.’ L supports </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_68_2">, which is, of course, quite tenable. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_68_2"> seems preferable, because (<emph>a</emph>) in giving a reason, as <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> implies, why ‘all the Greeks’ will be pained, it seems less fitting to place the personal concern of Neoptolemus in the foreground; and (<emph>b</emph>) the necessary emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</quote> in v. 70 would have a slightly awkward effect if the same pron. had been emphasised in v. 69. Cp. n. on 47, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτό μ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πέδον_68">, the land of Dardanus,—meaning <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροία</quote> in its larger sense, the town with its territory (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 920">cp. 920</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ Τροίας πεδία</quote>, 1435 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλεῖν τὸ Τροίας πεδίον</quote>). So <ref n="Soph. OC 380"> <title>O. C.</title> 380</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Καδμείων πέδον</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβης πέδον</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 415). Dardanus, son of Zeus, was fifth ancestor of Priam ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.215"> <title>Il.</title> 20. 215</ref> ff.). Cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 13"> Pind. <title>O.</title> 13. 56</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸ Δαρδάνου τειχέων</quote>: <title>Helen.</title> 1493 <quote xml:lang="grc"> Δαρδάνου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_οὐχί_70"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 2. 5. 35"> Xen. <title>An.</title> 2. 5. 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ πάντες μὲν οὐκ ἦλθον, Ἀριαῖος δὲ καὶ Ἀρτάοζος κ.τ.λ.</quote> —Odysseus anticipates the objection that, if there is to be a stratagem, he should conduct it himself,—as Aesch. and Eur. had made him do: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 13">cp. 13</ref> n.</gloss>
lemma: ὡς δ’ ἔστ’ ἐμοὶ μὲν οὐχί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμιλία_70">, merely ‘intercourse,’ in a general sense: the special meaning, ‘colloquy,’ (seen in the Gk. <quote xml:lang="grc">μοδ. ὁμιλέω</quote>=‘to speak,’) is postclassical.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστὴ_70">, trusted by Philoctetes; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1272">cp. 1272</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βέβαιος_70">, safe for Neoptolemus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνορκος_72">. Odysseus was bound by the oath which all the suitors of Helen had sworn to her father Tyndareus,— that they would come to her husband's aid, if he was robbed of her: <title>I. A.</title> 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου γυνὴ γένοιτο Τυνδαρὶς κόρη</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ ξυναμυνεῖν, εἴτις ἐκ δόμων λαβὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχοιτο</quote>. So Ajax came to Troy <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕνεχ’ ὅρκων οἷσιν ἦν ἐνώμοτος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1113"> <title>Ai.</title> 1113</ref>). Paus. was shown the place, called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἵππου μνῆμα</quote>, on the road from Lacedaemon into Arcadia, where Tyndareus, having sacrificed a horse, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς Ἑλένης ἐξώρκου μνηστῆρας</quote> (3. 20. 9).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἀνάγκης_72">: Odysseus feigned madness, in order to avoid going to Troy, but Palamedes detected the trick: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1025">cp. 1025</ref> n.<emph>—<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ πρώτου στόλου</quote> </emph>, partit. gen., thou hast not sailed ‘on’ (=‘as a member of’) the first expedition. Cp. <ref n="Dem. 21.202">Dem. or. 21 § 202</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμοῦ πώποτε ὁ Μειδίας τῶν συνηδομένων οὐδὲ τῶν συγχαιρόντων ἐξητάσθη τῷ δήμῳ</quote> (‘has nowhere figured in the ranks of those who share the pleasure and joy of the people’).—The <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῶτος στόλος</quote> is the original Greek expedition, as distinguished from the voyage of Odysseus and Phoenix when they brought Neop. from Scyros (343 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκρατὴς_75">: for the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>, even when, as here, the adj. marks a condition, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1327"> <title>Ant.</title> 1327</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βράχιστα γὰρ κράτιστα τἀν ποσὶν κακά</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">βράχιστα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτιστά</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄλωλα_75">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1166"> <title>O. T.</title> 1166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλας, εἴ σε ταῦτ’ ἐρήσομαι πάλιν</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 8. 12">Xen. <title> An.</title> 1. 8. 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν τοῦτ’, ἔφη, νικῶμεν, πάνθ’ ἡμῖν πεποίηται</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Pl. Am. 1.1">Plaut. <title>Amphitruo</title> 1. 1. 64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">perii, si me adspexerit.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσδιαφθερῶ_75"> ought not to be changed (as Tournier proposed) to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσδιαφθείρω_75">. The force of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλα</quote>, used in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοῦμαι</quote>, would be weakened, not enhanced, by a repetition of the device; while, on the other hand, the natural future <quote xml:lang="grc">προσδιαφθερῶ</quote> makes the rhetorical <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλα</quote> more impressive: cp. <title>I. T.</title> 1002 <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτου δὲ χωρισθεῖσ’</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χωρισθήσομαι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ μὲν ὄλλυμαι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἂν τὸ σαυτοῦ θέμενος εὖ νόστου τύχοις</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸ_τοῦτο_77"> prepares the emphasis on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλοπεὺς_77">, while it also refers back to 54 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν Φιλοκτήτου...ἐκκλέψεις</quote>. The connection of thought is:—‘No; open force is out of the question; the object which our ingenuity must compass is precisely that (which I have already indicated),—viz., how the bow can be taken by craft.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφισθῆναι_77">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1401">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1401</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρίεντά γ’, ὦ πρεσβῦτ’, ἐσοφίσω καὶ σοφά.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλοπεὺς_γενήσει_77">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 721"> <title>O. T.</title> 721</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονέα γενέσθαι πατρός</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 582"> <title>O. C.</title> 582</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν θάνω )γὼ καὶ σύ μου ταφεὺς γένῃ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξοιδα_Παῖ_79">. Erfurdt's correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_79"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖ_79"> appears certain. The caressing tone of <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖ</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1008"> <title>O. T.</title> 1008</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1289"> <title>Ant.</title> 1289</ref>) is dramatically happy at this moment, when he has just used the jarring word <quote xml:lang="grc"> κλοπεύς</quote>. The arguments in defence of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_79_2"> are examined in the Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσει_79"> is excusably added to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεφυκότα_79">, since the force of the latter had become weakened by usage (<quote xml:lang="grc">πεφυκέναι</quote> oft. meaning little more than <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>): as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφυκότα...τεχνᾶσθαι</quote> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσει</quote>) would not necessarily mean more than ‘<emph> apt</emph> to contrive,’—whether the aptitude was innate, or acquired. So <ref n="Eur. Ba. 896">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 896</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσει πεφυκός</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 389c">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 389 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φύσει ἑκάστῳ πεφυκὸς ὄργανον. </quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνεῖν_79">: for the inf. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφυκότα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 88">cp. 88</ref>, 1052. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδὺ_κτῆμα_81"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κτῆμα</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_λαβεῖν_81"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>): the possession consisting in victory (defining gen.) is a pleasant possession to win. <quote xml:lang="grc"> κτῆμα</quote>, which, without an art., stands as predicate, has to be supplied, with an art., as subject. So <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Theaet. 209e">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 209 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδὺ χρῆμ’ ἂν εἴη τοῦ καλλίστου τῶν περὶ ἐπιστήμης λόγου</quote> </cit>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ χρῆμα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ...λόγου ἡδὺ χρῆμ’ ἂν εἴη</quote>, (‘our most successful definition of knowledge would be a pretty affair’): where (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ χρῆμα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ...λόγου</quote> is a mere periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...λόγος</quote>. <ref n="Eur.
