Andr. 957">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 957</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφόν τι χρῆμα τοῦ διδάξαντος βροτοὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγους ἀκούειν</quote>, ‘a wonder of wisdom was he who taught’ (etc.), where (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ χρῆμα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ διδάξαντος</quote> is a periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ διδάξας</quote>. Sometimes the defining gen. has no art.: <ref n="Eur. Andr. 181">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίφθονόν τι χρῆμα θηλειῶν ἔφυ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">θηλειῶν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆμα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίφθονόν τι χρῆμά ἐστι</quote>.—The reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδὺ γάρ <emph>τοι</emph> </quote> (instead of </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡδὺ χρῆμ’ ἂν εἴη τοῦ καλλίστου τῶν περὶ
							ἐπιστήμης λόγου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_81">) is preferred by several edd. The combination <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ... γάρ τοι</quote> is unusual (no example occurs in Soph. ); but that matters little, since here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ γάρ</quote> is not elliptically used (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 148">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 148</ref> n.); <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμα</quote>, and therefore <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>, in the parenthetic clause, could be followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> as legitimately as if there were no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> in question. The reasons for preferring </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_81_2"> seem to be these: (<emph>a</emph>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_81"> would be bluntly sententious, while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_81_3"> has a more delicate persuasiveness: (<emph>b</emph>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_81_4"> is elsewhere associated with the peculiar constr. used here: see <ref n="Eur. Andr. 181">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 181</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Andr. 957">Eur. Andr. 957</ref>, quoted above, and <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 727"> <emph>ib.</emph> 727</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνειμένον τι χρῆμα πρεσβυτῶν ἔφυ</quote> </cit> .—(<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κτῆμα</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆςvίκης_81">: for the defining gen., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 159">cp. 159</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 403">403 f.</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1474"> <title>O. T.</title> 1474</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ φίλτατ’ ἐκγόνοιν ἐμοῖν</quote> (‘my darlings—my two daughters’), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 471"> <title>Ant.</title> 471</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γέννημα τῆς παιδός.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_81"> epexeg., as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 439"> <title>Ant.</title> 439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦθ’ ἥσσω λαβεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πέφυκε</quote>, n. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαχεῖν_81"> would be as good, but no better. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμα_82">, bring thyself to do it: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 481">cp. 481</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 184"> <title>O. C.</title> 184</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 528."> <title>Ai.</title> 528.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαιοι_ἐκφανούμεθα_82"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντες</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1063"> <title>O. T.</title> 1063</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφανεῖ κακή</quote> ): <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.278"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ ὅ τε δειλὸς ἀνήρ, ὅς τ’ ἄλκιμος, ἐξεφαάνθη.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_82">, afterwards, — some other day: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1204"> <title>Ant.</title> 1204</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δ_83"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, has been suggested by the contrast with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_83">, and so the thought already conveyed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμα</quote> is re-stated more explicitly: then <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμέρας μέρος βραχύ</quote> suggests, in its turn, <quote xml:lang="grc">κᾆτα τὸν λοιπὸν χρόνον κ.τ.λ.</quote>, which repeats the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαιοι δ’ αὖθις ἐκφανούμεθα</quote>. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 465"> <title>Ant.</title> 465</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_βραχὺ_83">, ‘for one little roguish day’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμέρας μέρος βραχὺ</quote>=‘a short space (consisting in) one day’ (cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1247">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 1247</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τήνδε γε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λαθοῦ βραχεῖαν ἡμέραν παίδων σέθεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄπειτα θρήνει</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέρος_83"> is a fraction of the life-time which is before him: and since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμέρας‐μέρος</quote> (‘dayspace’) forms one notion, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναιδὲς_83"> has the same force as if it were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναιδοῦς</quote>, agreeing with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἡμέρας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος—ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote>, n.). For several epithets joined (without copula) to one subst., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 586">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 586</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποντίαις ... δυσπνόοις</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">Θρῄσσαισιν... πνοαῖς</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_83"> marking a limit of time, cp. below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1076">1076</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον τοσοῦτον εἰς ὅσον τά τ’ ἐκ νεὼς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στείλωσι ναῦται, κ.τ.λ.</quote> —Others take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἀναιδὲς_83"> by itself, as=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἀναίδειαν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμ. μέρος βρ.</quote> being acc. of duration of time), ‘for shamelessness.’ Such an abstract sense for the neut. adj., without the art., seems impossible. Campbell compares <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 504c">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 504 C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ γὰρ δοκεῖ ταῖς μὲν τοῦ σώματος τάξεσιν ὄνομα εἶναι ὑγιεινόν</quote> (as though <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑγιεινόν</quote> stood for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ὑγιεινόν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑγίειαν</quote>): but cp. Cope's ver sion: ‘For my opinion is, that order in the body of every kind bears the name of ‘healthy’:’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄνομα</quote> is equiv. to ‘epithet.’ In <ref n="Thuc. 5. 18">Thuc. 5. 18</ref>§ 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίῳ χρήσθων καὶ ὅρκοις, δικαίῳ</quote> is certainly a subst. (‘law,’ in the sense of ‘legal procedure’): but that does not warrant <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναιδὲς</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναίδεια</quote>. —Blaydes, again takes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἀναιδὲς_83_2"> in a concrete sense, as=‘for a shameless <emph>deed</emph>’ (supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote>). We can hardly supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote>, though we might perhaps supply <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 517">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 517</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1312">Soph. O. T. 1312</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 687"> Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 687</ref>). This view seems just possible, but very improbable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δός_σεαυτόν_84">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> allow me to overrule your scruples, a phrase applicable to friendly remonstrance, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1117"> <title>Tr.</title> 1117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δός μοι σεαυτόν, μὴ τοσοῦτον ὡς δάκνει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμῷ δύσοργος</quote>: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 718."> <title>Ant.</title> 718.</ref>Brunck cp. <title>Adelph.</title> 5. 3. 838 <title>Mitte iam istaec: da te hodie mihi:</title> | <title>Exporge frontem.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἐκλησο_84">: for the perf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 119">cp. 119</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 366"> <title>El.</title> 366</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 736."> <title>Tr.</title> 736.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_86"> merely emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref> n.); it is not opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 90">90</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λαερτίου_86">: the same form (always in the 1st or 5th place, the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_86"> being long) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 417">417</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 628">628</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1357">1357</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 101"> <title>Ai.</title> 101</ref>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">Λαέρτου</quote> below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 366">366</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 614"> 614</ref>, <ref>fr. 827</ref>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">Λαρτίου</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 401">401</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1286">1286</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1"> <title>Ai.</title> 1</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 380">380</ref>. Eur. , too, has these three forms: while in the Hom. <title>Od.</title>, where the name occurs 22 times, <quote xml:lang="grc">Λαέρτης</quote> alone is used.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούσδε_86">, referring back to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὓς_ἂν_86">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1332"> <title>O. C.</title> 1332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς ἂν σὺ προσθῇ τοῖσδ’ ἔφασκ’ εἶναι κράτος</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. El. 441"> <title>El.</title> 441</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 23"> <title>Tr.</title> 23</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 820."> Tr. 820.</ref>Prose would here use <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους</quote>, because <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος</quote> regularly (though not always) points back, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> points forward. Buttmann's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_δὲ_86">, though admissible, would be too emphatic: see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τέχνης_88">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>=‘by means of,’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 563">cp. 563</ref>, 710, <ref n="Soph. El. 279"> <title>El.</title> 279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δόλου</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475"> <title>Ant.</title> 475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπτὸν ἐκ πυρὸς περισκελῆ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_88">: for the inf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 80">cp. 80</ref>: for the repetition of the word from 87, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title> 554</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 76"> <title>Ant.</title> 76</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_αὐτὸς_88"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">γὰρ αὐτὸς ἔφυν, οὔτε ὁ ἐκφύσας</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφυ</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 461"> <title>O. C.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάξιος μέν, Οἰδίπους, κατοικτίσαι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός τε παῖδές θ’ αἵδ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔθ_οὑκφύσας_88">: as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.312"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 312</ref>Achilles says, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρὸς γάρ μοι κεῖνος ὁμῶς Ἀΐδαο πύλῃσιν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς χ’ ἕτερον μὲν κεύθῃ ἐνὶ φρεσίν, ἄλλο δὲ εἴπῃ</quote>: and in <title>I. A.</title> 926 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ἐν ἀνδρὸς εὐσεβεστάτου τραφεὶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Χείρωνος ἔμαθον τοὺς τρόπους ἁπλοῦς ἔχειν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_βίαν_90">: so 594 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἰσχύος κράτος</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἡδονήν, πρὸς χάριν</quote>, etc.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγειν_90">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγειν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 941">cp. 941</ref>), as 102, 985 etc. —<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καὶ μὴ δόλοισιν</emph>· μή</quote> is generic (it does not, and could not here, go with the inf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγειν</quote> ): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the phrase means strictly, ‘and by such means as are not frauds’: cp. on 409 (<quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν δίκαιον</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 494"> <title>Ant.</title> 494</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μηδὲν ὀρθῶς...τεχνωμένων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γὰρ_90"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> implies, ‘this ought to satisfy thee, <emph>for</emph> force cannot fail': it is thus already a trace of irresolution. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ποδὸς_90">, lit. starting from one foot,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘when he has the use of only one foot,’—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> marking that <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς πούς</quote> is the condition which makes his victory impossible. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 848"> <title>O. C.</title> 848</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν ποτ’ ἐκ τούτοιν γε μὴ σκήπτροιν ἔτι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιπορήσῃς</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. El. 455"> <title>El.</title> 455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀρέστην ἐξ ὑπερτέρας χερὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθροῖσιν...ἐπεμβῆναι</quote>, (‘that, with victorious might, he may trample on his foes,’) the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερτέρα χείρ</quote> is similarly the antecedent condition.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσούσδε_90"> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντας</quote>, cp. n. on <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐγκρατής</quote>, 75): the 15 seamen who form the Chorus will be at hand to help them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεμφθείς_μέντοι_93">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> γε</quote> emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">πεμφθείς</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">μέντοι</quote>=‘however’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 442"> <title>O. T.</title> 442</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προδότης_93">: he is thinking of what Od.said in v. 53.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>βούλομαι</emph> δ’</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85"> <title>Ant.</title> 85</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαμαρτεῖν_93">, aor., ‘to fail’ (regarded as an event occurring at some one moment); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νικᾶν_93">, pres., ‘to be victorious’ (a continuing state: <ref n="Soph. OT 437"> <title>O. T.</title> 437</ref> n.). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1397">Cp. 1397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχειν...παθεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργόν_97">, fem.: in good Attic prose as well as verse this adj. is of two terminations, and the v. l. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργήν</quote> here represents only a later usage: Aristotle (if our MSS. may be trusted) wrote in <title>Meteor.</title> I. 14 (Berl. ed. p. 352 <emph>a</emph> 13) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μὲν γὰρ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χώρα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργὴ γέγονε</quote>, yet in <ref n="Xen. Ec. 2"> <title>Oecon.</title> 2</ref>(Berl. ed. p. 1348 <emph>a</emph> 3) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς χώρας ἀργοῦ γενομένης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἐξιὼν_98">, going forth to those contests of real life by which the adult tries the maxims learned in youth. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεγχος</quote> here is the test which the man applies to the principle (<quote xml:lang="grc">γλῶσσαν μὲν ἀργόν κ.τ.λ.</quote>); not the test applied to the man himself. The latter is, however, the usual sense of this phrase: cp. fr. 101 <quote xml:lang="grc">γένος γὰρ εἰς ἔλεγχον ἐξιὸν καλὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔκλειαν ἂν κτήσαιτο</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 640"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδειξας εἰς ἔλεγχον ἐξελθὼν ὃς εἶ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 278c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 278 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἔλεγχον ἰὼν περὶ ὧν ἔγραψε.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: εἰς ἔλεγχον ἰὼν περὶ ὧν ἔγραψε.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτοῖς_98"> ‘<emph>for</emph> men,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in their estimation, an ethic dat. ( <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 445">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 445</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσι νικᾶν τοῖς κριταῖς</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάνθ_ἡγουμένην_98">, ‘taking the lead, having the chief influence (absol., as 386) in all things’: for the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 904">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 904</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἀνάσσων</quote>.—We might also take <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖς</quote> directly with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγουμ</quote>. as= ‘<emph>showing the way</emph> in all things to men’) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 133">cp. 133</ref>): but here the notion required is that of ‘swaying’ rather than ‘guiding’; and in the former sense <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγουμένην</quote> would take the genit. <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν</quote>. There is an allusion to the Athenian demagogues (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 388">cp. 388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδασκάλων λόγοισι</quote>): as Cleon says ( <ref n="Thuc. 3. 38">Thuc. 3. 38</ref>) of the citizens in the ecclesia, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰώθατε θεαταὶ μὲν τῶν λόγων γίγνεσθαι, ἀκροαταὶ <*>ὲ τῶν ἔργων</quote> (‘absorbed in the drama of debate, but content with rumours from the field of action’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_οὖν_100">. With Nauck, I adopt Wakefield's transposition here, while recognising that much may be said for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_μ_100">. Two questions are involved, and should be kept distinct; viz. (1) whether Tragedy ever used the licence, denied to it by Porson ( <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 892"> <title>Phoen.</title> 892</ref>), of hiatus after <quote xml:lang="grc">τί</quote>: and (2) whether Sophocles is likely to have written <quote xml:lang="grc">τί οὖν μ’</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ οὖν</quote> here. As to (1), the strongest instance is <ref n="Aesch. Seven 704">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 704</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί οὖν ἔτ’ ἂν σαίνοιμεν ὀλέθριον μόρον</quote>; where <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ οὖν, τί νῦν, τί δῆτ’</quote> are all improbable. It would seem, then, that Aesch. , at least, sometimes admitted the hiatus; so that, if we transpose <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’</quote> here, it does not necessarily follow that the same transposition should be made in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 902">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 902</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί οὖν μ’ ἄνωγας τῇδ’ ἐφυμνῆσαι χθονί</quote>; But the prevailing character of Sophoclean verse certainly favours <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ οὖν</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">τί οὖν μ’</quote>. As against conjecturing <quote xml:lang="grc">τί νύν μ’</quote>, it is well to note <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1247">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1247</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν ἄνωγας οὖν με πανδίκως τάδε</quote> ; <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1364">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωγας οὖν με τὸν νεκρὸν θάπτειν ἐᾶν</quote>; though no argument can be drawn from the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> precedes <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> in those places. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 733">Cp. 733</ref>, 753, 917. See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἄλλο_100">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 310">cp. 310</ref>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 497"> <title>Ant.</title> 497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλεις τί μεῖζον ἢ κατακτεῖναι μ’ ἐλών</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_λαβεῖν_101">, ‘I say that thou art to take.’ In this constr., ‘say’ means ‘command’; the act commanded is denoted by the inf., and the agent by the acc., as subject to the inf. Cf. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1047"> <title>Ai.</title> 1047</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ φωνῶ τόνδε τὸν νεκρὸν χεροῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ συγκομίζειν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 932"> <title>O. C.</title> 932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπον μὲν οὖν καὶ πρόσθεν, ἐννέπω δὲ νῦν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς παῖδας ὡς τάχιστα δεῦρ’ ἄγειν τινά</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 137"> <title>Tr.</title> 137</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ...ἐλπίσιν λέγω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ αἰὲν ἴσχειν</quote>. The agent can also stand in the dat. as object to the verb of saying: <ref n="Soph. OC 840"> <title>O. C.</title> 840</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλᾶν λέγω σοι</quote>.—This verse has no caesura: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1369">cp. 1369</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1021"> <title>Ant.</title> 1021</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὄρνις εὐσήμους ἀπορροιβδεῖ βοάς</quote>, with n. there. Distinguish these rare examples from those which have an elision after the 3rd foot (‘quasi-caesura’), as 276: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44"> <title>Ant.</title> 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γὰρ νοεῖς θάπτειν σφ’, ἀπόρρητον πόλει</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δόλῳ_102">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 60">cp. 60</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσαντ_102">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> by persuading him that it is for his own good to come to Troy,—instead of deceiving him by a pretext of taking him home to Greece (58). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πίθηται_103">, a strong denial: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 381">cp. 381</ref>, 418, <ref n="Soph. OC 849"> <title>O. C.</title> 849</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_δ_103">: persuasion will be in vain: and, when persuasion has failed, force will be useless (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 90">cp. 90</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_104">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_δεινὸν_104"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> It seems truer to regard <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> as adj. with <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν</quote> than as adv. with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως</quote>: cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 1. 24">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5. 1. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω δεινός τις ἔρως...ἐγγίγνεται</quote>. For the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> preceding its noun, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 519">cp. 519</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 280"> <title>O. C.</title> 280</ref> n. In Herod. , indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω δή τι</quote> (‘so <emph>very</emph>...’) often qualifies adjectives (<ref n="Hdt. 1.185">1. 185</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 3.12">3. 12</ref>, etc.): but in the Attic examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω τι</quote> with an adj. the latter is usually a neut. sing., as Posidippus <quote xml:lang="grc">Μύρμηξ</quote> fr. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω τι πολύπουν ἐστὶν ἡ λύπη κακόν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσχύος_θράσος_104">, strength-confidence, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, strength which makes him confident. Against </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτος_104">, conjectured by Nauck and Blaydes, (the latter placing it in the text,) observe that <quote xml:lang="grc">θράσος</quote> agrees well with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐμὴ_πίθηται_104">. If Philoctetes is deaf to persuasion, and risks the alternative of having force used against him, he must have some resource which inspires him with such boldness. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰοὺς_105">: we might expect </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰούς_γ_105">, since the question in 104 is not, ‘what resource has he?’ but, ‘has he some resource?’ And in 104 we cannot well change </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_105"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_105">. L has probably lost </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_105"> in some other places: see, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 648"> <title>Ant.</title> 648</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1241."> Ant. 1241.</ref>But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_105_2"> is not indispensable, and by its absence the reply gains a certain abrupt force. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 985">Cp. 985</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 629."> <title>Tr.</title> 629.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνῳ_γ_106">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the pron., ‘then we dare not even approach <emph>him</emph>,’—a man with such arrows as those.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσμεῖξαι_106">: the spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">μείξω, ἔμειξα</quote>, is attested for <emph>saec.</emph> VI.—IV. B.C. by numerous Attic inscriptions (Meisterhans, p. 87, n. 690).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρασύ_106">, a thing that may be dared; Pind. has this pass. sense, <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θρασύ μοι τόδ’ εἰπεῖν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 450E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 450E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀληθῆ εἰδότα λέγειν ἀσφαλὲς καὶ θαρραλέον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_λαβόντα_107">, acc. as subj. to <quote xml:lang="grc">προσμεῖξαι</quote>, (it is not safe that one should approach him,) unless one has ensnared him; for <quote xml:lang="grc"> λαβόντα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 47">cp. 47</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ψευδῆ_108">. The art. with the neut. adj. gives a certain emphasis (‘those things which are false’), and the objection to it here would have point only if, instead of the adj., we had the subst., <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ψεύδη</quote>. For the same reason, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὰ</emph> ψευδῆ λέγειν</quote> seems rather better than <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὸ</emph> ψευδῆ λέγειν</quote>, though at first sight the latter is specious. In fr. 325 <quote xml:lang="grc">καλὸν μὲν οὖν οὐκ ἔστι τὰ ψευδῆ λέγειν</quote>, also, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ</quote> appears sound. Cp. <ref n="Antiph. 1.10">Antiphon or. 1 § 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη γὰρ καὶ τοὺς τὰ ψευδῆ παρεσκευασμένους λέγειν τἀληθῆ κατηγορεῖν ποιήσει</quote>. Cavallin changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτα_τὰ_108"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλαδὴ_108">, ‘evidently’ ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1501"> <title>O. T.</title> 1501</ref>): but this would be better suited to ironical reproof than to ingenuous surprise. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_σωθῆναι_109">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the success of the Greeks at Troy. The aor. inf. is used because the speaker is thinking of that success as an event (to be marked by the fall of Troy), not as a process or state (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σῴζεσθαι</quote>). Cp. Andoc. or. 2 § 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ περὶ τοῦ σῶσαι τὰς Ἀθήνας ὁ κίνδυνος ἦν αὐτοὺς μᾶλλον ἢ περὶ τοῦ μηδὲ αὐτοὺς σωθῆναι</quote> (referring to the Athenian army at Samos in 411 B.C.): where, as here, the aorists infinitive mark the critical moments. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_βλέπων_110"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> By using <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>, he concedes (at least for argument's sake) what Od.has just said. ‘Granting, then, that a falsehood is not disgraceful when it has such a motive, how is one to have the face to tell it?’ In vv. 91 ff. we saw the first trace of irresolution: this verse marks a further step. He now demurs to play the part, not (as in v. 108) because it is immoral, but because it is distasteful and difficult. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_οὖν_110">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 124"> <title>O. T.</title> 124</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὖν ὁ λῃστής</quote> (‘granting that there was a robber, how <emph>then</emph>...?’): for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_βλέπων_110_2">, <ref n="Soph. OT 1371"> <title>O. T.</title> 1371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμασιν ποίοις βλέπων</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λακεῖν_110"> of bold or impudent utterance, as <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1046">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1046</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτα λάσκων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1094"> Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1094</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_κέρδος_111">, for it, with a view to it: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.304"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 304</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατὴρ δέ οἱ ἄγχι παραστὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μυθεῖτ’ εἰς ἀγαθά</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 395">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 395</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐς τὸ κέρδος παρὰ φύσιν δουλευτέον</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 8. 1. 33">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8. 1. 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδὼν ἂν αὐτοὺς ἡγήσω τῷ ὄντι εἰς κάλλος ζῆν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱρεῖ_113">, the oracular pres., denoting what <emph>is to</emph> happen: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 126">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 126</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ μὲν ἀγρεῖ Πριάμου πόλιν ἅδε κέλευθος</quote>: id. <ref n="Aesch. PB 170">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ νέον βούλευμ’ ὑφ’ ὅτου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρον τιμάς τ’ ἀποσυλᾶται</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 3. 155">Her. 3. 155</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη ὦν, ἢν μὴ τῶν σῶν δεήσῃ, αἰρέομεν Βαβυλῶνα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐφάσκετ_114">: when Odysseus and Phoenix visited Scyros, and persuaded him to come to Troy (345 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_ἂν_115"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρσειας</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 364"> <title>El.</title> 364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς σῆς δ’ οὐκ ἐρῶ τιμῆς τυχεῖν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἂν σύ, σώφρων γ’ οὖσα</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῴης</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 462"> <title>Tr.</title> 462</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔπω τις...ἠνέγκατ’...ὄνειδος</quote>)<quote xml:lang="grc">· ἥδε δ’ οὐδ’ ἂν</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνέγκαιτο</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρατἔ_ἄν_116">, they would then <emph> become</emph> (by logical inference) desirable prizes: cp. <ref n="Plat. Prot. 338C">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 338C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ αἱρήσεσθε..., αἰσχρὸν γὰρ τοῦτο τῷδε γίγν εται</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνομαι</quote> denotes arithmetical process ( <ref n="Thuc. 3. 17">Thuc. 3. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ πᾶσαι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">νῆες</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμα ἐγίγνοντο διακόσιαι κ.τ.λ.</quote>), or legal succession to property (Isae. or. 5 § 44 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ ἐκείνων ἐγίγνετο</quote>). This usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνομαι</quote> is decisive for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γίγνοιτ_116"> against </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένοιτ_116">, a v. l. found in a few late MSS.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_116">, the conjecture of Triclinius, seems better here than the other corrections, <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρατἔ ἂν γίγνοιτ’ ἄν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνοιτ’ ἄῤ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_φέρει_117">, (be sure) that thou winnest: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 39"> <title>Ai.</title> 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΘ. ὡς ἔστιν ἀνδρὸς τοῦδε τἄργα ταῦτά σοι</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1664">Eur. <title> Ph.</title> 1664</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΡ. ὡς οὔτις ἀμφὶ τῷδ’ ὑγρὰν θήσει κόνιν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 400">id. <title>Hec.</title> 400</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΚ. ὡς τῆσδ’ ἑκοῦσα παιδὸς οὐ μεθήσομαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_117">, <emph>reportas:</emph> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 500"> <title>O. T.</title> 500</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_118">: for the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 442"> <title>O. C.</title> 442</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δρᾶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἠθέλησαν</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 47 n. —Neoptolemus was already all but per suaded by the promise that he should take Troy. If, besides that reward, there is yet another, then his mind is made up. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφός_κἀγαθός_119">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφὸς μὲν διὰ τὸ κλέψαι, ἀγαθὸς δὲ διὰ τὸ πορθῆσαι</quote>. Cp <ref n="Soph. El. 1088"> <title>El.</title> 1088</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο φέρειν ἐν ἑνὶ λόγῳ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφά τ’ ἀρίστα τε παῖς κεκλῆσθαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸς_119">: <ref n="Soph. OT 458"> <title>O. T.</title> 458</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὸς αὑτὸς καὶ πατήρ</quote>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκλᾖ_119">: for the forms of the optat. perf. pass., see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 49"> <title>O. T.</title> 49</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴτω_120"> is a defiance of the possible consequences (‘happen what may’): cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 819">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 819</ref>(Medea, having taken her resolve to kill the children) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτω· περισσοὶ πάντες οὑν μέσῳ λόγοι</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποήσω_120">. Numerous Attic inscriptions of the 5th and 4th centuries B.C. show that in this verb the letter </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_120"> was regularly omitted before a following E-sound (<quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote>), though never before an O-sound (<quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ω</quote>): hence (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖ, ποήσας</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιοῦσι, ποιῶν</quote>. It should be noted that <quote xml:lang="grc">πο‐</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ποι‐</quote>, according as the E- or O-sound follows, sometimes occur in the same inscription: thus in <title>C. I. A.</title> 167, 55 (of 334—326 B.C.) we find <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιῶν</quote> (thrice) in company with <quote xml:lang="grc">ποήσας</quote>. The omission of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_120_2"> before the E-sound was not, indeed, rigorous; thus an Attic inscription of the 5th cent. B.C. gives <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὔφρων ἐξεποίησ’ οὐκ ἀδαὴς Πάριος</quote>: but the facts prove that it was usual. See Meisterhans, <title>Gramm. d. Att. Inschriften</title>, p. 27. As to L, its practice is not constant. It almost always gives <quote xml:lang="grc">πο</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ποι</quote>, before <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote>, when the first syllable is short,—as here, and in vv. 552, 752, 926, 1010. In a few such places where the first hand had written <quote xml:lang="grc">ποι</quote> it has been corrected to <quote xml:lang="grc">πο</quote>. But there are also a few places where <quote xml:lang="grc">ποι</quote> remains. See Appendix. In writing </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποήσω_120_2">, etc., I. rely primarily on the epigraphic evidence belonging to the poet's own time: but L's prevailing practice must also be considered as strengthening the grounds for believing that those inscriptions represent the general rule.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσαν_ἀφείς_120">. This verse does not (as some have objected) mark an abrupt change of mind; that change has come by a series of steps which the poet has indicated by light touches (91 ff., 110, 116, 118). Rather this very phrase hints that the feeling shown in v. 110 still lingers with him. He will do the deed, but there is still a sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνη</quote> which it costs an effort to shake off. These are the words of one who may yet feel remorse. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μνημονεύεις_121">. Almost all recent edd. adopt the conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μνημονεύσεις_121">, but without necessity. The question, ‘dost thou remember my advice?’ naturally implies here, ‘dost thou intend to observe it?’—and so N.'s answer, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σάφ_ἴσθ_121">, follows the present tense just as fitly as it would follow the future. For the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> in the verse, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1205"> <title>O. C.</title> 1205</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1247."> <title>Tr.</title> 1247.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_παρῄνεσα_121">: referring to 56 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνῄνεσα_121">, ‘agreed’: <ref n="Soph. OC 1508"> <title>O. C.</title> 1508</ref> n. Remark the two compounds of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνέω</quote> at the end of two successive verses: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1370">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1370</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μετεγγραφήσεται</quote>, | ...<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐγγεγράψεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1265">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1265</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγνωμοσύνην</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνωμοσύνην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκδέχου_123">, <emph>excipe.</emph> The idea of the compound is, ‘be ready for him,’—prepared to deal with him the moment that he appears. The figurative use of the word in <ref n="Hdt. 4.1">Her. 4. 1</ref> is essentially the same, —<quote xml:lang="grc">Σκύθας...κατιόντας ἐς τὴν σφετέρην ἐξεδέξατο οὐκ ἐλάσσων πόνος</quote> (as if it had been lying in wait for them). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατοπτευθῶ_124">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 829"> <title>Ai.</title> 829</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἐχθρῶν του κατοπτευθείς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σκοπὸν_124"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote> is the attendant of Neoptolemus who had been sent to watch the neighbourhood of the cave, lest Philoctetes should take Odysseus by surprise (46). Now that Odysseus is going back to his ship, such a <quote xml:lang="grc"> σκοπός</quote> is no longer needed. And it is natural that Odysseus should expect to meet the sentinel, since the latter would be keeping watch on that side of the cave at which Odysseus himself had hitherto been standing; viz., the side nearest to the ships. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναῦν_124"> here means the ship of Neoptolemus. See Introduction, p. xx, n. 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δεῦῤ_126">. If any undue delay occurred, Odysseus might reasonably suppose that Neoptolemus was failing to persuade Philoctetes. In case of such delay, then, Odysseus will send back N.'s man, disguised as the captain of a ship. The object of the disguise is that the supposed captain may tell a story which shall quicken the desire of Philoctetes to leave Lemnos, and shall also confirm his trust in Neoptolemus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_κατασχολάζειν_126">. Nauck's conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα σχολάζειν</quote> would suit the sense; but it would leave the origin of the vulgate unexplained. I believe that Soph. has used <quote xml:lang="grc">κατασχολάζειν τοῦ χρόνου</quote>, somewhat boldly, in the sense of ‘to lag <emph>behind</emph> the due time,’—the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολάζειν</quote> in the sense ‘to linger,’ ‘to delay,’ permitting a genitive to be used, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑστερεῖν, λελεῖφθαι</quote>, etc. The compound <quote xml:lang="grc">κατασχολάζειν</quote> may be compared with <quote xml:lang="grc">καθυστερεῖν</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote> merely implies that the delay is to be regretted or blamed. At first sight there is much in favour of the more generally received view, that <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ χρόνου τι κατασχολάζειν</quote> means, ‘to <emph>waste</emph> part of our (precious) time <emph>by lingering.</emph>’ But the tone of that phrase seems very unsuitable here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπέμψω_126">. The prep. is not otiose, as Burges thought, but marks that the person sent will come as the sender's agent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναυκλήρου_128">: the man, when he comes, pretends to be the captain of a small merchant-ship, trading between Greece and the Troad (547 ff.). In <ref n="Pl. Mil. 4.3">Plaut. <title> Mil.</title> 4. 3. 41 ff.</ref> the ‘skipper's dress’ (<quote xml:lang="lat">ornatus nauclericus</quote>) consists of a darkcoloured hat with broad brim (<quote xml:lang="lat">causia</quote>), and a garment of the same colour, well girt-up, and looped on the left shoulder, leaving the right arm bare, like the Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξωμίς</quote>. The colour of both hat and tunic is described by <quote xml:lang="lat">ferrugineus</quote>, (<quote xml:lang="lat">‘nam is colos thalassicust’</quote>). This was a dark violet, rather than, as Nonius (p. 549) makes it, iron-grey: see Conington on <ref n="Verg. G. 1.467">Verg. <title>G.</title> 1. 467</ref>, and Munro on <ref n="Lucr. 4.76">Lucr. 4. 76.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρόποις_128">, as <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 479"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόποισιν οὐ τυραννικοῖς θανών</quote>: often with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, as <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 918">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 918</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικὸς ἐν τρόποις</quote>. Not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρόπον_128">, which would mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ναύκληρος δολοῖ τὴν μορφήν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 57">Her. 2. 57</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρνιθος τρόπον... φθέγγεσθαι</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ὄρνις φθέγγεται</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνοία_129">: ignorance, on the part of Philoctetes, as to the real quality of the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote>. Disyllables in <quote xml:lang="grc">οια</quote> (as <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροία</quote>) have final <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, but longer words have <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>. Other exceptions are: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 350"> <title>Tr.</title> 350</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνοία μ’ ἔχει</quote>: fr. 521 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνοία τρέφει</quote>: fr. 748 <quote xml:lang="grc">παλιρροία βυθοῦ</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 402"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνοία τινί</quote> (Blomf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννοίᾳ</quote>): <ref n="Eur. Andr. 520"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 520</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ ἀνοία</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλη</quote> (in anapaests): Ar. fr. 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παρανοία καὶ ἀναιδεία</quote> (do.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσῇ_129">, may be an attendant circumstance, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, may aid our plan: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1251">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1251</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ τ’ ἄγαν σιγὴ βαρὺ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ προσεῖναι</quote>, n. — The reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνοίᾳ_129"> is certainly wrong: the sense would then be, ‘that he (the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote>) may be added to your company without suspicion’ (dat. of circumstance): it could not be, as Musgrave took it, ‘that thou mayest approach the man without seeming to know him’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">προσῇ</quote> being then 2nd pers. subj. aor. midd. of <quote xml:lang="grc">προσίημι</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_δῆτα_130">. Blaydes conjectures </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_σύ_130">. But <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆτα</quote> is right. It means, ‘then, of course’—differing from <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> by implying more clearly that the step prescribed by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέχου</quote> is the obvious one.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_130">=<quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ οὗ</quote>, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέχου_130">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1163"> <title>O. T.</title> 1163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεξάμην δέ του</quote>. This is better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὗ...αὐδωμένου</quote> as gen. absol.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποικίλως_130">, ‘craftily,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in terms fitted to beguile Philoctetes. Not, ‘in <emph>riddling</emph> speech,’ as if the point of the artifice lay in second meanings which Neoptolemus was to divine. The word could, indeed, mean that (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 130"> <title>O. T.</title> 130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ποικιλῳδὸς Σφίγξ</quote>): but the more general sense agrees better with vv. 542—627.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐδωμένου_130">, midd., as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 852">852</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 772"> <title>Ai.</title> 772</ref>: the pass. occurs below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 240">240</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 430">430</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_συμφέροντα_130">: for the neut., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 24">cp. 24</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἀεὶλόγων_130">, the words spoken by him from moment to moment,—the tenor of his discourse. The phrase is explained by the dialogue between the disguised <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote> and Neo ptolemus (vv. 542—627). The <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</quote> tells a story; N. follows his lead, and strikes in from time to time with artful comments, —reasserting his hatred of the Atreidae, his sympathy with Philoctetes, etc. These opportunities, or ‘cues,’ are ‘the useful hints’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ συμφέροντα</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_τάδε_130">, ‘having committed these matters to thee,’ —a parting reminder of the responsibility. Not, ‘having given thee these directions.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑρμῆς_δόλιος_133">=<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς δόλιος ὃς πέμπει</quote>, the god of stratagems, who escorts men on their way.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πέμπων_133">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 620"> <title>Tr.</title> 620</ref>(the herald Lichas speaks) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἴπερ Ἑρμοῦ τήνδε πομπεύω τέχνην</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 91">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 91</ref>(Apollo to Hermes) <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπαῖος ἴσθι, τόνδε ποιμαίνων ἐμὸν ἱκέτην</quote>. So he is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδιος, ἐνόδιος, ἡγεμόνιος, ἁγήτωρ</quote>, and, in relation to the dead, <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχοπομπός</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1548">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1548</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόλιος_133">. Near Pellene in Achaia Pausanias saw a statue of <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς,—ἐπίκλησιν μὲν Δόλιος, εὐχὰς δὲ ἀνθρώπων ἕτοιμος τελέσαι</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 7.27.1">(7. 27. 1)</bibl> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1202">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 1202</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆ δόλιε, ταυτὶ μὲν ἔτι καλῶς ποιεῖς</quote>. The Corcyrean month <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυδρεύς</quote> was probably sacred to Hermes as <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυδρός</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδής</quote>). In Babrius fab. 57 Hermes is conducting ‘a waggon-load of lies,’ when he is way-laid and robbed of his whole stock by Arabs. Especially, he is the arch-thief ( <ref n="Hor. Carm. 1. 10. 7">Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 1. 10. 7</ref>: cp. Ovid <ref n="Ov. Fast. 5. 691"> <title>Fast.</title> 5. 691</ref>).— His character of <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλιος</quote> is similarly combined with that of <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπός</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 1396"> <title>El.</title> 1396</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς σφ’ ἄγει δόλον σκότῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύψας, κ.τ.λ.</quote>: and in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 216">[Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ σ’ ὁ Μαίας παῖς ἐκεῖσε καὶ πάλιν | πέμψειεν Ἑρμῆς, ὅς γε φηλητῶν ἄναξ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἑρμῆς,—ἐπίκλησιν μὲν Δόλιος, εὐχὰς δὲ ἀνθρώπων
							ἕτοιμος τελέσαι

ἀλλ’ εὖ σ’ ὁ Μαίας παῖς ἐκεῖσε καὶ πάλιν |
							πέμψειεν Ἑρμῆς, ὅς γε φηλητῶν ἄναξ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῷν_133">, dat.: cp. <ref target="cl98">on 98.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νίκη_Πολιάς_134">. The personified <quote xml:lang="grc">Νίκη</quote> meant Victory not merely in war but in any contest. She was especially associated with Zeus; but his daughter was the only goddess with whom she was actually identified. Thus Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Ion 452">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 452</ref> ff.): <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν...ἐμὰν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθάναν ἱκετεύω</quote>, | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μάκαιρα Νίκα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλε</quote>. And Aristeides, in his oration on Athena, says of her, <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνη μὲν ἁπάντων θεῶν, ὁμοίως δὲ πασῶν, οὐκ ἐπώνυμος τῆς vίκης ἐστίν</quote> [in such epithets as <quote xml:lang="grc">νικηφόρος</quote>], <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὁμώνυμος</quote> (I. p. 29). At Athens the small Ionic temple of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηνᾶ Νίκη</quote> stood on the platform of a bastion (<quote xml:lang="grc">πύργος</quote>) springing from the south wing of the Propylaea, on the right hand of one ascending to the Acropolis. The figure of the goddess, probably a work of Calamis, bore a helmet in her left hand, and in her right a pomegranate (<quote xml:lang="grc">σίδη</quote>), her regular attribute in the Athena-cult at Sidè in Pamphylia. As Benndorf has shown (<title>Ueber das Kultusbild der Athena Nike</title>, Vienna, 1879), the temple probably commemorated Cimon's victory over the Persians at the mouth of the Eurymedon, near Sidè (466 B.C.). This <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηνᾶ Νι—/κη</quote> was the figure which at Athens came to be popularly known as the Wingless Victory, <quote xml:lang="grc">Νίκη Ἄπτερος</quote>. Wings were the distinctive attribute of <quote xml:lang="grc">Νίκη</quote> in art: and Athenians were familiar with the winged <quote xml:lang="grc">Νίκη</quote> which the chryselephantine Athena of Pheidias, in the Parthenon, held in her outstretched right hand (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 574">Ar. <title> Av.</title> 574</ref>). The conception of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηνᾶ Νίκη</quote> was not exclusively Athenian. Thus Pausanias saw at Megara <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερὸν Ἀθηνᾶς...καλουμένης Νίκης</quote> (1. 42. 4).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πολιάς_134">. At Athens it denoted Athena as guardian of citadel, city, and land. Athena Polias was represented by the old <quote xml:lang="grc">βρέτας</quote> of olive-wood in the Erechtheum. But she bore the title <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολιάς</quote> in many other places also, especially in the Ionic cities of Asia Minor,—as at Erythrae, Prienè, Teos, Phocaea (<ref n="Paus. 7.5.3">Paus. 7. 5. 3, 4</ref>: <ref n="Paus. 2.31.9">2. 31. 9</ref>). Equivalent titles were <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολιᾶτις, Πολιοῦχος</quote>, and (in a case noticed by Leake, <title>Morea</title>, II. p. 80) <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἁγησίπολις</quote>. Cp. Aristeides I. p. 21: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ εἰσὶν αἱ πόλεις δῶρα Ἀθηνᾶς· ὅθεν δὴ καὶ Πολιοῦχος ἄπασι κέκληται</quote>. Thus Sophocles, though writing for Athenians, is not making purely local allusions.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣ_ἀεί_134">: as in the <title>Odyssey</title>. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 14"> <title>Ai.</title> 14</ref> he calls her <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλτάτης ἐμοὶ θεῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ξένον_135">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 685">cp. 685</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. OC 184"> <title>O. C.</title> 184</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξεῖνος ἐπὶ ξένης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέγειν_λέγειν_135">: for the likeness of form in the words (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρονομασία</quote>), combined with likeness of sound (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρομοίωσις</quote>), cp. <ref n="Isoc. 4.186">Isocr. or. 4 § 186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμην δὲ καὶ μνήμην.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπόπταν_135">: the subst. expresses a fixed habit of mind more forcibly than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕποπτον</quote> would have done: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 60">Thuc. 6. 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δῆμος...χαλεπὸς ἦν τότε καὶ ὑπόπτης ἐς τοὺς περὶ τῶν μυστικῶν τὴν αἰτίαν λαβόντας</quote>. <ref n="Xen. Horse. 3. 9"> Xen. <title>Eq.</title> 3. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς...ὑπόπτας φύσει</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππους</quote>), naturally shy. The Chorus, now entering the orchestra for the first time, cannot be conceived as thinking of what Hom. Od.has said (70 ff.): but they know how Philoctetes has been treated, and may naturally expect him to be ‘shy’ of Greek strangers. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέχνα_137">, the skill of the ruler, whose art is the highest of all: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 380"> <title>O. T.</title> 380</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">τέχνη τέχνης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερφέρουσα. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑτέρας_137">, not, another kind of skill, but rather, skill in another man: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 230"> <title>O. C.</title> 230</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάταις ἑτέραις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμα_137">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας προὔχει</quote>. As dist. from <quote xml:lang="grc">τέχνη</quote>—the art of ruling — <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote> here is intellectual power generally. The latter would not be separately ascribed to the king, if we adopted </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμας_137">, which is thus the weaker reading.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ὅτῳ_137">: in whose keeping. The anteced. is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνου</quote> understood: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 956">cp. 956</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1388"> <title>O. C.</title> 1388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτανεῖν θ’ ὑφ’ οὗπερ ἐξελήλασαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1050"> <title>Ai.</title> 1050</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῦντ’ ἐμοί, δοκοῦντα δ’, ὃς κραίνει στρατοῦ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_σκῆπτρον_137">, the godlike Zeus-sceptre, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, sceptre derived from Zeus (gen. of source), <quote xml:lang="grc">διόσδοτον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάσσεται_137"> implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάσσω σκῆπτρον</quote> (an almost adverbial cogn. acc.), as=‘to rule with sceptre’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 449"> <title>O. C.</title> 449</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρα κραίνειν</quote>, to have sceptred sway.—The tone here is genuinely Homeric. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.98"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 98</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαῶν ἐσσὶ ἄναξ καί τοι Ζεὺς ἐγγυάλιξεν | σκῆπτρόν τ’ ἠδὲ θέμιστας, ἵνα σφίσι βουλεύῃσθα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
λαῶν ἐσσὶ ἄναξ καί τοι Ζεὺς ἐγγυάλιξεν |
							σκῆπτρόν τ’ ἠδὲ θέμιστας, ἵνα σφίσι βουλεύῃσθα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_ἐλήλυθεν_141">, hath come to thee. There is perhaps no other example of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι</quote> with acc. of person: but there is an exact parallel in the rare use of <quote xml:lang="grc">βαίνω</quote> with acc. of person, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1371">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν ὀδύνα μ’ ὀδύνα βαίνει</quote>. Eur. Itis doubtless more than a mere coincidence that both these instances are lyric; and that a lyric boldness was felt in them may be inferred from the parody in <title>Nub.</title> 30, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτὰρ τί χρέος ἔβα με</quote>; If <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ δ’...ἐλήλυθεν</quote> occurred in an iambic trimeter, the case for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶδ_141"> would be strong: but here, in lyrics, we should keep </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_δ_141">.—We cannot properly compare <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκνεῖσθαι</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκάνειν</quote>, after which an acc. of pers. was common.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_κράτος_141">, complete (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> sovereign) power. Distinguish the phrase with the art., <ref n="Hdt. 6. 35">Her. 6. 35</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχε μὲν τὸ πᾶν κράτος Πεισίστρατος</quote>, which gives the same meaning in a slightly different way (‘<emph>the whole</emph> power’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠγύγιον_141">, predicate with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλήλυθεν</quote>, ‘from of old,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘from thine ancestors’: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠγύγιον</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. OC 1770"> <title>O. C.</title> 1770</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_141">, ‘therefore’: <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.176"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τάδ’ οὐκ ἐγένοντο· τὸ καὶ κλαίουσα τέτηκα</quote>. So, in Attic, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote>, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 1. 21">Xen. <title> An.</title> 4. 1. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’ ἔσπευδον καὶ διὰ τοῦτο οὐχ ὑπέμενον</quote>: esp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’ ἄρα</quote> , <title>Nub.</title> 319, etc.—For the like use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 511"> <title>O. T.</title> 511</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχατιαῖς_141">, locative dat. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 411"> <title>O. C.</title> 411</ref> n.), ‘in the extreme parts’ of the island,— those, namely, which are on the edge of the sea. This reading, which has the best authority, is also intrinsically better than the gen. sing.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τόπον ἐσχατιᾶς</quote> (‘region, part, of the sea-marge’) would be an unusual phrase. Homer, indeed, uses only the sing. of this word: and it is very likely that Soph. was thinking of <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.182">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 9. 182</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα δ’ ἐν ἐσχατιῇ σπέος εἴδομεν ἄγχι θαλάσσης</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.238"> <emph>ib.</emph> 5. 238</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νήσου ἐπ’ ἐσχατιῇ</quote> </cit>, etc. But that is no reason why Soph. should not have used the plur., which was familiar in Attic (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <title> H.</title> 2. 4. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἀγρῶν... εἰς τὰς ἐσχατιάς</quote> ).</gloss>

data.quote: ἔνθα δ’ ἐν ἐσχατιῇ σπέος εἴδομεν ἄγχι θαλάσσης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅντινα_κεῖται_141">, in which he is situated, abides. The verb is esp. suitable to a crippled sufferer; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 183">cp. 183</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.721"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 721</ref>(of Philoctetes) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν νήσῳ κεῖτο κρατέρ’ ἄλγεα πάσχων</quote>. Verbs of position (as ‘sitting’ or ‘standing’) sometimes take an acc. (which may be regarded as a kind of ‘cognate’ acc.), denoting the place <emph>in</emph> or <emph>on</emph> which one sits, stands, etc. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 183"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 183</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέλμα...ἡμένων</quote> (on the same principle as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδραν ἕζομαι</quote>): <title> Suppl.</title> 987 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτ’ αἰθερίαν ἕστηκε πέτραν</quote>; (as if one said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕστηκε πετρίνην στάσιν</quote>): <emph> ib.</emph> 657 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιὸν τεταγμένους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κέρας</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τάξιν</quote>). Poetry could say, then, <quote xml:lang="grc">τόπον...ὅντινα ἕστηκε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτακται</quote>: and so also <quote xml:lang="grc">κοῖται</quote>. It is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται τόπον</quote> is not precisely like <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται θέσιν</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1. 37">Thuc. 1. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πόλις...αὐτάρκη θέσιν κειμένη</quote>): for <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι</quote> served as perf. pass. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθημι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τέθειμαι</quote> being midd.), and in <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται θέσιν</quote> the acc. is therefore as strictly ‘cognate’ as it would be in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτέθη θέσιν</quote>. But the difference between <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται θέσιν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται τόπον</quote> is, in principle, only the same as that between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕζομαι ἕδραν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕζομαι ζυγόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_μελάθρων_147">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> I read <quote xml:lang="grc">οὑκ</quote>. Wakefield and Hermann were right in feeling that the sense required <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’ ἐκ μελάθρων</quote> to be connected with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁδίτης_147"> (‘metuendus vir <emph>qui ex hoc antro abiit</emph>’), and not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλῃ_147">. Then, however, the article <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ</quote> becomes indispensable. Let it be granted that we could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶ ὁδίτην ἐκ τῶν μελάθρων</quote>, ‘I see one leaving the abode,’—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδίτην</quote> having the constr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδεύω</quote>: as, in <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς...κινήσεις τῷ σώματι</quote> ( <title>Legg.</title> 631 C), the dat. after <quote xml:lang="grc">κινήσεις</quote> is that which might follow <quote xml:lang="grc">κινεῖσθαι</quote>. But no Greek could have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπόταν μόλῃ ὁδίτης ἐκ τῶν μελάθρων</quote> in the sense, ‘when he who has <emph>left</emph> the abode shall <emph>return</emph> to it,’—the movement denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τῶν μελάθρων</quote> being opposite to that denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλη</quote>. For the order of words, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_μελάθρων_147">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ τῶνδε ἐκ μ</quote>., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 735"> <title>O. T.</title> 735</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τίς χρόνος τοῖσδ’ ἐστὶν οὑξεληλυθώς</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ τοῖσδε ἐξεληλυθώς</quote>. For other examples of this crasis in Soph. , cp. below, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 639">Soph. 639</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεῦμα τοὐκ πρῴρας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1540">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1540</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐκ θεοῦ παρόν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 731">Soph. <title>El.</title> 731</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνοὺς δ’ οὑξ Ἀθηνῶν δεινὸς ἡνιοστρόφος</quote>.—With the simple </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_147">, only two versions are possible. (1) Taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_147_2"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλῃ_147_2">: ‘when he shall come forth from this abode.’ But N. knows that Ph. is not now in the cave, and he cannot assume that, on returning, Ph. will enter it from the landward side, to emerge at the other. Philoctetes is, in fact, outside of the cave from his first appearance at v. 219 up to v. 675. (2) Taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_147_3"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προχωρῶν_147">, as referring to the Chorus: ‘moving forth from this abode.’ But the Chorus never enter the cave,—they only look into it: and, in any case, a <emph> gradual</emph> retreat from it (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἐμὴν αἰεὶ χεῖρα</quote>) would be unsuitable. — Seyffert refers <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’ ἐκ μελάθρων</quote> to the Chorus, but does not connect it with <quote xml:lang="grc">προχωρῶν</quote>, taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> in the pregnant sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω ὤν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘having quitted the cave (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>), advance ever at my beck,’ etc. This seems impossible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_προχωρῶν_148">, coming forward towards my (beckoning) hand,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, at a sign given by me,—from time to time (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰεί</quote>). This phrase is explained by the part which the Chorus actually plays in the dialogue between Philoctetes and Neoptolemus,—interposing, from time to time, with some utterance fitted to confirm the belief of Philoctetes in the story which Neoptolemus is telling (317, 391, 508, 676, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προχωρῶν_148">, because the sailors would remain at some distance from their master while he conversed with Ph. , but would naturally move a step or two nearer at the moments when they offered their own remarks. Not in a fig. sense, ‘directing your course of action.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_χεῖρα_148">, too, is literal (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it does not mean simply, ‘following the lead’ of my words). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_θεραπεύειν_149">, to provide for the need of the moment. Cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.307">Dem. or. 18 § 307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ὑπὲρ τῶν ἐχθρῶν καιροὺς ἀντὶ τῶν τῆς πατρίδος θεραπεύειν</quote>. Cp. the proverb <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ παρὸν εὖ ποιεῖν</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 499C">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 499C</ref>), ‘to do the best one can.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλον_μέλημα_150">: with a certain emphasis; cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 868">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 868</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείμ’ <*> δειμαίνεις</quote>. The Chorus first reply to the last words of N., and then respond to his suggestion that perhaps they wish to see the abode of Ph. —The text is sound, when, with Triclinius, we have ejected <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν</quote> (see cr. n.),—a gloss added by some one who, taking the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄμμα</quote> to be that of N., naturally felt the want of the pos sessive pron. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_150"> is that of the Chorus, and is the subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρουρεῖν_150">: this appears certain, when we compare <ref n="Soph. Trach. 225">Soph. <title> Tr.</title> 225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ μ’ ὄμματος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρὰν</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρὰ</quote> L] <quote xml:lang="grc">παρῆλθε</quote>, ‘nor hath it escaped my watchful eye.’ Dindorf takes <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμ’</quote> as a sort of ‘cognate’ acc., ‘to watch <emph>with</emph> the eye,’ and compares <ref n="Soph. Trach. 914">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 914</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ λαθραῖον ὄμμ’ ἐπεσκιασμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρούρουν</quote>: but there the partic. is the warrant for it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_καιρῷ_150">=lit., ‘for thine <emph> occasion</emph>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘for the moment at which a thing can be done for thine advantage.’ The use of the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρός</quote> with the possessive is rare, but is akin to a freq. use of the plur., as <ref n="Isoc. 6.80">Isocr. or. 6 § 80</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς ἡμετέροις καιροῖς</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> at the moments advantageous for us) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μὴ τοῖς αὑτῶν ποιήσασθαι τοὺς κινδύνους</quote>. And how naturally <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ σὸς καιρός</quote> might approximate (esp. in lyric poetry) to the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν κέρδος</quote>, is suggested by such phrases as that in <ref n="Hdt. 1. 206">Her. 1. 206</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἂν εἰδείης εἴ τοι ἐς καιρὸν ἔσται ταῦτα τελεόμενα</quote>, ‘seasonably for thee,’=‘for thine advantage.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐλὰς_ποίας_152">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 30">cp. 30</ref>. The plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλαί</quote> could denote a single chamber ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 945"> <title>Ant.</title> 945</ref>). The Chorus are not supposed to have been present when the cave was found, and do not yet know the nature of Ph. 's quarters.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνεδρος_152">, ‘resident,’ is not pleonastic, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίει_152"> can be said of a wanderer with ref. to the place that he is in at a given moment: 892: <ref n="Soph. OC 117"> <title>O. C.</title> 117</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χῶρον_ἔχει_152">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, where he is <emph>now:</emph> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 22">cp. 22</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 37."> <title>O. C.</title> 37.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μοί_152"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαθεῖν_152">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τό</quote> is pron. in nom. case (cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1172"> <title>Tr.</title> 1172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ἦν ἄρ’ οὐδὲν ἄλλο</quote>): <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθεῖν</quote>, epexeg. inf.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπεσών_152">: 46 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_θυραῖον_157">; The iteration, and the want of coherence, are meant to mark eager anxiety.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_στίβον_157"> here=‘is planting his steps’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 29">cp. 29</ref>), rather than, ‘is following a path’ (48): hence the narrow space implied by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔναυλον_157"> is no objection. But with Wakefield's <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἔχει <emph>στίβος</emph> </quote> the sense ‘path’ would be unavoidable. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶκον_κοίτης_159">, a home consisting in a rocky sleeping-place (defining gen.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 81">cp. 81</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίθυρον_159">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 16">cp. 16</ref>.— The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_159"> implies, ‘but where <emph>he</emph> is, I know not.’ The Chorus then say, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_γὰρ_159"> ...; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, (you surprise me;) <emph> for</emph> (if he is not here) where can he be? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορβῆς_162">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 43">cp. 43</ref> .—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὀγμεύει</emph>, ὄγμος</quote>, from rt. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀγ</quote>. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγω</quote> (cp. <emph>agmen</emph>), is prop. any <emph>line</emph> drawn out by movement; then, esp., a <emph> furrow</emph> in ploughing ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.546"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 546</ref>, Theocr. 10. 2); or the track (‘swathe’) made by reapers through corn. Here the image is from ploughing; the furrow which the ploughman leaves behind him is compared to the track left by the helpless foot which Philoctetes drags after him. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 291">Cp. 291</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰλυόμην, δύστηνον ἐξέλκων πόδα</quote> (where see n.). The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀγμεύει</quote> also serves to suggest the laborious character of the progress. Cp. Lucan 9. 721 (of a serpent) <emph>contentus iter cauda sulcare.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_162"> seems a necessary correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδε_162">, since <quote xml:lang="grc"> στίβον...τόνδε</quote> would mean, ‘his path <emph>yonder</emph>’ (as though N. could point to it): not merely ‘his path <emph>in this neighbourhood</emph>’ (though invisible). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτην_164">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> by making expeditions in quest of food,—referring to 162 f. Others take it to mean, ‘in the following manner,’—viz., <quote xml:lang="grc"> θηροβολοῦντα</quote>. This is possible ( <ref n="Soph. OC 787"> <title>O. C.</title> 787</ref>): but then we should expect <quote xml:lang="grc">θηροβολεῖν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_164">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τρόπον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγερὸν_στυγερῶς_166">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1369">cp. 1369</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς.. κακούς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 479"> <title>O. T.</title> 479</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλεος μελέῳ ποδὶ χηρεύων</quote>. Tragedy applies <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγερός</quote>, in the sense of ‘wretched,’ not only to things ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1016"> <title>Tr.</title> 1016</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου...τοῦ στυγεροῦ</quote>), but also to persons, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 144"> <title>Ant.</title> 144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖν στυγεροῖν</quote> (the sons of Oed.: where see n.). Almost all recent editors, however, have received Brunck's conjecture, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμυγερὸνσμυγερῶς_166">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘with painful toil.’ At first sight, this is favoured by the schol. here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμόνως</quote>, which can scarcely be explained as meaning ‘with grim resolve,’ and ought doubtless to be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιπόνως</quote>. Now, as Brunck points out, Hesych. has <quote xml:lang="grc">σμυγερὸν, ἐπίπονον, οἰκτρόν, μοχθηρόν· σμυγερῶς, ἐπίπονως</quote>. So Eustath. p. 1463 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δὲ σμυγερῶς, τὸ ἐπιπόνως, ὀδυνηρῶς</quote>. Yet the following considerations make me hesitate to forsake the MSS. (1) Is it so clear that, in this context, the schol. could not have used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιπόνως</quote> to explain <quote xml:lang="grc"> στυγερῶς</quote>, seeing that the notions of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνος</quote> and ‘wretchedness’ are often so near to each other, esp. in poetry? (2) Apollonius Rhodius seems to be the earliest extant writer who uses <quote xml:lang="grc">σμυγερός</quote>: 2. 374 <quote xml:lang="grc">σμυγερώτατοι ἀνδρῶν</quote> (most laborious'): 4. 380 <quote xml:lang="grc">σμυγερῶς</quote>, ‘painfully.’ Homer has only <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισμυγερῶς</quote>: Hesiod has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισμυγερός</quote>. On the other hand, the form <quote xml:lang="grc">μογερός</quote> is used five times by Aesch. , thrice by Eur. , once by Soph. ( <ref n="Soph. El. 93">Soph. <title> El.</title> 93</ref>), and once by Ar. ; but <quote xml:lang="grc"> σμυγερός</quote> never. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπινωμᾶν_168">, intrans., ‘direct his course towards,’ ‘approach’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 717">cp. 717</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">προσενώμα</quote> also seems to be intrans., ‘bent his course towards’ the water. This intrans. use must come from the trans. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">νωμάω</quote> ‘to ply’ the limbs, or ‘guide’ a chariot, etc., as <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.358"> <title>Il.</title> 10. 358</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γούνατα νωμᾶν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OT 468"> <title>O. T.</title> 468</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδα νωμᾶν</quote> : <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίφρους τε νωμάσοισιν</quote>: we must mentally supply <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδα, ὁδόν</quote>, or the like. Apart from the two instances in this play, there appears to be no sound example of an intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">νωμάω</quote>. See Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκτίρω_169">, the spelling attested by Attic inscriptions ( <ref n="Soph. OT 13"> <title>O. T.</title> 13</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_169">, ‘(thinking) how’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 510"> <title>Ai.</title> 510</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκτιρε δ’, ὦναξ, παῖδα τὸν σόν,...ὅσον κακὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνῳ τε κἀμοὶ τοῦθ’, ὅταν θάνῃς, νεμεῖς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_του_170">. The force of <quote xml:lang="grc"> μή</quote> (as dist. from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>) is here ‘generic,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it presents the situation as typical of a class; ‘in <emph>a</emph> case <emph>where</emph> there is none to tend’: and this implies the <emph>cause</emph> of pity,—‘seeing that there is none to tend.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 256">Cp. 256</ref>, 715: <ref n="Soph. OT 397"> <title>O. T.</title> 397</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲ_170"> seems better than any of the proposed corrections (see cr. n.): and for <quote xml:lang="grc">μή του <emph> κηδομένου</emph>..., μηδὲ <emph>ἔχων</emph> </quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 737"> <title>O. C.</title> 737</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐξ ἑνὸς στείλαντος, ἀλλ’ ἀστῶν ὕπο | πάντων κελευσθείς</quote> </cit>, n.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 291"> <title>Tr.</title> 291</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν σοι τέρψις ἐμφανὴς κυρεῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μὲν παρόντων, τὰ δὲ πεπυσμένῃ λόγῳ</quote>.— The second syllable of the verse might be short (see Anal.), and therefore </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἐξ ἑνὸς στείλαντος, ἀλλ’ ἀστῶν ὕπο |
							πάντων κελευσθείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύντροφον_170"> could stand. But, as there is no other instance of that syllable being shortened in this strophe or antistrophe, Brunck's </gloss>
