<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύντροφον_170"> is better, and is received by Heinrich Schmidt (<title> Compositionslehre</title>, p. clxii.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυντρ_ὄμμ_170">, the face of a man who lives with one; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 977"> <title>Ai.</title> 977</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλτατ’ Αἴας, ὦ ξύναιμον ὄμμ’ ἐμοί</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλύει_174">, properly, ‘wanders in mind’; hence, here, of despairing perplexity, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1194">cp. 1194</ref>. This use of the word might be illustrated by Alexis <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυβερνήτης</quote> 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶθ’ οἱ μὲν εὐποροῦμεν, οἱ δ’ ἀλύομεν</quote>, ‘some of us are rich, while others are at their wits' ends.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντί_χρείας_174"> =each item or article of need, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, each new form in which need besets him. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1229"> <title>Ant.</title> 1229</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφορᾶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱσταμένῳ_174">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἵσταται</quote>, as it arises. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1263">Cp. 1263</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.240"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκώμενον ἵστατο κῦμα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Θεῶν_177">: the ‘devices’ of the gods are their mysterious dispensations, which can bring such misery on a man who was once fortunate. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρίσκοντο θεῶν παλάμαις τιμάν</quote>. I have accepted Lachmann's conjecture here, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_177"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητῶν_177">, because (1) there is a <emph>prima facie</emph> case for a short penult., answering to that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθυρόστομος</quote> (188); and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητῶν</quote>, so closely followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν</quote>, is very awkward, while <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν</quote> not only gives a forcible contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν</quote>, but suggests a thought well suited to the solemn pathos. Hermann defends </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητῶν_177_2"> at the cost of reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθυρόγλωσσος</quote> in 188. Heinrich Schmidt also keeps it, but re tains <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀθυρόστομος</quote> in 188, on the ground that, in this logaoedic measure, the ‘irrational syllable’ is admissible in the choree (<quote xml:lang="grc">αι— θνητ</quote>). A probably authentic example is <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν</quote> in 218 (=<quote xml:lang="grc">θροεῖ</quote> in 209). <quote xml:lang="grc">παλάμαι <emph>θνητῶν</emph> </quote>, if sound, would mean, ‘the resources of men’ (as shown by Philoctetes): so Theognis 623 <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοῖαι κακότητες ἐν ἀνθρώποισιν ἔασιν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοῖαι δ’ ἀρεταὶ καὶ βιότου παλάμαι</quote>. Cp. the praise of man as <quote xml:lang="grc"> παντοπόρος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 360.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 360.</ref>Not, ‘the (wicked) devices of men,’ as seen in the hero's enemies. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένη_178">, ‘races,’ in the narrower sense of ‘houses’ or ‘families,’ such as the princely house to which Philoctetes belonged: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.533"> <title>Od.</title> 15. 533</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμετέρου δ’ οὐκ ἔστι γένος βασιλεύτερον ἄλλο</quote>. Not ‘generations,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεαί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1186"> <title>O. T.</title> 1186</ref>): nor, again, ‘<emph>sons</emph> of men,’—a sense which could not be justified by the bold phrase in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 784"> <title>Ai.</title> 784</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Τέκμησσα, δύσμορον γένος</quote>. In <ref n="Hdt. 3. 159">Her. 3. 159</ref> read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα σφι γενεὴ</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc"> γένεα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπογίνηται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_αἰών_179">. <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἰών</quote> here= not ‘life’ merely (as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 583"> <title>Ant.</title> 583</ref>), but ‘fortune in life,’ as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 34"> <title>Tr.</title> 34</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτος αἰὼν εἰς δόμους τε κἀκ δόμων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰεὶ τὸν ἄνδρ’ ἔπεμπε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ__179">, ‘such as to exceed the ordinary lot,’—in prosperity, and afterwards in misery. The more highly placed a man is, the greater may be his fall. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 505">Cp. 505</ref> f.: <ref n="Soph. OT 1186"> <title>O. T.</title> 1186</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. OT 1282"> O. T. 1282</ref> ff. (the reverses of Oed.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1161"> <title>Ant.</title> 1161</ref> ff. (those of Creon). <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 528">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 528</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὶ μέσῳ τὸ κράτος θεὸς ὤπασεν</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Med. 123">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 123</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ γοῦν ἐπὶ μὴ μεγάλοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀχυρῶς εἴη καταγηράσκειν</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν γὰρ μετρίων πρῶτα μὲν εἰπεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔνομα νικᾷ</quote>: where it is added that ‘excess of good fortune’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὑπερβάλλοντα</quote>) ‘brings greater calamities on houses, when the god is wroth,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μείζους δ’ ἄτας, ὅταν ὀργισθῇ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων οἴκοις ἀπέδωκεν</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 7. 10">Her. 7. 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλέει γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τὰ ὑπερέχοντα πάντα κολούειν</quote>.—Others take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_μέτριος_179"> as, ‘exceeding the ordinary measure of woe.’ Cp. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 717"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 717</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ μέτρια πάσχομεν κακά</quote>. I prefer the former view, because (<emph>a</emph>) the sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένη_179"> suggests the greatness that precedes the abasement; and (<emph>b</emph>) vv. 180 ff., which comment on <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς μὴ μέτριος αἰών</quote>, show that these words suggested a contrast between Ph. 's past and present fortunes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρωτογόνων_180">: schol. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐγενῶν_180">. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">πρωτόγονος</quote> always = ‘firstborn.’ But as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαιόγονος</quote> can mean ‘of ancient race’ ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 981"> <title>Ant.</title> 981</ref>), so <quote xml:lang="grc">πρωτόγονος</quote> ‘of foremost race.’ Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 65">Thuc. 3. 65</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρες ὑμῶν οἱ πρῶτοι καὶ χρήμασι καὶ γένει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσως_180"> does not imply a doubt as to whether Philoctetes is of noble birth, but merely gives a certain vagueness to the surmise that no one else was nobler. Yet Nauck (following Burges) changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσως_180_2"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεγὼς_180"> because the Chorus must have <emph>known</emph> the hero to be noble. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 301"> <title>Tr.</title> 301</ref>(Deianeira is pitying the captives sent to her by Heracles), <quote xml:lang="grc">αἳ πρὶν μὲν ἦσαν ἐξ ἐλευθέρων ἴσως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσως</quote> does not mean that she doubts their former freedom, but merely that she does not know their fathers' names. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">που</quote> in <ref n="Thuc. 7. 77">Thuc. 7. 77</ref>§ 2 (Nicias speaking of himself), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ εὐτυχίᾳ δοκῶν που ὕστερός του εἶναι</quote>. So we sometimes guard a statement by saying, ‘perhaps’ the greatest, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδενὸς_180"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρός. οὐδεὶς οἴκων πρωτογόνων</quote> could mean either (<emph>a</emph>) no <emph>house</emph> of those houses, or (<emph> b</emph>) no <emph>man</emph> belonging to them; cp. <ref n="Plat. Prot. 316B">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 316B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπολλοδώρου υἱός, οἰκίας μεγάλης</quote>. Here it is possible, indeed, to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκου</quote> (‘a man inferior to no house,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘to no <emph>member of</emph> a house’). But in compressed Greek comparison the type <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐκείνου γένος οὐχ ὕστερόν ἐστι τῶν βασιλέων</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ γένους</quote>), is commoner than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνος</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐκείνου γένος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὕστερός ἐστι τοῦ τῶν βασιλέων γένους</quote>,—which latter would be the type here. Further, the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὸς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκου</quote>) depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων</quote> would increase the awkwardness. The reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκων_180"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκων_180"> is specious; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 636">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 636</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>δς</emph> ἐκ πατρῴας ἥκων γενεᾶς <ἄριστος</quote>>: though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστος</quote> is there doubtful. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκων_180_2"> is confirmed (<emph>a</emph>) by <ref n="Eur. Ion 1073">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1073</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ τῶν εὐπατριδᾶν γεγῶσ’ οἴκων</quote>: and (<emph>b</emph>) by the bold use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρωτογόνων</quote>, which <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων</quote> helps to interpret.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδενὸς_ὕστερος_180">, as Plat. <title>Tim.</title>p. 20 A <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐσίᾳ καὶ γένει οὐδενὸς ὕστερος ὢν τῶν ἐκεῖ</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_βίῳ_182"> belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμμορος_182"> (destitute, in his life, of all things): it cannot go with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντων_182">, as if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων τῶν ἐν βίῳ</quote>: but the sense is virtually the same. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνος_ἄλλων_183">, alone, apart from his fellows: an epic phrase; <title>Hymn. Hom.</title> 3. 193 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δὲ ταῦρος ἐβόσκετο μοῦνος ἀπ’ ἄλλων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.239"> <title>Od.</title> 16. 239</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μούνω ἄνευθ’ ἄλλων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στικτῶν_183"> (the epithet of an </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλαφος_183"> in <ref n="Soph. El. 568"> <title>El.</title> 568</ref>, and of <quote xml:lang="grc">νεβρίδες</quote> in <ref n="Eur. Ba. 111">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 111</ref>) naturally suggests deer, and, acc. to one interpretation, is meant here to denote the class of ‘peaceful animals,’ as dist. from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λασίων_183">, beasts of prey. The latter epithet, it might be objected, could equally well suggest goats and sheep. Another and stranger view is that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στικτῶν_183_2"> means birds (<emph>pictae volucres</emph>), as dist. from beasts. Obviously the poet used the epithets simply in order to call up a general picture of creatures that haunt the wilds; he was not carefully classifying them. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 937">Cp. 937</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετὰ_183">. The last syllable of a verse is of variable value (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδιάφορος</quote>, <emph>anceps</emph> ); <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a short may stand for a long, as here, or <emph>vice versa.</emph> Cp. Heinrich Schmidt, <title>Rhythmic and Metric</title>, p. 58, who cites <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1531">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1531</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπάλαμον μέριμναν | ὅπα</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the <quote xml:lang="grc">‐α^ν</quote> there serves as <quote xml:lang="grc">‐α—ν</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. Phil. 188">188</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθυρόστομ<emph> ος</emph> </quote>), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1089">1089</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἆμ<emph>αρ</emph> </quote> ), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1104">1104</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὕστερ<emph> ον</emph> </quote>), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1110">1110</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χερς<emph>ὶν</emph> </quote>), etc. It is needless, then, to alter <quote xml:lang="grc">μετά.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_ὀδύναις_183">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀδύναις τε</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref> n.): for this <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐν</quote> of circumstance, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1112">ib. 1112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν...μακρῷ | γήρᾳ</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀρεία_δ_187">, MeklerS. 's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρεῖα_δ_187">, occurs in his revision of <ref>Dindorf (6th Teubner ed., 1885)</ref>, where it is placed in the text. But, so far as I know, the arguments for it have not yet been stated. It is one of those emendations, the probability of which cannot be adequately estimated at a first glance, but must be carefully considered in relation to the peculiarities of the MS. (1) We observe, then, that L has <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρεῖ</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">αδ’ ἀθυρόστομος</quote>. This favours the view that v. 188 began with <quote xml:lang="grc">α δ’</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δ’</quote>. But, as metre proves, that <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> must have been <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>: and this points to an ending <quote xml:lang="grc">‐εί</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρεί</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote>. (2) Some corruptions in L, as in other MSS., have arisen through the genuine word being mistaken for one resembling it in form; as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος</quote> has become <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχθος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1355."> <title>O. T.</title> 1355.</ref>And this could occur even when the initial of the false word did not belong to the true word. See <ref n="Soph. Trach. 887"> <title>Tr.</title> 887</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στονόεντος ἐν τομᾷ σιδάρου</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τομᾷ</quote>, L there has <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>στ</emph>ομαῖ</quote>: and this, not merely through the influence of <quote xml:lang="grc">στονόεντος</quote>, but, evidently, because the scribe was thinking of <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα</quote>. Thus, even without assuming an intermediate <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεί</quote> |<quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεῖ</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote>, we see that <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρεῖ</quote> |<quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> was a possible corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρεί</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote>. Note, as increasing the probability, that v. 208, <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρεῖα τηλόθεν αὐδά</quote>, stands in L in the middle of the same page (82 A) which contains this verse. (3) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρεία_187">, as an epithet for Echo here, is illustrated by the only other place in the play where such echo is spoken of: 1458 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ δὲ φωνῆς τῆς ἡμετέρας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμαῖον ὄρος παρέπεμψεν ἐμοί</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">θῆρες</quote> have just been mentioned (185); and at 937 we have <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρῶν ὀρείων</quote>. Cp. <title>Hymn. Hom.</title> 19. 21 <quote xml:lang="grc">κορυφὴν δὲ περιστένει οὔρεος ἠχώ</quote>. Echo is the ‘neighbour’ of Pan (Moschus 6. 1), himself <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὀρεσσιβάτης</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1100"> <title>O. T.</title> 1100</ref>).—For other conjectures, see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθυρόστομος_187"> is not extant elsewhere. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 903">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 903</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ τις ἀθυρόγλωσσος, ἰσχύων θράσει</quote> </cit> . <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 838">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 838</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντ’ ἀχάλινον ἀκρατὲς ἀθύρωτον στόμα</quote>. Theognis 421 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖς ἀνθρώπων γλώσσῃ θύραι οὐκ ἐπίκεινται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμόδιαι</quote>.—Wecklein reads </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθυροστομοῦς_187"> (comparing <quote xml:lang="grc">θρασυστομεῖν, πολυστομεῖν</quote>), for the sake of the long final syll.: but see on <quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ</quote> in 184. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀχὼ_τηλεφανὴς_189">, Echo, appearing afar,—as if she came forth from her secret abode in response to the voice. Eur. introduced Echo as an (unseen) speaker in his <title>Andromeda;</title> cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1059">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 1059</ref> ff. (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠχώ, λόγων ἀντῳδός, ἐπικοκκάστρια</quote> ‘mocker’). But she was not, in the classical age, a distinctly recognised <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</quote>: though Paus. (2. 35. 10) saw at Corinth a <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερόν</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Χθονία</quote>, locally called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠχώ</quote>. Cp. Wieseler, <title>Die Nymphe Echo</title> (Göttingen, 1854).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικραῖς_ὑπακούει_189"> is the best correction yet proposed for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρᾶς_οἰμωγᾶςὑπόκειται_189">. With the latter verb, the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">πικραῖς οἰμωγαῖς</quote> would be required. The sense would then be, ‘Echo <emph>is subject to</emph> his cries,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, attends upon them, follows them, as a kind of understrain or accompaniment. Such a use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόκειται</quote> is not merely forced; it is (to my mind) inconceivable. Prof. Campbell, keeping the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρᾶς οἰμωγᾶς</quote>, renders <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόκειται</quote> ‘lies close to, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> keeps following upon’: and quotes <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 465B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 465B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ μὲν οὖν ἰατρικῇ...ἡ ὀψοποιϊκὴ κολακεία ὑπόκειται</quote>: but that means, ‘Cookery is a flattery which <emph>ranges under</emph> medicine,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, corresponds to it, as the counterfeit to the genuine art. And, on any view of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόκειται</quote>, the dat. is needful. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπακούει_189">, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.283">Hom. <title> Od.</title> 4. 283</ref>(Helen was calling to the heroes in the wooden horse; they were eager) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἐξελθέμεναλ, ἢ ἔνδοθεν αἶψ’ ὑπακοῦσαι</quote>, ‘to come forth, or to <emph>answer</emph> promptly from within.’ And <emph>ib.</emph> 10. 82 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιμένα ποιμὴν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠπύει εἰσελάων, ὁ δέ τ’ ἐξελάων ὑπακούει</quote>, ‘herdsman <emph>hails</emph> herdsman as he drives in, and the other, as he drives forth, <emph>makes answer.</emph>’ In classical prose, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπακούειν</quote> more often means to ‘respond’ in the sense of ‘comply.’ But the passages just cited prove that the word was also familiar as=‘to <emph>speak</emph> in reply’: nor was this latter sense confined to poetry; cp. <ref n="Aristot. Top. 8.11">Arist. <title>Top.</title> 8. 11</ref>(Berl. ed. p. 157 <emph>b</emph> 14) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρωτώμενοι τἀναντία καὶ τὸ ἐν ἀρχῇ πολλάκις ὑπακούουσιν</quote>, =<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀποκρίνονται</quote> (the word used previously in the same passage).—Other emendations of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόκειται</quote> are examined in the Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῖα_192">, predicate, ‘from the gods’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1039">cp. 1039</ref>, 1326.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀγώ_192">, I also (as well as others): the <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> gives a modest tone; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 719"> <title>Ant.</title> 719</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κεῖνα_192">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> here = ‘e'en’: its force is to mark that, from the very beginning, his troubles were heaven-ordained. This seems better than to take it as ‘both,’ answering to the <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν</quote> in 195.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_Χρύσης_192">, gen. of source, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέβη_192">. Such a simple gen. usu. denotes the <emph> place</emph> whence ( <ref n="Soph. OT 152"> <title>O. T.</title> 152</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πυθῶνος ἔβας</quote>), but the idea of ‘source’ could easily be connected with a person also; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1515"> <title>O. C.</title> 1515</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στράψαντα χειρὸς τῆς ἀνικήτου βέλη</quote>. It is also possible to join </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθήματα_Χρύσης_192"> as ‘sufferings inflicted by her’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 422">cp. 422</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ κείνων κακά</quote>, n.: but (<emph>a</emph>) the order of words renders this less natural: and (<emph>b</emph>) a gen. after <quote xml:lang="grc">πάθημα</quote> ought to denote the sufferer.— Philoctetes was bitten by a serpent that guarded the altar of Chrysè, in the islet of the same name, near Lemnos: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1326">cp. 1326</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠμόφρονος_192">, as cruelly punishing his intrusion. The <title>Iliad</title> (2. 723) speaks of him as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλκει μοχθίζοντα κακῷ ὀλοόφρονος ὕδρον</quote>. The relation of Chrysè to the gods is like that of Calypso in the <title> Odyssey.</title> The <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</quote> can work her will on the mortal; but only so far as the higher powers permit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_οὐ_196">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πονεῖ</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> instead of the usual <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 750"> <title>Ant.</title> 750</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην ποτ’ οὐκ ἔσθ’ ὡς ἔτι ζῶσαν γαμεῖς</quote>. This shows that we ought not to read <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ὅπως οὐ θεῶν μελέτῃ</quote> (omitting <quote xml:lang="grc">του</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_τεῖναι_197">, ‘in order that he should not bend...’ <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μή</quote> is not to be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">μελέτῃ</quote> (‘care to prevent his bending’), but with the whole preceding sentence. This constr. occurs (<emph> a</emph>) after words of hindering, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 5. 11">Xen. <title> An.</title> 3. 5. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς...ἀσκὸς δύο ἄνδρας ἕξει τοῦ μὴ καταδῦναι</quote>: (<emph>b</emph>) where the notion of hindering is not expressed, but only implied, as <ref n="Thuc. 1. 23">Thuc. 1. 23</ref>§ 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς αἰτίας προέγραψα..., τοῦ μή τινα ζητῆσαί ποτε ἐξ ὅτου τοσοῦτος πόλεμος...κατέστη</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 22">Id. 2. 22</ref>§ 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκλησίαν οὐκ ἐποίει..., τοῦ μὴ ὀργῇ...ξυνελθόντας ἐξαμαρτεῖν</quote>. So, in affirmative sentences, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>: id. 1. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">τό τε λῃστικὸν ...καθῄρει..., τοῦ τὰς προσόδους μᾶλλον ἰέναι αὐτῷ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεῖναι_βέλη_197">, <emph>point</emph> them, like <emph>sagittas tendere</emph> ( <title>C.</title> 1. 29. 9): we need not suppose that a word proper to the bow (‘stretch’) is transferred to the arrows. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_θεῶν_197">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, given by the gods (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 140">cp. 140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς σκῆπτρον</quote>), because the bow of Philoctetes was originally the gift of Apollo to Heracles ( <ref n="Apollod. 1.4">Apollod. 1. 4. 11</ref>§ 9). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 943">Cp. 943</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶνἐξήκοι_199">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶνἂν_ἐξήκῃ_199">, although the tense of the principal verb (<quote xml:lang="grc">πονεῖ</quote>, understood in v. 196) is primary, since a secondary tense is implied in the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν του μελέτῃ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘he is suffering, because the gods <emph>ordained</emph> that he should suffer, until the time should be fulfilled,’ etc. Cp. <ref n="Dem. 22.11">Dem. or. 22 § 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον ἔχει τὸν τρόπον ὁ νόμος...ἵνα μηδὲ πεισθῆναι μηδ’ ἐξαπατηθῆναι γένοιτ’ ἐπὶ τῷ δήμῳ</quote>: ‘the law <emph>stands</emph> thus, that the people <emph>might</emph> not even have the power’: where ‘stands’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote>) implies ‘was made’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτέθη</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">γένηται</quote> would be regular there, as <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἂν ἐξήκῃ</quote> would be here: yet in both places the optat. is natural. The speaker is tracing a present fact to a past motive.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγεται_199">: the Trojan seer Helenus had said that Troy was to be taken by Philoctetes before the summer was over (1340).—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τῶνδε</emph>, τῶν βελῶν</quote>: cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc"> τούσδε</quote>, 87. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔστομ_ἔχε_201">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σῖγα ἔχε</quote>: for the neut. pl. as adv., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</ref> n. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 171">Her. 2. 171</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτης...μοι πέρι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς τελετῆς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔστομα κείσθω</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘it will be best to observe silence.’ Aelian <title>De Nat. An.</title> 14. 28 <quote xml:lang="grc">τά <emph> γε</emph> παῤ ἐμοῦ ἔστω πρὸς αὐτοὺς</quote> (the gods) <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔστομα</quote>. <title>Nub.</title> 833 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐστόμει</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφήμει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_τόδε_201">; ‘what now?’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">τί τοῦτο</quote>; <ref n="Soph. OC 513."> <title>O. C.</title> 513.</ref>Here, as in 210 f., the rapid tribrachs mark excitement.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐφάνη_201">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 189">cp. 189</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλεφανής</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύντροφος_203">, sharing his life, <emph> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> </emph>, constantly attending on him, habitual to him: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 639"> <title>Ai.</title> 639</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτι συντρόφοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργαῖς ἔμπεδος</quote>, the dispositions that have grown with his growth,—the promptings of his own nature. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 50">Thuc. 2. 50</ref>(the plague) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδήλωσε...ἄλλο τι ὂν ἢ τῶν ξυντρόφων τι</quote> (the <emph> familiar</emph> maladies). Polyb. 4. 20 (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μουσικὴν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">σύντροφον ποιεῖν</quote>. The genit. (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτὸς_203">), though less usual than the dat., hardly requires us to view <quote xml:lang="grc">σύντροφος</quote> as a subst. (‘companion’): cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 96"> <title>Phaed.</title> 96D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ αὐτῶν οἰκεῖα</quote>, the things appropriate to them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤ_που_204">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 215">cp. 215</ref>. Cavallin reads </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_που_204"> with a note of interrogation after <quote xml:lang="grc">τόπων</quote>: but they do not doubt that it comes from one of the two quarters. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδ_τῇδε_204">: <ref n="Soph. OT 857"> <title>O. T.</title> 857</ref> n. The Attic form seems warranted by the colloquial tone; then, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτύμα</quote>, we return to lyric Doricism. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόπων_204"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 108"> <title>O. T.</title> 108</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ γῆς;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάλλει_204">: the fuller phrase in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1187"> <title>Ant.</title> 1187</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί με φθόγγος</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλλει δἰ ὤτων</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc"> βάλῃ</quote>, simply, of smell, <emph>ib.</emph> 412.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτύμα_204">, real,—not due to a hallucination of the senses. Cp. Theocr. 15. 82 (with ref. to painted figures), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔτυμ’ ἑστάκαντι καὶ ὡς ἔτυμ’ ἐνδινεῦντι</quote> (‘move in the dance’), | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμψυχ’, οὐκ ἐνυφαντά</quote>. Elsewhere the Attic fem. is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτυμος</quote> ( <title> Helen.</title> 351, <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 114">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 114</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στίβον_ἕρποντος_206">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1223">cp. 1223</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 287"> <title>Ai.</title> 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξόδους ἕρπειν κενάς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἀνάγκαν_206">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, under stress of pain: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 215">cp. 215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πταίων ὑπ’ ἀνάγκας</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 88"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὠδίνων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λοχίαις ἀνάγκαισι</quote>.—The reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στίβου_ἀνάγκαν_206"> would mean, ‘with painfulness of movement,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">στίβου</quote> being then the act of planting the steps (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 29">cp. 29</ref>, 157). This is tenable; but I prefer </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στίβον_206">, because (<emph>a</emph>) the phrase with <quote xml:lang="grc">στίβον</quote> seems a little forced, and (<emph>b</emph>) with the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">του... ἕρποντος</quote> the effect of an interposed gen. would be somewhat unpleasing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_λάθει_207">: this reiteration is natural, since the sound continues, and draws nearer. For the negative form after the positive, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 222"> <title>El.</title> 222</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξοιδ’, οὐ λάθει μ’ ὀργά</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 131: <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.563"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 563</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δέ σε γιγνώσκω, Πρίαμε, φρεσίν, οὐδέ με λήθεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρεῖα_207">, grievous, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, expressing pain; so <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 791">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title> 791</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠχὼ βαρεῖα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρυσάνωρ_207"> should properly be active, ‘man-wearing,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">φθισήνωρ</quote>, ‘man-destroying’ (epith. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεμος</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.833"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 833</ref>). And so some understand <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ τρυσάνωρ</quote> as=a lament for a disease that wears one: cp. on 695 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">στόνον...βαρυβρῶτ’</quote>. This, however, seems verystrained. More probably the poet has boldly used <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ τρυσάνωρ</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ ἀνδρὸς τετρυμένου</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα ἀνδρόφθορον</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα ἀνδρὸς ἐφθαρμένου</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1022"> <title>Ant.</title> 1022</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος διόβολος</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος δίου βέλους</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1463"> <title>O. C.</title> 1463</ref>). Cp. <title>Nub.</title> 421 <quote xml:lang="grc">φειδωλοῦ καὶ τρυσιβίου γαστρός</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">τρυς</quote>. seems to be pass.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">τετρυμένον βίον ἔχοντος</quote>, rather than act. (as if it imposed the hardships).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διάσημα_θροεῖ_207">. This, the reading of the MSS., seems sound. As to the metre, see n. on 218 (<quote xml:lang="grc">προβοᾷ τι γὰρ δεινόν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἔχε_210">. In many editions XO. is printed before these words, presumably to mark that they open the 3rd antistrophe. But, as the part of the Chorus is continuous from v. 202, a XO. here is confusing. It does not exist in L.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_210">, hortative, like, ‘come, then.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχε_vέας_210">: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> turn from thy reflections on his hard lot (162—168) to such thoughts as the moment of action demands. The effect of N.'s interpellation, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγ_τι_210">, is to mark excitement, and to bring out the reply with greater force. See n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 645."> <title>O. C.</title> 645.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔξεδρος_211">: for they have now learned that he has a permanent dwelling here (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 153">cp. 153</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολπὰν_ἔχων_211">, <emph>engaged in</emph> it; cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.515"> <title>Od.</title> 24. 515</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετῆς πέρι δῆριν ἔχοντες.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύριγγος_211">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.525"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 525</ref>(on the shield) <quote xml:lang="grc">δύω θ’ ἅμ’ ἕποντο νομῆες</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τερπόμενοι σύριγξι</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 399D"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 399D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύρα δή σοι...καὶ κιθάρα λείπεται, καὶ κατὰ πόλιν χρήσιμα· καὶ αὖ κατ’ ἀγροὺς τοῖς νομεῦσι σύριγξ ἂν εἴη</quote>:—a good illustration of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγροβότας</quote> here. Theocr. 7. 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">φαντὶ τὺ πάντες</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">συρικτὰν ἔμεναι μέγ’ ὑπείροχον ἔν τε νομεῦσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τ’ ἀμητήρεσσι</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλυρος, ἀφόρμικτος, ἀκίθαρις, ἄχορος</quote>, as epithets of wailing, etc. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1223">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1223</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποιμὰν_211">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιμὴν</quote>, is surely required here, where <quote xml:lang="grc">μολπὰν</quote> precedes and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγροβότας, ἀνάγκας, ἰωάν</quote> follow. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 132"> <title>O. C.</title> 132</ref>, where L has <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς εὐφήμου. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγροβότας_βόσκων_211">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1103"> <title>O. T.</title> 1103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάκες ἀγρόνομοι</quote>, n. Philoctetes is returning from wild places to his dwelling. This suggests the contrast with a shepherd who, playing his pipe, comes cheerily home from the ‘otia dia pastorum.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_που_215"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> After <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μολπὰν <emph>ἔχων</emph> </quote> we ought to have had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ ...βοῶν</quote>: but a finite verb, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βοᾷ_215">, takes the place of a second participle, as oft.: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 351."> <title>O. C.</title> 351.</ref>—Join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπἀνάγκας_215"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πταίων_215"> rather than with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βοᾷ_215_2">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκη</quote>, or stress of pain (206),—from the ulcered foot which he drags after him (291),— causes him to stumble on the rough ground.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλωπὸν_ἰωάν_215">, a cry heard from a distance. <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλωπός</quote> = (1) ‘of distant aspect,’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὤψ</quote>,) <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘seen afar’: then (2) simply, ‘distant,’ though the object is not visible: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 564"> <title>Ai.</title> 564</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλωπὸς οἰχνεῖ</quote>. It is in this general sense of ‘distant’ that <quote xml:lang="grc"> τηλωπός</quote> is here applied to a sound heard from afar. We cannot properly compare <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλεφανής</quote>, said of the personified Echo (189). In <ref n="Aesch. Seven 103">Aesch. <title> Theb.</title> 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπον δέδορκα</quote> may imply the mental picture called up by the clash of arms, as Verrall observes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναὸς_ὅρμον_217">, a haven that has no ship for its guest; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1383"> <title>O. C.</title> 1383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτωρ ἐμοῦ</quote>, ‘having no father in me’: and <emph>ib.</emph> 677 n. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξενος</quote> is here the opposite of <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύξενος</quote> rather than of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔξενος</quote>. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ναὸς ἄξενον</quote> is not less correct, while it is more forcible, than <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυσὶν ἄξενον</quote> would be. The waters off the rock-bound coast are a <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρμος ἄνορμος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 302">cp. 302</ref>).— Others render, ‘the inhospitable anchorage <emph>of our ship</emph>,’—which was not visible from the cave (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 467">cp. 467</ref>), but might have been seen by Philoctetes from another point. The sense seems, however, to be:—‘his cry is caused, either by physical pain, or by a feeling of despair as he looks at the lonely sea.’ The Chorus have been dwelling on his two great calamities—disease, and solitude (173 f., 185 f.). In this closing strain, it is natural that the two motives of their pity should be identified with the two sources of his anguish.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβοᾷ_δεινόν_217">. Wunder thus transposes <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ τι</quote>. It was hardly needful to defend the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> by <title>I. T.</title> 1036 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποπτεύω τι γάρ</quote>): cp. below, 1450 f. Heinrich Schmidt seems right in holding that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινόν_217"> here does not require <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνεῖ</quote> (for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θροεῖ_217">) in 209. Lachmann's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">προβοᾷ γὰρ <emph>αἴλινον</emph> </quote>, hardly deserved to be adopted by Blaydes. See Metrical Analysis.—Hermann's view, that in 209 <quote xml:lang="grc">διάσημα θροεῖ γάρ</quote> should be read, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">προβοᾷ <emph> δέ</emph> τι δεινόν</quote>, involves the arbitrary substitution of <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>. It would be obvious to suggest <quote xml:lang="grc">προβοᾷ τι γὰρ αἰνόν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">προβοᾷ γὰρ ἐλεινόν</quote>: but neither is probable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_ξένοι_219">, ‘extra metrum,’ as 736 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ θεοί</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1468"> <title>O. T.</title> 1468</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’, ὦναξ</quote>, etc. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰώ</quote> is a cry of surprise. In <ref n="Soph. OC 822"> <title>O. C.</title> 822</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ ξένοι</quote> (within the verse) is a despairing appeal (‘Alas, friends...’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκ_πάτρας_220">. In judging between this reading and the variant </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναυτίλῳ_πλάτῃ_220"> (see crit. n.), the probabilities of corruption must be carefully weighed. Suppose, first, that the poet wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ ποίας πάτρας</quote>. A transcriber who found <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας πάτρας</quote> in v. 222 might well assume that there was a fault either there or in v. 220: and since in v. 222 the words fit the construction, he might think that the fault was in v. 220. The substitute, <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτίλῳ πλάτῃ</quote>, might then be suggested by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέσχετ_220"> itself: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1207">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 1207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτίλῳ πλάτῃ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄργος κατασχών</quote> (from the <title>Archelaus</title> of Eur. ). Emendations not less arbitrary were sometimes made in early times: see, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, on <ref n="Soph. OT 134">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 134</ref> and 1529. Next, suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτίλῳ πλάτῃ</quote> was the true reading. It is clear and neat. To account for the variant <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ ποίας πάτρας</quote>, we must then suppose either (<emph>a</emph>) that a scribe wrote those words by an oversight,—his eye having wandered to v. 222; which is the less likely, since v. 222 did not give him <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ</quote>: or (<emph> b</emph>) that, <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτίλῳ πλάτῃ</quote> having been somehow lost, he filled the gap with a clumsy loan from v. 222. Neither hypothesis seems so probable as that a double <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας πάτρας</quote> should have led to guess-work in v. 220. Another point, though not a strong one, in favour of <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ ποίας πάτρας</quote> is that the two questions (‘who, and whence?’) are habitually combined in such inquiries: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> , <ref n="Soph. El. 779">Soph. <title>El.</title> 779</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρετ’, ὦ ξένοι· τίνες</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθεν πορεύεσθ’, ἐστέ τ’ ἐκ ποίας χθονός</quote>; <ref n="Hdt. 1. 35">Her. 1. 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς τε ἐὼν καὶ κόθεν...ἥκων</quote>: id. 2. 115 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς εἴη καὶ ὁκόθεν πλέοι</quote>: 4. 145 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνες τε καὶ ὁκόθεν εἰσί</quote>. On the other hand, we cannot insist on L's authority as against A's; for L has sometimes lost a true reading which A has kept (as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 28">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 28</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέσχετ_221">. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχειν</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦν</quote>, though <quote xml:lang="grc">νηΐ</quote> is sometimes added) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τόπον</quote> is the usu. prose constr., but poets use also a simple acc., as <title>Helen.</title> 1206 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδαπὸς δ’ ὅδ’ ἁνὴρ καὶ πόθεν κατέσχε γῆν</quote>; The difference between <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέχω</quote> (236) is like that between ‘to put into harbour’ and to ‘touch at’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the latter implies a further destination; the former does not necessarily imply it, though it does not exclude it (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 270">cp. 270</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεως_221"> instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάτρας_221">. But this does not affect the question of metre. It is more probable that, with Triclinius, we ought simply to place <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">γένους</quote> than (1) that Soph. wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας πάτρας</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς ἄν, κ.τ.λ.</quote>,— a verse like 101: or (2) that we should read <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας ἂν ὑμᾶς πατρίδος</quote>, with Dind., who remarks that <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρίδος</quote> holds that place in <ref n="Soph. OT 641">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 641</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 825">Soph. O. T. 825</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 428."> Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 428.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύχοιμ_εἰπών_223">, be right in calling. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1232">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1232</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί νιν καλοῦσα δυσφιλὲς δάκος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχοιμ’ ἄν</quote>; So <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ</quote> <ref n="Soph. El. 663.">Soph. <title>El.</title> 663.</ref>For the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 339"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 339</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχῆμα_223"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The sense is, <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα στολῆς ὑπάρχει Ἑλληνικόν, προσφιλέστατον ἐμοί</quote>. But, instead of that, we have </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχῆμα_ὑπάρχει_223"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλάδος_στολῆς_223">, and the epithet (<quote xml:lang="grc">προσφιλέστατον</quote>), which would more naturally go with <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote>, is joined to <quote xml:lang="grc">στολῆς</quote>:—‘the fashion is, to begin with, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπάρχει</quote>,) that of Hellenic garb,—the garb which I love best.’ The <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote> (<emph> habitus</emph>) denotes the general ‘fashion,’ or effect to the eye: <quote xml:lang="grc">στολὴ Ἑλλάς</quote> refers to the actual garments distinctive of Hellenes, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">χιτών</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱμάτιον</quote>. Cp. Eur. fr. 476 <quote xml:lang="grc">Τευθράντιον δὲ σχῆμα Μυσίας χθονός</quote> (the fashion of garb worn by the people of Teuthrania in Mysia). In <title>I. T.</title> 246 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδαποί; τίνος γῆς ὄνομ’ ἔχουσιν οἱ ξένοι</quote>; Monk conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμ’</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομ’</quote>.—Nauck rejects this verse, because the hero loves the land, not the clothes, of Hellas; and because he cannot yet be sure that these Greeks are friends. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄκνῳ_225"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> It seems simplest and best to construe thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ ὄκνῳ ἐκπλαγῆτε, δείσαντές με ἀπηγριωμένον</quote> (cp. <title>I. A.</title> 1535 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβοῦσα τλήμων κἀκπεπληγμένη φόβῳ</quote>); though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπλαγῆτε</quote> could directly govern <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1045"> <title>El.</title> 1045</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἐκπλαγεῖσά σε</quote>). In <ref n="Soph. OC 1625"> <title>O. C.</title> 1625</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στῆσαι φόβῳ δείσαντας ἐξαίφνης τρίχας</quote>, the dat. is causal (‘through fear’): in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 176"> <title>Tr.</title> 176</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc"> φόβῳ...ταρβοῦσαν</quote>, it has an adverb. force (‘sorely afraid’; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 65"> <title>O. T.</title> 65</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπηγριωμένον_225">, made like to an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγριος</quote>, or wild man: cp. the description of Philoctetes, as Diomedes and Odysseus found him at Lemnos, in Quintus Smyrnaeus 9. 364 ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐαλέαι δέ οἱ ἀμφὶ κόμαι περὶ κρατὶ κέχυντο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρὸς ὅπως ὀλοοῖο</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">καί οἱ πᾶν μεμάραντο δέμας, περὶ δ’ ὀστέα μοῦνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥινὸς ἔην, ὀλοὴ δὲ παρηΐδας ἀμφέχυτ’ αὐχμὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λευγαλέον ῥυπόωντος</quote>. Attius <title>Phil.</title>fr. 14 <emph>quod te obsecro</emph>, <emph>aspernabilem ne haec taetritudo mea me inculta faxit.</emph> Cp. Tennyson, <title>Enoch Arden:</title> ‘Downward from his mountain gorge | Stept the longhair'd long-bearded solitary, | Brown, looking hardly human, strangely clad’... </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούμενον_228">, suffering hardship. Cp. <title> Helen.</title> 268 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς θεῶν κακοῦται</quote> (he suffers reverses). <title>Legg.</title> 932 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν κακούντων ἢ κακουμένων</quote>, those who inflict or who suffer injury. This is a certain correction of the vulg. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλούμενον_228">, which cannot be defended either as (1) pass., ‘called,’—explained by Blomfield as being here little more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>: or (2) midd., ‘invoking’ you. Soph. once uses the midd., <ref n="Soph. OC 1385">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1385</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρὰς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅς σοι καλοῦμαι</quote>: but here the obscurity would be extreme. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_230">, appealing, ‘nay’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 237"> <title>O. C.</title> 237</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῶν_γ_230">, lit., to be disappointed, in regard to this, on your part. The gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῶν_230"> is not construed directly with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμαρτεῖν</quote> (as though ‘to fail of you’ meant ‘to be repulsed by you’), but is like the gen. in <ref n="Soph. OT 580"> <title>O. T.</title> 580</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐμοῦ κομίζεται</quote> (‘from me’), <emph>ib.</emph> 1163 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεξάμην δέ του</quote>. The acc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_230">, again, is not directly governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμαρτεῖν</quote>, but is analogous to the acc. of pronouns or adjectives which can stand, almost adverbially, after <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ</quote>, as <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 711">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 711</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνειν τὰ πρόσφορα</quote>: see <ref n="Soph. OT 1298">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1298</ref> n. In L the reviser has written <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τοῦτο</quote> over <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote>, showing that he understood it thus. Cp. Eupolis fr. 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγ’ ὅτου )πιθυμεῖς, κοὐδὲν ἀτυχήσεις ἐμοῦ</quote> (so Meineke, with Priscian 18. 1175, who has <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὐδὲν</quote>: though Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> 462 gives <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ</quote>). Thus Wunder's change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτό_γ_230"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδέ_γ_230"> seems needless, though the double gen. could be illustrated by 1315 (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1170"> <title>O. C.</title> 1170</ref> n.), and the phrase by <ref n="Eur. Med. 867">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 867</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τἂν ἁμάρτοις τοῦδέ γ’, ἀλλ’ ἀκούσομαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_232">, in assent: 48 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕνεκα_232">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 63"> <title>Ant.</title> 63</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_234">, expressing joyful wonder: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1724">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1724</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φεῦ φεῦ τῆς ὥρας, τοῦ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κάλλους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_λαβεῖν_234">, ‘that I should <emph>e'en</emph>, really, have received....’ Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1051">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1051</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τῆς ἐμῆς κάκης</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καὶ προσέσθαι μαλθακοὺς λόγους φρενί</quote> (‘nay, out upon my cowardice,—that I should e'en have admitted such soft pleadings to my soul!’). <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 2. 2. 3">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2. 2. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπε πρὸς αὑτόν· Τῆς τύχης, τὸ ἐμὲ νῦν κληθέντα δεῦρο τυχεῖν</quote> (‘to think that...!’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῦδ_ἀνδρὸς_234">, not merely a Greek, but one of such gentle breeding as is announced by the stranger's mien and speech.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μακρῷ_234">, <emph>after</emph> it: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 305">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ σὸν ὄμμα μυρίαις ἐν ἁμέραις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">προσεῖδον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 88">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 88</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέσχε_236"> has been much suspected, because <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέχω</quote>, in its nautical use, means, ‘touch at’ a place, not, ‘<emph>cause</emph> one to touch at it.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέχω</quote>, as=‘touch at,’ meant properly, ‘to guide one's ship towards’ ( <ref n="Hdt. 9. 99">Her. 9. 99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσσχόντες τὰς νέας</quote>),—<quote xml:lang="grc"> ναῦν</quote> being commonly understood. Where prose, then, would say, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος χρῄζων προσέσχες</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ναῦν</quote>); poetry might surely say, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς χρεία προσέσχε σε</quote>; ‘what need guided thy course to land?’ It may be added that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσήγαγεν_236"> is itself an argument for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέσχε_236_2">. ‘Brought thee to this shore,—aye, brought thee to my side.’ <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσέσχε</quote> implies only a passing visit to the coast; <quote xml:lang="grc">προσήγαγε</quote> supplements it in a way suitable to the forlorn man's eager hope. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_φίλτατος_237">;=<quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνεμος</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέμων ὁ φίλτατος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>); the art. emphasizes the superl.: see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 100"> <title>Ant.</title> 100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κάλλιστον...τῶν προτέρων φάος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέγωνε_237">, imperat. of the perf. <quote xml:lang="grc">γέγωνα</quote>, of which the subjunct. <quote xml:lang="grc">γεγώνω</quote> occurs <ref n="Soph. OC 213"> <title>O. C.</title> 213</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_εἰδῶ_237"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 776"> <title>Ant.</title> 776</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 6"> <title>Ai.</title> 6</ref>, etc.: <ref n="Soph. OC 889"> <title>O. C.</title> 889</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξαθ’, ὡς εἰδῶ τὸ πᾶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένος_239">, acc. of respect: <ref n="Soph. El. 706"> <title>El.</title> 706</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰνιὰν γένος</quote>: fr. 61. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀργείᾳ γένος</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 8.114">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 8. 114</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Qui genus</quote> </cit> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">estis</foreign>)? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σκύρου_240">: for the gen., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 236"> <title>O. T.</title> 236</ref> n. Scyros (still <title>Skyro</title>), the small island, about 25 miles long from N.W. to S.E. , which lies about 35 miles E. of Euboea, nearly in the latitude of Trachis. In 469 B.C. Cimon expelled the predatory Dolopes from the island, and brought the reputed remains of Theseus to Athens. Scyros then became a possession of the Athenians, in whose estimation it was a dreary and insignificant little place (<ref n="Dem. 52.9">[Dem.] or. 52 § 9</ref> ): <quote xml:lang="grc">Σκυρία ἀρχή</quote> suggested the same idea as <emph>vacuis aedilis Ulubris.</emph> The name means ‘stony.’ <quote xml:lang="grc"> σκύρος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ</quote>), which Curtius connects with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύειν</quote> ‘to scrape,’ was used to denote ‘chips from hewn stones’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">λατύπη</quote>). At Cyrene the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκυρωτὴ ὁδός</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. P. 5">Pind. <title>P.</title> 5. 93</ref>) was not a ‘paved’ road, but a road ‘hewn’ out of the solid rock.—Cp. Apollodorus 3. 13. 8: ‘Thetis, in the foreknowledge that Achilles must perish if he went to the war, disguised him in woman's attire, and left him, as a girl, in Scyros. There he grew up, and married Deidameia (<quote xml:lang="grc">Δηϊδάμεια</quote>) daughter of Lycomedes [king of Scyros]; and a son was born to them, Pyrrhus, afterwards called Neoptolemus.’< </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νεοπτόλεμος_241">: <quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘˘˘</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 4">cp. 4</ref>n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθα_πᾶν_241">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 389">cp. 389</ref>: 1240: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 402"> <title>Ant.</title> 402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐπίστασαι</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_χθονός_242">, O thou (who belongest to) a dear land. The possessive gen. can thus be used, without a subst., just as the gen. of origin (a special form of the possessive) is so used, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 379"> <title>Ant.</title> 379</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δύστηνος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δυστήνου πατρός</quote>. We cannot well repeat </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖ_242"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλης χθονός</quote>, because, though classical idiom allowed <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδες Ἑλλήνων</quote>, it would hardly allow <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς Ἑλλάδος</quote> in the fig. sense, ‘a son of Greece.’ That would mean rather, ‘a boy belonging to Greece’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάδος νεανίαι</quote>, <title>I. A.</title> 52). It seems needless to write <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὦ )κ</emph> φίλης χθονός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρέμμα_Λυκομήδους_243">, because the infant Neoptolemus was left to the care of his maternal grandfather, Lycomedes (239 n.), after his father had gone to Troy. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.326"> <title>Il.</title> 19. 326</ref>(Achilles speaks) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς Σκύρῳ μοι ἔνι τρέφεται φίλος υἱός</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OT 1143"> <title>O. T.</title> 1143</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θρέμμα</quote> =‘foster-son.’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόλῳ_243"> (causal dat.), mission, errand: <ref n="Soph. OC 358"> <title>O. C.</title> 358</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς σ’ ἐξῆρεν οἴκοθεν στόλος</quote>;— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέσχες_γῆν_243">. The usual dative would be awkward here, on account of <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι στόλῳ</quote>: and the acc. is warranted by the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχω γῆν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχω εἰς γῆν</quote> (221 n.): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 355">cp. 355</ref> f. In Polyb. 2. 9. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέρος δέ τι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν λέμβων</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέσχε τὸν Ἐπιδαμνίων λιμένα</quote>, Bekker is probably right in adding <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">τόν</quote>.—After <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν</quote> a comma seems better than a note of interrogation: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς πόθεν εἶς</quote>, etc. (220 n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_δὴ_245"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> Here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_245"> =‘you must know,’ and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_245">=‘then’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘since you ask me’). The effect of the particles (which could be properly represented only by voice and manner) is to give an easy, ready tone to the answer. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1171"> <title>O. T.</title> 1171</ref>(though the tone there is somewhat different) <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνου γέ τοι δὴ παῖς ἐκλήζεθ’</quote>. Burges thinks that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_δὴ_245">, without a preceding <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>, is strange, and conjectures </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὼ_δὴ_245">. But <quote xml:lang="grc">τοί</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> have each their proper force, which does not depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>: and here a <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> would have over-emphasised <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰλίου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γ_246">: (How can you be coming from Troy?),—for you certainly did not <emph>go</emph> there with us at first. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δή...γε</quote> in rejecting an alternative supposition, see <ref n="Soph. OC 110"> <title>O. C.</title> 110</ref> n.—The order of the words almost compels us to join </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_στόλου_246">: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 664 E <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἀρχὰς τῶν λόγων</quote>. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦσθα ναυβάτης</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1027">cp. 1027</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν</quote> (dat. of interest)=‘sailedst in our fleet.’ But it would also be possible to join <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυβάτης</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ στόλου</quote> (partitive gen., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 73">cp. 73</ref>), taking <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἀρχήν</quote> as=‘originally.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γὰρ_248"> in eager question: 322, 654, <ref n="Soph. OC 64."> <title>O. C.</title> 64.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνου_248">, of warfare, the peculiarly Homeric use (as <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.601"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 601</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορόων πόνον αἰπὺν ἰῶκά τε δακρυόεσσαν</quote>), also freq. in Herod. (<ref n="Hdt. 9.27">9. 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖσι Τρωϊκοῖσι πόνοισι</quote>): cp. <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 107">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἰλίου τε κἀπὸ Τρωϊκῶν πόνων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γὰρ_249">...; As in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γάρ</quote> (248) and <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς γάρ</quote> (250), the <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> marks surprise (‘your words are strange, — for ...’ etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθα_κάτοιδ_249">, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1063"> <title>Ant.</title> 1063</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἴσθι...κατισθι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 922"> <title>El.</title> 922</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. οὐκ οἶσθ’ ὅποι γῆς οὐδ’ ὅποι γνώμης φέρει. ΧΡ. πῶς δ’ οὐκ ἐγὼ κάτοιδ’ ἅ γ’ εἶδον ἐμφανῶς;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_κάτοιδ_249">; ‘how do I know?’=‘of course I do not know.’ This form is more emphatic, because more direct, than <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν ᾔδη</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδείην</quote>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἔμελλον εἰδέναι</quote>; But it is rare except in affirmation (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὐ</quote>...), as in <title>El. l.c.</title>; <ref n="Xen. Ec. 18">Xen. <title>Oecon.</title> 18</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο μὲν οἶσθα...τί δ’ οὐκ, ἔφην ἐγώ, οἶδα</quote>; See, however, <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.75">Her. I. 75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῶς γὰρ...διέβησαν αὐτόν;</quote> </cit> (=‘how <emph>can they</emph> have crossed the river?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνοὐ_ἄῤ_251"> is better than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομά γ’</quote> (cp. fr. 315 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς μὲν οὐκ ἄρ’ ᾖστε τὸν Προμηθέα</quote>;). The variant <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ οὔνομ’</quote> might seem to favour </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_τοὔνομ_251">, but the latter (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρα</quote>) would be too abrupt: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_251"> is clearly genuine. More probably <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔνομ’</quote> (a form unknown to Tragedy) was merely a late attempt to mend the metre.— Ph. here asks, in effect: ‘Finding me, a lonely sufferer, here in Lemnos, cannot you guess who I am?’—and the youth answers, No. This is quite consistent with <ref n="Soph. Phil. 261">261</ref>, where Ph. assumes that his name, when announced, will be recognised. Neoptolemus might have heard of him as possessing the bow of Heracles (262), and yet not have heard of him as suffering on Lemnos. We could not join <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα</quote>, as=‘mention,’ with <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διωλλύμην_251">, all these ten years, while my former comrades have been active at Troy.< </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθι_εἰδότ_253">, rest assured that I know nothing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_253"> marks the mental point of view (‘regard me in the light of one who knows nothing’): distinguish this use of it from that in 117 (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_253"> is generic (170 n.), not due to the imperat.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 415">cp. 415</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1063"> <title>Ant.</title> 1063</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὡς</emph> μὴ )μπολήσων ἴσθι τὴν ἐμὴν φρένα</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_253_2"> cp. also below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 567"> Ant. 567</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 848."> <title>O. T.</title> 848.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλλ_254"> adv., ‘very’: <ref n="Soph. OC 1514"> <title>O. C.</title> 1514</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρὸς_254">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐχθρός</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 955">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 955</ref>(a soothsayer) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν μὲν ἐχθοὰ σημήνας τύχῃ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὸς καθέστηχ’ οἶς ἂν οἰωνοσκοπῇ</quote>, ‘odious.’ Hence the conject. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγνὸς_254"> (Nauck) is wholly needless. The active sense, ‘hostile,’ is more freq., as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1359">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1359</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν φίλοι καὖθις πικροί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_μηδὲ_255"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, a man of whom no report (the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, 170n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκαδε_255">, to Malis (4 n.),—where the tidings would have had a special interest: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_μηδαμοῦ_255">, nor in any part of Hellas (for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 204">cp. 204</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆδε τόπων</quote> n.). As Neoptolemus is coming from Troy, the words have more force if we suppose the poet to use <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάς</quote> in the larger sense which was so familiar in his own day,— as including all lands inhabited by Greeks. Thus the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάς</quote> of Her. comprises Ionia (I. 92) and Sicily (7. 157); and Soph. himself ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1060">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1060</ref>) has <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ Ἑλλὰς οὔτ’ ἄγλωσσος</quote>. The thought will then be, ‘he had not heard of me from the mainland of Greece before he left Scyros; nor has he heard of me, since he has been at Troy, from any part of the Greek world.’ It is no objection, of course, that the Homeric poems do not recognise the Greek colonies in Asia Minor; the Attic drama was not careful in such matters. Even, however, if we restricted <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάδος γῆς</quote> to Greece Proper, it would still be natural that Ph. should say, ‘neither to Malis, nor to any part of Greece.’ Nauck, thus limiting <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάς</quote>, pronounces the distinction unmeaning; he further objects to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_ἔχοντος_255"> (because Ph. means that, not merely his plight, but his existence, is unknown); and therefore rejects <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦδ’ ἔχοντος οἴκαδε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ Ἑλλάδος γῆς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδαμοῦ_255"> need not be changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμοῖ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1019"> <title>O. C.</title> 1019</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῦ ...<emph> τῆς</emph> ἐκεῖ</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε</quote>): <ref n="Soph. El. 1099"> <title>El.</title> 1099</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιποροῦμεν ἔνθα</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc"> χρῄζομεν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πω_255"> should prob. be read instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_255">. The long lapse of time imagined renders <quote xml:lang="grc">πω</quote> forcible; while <quote xml:lang="grc">που</quote> could mean only, ‘I ween’; it could not go with <quote xml:lang="grc"> μηδαμοῦ</quote> as=‘to no place whatsoever.’ In <ref n="Soph. OC 1370"> <title>O. C.</title> 1370</ref>, where </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πω_255_2"> is certain, L has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_255_2"> from the first hand. In fr. 467, again, <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγῳ γὰρ ἕλκος οὐδὲν οἶδά που τυχεῖν</quote>, the correction <quote xml:lang="grc">πω</quote> (Dindorf) is clearly right. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῖγ_ἔχοντες_258">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, saying nothing about Ph. 's fate, but allowing it to pass out of men's minds. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 954">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 954</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ ῥα κελαινώπαν θυμὸν ἐφυβρίζει πολύτλας ἀνήρ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">γελᾷ δὲ τοῖσδε μαινομένοις ἄχεσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺν γέλωτα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθηλε_258">: <ref n="Soph. El. 260"> <title>El.</title> 260</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">πήματα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλλοντα μᾶλλον ἢ καταφθίνονθ’ ὁρῶ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὶ_ἔρχεται_258">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 638"> <title>O. T.</title> 638</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τὸ μηδὲν ἄλγος εἰς μέγ’ οἴσετε</quote>; <ref n="Thuc. 1. 118">Thuc. 1. 118</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ μέγα ἐχώρησαν δυνάμεως</quote>: 4. 117 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ μεῖζον χωρήσαντος αὐτοῦ</quote> (when he had made further progress). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_260">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 910">cp. 910</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 193"> <title>Ant.</title> 193</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παίδων τῶν ἀπ’ Οἰδίπου</quote>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδ_σοι_260"> (ethic dat.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνος_260">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 665"> <title>El.</title> 665</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε σοι κείνη πάρα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 138"> <title>O. C.</title> 138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἐγώ</quote>, n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύεις_260">, pres., knowest by hearsay: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 591">cp. 591</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 305"> <title>O. T.</title> 305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ μὴ κλύεις τῶν ἀγγέλων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 792"> <title>O. C.</title> 792</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσῳπερ κἀκ σαφεστέρων κλύω.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_Ἡρ_260">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπλων_260">: the bow given by Apollo to Heracles, and by him to Ph. , as a reward for kindling the pyre on Oeta: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 198">cp. 198</ref> n., 670 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεσπότην_260">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 27"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιῶνδε δεσπότης μαντευμάτων</quote>. So <emph>dominus.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_οἱ_263">: for the art. at the end of the v., see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 409"> <title>Ant.</title> 409</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ κατεῖχε τὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_ἄναξ_263">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.631"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 631</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰρ Ὀδυσσεὺς ἦγε Κεφαλλῆνας μεγαθύμους</quote>: who are there described as inhabiting Ithaca, Zacynthus, Samos (=Cephallenia, first so called in <ref n="Hdt. 9. 28">Her. 9. 28</ref>), and other islands off the coast of Acarnania, as well as part of the mainland itself. So <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.378">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 24. 378</ref>(Laertes) <quote xml:lang="grc">Κεφαλλήνεσσιν ἀνάσσων</quote>. Buttmann thinks that both here and in 791 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦξένε Κεφαλλήν</quote>) the name is used scornfully. Its Homeric associations, at least, are honourable (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.330">Hom. <title>Il.</title> 4. 330</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">Κεφαλλήνων ἀμφὶ στίχες οὐκ ἀλαπαδναί</quote>). To assume that the Cephallenians were despised because the Taphii, their neighbours, were pirates ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.427">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 15. 427</ref>), seems a little unfair to them. But it is very likely that the name is used, if not with scorn, yet with a tone of dislike,—‘king of those crafty islanders.’ The Athenians had experienced the skill of Cephallenians in laying a deadly ambuscade ( <ref n="Thuc. 2. 33">Thuc. 2. 33</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγρίᾳ_265"> is followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγρίῳ_265"> in 267. The effect is certainly unpleasing. But with regard to such repetitions it must always be remembered that ancient poetry was far less fastidious than modern: see n. on 88 (<quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν</quote>). On the other hand, Eustathius, the witness for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοινίῳ_265"> in 267 (cp. cr. n.), was frequently loose in citation: see <title>Ant.</title>, append., p. 249. The recurrence of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρημον_265"> in 269 offends less, but is noteworthy. It is not surprising that interpolation should have been suspected. Three views have been held. (1) Prinz would reduce vv. 264—269 to three (see crit. note). This reconstruction is too violent to be probable. (2) Nauck would omit the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρημον, ἀγρίᾳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσῳ καταφθίνοντα</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν ᾗ</quote> in 268 confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">vόσῳ</quote>: it could not refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχίδνης</quote>. And <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν ᾧ</quote> would hardly have become <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν ᾗ</quote>. (3) Jacob A. proposed to omit vv. 268-70. This would obviate the repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρημον</quote>, and of <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾤχοντ’</quote> (273); but it would also suppress the notice of Chrysè; which, however, Ph. would naturally mention, as he supposes that the whole story is new to the youth. I believe that there has been no interpolation, though Soph. has written with some verbal negligence. The point of vv. 264—267 is the putting ashore (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ριψαν</quote>): that of 268—270, the desertion (<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾤχοντ’</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἐχίδνης_266">, that which guards Chrysè's shrine (1327). The definite art. is sufficiently natural, as Ph. is following the train of his own memories, —even if he supposes that N. has not heard of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχιδνα</quote> before (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 255">cp. 255</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαράγματι_266">, the rent left by the serpent's bite: cp. <title> Anacreontea</title> 26 <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὸς χάραγμ’</quote> (brand of fire,—on horses): [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 73 <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτον χαραχθείς</quote> (wounded). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_ᾗ_268">, referring to <quote xml:lang="grc"> νόσῳ</quote>,— ‘in company with’ it,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ ξυνόντα</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1022">cp. 1022</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 17"> <title>O. T.</title> 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν γήρᾳ βαρεῖς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾤχοντ_268"> would probably follow </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρημον_268">, but gains emphasis by coming first; for the irregular order of words, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1251"> <title>O. T.</title> 1251</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_Χρύσης_268">, the small island near Lemnos (see n. on 8 ff., and Introd.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέσχον_268">: 221 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσμενοι_271">, because they could now slip away without being vexed by his entreaties and reproaches. The word adds an effective touch to the picture of their heartlessness. Dindorf's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσμενον_271"> (received by Nauck and Blaydes) is far weaker; nor is it suitable. In <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 1.13">Lys. or. 1 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκάθευδον ἄσμενος</quote> </cit> means, ‘I gladly <emph>went to</emph> sleep’ (inchoative imperf.). Here, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσμενον εὕδοντα</quote> would mean, not ‘gladly going to sleep,’ but ‘gladly sleeping,’—as though with conscious satisfaction.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_σάλου_271">, after the rough passage from the islet of Chrysè to Lemnos (<ref target="cl8">see on 8</ref> ff.). Cp. <ref n="Soph.
