Ant. 150"> <title>Ant.</title> 150</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ ...πολέμων</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 163 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῷ σάλῳ σείσαντες</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηρεφεῖ_271">, roofed over, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, here, over-arching,—forming a cave; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 885"> <title>Ant.</title> 885</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέτρᾳ_271">, ‘a rock,’ is a necessary correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέτρῳ_271">, ‘a stone.’ <quote xml:lang="grc"> πέτρος</quote> is never used in the larger sense, nor could the epithet here justify such a use. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 3. 11">Xen. <title> An.</title> 4. 3. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πέτρᾳ ἀντρώδει</quote>: but <emph>ib.</emph> 7. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς πέτρος</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, none of the <quote xml:lang="grc"> λίθοι</quote> mentioned in § 10) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωθεν ἠνέχθη</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_273">, adv.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 293">cp. 293</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 751"> <title>O. T.</title> 751</ref>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτὶ_δυσμόρῳ_273">, as for some poor wretch, some beggar, for whom their least gifts were good enough. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 424">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦ φιλοκτήτου τὰ τοῦ πτωχοῦ λέγεις</quote>; (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥάκη</quote>). Not, ‘rags such as my wretched state required’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> for dressing his wound, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 39">cp. 39</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_καὶ_274">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 308">cp. 308</ref>: fr. 304 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δή τι καὶ παρεῖκα τῶν ἀρτυμάτων</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 1. 107">Thuc. 1. 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τι</quote> (adv.) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοῦ δήμου καταλύσεως ὑποψίᾳ</quote>: id. 2. 17 <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τι καὶ Πυθικοῦ μαντείου ἀκροτελεύτιον</quote>: <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title> O.</title> 1. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί πού τι</quote> (adv.) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ βροτῶν φάτις κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βορᾶς_274">: to avoid the pollution of directly causing his death by starvation: cp. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 775"> <title>Ant.</title> 775</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβῆς τοσοῦτον ὡς ἄγος μόνον προθείς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_τύχοι_274">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 315">cp. 315</ref>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 3"> Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 2. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴομαι γὰρ ἂν ἡμᾶς τοιαῦτα παθεῖν οἶα τοὺς ἐχθροὺς οἱ θεοὶ ποιήσειαν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_δὴ_276"> (which has been needlessly altered, see cr. n.) suits the earnest appeal: ‘try to imagine for yourself what I felt.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάστασιν_276">, cogn. acc. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στῆναι_276"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναστῆναι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 50"> <title>O. T.</title> 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στάντες τ’ ἐς ὀρθόν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποἶ_ἐκδακρῦσαι_278"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> δάκρυα</quote>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1344">Eur. <title>Ph.</title> 1344</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ ἐκδακρῦσαί γ’</quote> </cit> (burst into tears).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποἶ_κακά_278">, ‘what <emph>woes</emph> I lamented.’ This version is recommended (<emph>a</emph>) by the fact that the following verses develope a picture of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote>: (<emph>b</emph>) by the ordinary use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποιμώζειν</quote> with an acc. of the object deplored, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1224"> <title>Ant.</title> 1224</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 31">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 31</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 635">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 635</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Alc. 768">Eur. Alc. 768</ref>: Aesch. fr. 134. But another version is also possible: ‘shrieked out <emph> reproaches.</emph>’ Here, however, he is speaking rather of his misery than of his resentment. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶντα_279"> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦς <emph>μὲν</emph> βεβώσας, ἄνδρα <emph>δὲ</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1136">cp. 1136</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶν μὲν αἰσχρὰς ἀπάτας, στυγνὸν δὲ φῶτ’ ἐχθοδοπόν</quote>. In both passages the irregular place of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> is due to the writer having begun as if he intended to repeat the partic. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁράω</quote>: as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶντα μὲν ναῦς...ὁρῶντα δὲ ἄνδρα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 25"> <title>O. T.</title> 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνουσα μὲν...φθίνουσα δ’</quote>, n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 372"> <title>Ai.</title> 372</ref> ff., n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_281"> is equivalent to a repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδένα</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστιςἀρκέσειεν_281">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 693">cp. 693</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ᾧ...ἀποκλαύσειεν</quote> (where, as here, the sentence is negative). <title>I. T.</title> 588 ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδένα γὰρ εἶχον ὅστις Ἀργόθεν μολὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς Ἄργος αὖθις τὰς ἐμὰς ἐπιστολὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψειε</quote>. And in a positive sentence, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 903">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 903</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύψασ’ ἑαυτὴν ἔνθα μή τις εἰσίδοι</quote>. The relative clause with the optative is, in such instances, virtually a final clause (as here, ‘no one <emph>to</emph> help’). Instead of such an optative, we usually find in Attic the fut. ind., even after a secondary tense; as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅστις ἀρκέσει</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <title>H.</title> 2. 3. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξε τῷ δήμῳ τριάκοντα ἄνδρας ἑλέσθαι, οἳ τοὺς πατρίους νόμους συγγράψουσι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσου_συλλάβοιτο_281">, put a helping hand to the disease, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, help to lighten its burden, for me in my suffering. As <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμβάνομαί τινος</quote>=to lay hold on a thing, so <quote xml:lang="grc">συλλαμβάνομαί τινός</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τινι</quote>=to lay hold on it <emph>along with</emph> another person; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to help him with it. <ref n="Eur. Med. 946">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 946</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συλλήψομαι δὲ τοῦδέ σοι κἀγὼ πόνου</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 47">Thuc. 4. 47</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνελάβοντο δὲ τοῦ τοιούτου οὐχ ἥκιστα</quote>, they mainly contributed to such a result. Cp. id. 4. 10 § 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ξυναράμενοι τοῦδε τοῦ κινδύνου.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_σκοπῶν_281">: <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85"> <title>Ant.</title> 85</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλὴν_ἀνιᾶσθαι_283">: for the absence of the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 608"> <title>O. C.</title> 608</ref> n.: Antiphanes fr. incert. 51 <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλείπεθ’ οὐδὲν ἕτερον ἢ τεθνηκέναι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρόν_283">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 959"> <title>El.</title> 959</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι μὲν στένειν</quote> | ... | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι δ’ ἀλγεῖν</quote>. Musgave cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hor. S. 2.5.68">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 2. 5. 68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">invenietque Nil sibi legatum praeter plorare suisque.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
invenietque Nil sibi legatum praeter plorare
							suisque.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐμάρειαν_283">, ease (704), hence, abundance. Cp. Aesch. fr. 237 <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔπω τις Ἀκταίων’ ἄθηρος ἡμέρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κενόν, πόνου πλουτοῦντ’ ἔπεμψεν οἴκαδε</quote>. The author of the ‘Letters of Phalaris’ had this passage in mind, <ref> <title>Ep.</title> 33</ref> (Schaefer) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδήλωσεν ὅτι πάντων ἐνδεεῖς ἐστὲ πλὴν λιμοῦ καὶ φόβου· τούτων δὲ</quote> [cp. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτου_δὲ_283"> here] <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς καὶ λίαν εὐμοιρεῖν</quote> [<emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὐπορεῖν</quote>]. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_χρόνου_285"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The text has been boldly altered by some editors (see cr. n.), in order to get rid of <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote>: but the iteration is itself a proof of soundness. Such iteration is constantly employed in expressing a succession of seasons or periods; ‘day by day,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτος εἰς ἔτος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 340"> <title>Ant.</title> 340</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ <*>μαρ ἡμέρα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 475"> <title>Ai.</title> 475</ref>), Mod. Gr. <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνο σὲ χρόνο</quote> (‘year after year’), <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">truditur dies die</quote> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 2. 18. 15">(Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 2. 18. 15)</bibl> </cit>, etc. The phrase </gloss>
lemma: ὁ μὲν χρόνος δὴ διὰ χρόνου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_χρόνου_285"> regularly means, ‘<emph>after an interval of time</emph>’<emph>:</emph> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 758">cp. 758</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 1.12">Lys. or. 1 § 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσμένη με ἑωρακυῖα ἥκοντα διὰ χρόνου</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 4. 28">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1. 4. 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤκω διὰ χρόνου</quote> </cit>. So here, </gloss>

data.quote: ἀσμένη με ἑωρακυῖα ἥκοντα διὰ χρόνου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_μοι_285">, time was ever moving on for me, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_χρόνου_285_2">, as (each) space of time was left behind. (The ‘each’ is implied in the imperfect <quote xml:lang="grc">προὔβαινε</quote>, which denotes not a single advance, but a series of advances.) Suppose that the interval denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote> is a month. ‘One month having elapsed’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote>—as each month came to an end), ‘time kept moving on’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> a new month began). Render, then, ‘Time went on for me, <emph>season by season.</emph>’ Cp. Tennyson, <title>Enoch Arden:</title> ‘Thus over Enoch's early-silvering head | The sunny and rainy seasons came and went | Year after year.’—Ellendt, rightly starting from the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote> as ‘after an interval,’ wrongly explains it here as simply <emph>tarde</emph>, ‘pausenweise’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘time went on with many a pause’: as if, to Philoctetes, time seemed, at moments, to stand still. The error here consists in excepting the intervals denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote> from the whole progress described by <quote xml:lang="grc">προὔβαινε</quote>.—Not: ‘time kept moving on <emph>through time</emph>’<emph>:</emph> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ χρόνος</quote> were the moving point, while <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote> denoted its course.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔβαινε_285">, cp. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 53">Her. 3. 53</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ χρόνου προβαίνοντος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 1.11">Lys. or. 1 § 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προϊόντος δὲ τοῦ χρόνου</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄδει_τι_286">. The sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_286"> here is nearly <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστόν τι</quote>, just as <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> sometimes =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστός τις</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1. 40">Thuc. 1. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ξυμμάχους αὐτόν τινα κολάζειν</quote>); a sense which the impf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔδει_286"> brings out, by implying successive needs at successive moments.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαιᾷ_286">, of size, as <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 447"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 447</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῆσος</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">βαιά</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διακονεῖσθαι_286">, midd.: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτῷ ἐξυπηρετεῖσθαι</quote>. That the midd. would suggest, to an Attic ear, ‘serving <emph>oneself</emph>,’ may be inferred from <title>Legg.</title> 763 A <quote xml:lang="grc">διακονοῦντές τε καὶ διακονούμενοι ἑαυτοῖς</quote>, ‘serving (the State), and serving themselves’ (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1017">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1017</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτῷ διακονεῖται</quote>). In later Greek, however, the midd. is sometimes no more than the act.; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> Lucian <ref n="Lucian Philops. 35"> <title>Philops.</title> 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιῶς ὑπηρέτει καὶ διηκονεῖτο ἡμῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑποπτέρους_288"> is perh. meant here to suggest ‘shy’ (and therefore hard to shoot); for the word often implies ‘taking wing’: cp. <title>Helen.</title> 1236 <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθίημι vεῖκος τὸ σόν, ἴτω δ’ ὑπόπτερον</quote>: id. fr. 420. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόπτερος δ’ ὁ πλοῦτος.</quote> So <ref n="Soph. Aj. 139"> <title>Ai.</title> 139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφόβημαι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πτηνῆς ὡς ὄμμα πελείας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_βάλοι_289">: for the optat. referring to an indefinite number of acts in past time, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 23.3">Lys. or. 23 § 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς τε ἐξευρίσκοιμι Δεκελέων, ἐπυνθανόμην</quote> </cit> : <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 3. 55">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5. 3. 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὓς μὲν ἴδοι,...ἠρώτα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νευροσπαδὴς_289">, ‘with drawn string,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘drawn back along with the string.’ The epithet pictures the moment of taking aim, and thus suggests, though it does not literally express, the idea, ‘sped from the string.’ Not, ‘drawing the string back’ (by the pressure of the notch). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1216"> <title>Ant.</title> 1216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἁρμὸν...λιθοσπαδῆ</quote>, an opening made by dragging stones away; where the adj. implies <quote xml:lang="grc">λίθων ἐσπασμένων</quote>, as here the adj. implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ νευρᾶς ἐσπασμένης</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτρακτος_289">. If the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀ</quote> be for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφ</quote> (as Curtius suggests, comparing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄ‐βολος</quote>, cloak), the word meant, ‘what turns (<quote xml:lang="grc">τρεπ</quote>) round’; hence (1) spindle; then (2) shaft, arrow: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 714"> <title>Tr.</title> 714</ref>: <ref n="Thuc. 4. 40">Thuc. 4. 40</ref>§ 2, where a Laconian uses it, and Thuc. explains it by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὀϊστόν</quote>. Aesch. adds the qualifying epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">τοξικῷ</quote> (fr. 139).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸς_289">, having no dog to fetch it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰλυόμην_291"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 702">cp. 702</ref>), ‘crawl’; cp. <ref n="Plat. Tim. 92A">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 92A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄποδα...καὶ ἰλυσπώμενα ἐπὶ γῆς</quote>. The word suggests that each step with the sound foot is followed by a slight halt, while the other foot is dragged after it. Thus the notion is different from that of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰλίποδες</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">βοῦς</quote>), where a ‘rolling’ gait is meant. Cp. on 163. A cornelian intaglio in the Berlin collection shows Philoctetes thus <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰλυόμενος</quote>, with the help of a stick in his left hand, while the righ<*> holds his bow and quiver; the left foot is the wounded one. (Milani, <title>Mito di Filottete</title> p. 78: see Introd.) It is clear from 215 (<quote xml:lang="grc">πταίων</quote>) and 894 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθώσει</quote>) that the poet imagines him as striving to walk erect, and not as creeping prone, with the knee of the sound leg against the ground.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_291"> with the iterative impf. in apodosis, after optat. in protasis, as oft.: cp. <ref n="Isoc. 6.52">Isocr. or. 6 § 52</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν παρελθόντα χρόνον, εἰ...εἷς μόνος Λακεδαιμονίων βοηθήσειεν, ὑπὸ πάντων ἂν ὡμολογεῖτο</quote> (‘it used to be allowed’) <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τοῦτον γενέσθαι τὴν σωτηρίαν αὐτοῖς</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 294">Cp. 294</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστηνον_291">, as 1377 <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστήνῳ ποδί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέλκων_291">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 303"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 303</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γήρᾳ τρομερὰν ἕλκω ποδὸς βάσιν. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἄν_291">: for the repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 223">cp. 223</ref> n.: that of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τοῦτο</quote> emphasises the limit of the painful effort. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγου_χυθέντος_293">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 853"> <title>Tr.</title> 853</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέχυται νόσος</quote>, ‘hath spread abroad’ (through his frame). Attius, <title>Prometheus</title> fr. 1 <emph>profusus gelus.</emph> Psalm cxlvii. 16: ‘He giveth snow like wool: he scattereth the hoar-frost like ashes. He casteth forth his ice like morsels.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_293">: 273 n.— Nauck would delete this verse, because it is unreasonable that the hero should delay providing himself with firewood until the frost has set in.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύλον_τι_293">. Lemnos is now almost devoid of wood, save for a few plane-trees in the water-courses, and a little undergrowth.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_293"> with iterative impf., cp. on 291 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πέτρον_296">. For the change of quantity, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 827">cp. 827</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνε</quote>): <ref n="Soph. OC 442"> <title>O. C.</title> 442</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τοῦ πατρὸς τω—| πατρὶ</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 883 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐχ ὕβρις τάδ);—ὕβρις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310"> <title>Ant.</title> 1310</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc"> δείλαι^ος...—δειλαίᾳ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 148"> <title>El.</title> 148</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ Ἴτυν, αἰὲν Ἴτυν ὀλοφύρεται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτρίβων_296">, rubbing hard (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>=‘thoroughly,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> till the spark comes). The <emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκθλίβων</quote> would mean, ‘pressing’ or ‘squeezing,’ and is unsuitable. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 2. 2. 15">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2. 2. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκ γε σοῦ πῦρ...ῥᾷον ἄν τις ἐκτρίψειεν ἢ γέλωτα ἐξαγάγοιτο</quote>. The use of two stones would suggest concussion rather than friction. The Eskimos kindle fire by striking a piece of iron pyrites with a piece of quartz (instead of flint); the Alaskans of North America, and the Aleutian islanders (in the North Pacific), use two pieces of quartz, smeared with native sulphur. (M. Elie Reclus, in <title>Encycl. Brit.</title>, art. ‘Fire.’) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτρίβων_296_2"> might, however, cover the case of a slanting or scraping blow. In Lucian <title>Ver. Hist.</title> 1. 32 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πυρεῖα συντρίψαντες</quote> refers to rubbing sticks together.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔφην_φῶς_296">, made the invisible light visible, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> drew the spark forth from its hiding place in the stone. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 647"> <title>Ai.</title> 647</ref>(Time) <quote xml:lang="grc">φύει τ’ ἄδηλα καὶ φανέντα κρύπτεται</quote>. Blaydes compares Synesius <title> Ep.</title> 138 <quote xml:lang="grc">σπινθῆρα κεκρυμμένον καὶ ἀγαπῶντα λανθάνειν</quote>. <title>G.</title> 1. 135 <title>Ut silicis venis abstrusum excuderet ignem.</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄφαντον_296"> could hardly be, ‘<emph>barely</emph> seen,’ as if the sense were that the feeble spark instantly vanished again. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκουμένη_οὖν_298">, ‘for <emph>indeed</emph>...’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 489"> <title>Ant.</title> 489</ref> n. Remark <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> in the thesis of the 3rd foot; so <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 23"> <title>O. C.</title> 23</ref>), and even <quote xml:lang="grc">περ</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 896).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_298"> has been suspected. But it serves to qualify the general sentiment by a reference to his special circumstances:—‘shelter and fire give all that a man needs—except, in <emph> my</emph> case, health.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρε_μάθῃς_300">, L's reading, presents an unexampled construction. Elsewhere the subjunctive after <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε</quote> occurs only in the first person, sing., as 1452, <title>Nub.</title> 787 <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρ’ ἴδω</quote>: or plur., as id. <title>Vesp.</title> 1516 <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε νυν...ξυγχωρήσωμεν</quote>. On the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρ’ εἰπέ</quote> occurs eight times in Soph. (433; <ref n="Soph. OT 390"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 390</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 536">Soph. O. T. 536</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1142">Soph. O. T. 1142</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 534">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 534</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 310">Soph. <title>El.</title> 310</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 376">Soph. El. 376</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 890"> Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 890</ref>). In <ref n="Hdt. 4. 127">Her. 4. 127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρετε, τούτους ἀνευρόντες συγχέειν πειρᾶσθε αὐτούς</quote>, the 2nd verb is imperat., not subj. If <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε...μάθῃς</quote> be retained, it can be defended only as an irregular equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε...φράσω</quote> or the like (cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 14">Her. 2. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε δὲ νῦν καὶ αὐτοῖσι Αἰγυπτίοισι ὡς ἔχει φράσω</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθε_300">. It is, however, improbable that, if </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθε_300_2"> had been the genuine reading—giving so plain a construction—it would have been corrupted to the unparalleled </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθῃς_300">. A more attractive conjecture is Seyffert's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἂν_μάθοις_300">. If </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἂν_300"> had once become </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_300"> (a most easy change), then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθοις_300"> might have been altered to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθῃς_300_2"> by a postclassical corrector. For the optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> in courteous proposal or request, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 674">cp. 674</ref> : <ref n="Soph. El. 637"> <title>El.</title> 637</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύοις ἂν ἤδη</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_νήσου_300">, its case, condition: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 8. 89">Thuc. 8. 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐδόκει μόνιμον τὸ τῆς ὀλιγαρχίας ἔσεσθαι</quote>: <title>Legg.</title> 712 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ τῶν ἐφόρων...τυραννικὸν...γέγονε</quote>: id. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 450"> <title>Gorg.</title> 450</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τῆς τέχνης</quote> : <ref n="Eur. Alc. 785">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 785</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τῆς τύχης</quote>. Hence </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_300"> is a needless conjecture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἐστίν_302">. The absence of a safe <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρμος</quote> is compatible with the existence of <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμένες</quote> (936 n.); and Philoctetes knows only the coast near his cave. If the <title>Iliad</title> calls Lemnos <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐϋκτιμένη</quote> (21. 40), it also calls it <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμιχθαλόεσσα</quote> (24. 753), which was probably understood in antiquity as ‘inhospitable’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">μίγνυμι</quote>); though a modern view co<*>nects it with <quote xml:lang="grc">μιχ‐, ὀμίχλη</quote>, (our <emph>mist</emph>,) as=‘smoky,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, volcanic. In the time of Sophocles, Lemnos possessed two towns,—Hephaestia, on the N. coast, of which the site has lately been identified by Conze (<title>Reise auf den Aeg. Inseln</title>); and Myrīna, now Kastro, on the W. coast. There was once an excellent harbour at Hephaestia; there still is one at Kastro, the present seat of trade. Geod anchorage is also afforded by a deep bay on the N. coast (now ‘Purnia’), and by another on the S. (now ‘Mudros’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_πλέων_302">: nor (is there a place), sailing to which, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεμπολήσει_κέρδος_302">, a man shall sell off his wares at a profit. There is no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπόριον</quote>. The acc. <quote xml:lang="grc"> κέρδος</quote> seems to be ‘cognate’ (=<quote xml:lang="grc">κερδαλέαν ἐξεμπόλησιν</quote>), rather than objective (as if <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐξεμπ</quote>. meant, ‘achieve by trading’): cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 1">Her. 1. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμπολημένων</quote> (Ion.) <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι σχεδὸν πάντων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1036">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1036</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξημπόλημαι</quote> n. (We cannot compare <ref n="Soph. Trach. 92">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γ’ <emph>εὖ</emph> πράσσειν...κέρδος ἐμπολᾷ</quote>, ‘brings in’ gain.) The subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμπολήσει</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>, easily supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυβάτης</quote> (301). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξενώσεται_302">, pass.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 48">cp. 48</ref> n. In Lycophron 92 this form is fut. midd. There is no class. example of <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενωθήσομαι</quote>. Attic, indeed, generally prefers the midd. form for the fut. pass. in ‘pure’ verbs (those of which the stem ends in a vowel).— For the fut. indic. in a relative clause of purpose, cp. <ref n="Dem. 1.2">Dem. or. 1 § 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρεσβείαν πέμπειν ἥτις ταῦτ’ ἐρεῖ καὶ παρέσται τοῖς πράγμασιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδ_304">=<quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 256">cp. 256</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμοῦ</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλοῖ_304">: this nom. pl. occurs also in <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 7. 7">Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 7. 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλοὶ πλοῖ</quote>: the dat. pl. in <ref n="Antiph. 5.83">Antiphon or. 5 § 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πλοῖς</quote>. In L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σώφροσι_304"> the omission of the <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ν</emph> ἐφελκυστικόν</quote> is doubtless a mere error; though Soph. sometimes lengthens <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">βρ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">βλ</quote> in compounds ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 336">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 336</ref> , <title>O.C.</title> 996 n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_304">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 279"> <title>O. C.</title> 279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸν εὐσεβῆ βροτῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάχ_ἔσχε_305">: ‘perhaps, indeed, some one has put in.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> has a concessive force; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1306">cp. 1306</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν</quote> n. When <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχα</quote> stands without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, it usu.= ‘quickly’: but cp. <title>Legg.</title> 711 A <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς δὲ τάχα οὐδὲ τεθέασθε τυραννουμένην πόλιν</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ἄν</quote> is impossible). Here the force of <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχα</quote> is, ‘I grant that visitors have come now and then; let us suppose such a moment.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχε_305">=<quote xml:lang="grc">προσέσχε</quote> , <emph>appulit:</emph> <ref n="Thuc. 6. 62">Thuc. 6. 62</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχον ἐς Ἱμέραν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 221">Cp. 221</ref>, 236.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_γὰρ_305"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘for such things (viz., such necessities as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκων</quote> implies) are likely to occur often (<quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ</quote> predicative adj., here practically equiv. to the adv.) in the long course of human life.’ Now and again in the course of his lifetime, a sailor might be driven to seek shelter even on such a coast as that of Lemnos. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μακρὸς ἀνθρώπων χρόνος</quote> is the long term of man's normal life; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 461"> <title>Ant.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ τοῦ χρόνου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν θανοῦμαι</quote>, ‘before my natural term.’ Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 32">Her. 1. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν γὰρ τῷ μακρῷ χρόνῳ πολλὰ μὲν ἔστι ἰδεῖν τὰ μή τις ἐθέλει, πολλὰ δὲ καὶ παθεῖν. ἐς γὰρ ἑβδομήκοντα ἔτεα οὖρον τῆς ζόης ἀνθρώπῳ προτίθημι</quote> . <ref n="Hdt. 5. 9">Id. 5. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο δ’ ἂν πᾶν ἐν τῷ μακρῷ χρόνῳ</quote>. A reminiscence of these phrases may have been in the poet's mind. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτοι_307"> referring to the indefinite <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> (305): cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 709"> <title>Ant.</title> 709</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖτοι</quote> referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> in 707 (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_οἰκτίραντες_307">. As a general rule, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεεῖν</quote>=‘to show pity or mercy in act’: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτίρειν</quote>, ‘to feel pity.’ Thus <ref n="Dem. 28.20">Dem. or. 28 § 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώσατε, ἐλεήσατε</quote>. [Dem. ] or. 57 § 45 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεοῖντ’ ἂν...δικαιότερον ἢ προσαπολλύοιντο</quote>. <ref n="Lys. 31.19">Lys. or. 31 § 19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς ἕτεροι διδόναι παρ’ ἑαυτῶν τι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐλεεῖν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">προῃροῦντο, διὰ τὴν ἀπορίαν οἰκτίραντες αὐτούς</quote>. See Heinrich Schmidt, <title>Synonymik der griechischen Sprache</title>, vol. III. pp. 577 ff.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_τι_307">: <ref target="cl274">see on 274</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στολήν_307">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 223">cp. 223</ref> f. n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖνο_θέλει_307"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιῆσαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 100">cp. 100</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῶσαί_οἴκους_311">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 737">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 737</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἤπειρον σεσῶσθαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 189">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 189</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κακοῖσι_311">. Some critics suspect <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖσι</quote> as not distinctive enough (see cr. n.); but it can surely denote those ‘hardships’ of his life which were superadded to the <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμός</quote> and the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_311"> at the end of the v., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 267"> <title>O. T.</title> 267</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1234."> O. T. 1234.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόσκων_311">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1167">cp. 1167</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδηφάγον_311">: so 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">διαβόρῳ</quote>: 745 <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύκομαι</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> is personified, as in 759 by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεπλήσθη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βία_314">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 321">cp. 321</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 38"> <title>Tr.</title> 38</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰφίτου βίαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_314">, Porson's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_314">, is certain. The sufferer prays that their sufferings may be like his own: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 275">cp. 275</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 927."> <title>Ant.</title> 927.</ref>With <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς</quote>, both <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖς</quote> (as= ‘themselves’) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίποιν’</quote> become comparatively tame.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοῦ_314">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 592"> <title>El.</title> 592</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τῆς θυγατρὸς ἀντίποινα λαμβάνεις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσα_317"> could imply either (1) ‘as fully as they pitied you in their hearts,’— the sense in which the speaker means Ph. to take it: or (2) ‘only as much as they showed you pity in their deeds’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 516">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 516</ref> n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἴσου.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐποικτίρειν_317"> is much better than Nauck's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐποικτιρεῖν_317">, which, as expressing a <emph> presentiment</emph>, would call too much attention to the ambiguity of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λόγοις_319">. If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> is sound, the phrase must mean ‘a witness present <emph>at</emph> (the utterance of) these words.’ Cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 115">Plat. <title> Phaedo</title> 115</ref>E <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲ λέγῃ ἐν τῇ ταφῇ</quote>, ‘<emph>at</emph> the funeral,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, while it is taking place. The expression is unusual; but I hesitate to receive Gernhard's conject. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὢν_319">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συντυχὼν_319">, ‘having found them bad men in my intercourse with them (<quote xml:lang="grc">σύν‐</quote>).’ The force of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">τυχών</quote> here prevails over that of the prep., and so a gen. replaces the regular dat. Since in <ref n="Soph. OC 1483"> <title>O. C.</title> 1483</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ τύχοιμι</quote> must be read for <quote xml:lang="grc">συντύχοιμι</quote>, there is no other extant example of <quote xml:lang="grc">συντυγχάνω</quote> with gen. But there are analogies for the exception: in 1333 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντυχὼν Ἀσκληπιδῶν</quote> is the only instance of a gen. (instead of dat.) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντυγχάνω</quote>, except <ref n="Hdt. 4. 140">Her. 4. 140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λελυμένης τῆς γεφύρης ἐντυχόντες</quote>. Again, <ref n="Hdt. 552">Her. 552</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προστυχόντι τῶν ἴσων</quote> and <ref n="Soph. El. 1463">Soph. <title>El.</title> 1463</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ κολαστοῦ προστυχών</quote> are isolated examples of a gen., instead of dat., with that compound. In 719 <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδὸς ὑπαντήσας</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδὶ</quote>) is also unique. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 17">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίτης ἐμπελασθῆναι</quote> (where the dat. would be normal). It may be added that here, where <quote xml:lang="grc">συντυχών</quote> expresses, not merely a meeting with the men, but an experience of their character, the gen. has a special excuse.—We cannot make <quote xml:lang="grc">συντυχών</quote> mean=‘having found them bad men, <emph>as you have done</emph>’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν σοι</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γάρ_322">, 248 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_πανωλέθροις_322">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 86">Soph. <title>El.</title> 86</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χἡ πανώλεθρος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθών_322">, and not merely <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μυκῆναι_322">, as the city of Agamemnon: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σπάρτη_322">, as that of Menelaus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνοῖεν_322">, after the optative <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 297">Aesch. <title> Eum.</title> 297</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθοι</quote> (‘may she come !’)... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως γένοιτο τῶνδ’ ἐμοὶ λυτήριος</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OT 506">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 506</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_γ_327">, <emph>euge:</emph> <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 213">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 213</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γ’, εὖ γε νὴ Δἴ, εὖ γε· λέγε, λέγ’, ὦγαθέ</quote>. There is no other example in Tragedy of this colloquial <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γε</quote> without a verb.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_γὰρ_327">: lit., ‘Now (<quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>, 249 n.), on account of what have you come thus charging them with (having provoked) the great anger (which you show)?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_327">, causal gen., not with <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν...χόλον</quote> alone, but with the whole sentence: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 751">cp. 751</ref>, 1308: <ref n="Soph. OT 698"> <title>O. T.</title> 698</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδαξον κἄμ’, ἄναξ, ὅτου ποτὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆνιν τοσήνδε πράγματος στήσας ἔχεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_327">, ‘thus’ (not ‘hither,’ as in <ref n="Soph. OT 7"> <title>O. T.</title> 7</ref>).—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>χόλον</emph> ἐγκαλεῖν κατά τινος</quote>=to make one's anger a subject of accusation against a person, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to charge him with having provoked it. The causal </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_327_2"> helps to explain the pregnant sense. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 702"> <title>O. T.</title> 702</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγ’, εἰ σαφῶς τὸ νεῖκος ἐγκαλῶν ἐρεῖς</quote>, ‘speak, if you can make a clear statement in imputing (the blame of) the feud.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξερῶ_ἐρῶ_329">. In such phrases <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλις</quote> usu. stands in the first clause, with <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1105"> <title>Ant.</title> 1105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλις μέν, καρδίας δ’ ἐξίσταμαι</quote>, n. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξερῶ—ἐρῶ</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 249">cp. 249</ref> f. The feeling is like that of Odysseus when asked by Alcinous to tell his story: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.12"> <title>Od.</title> 9. 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>σοὶ δ’ ἐμὰ κήδεα θυμὸς ἐπετράπετο στονόεντα</l> <l>εἴρεσθ’, ὄφρ’ ἔτι μᾶλλον ὀδυρόμενος στεναχίζω.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολών_329">: to Troy, 353. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχε_331">. The pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote> would mean ‘constrains’; cp. <title>I. T.</title> 1065 <quote xml:lang="grc">τρεῖς μία τύχη τοὺς φιλτάτους</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ γῆς πατρῴας νόστος, ἢ θανεῖν, ἔχει</quote>. The aor. (‘ingressive’) =‘came upon him with constraint’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1117">cp. 1117</ref>: fr. 529 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ δουλείας</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζυγὸν ἔσχ’ ἀνάγκας</quote> : <ref n="Eur. Hec. 4">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ Φρυγῶν πόλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κίνδυνος ἔσχε δορὶ πεσεῖν Ἑλληνικῷ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσῃς_πέρα_332">: for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 67">cp. 67</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοξευτὸς_δαμείς_335">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τόξοις δαμείς</quote>. The adj. here defines the instrument, as oft. elsewhere the place ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1411"> <title>O. T.</title> 1411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάσσιον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκρίψατ’</quote>), or the manner ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1637"> <title>O. C.</title> 1637</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῄνεσεν τάδ’ ὅρκιος</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_λέγουσιν_335"> implies that there was something mysterious in the death; Paris might seem to have inflicted it, but, in men's belief, the true slayer was Apollo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_335">, however, does not here denote ulterior, as dist. from immediate, agency (‘<emph>by doom of</emph> Phoebus’; <ref n="Soph. OT 1453"> <title>O. T.</title> 1453</ref>); it is here no more than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπό_335">.—According to one account, Paris shot Achilles, but with the aid of Apollo (as Athena had helped Achilles against Hector): so <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.416"> <title>Il.</title> 19. 416</ref>(the immortal steed Xanthus to Achilles) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ σοὶ αὐτῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρσιμόν ἐστι θεῷ τε καὶ ἀνέρι ἶφι δαμῆναι</quote>: and Hector's prophecy ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.359"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 359</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε κέν σε Πάρις καὶ Φοῖβος Ἀπόλλων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθλὸν ἐόντ’ ὀλέσωσιν ἐπὶ Σκαιῇσι πύλῃσιν</quote>. <ref n="Verg. A. 6. 57">Verg. <title> Aen.</title> 6. 57</ref> <title>Phoebe</title>, ...<title>Dardana qui Paridis direxti tela manusque</title> | <title>Corpus in Aeacidae.</title> Another account speaks of Apollo without naming Paris: so <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.278"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 278</ref>Achilles says that Thetis had predicted to him that he should die <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπόλλωνος βελέεσσιν</quote>. Cp. Aesch. fr. 350. 8 (Thetis speaks of Apollo) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός ἐστιν ὁ κτανὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν παῖδα τὸν ἐμόν</quote>. So, too, <ref n="Hor. Carm. 4. 6. 1">Hor. <title> Carm.</title> 4. 6. 1</ref> ff. Quintus Smyrn. 3. 61 (Apollo, hidden in a mist) <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγερὸν προέηκε βέλεμνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καί ἑ θοῶς οὔτησε κατὰ σφυρόν</quote>. As to the vulnerable heel of Achilles, cp. <ref n="Stat.. Ach. 1.269">Statius <title> Ach.</title> 1. 269</ref>, where Thetis says: <emph>progenitum Stygis amne severo</emph> | <title>Armavi</title> (<emph>totumque utinam!</emph>). Hyginus ( <ref n="Hyg. Fab. 107"> <title>Fab.</title> 107</ref>) fused the two versions by making Apollo take the guise of Paris.—The ‘cyclic’ epic which related the death of Achilles was the <title>Aethiopis</title>, ascribed to Arctînus of Miletus, <emph>c.</emph> 776 B.C. (<title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 153). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μὲν_336">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλά</quote>=‘well’ (said as if with a sigh); <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> has a reflective tone, ‘certainly,’—‘it must be granted.’ Cp. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 460A">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 460A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐγὼ μὲν οῖμαι</quote>, <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> (The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_336"> in 337 does not answer to this <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>.) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524">Cp. 524</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλὰ...μέντοι</quote> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτανών_θανών_336">: A frequent <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1263"> <title>Ant.</title> 1263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ κτανόντας τε καὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντας βλέποντες ἐμφυλίους</quote> . <title>I. T.</title> 553 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ κτανοῦσα χὡ θανών</quote> . <ref n="Eur. Alc. 488"> <title>Alc.</title> 488</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτανὼν ἄρ’ ἥξεις ἢ θανὼν αὐτοῦ μενεῖς</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλέγχω_στένω_338">: delib. subjunct.: for the pres., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶμαι_μὲν_339">, as <title>O.T.</title> 1051: so <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ μέν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 995"> <title>O. C.</title> 995</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ς_339">, ‘<emph>even</emph>...’. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1216"> <title>Tr.</title> 1216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀρκέσει καὶ ταῦτα</quote>. For the elision in <quote xml:lang="grc">σ’</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 64"> <title>O. T.</title> 64</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιγαροῦν_341"> (‘so, then’) occurs also in <ref n="Soph. OT 1519"> <title>O. T.</title> 1519</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 490"> <title>Ai.</title> 490</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1257."> <title>El.</title> 1257.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_πάλιν_341"> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1418"> <title>O. C.</title> 1418</ref> n.) refers to 329 —331. He had there intimated that, on reaching Troy, he had suffered some grievous wrong. Yet it has been proposed to reject v. 342 on the ground that Ph. could not ask for the repetition of a story which he had not yet heard.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτῳ_341">, ‘wherein,’ dat. of respect: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 65">Thuc. 2. 65</ref>§ 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">σφαλέντες...ἄλλῃ τε παρασκευῇ καὶ τοῦ ναυτικοῦ τῷ πλείονι μορίῳ</quote>: id. 4. 73 § 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ βελτίστῳ τοῦ ὁπλιτικοῦ βλαφθῆναι</quote>. The dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ</quote> has not been influenced by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> in the compound. For the acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνυβρίζω</quote>, cp. Kaibel <title>Epigr. Gr.</title> 195 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μου ἐνυβρίξῃς ἁγνὸν τάφον</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> has the same force as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγελᾶν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 68">Soph. <title> El.</title> 68</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς ἐμοῖς γὰρ οὐκ ἐνύβρισας κακοῖς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποικιλοστόλῳ_343">, ‘with gaily decked prow’: not simply ‘gaily drest’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκόστολος</quote>, etc.). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 408">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 408</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐθὺς δὲ ναῦς ἐν νηῒ χαλκήρη στόλον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαισεν</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλος</quote> poetically=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμβολον</quote>, the beak or ram, which was attached to the ship below the prow. The term <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀκροστόλιον</quote> was sometimes applied to the ‘figure-head’ (such as the head and neck of a swan). Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποικιλοστόλῳ</quote> seems to denote some special adornment, intended to mark the solemnity of the mission. These envoys came, not only to announce the death of Achilles, but to salute his heir. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title> P.</title> 2. 62</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐανθέα δ’ ἀναβάσομαι στόλον</quote>: where (whether <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλον</quote> be taken as ‘prow,’ or, more tamely, as ‘voyage’) the epithet refers to the adorning of the ship with garlands on a festal occasion. So, too, when the Salaminia was about to leave Athens on the annual <quote xml:lang="grc">θεωρία</quote> to Delos, the priest of Apollo crowned the stern with garlands ( <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 58c"> <title>Phaed.</title> 58</ref>C).—Others regard <quote xml:lang="grc">ποικιλοστόλῳ</quote> as merely a constant epithet, equiv. to the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">μιλτοπάρῃος</quote> (now explained by some as referring ‘to a literal painting of a face upon the bows’; Leaf, <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.637"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 637</ref>). The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποικιλοστόμῳ_343"> is merely a prosaic corruption. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῖός_Ὀδυσσεὺς_344">: this is the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύτλας δῖος Ὀδυσσεύς</quote>. The epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">δῖος</quote> (‘bright’) may be rendered ‘princely,’ or ‘noble,’ when applied to a chief (the idea of personal comeliness being included therein): or by the more general word, ‘goodly,’ in other cases. Cp. Note 2 to Butcher and Lang's <title>Odyssey:</title> ‘Froissart and Brantome apply respectful terms of moral excellence to knights and ladies whom they describe as anything but moral.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_τροφεὺς_344">: Phoenix, who, having been driven from the house of his father Amyntor, was received by Peleus, and entrusted with the care of the infant Achilles: to whom he says in <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.485"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 485</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σε τοσοῦτον ἔθηκα</quote> (reared thee up to manhood), <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς ἐπιείκελ’ Ἀχιλλεῦ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ θυμοῦ φιλέων</quote>. Another legend represents Achilles as brought up by Cheiron ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.832"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 832</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτ_οὖν_345">, ‘or, after all (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρα</quote>), it may be (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>)’: for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> with the second <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 90"> <title>O. T.</title> 90</ref>, and n. <emph>ib.</emph> 1049.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀληθὲς_345"> does not occur as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθῶς</quote>, though <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γε</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθές</quote> is so used, like <emph>re vera.</emph> Here it has, indeed, an adverbial force, but is properly the acc. governed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγοντες_345">. So in <ref n="Eur. Ion 275">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 275</ref> we may point thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δαὶ τόδ); ἆρ’ ἀληθές; ἢ μάτην λόγος</quote>; For <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτην</quote>, <emph> falso</emph>, cp. also <ref n="Soph. El. 63">Soph. <title>El.</title> 63</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1298.">Soph. El. 1298.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γίγνοιτ_345">: they said, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θέμις γίγνεται</quote>, it <emph>becomes</emph> unlawful (by the fact that Achilles is now dead): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 116">cp. 116</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλον_μ_347">. If there had been any previous indication of Neoptolemus, the reading <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄλλον <emph>ἤ μ’</emph> </quote> would have been tenable (<ref target="cl47">see on 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτό μ’</quote>): as there is none, we surely require </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢμ_347">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 83"> <title>Ant.</title> 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )μοῦ προτάρβει</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πολὺν_348"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘they did not cause me to make any long delay, or to refrain from sailing at once’: another way of saying, ‘they filled me with burning eagerness to sail at once.’ He speaks with a certain bitterness, meaning, ‘they well knew how to act their part, when they put the matter in that light.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέχω τινά</quote> as=‘to <emph>cause one to pause</emph>,’ see <ref n="Thuc. 4. 5">Thuc. 4. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τι καὶ αὐτοὺς ὁ στρατὸς ἔτι ἐν ταῖς Ἀθήναις ὢν ἐπέσχε</quote>, ‘partly, too, the fact that their army was in Attica <emph>caused them to delay</emph>’ (instead of marching out at once). <ref n="Thuc. 1. 129">Id. 1. 129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σε μήτε νὺξ μήτε ἡμέρα ἐπισχέτω</quote> (pres. imper.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε ἀνεῖναι πράσσειν τι</quote>. This sense of the trans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέχω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσχω</quote> is not precisely the same as that in <ref n="Soph. El. 517">Soph. <title> El.</title> 517</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς σ’ ἐπεῖχ’ ἀεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτοι θυραίαν οὖσαν αἰσχύνειν φίλους</quote>, ‘<emph> restrained</emph> thee’ (by compulsory detention): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πολὺν χρόνον μ’ ἐπέσχον</quote> is not, ‘they did not <emph>succeed in restraining me</emph> long’ (as if they had been trying to do so); but rather, ‘they gave me no cause for delaying long’;— not, ‘non diu me cohibuerunt,’ but ‘effecerunt ne diu morarer.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_348">, as subject to <quote xml:lang="grc"> ναυστολεῖν</quote>, may seem slightly inelegant; but it is not grammatically objectionable.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_εἰδόμην_351">, ‘for I had (never) seen him.’ The comment <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντα</quote>, written after <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδόμην</quote> in the margin of L, represents the simplest and best interpretation. Neoptolemos was born in Scyros, and remained there, under the care of his maternal grandfather, Lycomedes (243), until he went to Troy (see n. on 239 f.). Soon after the birth of Neoptolemus, his father Achilles had returned to Phthia; whence, some eight or ten years later, he went to Troy, without revisiting Scyros. Hence Neoptolemus can say that he had never seen his father. In this conception Sophocles is following the <title>Iliad.</title> From <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.765"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 765</ref>—782 it appears that Phthia, not Scyros, was the place from which Achilles went to Troy. And in <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.331"> <title>Il.</title> 19. 331</ref> f. Achilles speaks of his son as having never seen Phthia; for, apostrophising the dead Patroclus, he says, ‘my soul had hoped that thou should'st return to Phthia,’—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄν μοι τὸν παῖδα θοῇ ἐνὶ νηὶ μελαίνῃ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Σκυρόθεν ἐξαγάγοις, καί οἱ δείξειας ἕκαστα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κτῆσιν ἐμὴν δμῶάς τε καὶ ὑψερεφὲς μέγα δῶμα</quote>.—Apollodorus (3. 13. 8) follows a different version, according to which Achilles had remained in Scyros till he was brought thence to Troy by Odysseus.—For the midd. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδόμην_351"> in dial., cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 977"> <title>El.</title> 977</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδεσθε</quote>, <emph> ib.</emph> 892 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατειδόμην</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 151"> <title>Tr.</title> 151</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσίδοιτο</quote>: in lyrics, below, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1113"> Tr. 1113</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 351"> <title>Ai.</title> 351</ref>; and in anapaests, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1004."> <title>Tr.</title> 1004.</ref>Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρωμένη</quote> (midd.) in dial., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 306."> <title>Tr.</title> 306.</ref>—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειτα_μέντοι_352">, answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα μέν</quote> (350): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 647"> <title>O. T.</title> 647</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα μέν...ἔπειτα</quote> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>): <emph> ib.</emph> 777 <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμάσαι μὲν ἀξία</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδῆς γε μέντοι κ.τ.λ.</quote> In <ref n="Eur. Med. 1145">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1145</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν μὲν... ἔπειτα μέντοι</quote> is not strictly similar, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα</quote> is there temporal.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_προσῆν_352">, ‘there was a further charm’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">καλός</quote> predicate) ‘in the reason suggested,—if indeed I was to take,’ etc.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁλόγος_352"> is the reason for going, suggested by the envoys, (as distinguished from the natural <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote> in the son's mind,) and is explained by <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἰ...αἱρήσοιμ’</quote>. Not, ‘the <emph>fame</emph>, too, was attractive.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσῆν_352"> cp. <title>H.</title> 3. 1. 28 <quote xml:lang="grc">μισθὸς μὲν ἡμῖν...εἴργασται..., ἢν δέ τι προσεργασώμεθα, καὶ ταῦτα προσέσται</quote>. For a different use, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 129">cp. 129</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_αἱρήσοιμ_352">, <emph>oratio obliqua:</emph> he said to himself, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ αἱρήσω</quote>. For the fut. opt., cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 3. 1. 3">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3. 1. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τινα φεύγοντα λήψοιτο, προηγόρευεν ὅτι ὡς πολεμίῳ χρήσοιτο</quote> (he said, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ λήψομαι ...χρήσομαι</quote>). For <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> with optat., where <emph>one's own former thought</emph> is indicated in dependence on a past tense, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 3.3">Lys. or. 3 § 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχυνόμενος, εἰ μέλλοιεν πολλοί μοι συνείσεσθαι, ἠνεσχόμην</quote> </cit> (his thought had been, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνομαι, εἰ μέλλουσι</quote> ).</gloss>

data.quote:
αἰσχυνόμενος, εἰ μέλλοιεν πολλοί μοι
							συνείσεσθαι, ἠνεσχόμην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὶ_πέργαμ_352"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 611">cp. 611</ref>), the citadel which crowns the city of Troy, the <quote xml:lang="grc">Πέργαμος ἄκρη</quote> of Homer ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.460"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 460</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.512"> Il. 6. 512</ref>),—who uses only the sing. Hence Ilios is called <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰπεινή, ὀφρυόεσσα</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.411"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 411</ref>): cp. <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 148. For the prep. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_352">, cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 32</ref>(Apollo and Poseidon) <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰλίῳ μέλλοντες ἐπὶ στέφανον τεῦξαι</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργων</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰών_352">, oft. added to a verb denoting enterprise: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 768"> <title>Ant.</title> 768</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέοντί_μοι_354">: dat. of relation, as oft. with ref. to time: cp. <title>H.</title> 2. 1. 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ ἦν ἡμέρα πέμπτη ἐπιπλέουσι τοῖς</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναίοις</quote>. The distance from Scyros to Sigeum is about 125 miles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀγὼ_354">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> in temporal parataxis (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 718"> <title>O. T.</title> 718</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρὸν_Σίγειον_354">. Sigeum, the N.W. promontory of the Troad (now Yeni Shehr), is fitly named, as being the point for which he, coming from Scyros in the S.W., would make; and also because the tumulus, traditionally known as the ‘tomb of Achilles,’ is near Sigeum. It is ‘bitter’ or ‘cruel’ to him, not only on account of his father's death, but through the memory of his wrongs. The epithet is here a fine dramatic touch: while the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ )π’ ἄκρον</quote>, which many recent edd. adopt, is tamely prosaic. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 17.448"> <title>Od.</title> 17. 448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τάχα πικρὴν Αἴγυπτον καὶ Κύπρον ἵκηαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐρίῳ_πλάτῃ_354">, instrum. dat.; sped by oars, while a W. S. wind also filled his sails. Cp. ‘velis remisque,’ ‘ventis remis,’ etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηγόμην_354">, was coming into harbour at, with acc. instead of the usual acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 244">cp. 244</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσέσχες...γῆν</quote> (n.). Poetry is bold in its use of the simple acc. after verbs of motion; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1175">cp. 1175</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 643"> <title>O. C.</title> 643</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους στείχειν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμνύντες_357"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατός_357">: so <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.755"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 755</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ ὥστε ψαρῶν νέφος ἔρχεται ἠὲ κολοιῶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖλον κεκλήγοντες</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1021"> <title>Ant.</title> 1021</ref> f. n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντ_πάλιν_357">: legend naturally revived the image of the father in his son; Nauck cites trag. fr. adesp. 363 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ παῖς Ἀχιλλέως, ἀλλ’ ἐκεῖνος αὐτὸς εἶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκειτ_359">, ‘lay low in death,’ a poet. equiv. for ‘had died’ (not=<quote xml:lang="grc">προέκειτο</quote>, ‘lay on the bier,’ ready for the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφορά</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1134"> <title>El.</title> 1134</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως θανὼν ἔκεισο τῇ τόθ’ ἡμέρᾳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβου πατρῴου κοινὸν εἰληχὼς μέρος</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1174"> <title>Ant.</title> 1174</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τίς φονεύει; τίς δ’ ὁ κείμενος</quote>; Simonides fr. 60 <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖσαι ζῶν ἔτι μᾶλλον τῶν ὑπὸ γᾶς ἐκείνων</quote>. It is natural to suppose that the son's wish to arrive before the burial (351) was fulfilled; for the tidings of the death would have been sent at once, and he would have reached Troy not later, perhaps, than five days after it (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 354">cp. 354</ref>). In Hector's case the funeral took place only on the tenth day after his remains had been brought home ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.785"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 785</ref>). The conciseness of the narrative here, which does not refer to the obsequies (unless in <quote xml:lang="grc">)δάκ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ρυσα</quote>), is Sophoclean: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 415"> <title>Ant.</title> 415</ref> n. The welcome by ‘all the host’ (356) cannot be considered as a direct allusion to the funeral rites; cp. the reception of Teucer by the army ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 721"> <title>Ai.</title> 721</ref> ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δάκρυσα_359">: for the prodelision of the augment, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1062"> <title>O. C.</title> 1062</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖ )πόρευσαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457"> <title>Ant.</title> 457</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_χρόνῳ_359">, <emph>after</emph> it: <ref n="Soph. OC 1648"> <title>O. C.</title> 1648</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ βραχεῖ στραφέντες</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἦν_361"> goes closely with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλους_361">,—‘friends, as it was reasonable to suppose them.’ The only peculiarity is that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰκὸς ἦν</quote> here refers to a just hope felt at a past moment, and not to the fitness of a past fact (as if the sense was, ‘friends, as they naturally <emph>were</emph>,’—or, ‘having gone, as I naturally <emph> did</emph>’). Cp. <ref n="Plat. Menex. 247B">Plat. <title>Menex.</title> 247B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλοι παρὰ φίλους ἡμᾶς ἀφίξεσθε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_ἦν_361">: Homer describes the <quote xml:lang="grc">κλισία</quote> of Achilles as handsomely furnished (cp., <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.597"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 597</ref>), and it now contained the treasures which Priam had brought as the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑκτορέης κεφαλῆς ἀπερείσἰ ἄποινα</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 228—236: 579). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλημονέστατον_λόγον_363">, here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναιδέστατον</quote>, most audacious, shameless (in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 562">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 562</ref> the same phrase=‘most <emph>courageous</emph> speech’): <ref n="Soph. El. 439"> Soph. <title>El.</title> 439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ τλημονεστάτη γυνὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πασῶν ἔβλαστε</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 383">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τλάμονι καὶ πανούργῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> χειρί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστί_σοι_364">, ‘it is open to thee,’ ‘thou hast free leave’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 213"> <title>Ant.</title> 213</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λαέρτου_364">: cp. n. on 86 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δακρύσας_367">, the tears of pain and anger started into his eyes. (For the aor. part., cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 116D"> <title>Phaed.</title> 116</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἅμα δακρύσας, μεταστρεφόμενος ἀπῄει</quote>.) Many recent editors change this to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούσας</quote>, or a compound of it (see cr. n.). But the traditional reading is incomparably more forcible; it is also thoroughly Homeric in spirit; <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.385"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 385</ref>(Diomedes, when Apollo strikes the whip from his hand in the chariot-race) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖο δ’ ἀπ’ ὀφθαλμῶν χύτο δάκρυα χωομένοιο</quote>. Cp. Iuv. 1. 168 <title>Inde irae et lacrimae.</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξανίσταμαι_367">: he had been seated, as in converse with friends.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργῇ_367">: modal dat., <ref n="Soph. OT 405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</ref> n., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρείᾳ_367">, vehement: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆνιν βαρεῖαν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1328"> <title>O. C.</title> 1328</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 656"> <title>Ai.</title> 656</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κᾳταλγήσας_367">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 767"> <title>Ant.</title> 767</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦς δ’ ἐστὶ τηλικοῦτος ἀλγήσας βαρύς</quote>. This compound (in which <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote> is intensive) occurs elsewhere only in later Greek. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_σχέτλἰ_369"> is said to Agamemnon: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_τολμήσατ_369"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 360">cp. 360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">)δάκρυσα</quote>) refers to him and Menelaus: so <ref n="Soph. OC 1104"> <title>O. C.</title> 1104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέλθετ’, ὦ παῖ</quote> (said to Antigone, entering with Ismene).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_ἐμοῦ_369">, before ye had <emph>heard from me</emph> (that ye might do so). The phrase is so far unusual that, when <quote xml:lang="grc">μανθάνω</quote> takes a gen. (of the person) <emph> only</emph>, it usually=‘to understand,’ as <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 463D"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 463D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὖν ἂν μάθοις ἀποκριναμένου</quote>; <ref n="Plat. Phileb. 51">Id. <title> Phileb.</title> 51</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μου μανθάνεις</quote>. Similar is <ref n="Soph. OT 545">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 545</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μανθάνειν...σοῦ</quote>, to comprehend thy teachings. Cp., however, <ref n="Soph. OT 541">Soph. O. T. 541</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν μαθόντες</quote>,=‘having made inquiries of them.’ So here <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθεῖν</quote> is little more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκοῦσαι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">πυθέσθαι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_Ὀδυσσεύς_371">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ</quote> is a substantival pronoun, and the proper name is added as by an after-thought: a Homeric use, as <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.402"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰρ ὁ βοῦν ἱέρευσεν, ἄναξ ἀνδρῶν Ἀγαμέμνων</quote> (cp. Monro <title>Hom. Gram.</title> § 258). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 780"> <title>Ai.</title> 780</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ εὐθὺς... Τεῦκρος</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 70b"> <title>Phaed.</title> 70B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ δ’ ὅς, ὁ Σωκράτης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὢν_κυρεῖ_371">. Hermann objected to the historic pres. as unsuitable to a parenthetic remark; but without cause. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 253"> <title>Ant.</title> 253</ref> f., with n.: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 963"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 963</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">σχές· τυγχάνω γὰρ ἐν μέσοις Θρῃκης ὅροις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπών, ὅτ’ ἦλθες δεῦῤ· ἐπεὶ δ’ ἀ φικόμην</quote>, <emph>...</emph> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς ταὐτὸν ἥδε συμπίτνει</quote>. Brunck's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_κυρῶν_371"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 544">cp. 544</ref>) is smoother, indeed, but could hardly have generated the MS. reading.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_371"> does not imply that the arms are present (one of Nauck's grounds for preferring </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεδράκας_371">), but only that they are the subject of conversation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρών_373">, not merely, ‘being here at Troy’ (while Neoptolemus was absent, 379), but, ‘being present at the critical moment’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1405">cp. 1405</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 283">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 283</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τασδὶ δὲ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ οὐκ ἄρα σχήσω παρὼν τολμήματος τοσούτου</quote>; <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1242"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1242</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἄνδρ’ ἄριστον βούλεται σῶσαι παρών</quote>; (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <emph>to go and</emph> save).— Acc. to Arctînus in the <title> Aethiopis</title>, it was Ajax who carried the body of Achilles out of the fray, while Odysseus kept the Trojans off (Proclus p. 479). In <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.309"> <title>Od.</title> 5. 309</ref> f. Odysseus speaks of the day, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε μοι πλεῖστοι χαλκήρεα δοῦρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρῶες ἐπέρριψαν περὶ Πηλείωνι θανόντι</quote>. In <ref n="Ov. Met. 13. 284">Ov. <title>Met.</title> 13. 284</ref> he says: <quote xml:lang="lat">his...humeris ego corpus Achillis | Et simul arma tuli.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤρασσον_374">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 725"> <title>Ai.</title> 725</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀνείδεσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρασσον ἔνθεν κἄνθεν</quote>: for the lit. sense, <ref n="Soph. OT 1276."> <title>O. T.</title> 1276.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖς_πᾶσιν_374">: the art. properly means, ‘with all the taunts that exist’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 716"> <title>Tr.</title> 716</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθείρει τὰ πάντα κνώδαλ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_ποιούμενος_374">, making (on my part) nothing deficient, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> leaving nothing unsaid that occurred to me. For this use of the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιοῦμαι</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1144"> <title>O. C.</title> 1144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ λόγοισι τὸν βίον</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδάζομεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπρὸν ποεῖσθαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἀφαιρήσοιτο_374">: he said, (<quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν ἐστιν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἀφαιρήσεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 353">cp. 353</ref> n. For the double acc., cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 613">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφείλου πατέρας ...τέκνα</quote>. Since the idea of the taker's interest is usually implied, the middle voice of this verb is more freq. than the active. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδ_ἥκων_377">, brought to that point.—provoked so strongly: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 687"> <title>O. T.</title> 687</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς ἵν’ ἥκεις</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 1158 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰς τόδ’ ἥξεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δύσοργος_377">: as his mother speaks of his <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγανοφροσύνη</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.203"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 203</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἁξήκουσεν_377"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηχθείς_377">, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠμείψατο_377">. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> with acc., as=‘in view of,’ can always represent the cause of a feeling; cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1211"> <title>Tr.</title> 1211</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ φοβεῖ πρὸς τοῦτο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_ἐξήκουσεν_377">, the taunts which had been addressed to him (382): here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ</quote> merely strengthens the notion of ‘being reviled,’ as in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐξονειδίζω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 676">cp. 676</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπειδὴ_λέγεις_380">, ‘since thou must speak thus,’—<quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> emphasising <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεις</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1129"> <title>O. T.</title> 1129</ref>: but <emph>ib.</emph> 412 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ καὶ τυφλόν μ’ ὠνείδισας</quote> is different, <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> going with <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλόν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μήποτ_380">: 103 n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴνΣκῦρον_380"> (240): the art. is scornful: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1060">cp. 1060</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπλεύσῃς_380"> implies a further taunt: having come out so late, he will not even now stay and fight.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούσαςκἀξονειδισθείς_380"> form a rhetorical climax,— ‘having been addressed, yes, insulted, with such taunts’: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακά_380"> is object to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούσας</quote>, and also ‘cognate’ acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξονειδισθείς</quote>. Wecklein's ingenious </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξονειδίσας_380"> seems unlikely, since N. is dwelling on his wrongs rather than on his own heat in resenting them.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_οἴκους_380">: the plur. implies, ‘the home country,’ as 60 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οἴκων μολεῖν</quote>. The sing. (suggesting rather the private home) occurs in 58, 240, 488, 548.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τητώμενος_380">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1200"> <title>O. C.</title> 1200</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκ_κακῶν_380">, as the reputed son of Sisyphus, 417 n. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1397"> <title>O. T.</title> 1397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακός τ’ ὢν κἀκ κακῶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_τέλει_385">, the Atreidae: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 67"> <title>Ant.</title> 67</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσα_385"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύμπας_385"> have here an adverbial force,—‘wholly’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 275"> <title>Ai.</title> 275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνός τε λύπῃ πᾶς ἐλήλαται κακῇ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστι_ἡγουμένων_385">: is under their influence: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 917"> <title>O. T.</title> 917</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι τοῦ λέγοντος</quote>, n. (But in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 738"> <title>Ant.</title> 738</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοῦ κρατοῦντος ἡ πόλις νομίζεται</quote>; ‘is deemed his property.’)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατός_385">, ‘army’ (with reference to the Greek army at Troy): not=<quote xml:lang="grc">δῆμος</quote>,—a sense which occurs in Aesch. and elsewhere ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 8">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 8</ref> n.), but which is nowhere requisite in Soph. , and which would be weak here, just after <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_βροτῶν_385"> (the gen. as in 304), the unruly; those who violate the rights of others, as Odysseus has done: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 730"> <title>Ant.</title> 730</ref> and 660.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδασκάλων_λόγοισι_385">. This play was brought out in the spring of 409 B.C. The Revolution of the Four Hundred, in the summer of 411 B.C., was emphatically a case in which <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἡγούμενοι</quote>—Peisander and his fellow oligarchs—had corrupted or intimidated a <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote>.
