The Army at Samos had illustrated the same process in the case of a <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατός</quote>,—the oligarchic officers, in correspondence with Alcibiades, having been the first agents of mischief. ( <ref n="Thuc. 8. 47">Thuc. 8. 47</ref> and 75: Grote VIII. pp. 9 and 63.) Thus, to the ears of an Athenian audience, the poet's verses might well suggest a lightly-hinted apology for those citizens who, against their will, had been compromised by the conspirators.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1537">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1537</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγος_πᾶς_389">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 241">cp. 241</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀτρείδας_389">. We notice the art with which, all through his story, Neoptolemus has contrived to throw the chief odium on the Atreidae. Thus, after calling their speech <quote xml:lang="grc">τλημονέστατος</quote> (363), he remarks incidentally that Odysseus was a goodtempered man (377); and though he calls him, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιστος</quote> (384), he hastens to add that the higher powers were more to blame (385). And now, at the close, he names the Atreidae alone. Thus he acts in the spirit of his mentor's advice (64 f.), but refines upon it.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλος_389">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 585">cp. 585</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρεστέρα_391">: cp. <title>Helen.</title> 1301, where the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρεία</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτηρ θεῶν</quote> is identified with Demeter. In order to appreciate the large significance of this epithet in relation to the ‘Phrygian Mother,’ we must remember that ‘Phrygia’ originally denoted the whole interior highlands of Asia Minor west of the Halys (Kiepert, <title>Anc. Geo.</title> § 64). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παμβῶτι_391">: cp. the epithets <quote xml:lang="grc">βιόδωρος</quote> (1162), <quote xml:lang="grc">φερέσβιος, πουλυβότειρα, φυσίζοος, κουροτρόφος</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μᾶτερ_Διός_391">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μητρῷον</quote> at Athens was sacred to Rhea Cybele: see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1070"> <title>Ant.</title> 1070</ref> ff. The name Rhea (probably connected with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρα</quote>, earth) was doubtless older than Cybele (see Welcker <title>Götterl.</title> I. 221), and in Crete the ancient cult of Rhea seems never to have passed into that of Cybele, while in Asia Minor Rhea and Cybele came to be identified. Hence Demetrius of Scepsis (in the Troad) could say that Rhea was not worshipped in Crete, because, by Rhea, he understood Cybele (Strabo p. 472). The legends of the Cretan Ida were easily transferred to the Mysian: there was a <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκτη</quote> in the Troad (Strabo <emph>l.c.</emph>) as well as in Crete. Cp. Apoll. <ref n="Apollon. 1.1139">Rh. 1. 1139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥόμβῳ καὶ τυπάνῳ Ῥείην Φρύγες ἱλάσκοντο</quote>. Propertius 3. 1. 27 <title>Idaeum Simoenta</title> (the river at Troy), <title>Iovis cunabula parvi.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πακτωλὸν_392">: mentioned here as the river on which Sardis was situated, —that city being a famous seat of Cybele's worship. <ref n="Hdt. 5. 102">Her. 5. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σάρδιες μὲν ἐνεπρήσθησαν</quote> (during the Ionian revolt in 502 C.B. ), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δὲ αὐτῇσι καὶ ἱρὸν ἐπιχωρίης θεοῦ Κυβήβης· τὸ σκηπτόμενοι οἱ Πέρσαι ὕστερον ἀντενεπίμπρασαν τὰ ἐν Ἕλλησιν ἱρά</quote>. Hence an Athenian poet might well think of Sardis in speaking of Cybele. Lydiawas included in the older and larger meaning of Phrygia (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 825"> <title>Ant.</title> 825</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔχρυσον_392">. When the attribute of a noun which has the article consists of more than one element (as here of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχρυσον</quote>), part of it may stand between the art. and noun, and the rest after the noun, without art.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 986">cp. 986</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ παγκρατὲς σέλας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἡφαιστότευκτον</quote>: <title>O.T.</title> 1199 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν γαμψώνυχα παρθένον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησμῳδόν</quote>. The Pactolus brought down gold dust from Mount Tmolus, the range just south of Sardis ( <ref n="Verg. A. 10. 142">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 10. 142</ref>: <ref n="Hor. Epod. 15. 19">Hor. <title>Epod.</title> 15. 19</ref>, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκεῖ_395">, at Troy also (as now in Lemnos).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπηυδώμαν_395">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεκαλούμην</quote>: the only classical example of this compound.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀτρειδᾶν_πᾶς_395">, ‘all’ their insolence,—referring to the full account of it which N. has just given (363 ff.). Others understand: (1) ‘the complete’ or ‘consummate’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 142">cp. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν κράτος</quote> (n.). Or (2), making <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσ’</quote> predicative, ‘went with all its force’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 385">cp. 385</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πάτρια_398">, which had belonged to his father, Achilles: a rare poetical use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτριος</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote>: cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 6">Pind. <title> O.</title> 6. 62</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρία ὄσσα</quote>, the voice of his father (Apollo). In <ref n="Soph. OT 1394">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1394</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πάτρια... δώματ’</quote>=‘the house of my <emph>fathers</emph>,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτριος</quote> having its usual sense. But that sense is impossible here, since Achilles had been the first possessor of the arms wrought by Hephaestus.<emph> —<quote xml:lang="grc">παρεδίδοσαν</quote> </emph> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 64">cp. 64</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_400"> calls on the goddess to note the wrong: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάκαιρα_400">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεά</quote>, as Sappho fr. 1. 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὺ δ’, ὦ μάκαιρα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μειδιάσαισ’ ἀθανάτῳ προσώπῳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταυροκτόνων_400">, a general epithet, marking the fierceness of the creatures whom the goddess subdues: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.579"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 579</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σμερδαλέω δὲ λέοντε δὔ ἐν πρώτῃσι βόεσσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦρον ἐρύγμηλον ἐχέτην.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεόντων_ἔφεδρε_400"> is best taken literally, of riding on lions. Cybele riding sideways on a lion was often represented in works of art (statues, reliefs, coins). Pliny 35. 109 says that Nicomachus painted <emph>deum...matrem in leone sedentem.</emph> This painter belonged to the Thebano-Attic school, and flourished <emph>c.</emph> 360 B.C.: we may well suppose, then, that the lion-<emph>riding</emph> Cybele was familiar in the time of Sophocles. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 202"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 202</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πτεροῦντος ἔφεδρον ἵππου</quote> (Bellerophon).—But, as the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππων</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβάς</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.328"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 328</ref>) refers to chariot-driving, so here <quote xml:lang="grc">λεόντων ἔφεδρε</quote> might also mean, in a car drawn by lions. An altarrelief of the Roman age, reproduced by Baumeister (<title>Denkm.</title> p. 801), from Zoega's <title>Bassiril.</title> (1. 13), shows her thus: two lions draw her car; she wears a shortsleeved chiton, while the long veil attached to the back of her mural crown flows down like a mantle; in her right hand is a laurel branch; her left rests on the rim of the tympanon, holding it upright on her left knee.—It is less likely that <quote xml:lang="grc">λεόντων ἔφεδρε</quote> means, ‘seated <emph>above</emph> lions’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, on a throne with lions crouching below at each side. Arrian (<title>Periplous</title> 9) mentions such a representation, which, like the other two, seems to have been frequent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβας_402"> must be acc. in appos. with <quote xml:lang="grc">τεύχεα</quote>: it cannot be (as the first schol. suggests) a vocative addressed to the goddess. The armour of Achilles, made by the god Hephaestus, is a <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβας</quote>, an object on which men gaze with reverent wonder. So Thetis describes these arms as <quote xml:lang="grc">καλὰ μάλ’, οἷ’ οὔπω τις ἀνὴρ ὤμοισι φόρησεν</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.11"> <title>Il.</title> 19. 11</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 685"> <title>El.</title> 685</ref>(Orestes) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσῆλθε λαμπρός, πᾶσι τοῖς ἐκεῖ σέβας</quote>. The dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαρτίου</quote> must be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεδίδοσαν</quote>, which requires it. And it seems best not to take that dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβας</quote> also. If we did so, the phrase would mean, ‘an object of reverence’ to Odysseus; not, an ‘honour’ or ‘glory’ to him. But, though <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαρτίου</quote> is not construed with <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβας</quote>, their juxtaposition is forcible; ‘to <emph>him</emph>—those peerless arms.’ The long separation of the verb from its dative is excused by the fact that the interposed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ μάκαιρα... ἔφεδρε</quote> prepares the indignant emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαρτίου</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβας_402_2"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέρας_402">. As Nauck remarks, the two words are confused in the schol. on <ref n="Eur. Orest. 383">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 383</ref>(vol. 2, p. 122, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 18">Eur. Or. 18</ref>Dind.). L affords an instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">γ</quote> corrupted to <quote xml:lang="grc">ς</quote> in 571 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσω</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote>). In uncials <quote xml:lang="grc"> σέβας</quote> might have originated from B for P. But the sense given by <quote xml:lang="grc">γέρας</quote> would be tamer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύμβολον_λύπης_403">, a grieftoken, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> a token consisting in your grief (defining gen.; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 159">cp. 159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶκον...κοίτης</quote>, n.). <quote xml:lang="grc"> σύμβολα</quote> were tallies, sometimes consisting of dice (<quote xml:lang="grc">λίσπαι</quote>, <ref n="Plat. Sym. 193A">Plat. <title> Symp.</title> 193A</ref>) or knuckle-bones (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀστράγαλοι</quote>) sawn in two. A message or request, purporting to come from a friend at a distance, could thus be tested. The bearer was asked to produce the other half of the divided token. See <ref n="Hdt. 6. 86. 2">Her. 6. 86. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποδεικνύντες τὰ σύμβολα, ἀπαίτεον τὰ χρήματα</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 613">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοις...πέμπειν σύμβολ’</quote>,=to give one credentials to friends abroad. When two persons established such signs between them, they were said <quote xml:lang="grc">σύμβολα ποιεῖσθαι</quote>: <title>C. I. G.</title> 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιησάσθω δὲ καὶ σύμβολα ἡ βουλὴ πρὸς τὸν βασιλέα τὸν Σιδωνίων, ὅπως ἂν ὁ δῆμος ὁ Ἀθηναίων εἰδῇ ἐάν τι πέμπῃ...δεόμενος τῆς πόλεως</quote>. As each half was called <quote xml:lang="grc">σύμβολον</quote>, the word can mean ‘counterpart’: <ref n="Plat. Sym. 191D">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 191D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζητεῖ δὴ ἀεὶ τὸ αὑτοῦ ἕκαστος ξύμ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">βολον</quote>.—Musgrave (ed. 1809) first compared Aristeides 1. 416 (=625 Dind.) <quote xml:lang="grc">διὸ καὶ πᾶσιν ἀνθρώποις ἱκανόν ἐστι πρὸς αὐτήν</quote> (Athens), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ἄλλο τι σύμβολον, αὐτὸ τὸ σχῆμα τῆς ἀτυχίας</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pl. Poen. 5.2">Plaut. <title>Poen.</title> 5. 2. 87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Ego sum ipsus quem tu quaeris.—Si ita est, tesseram conferre si vis hospitalem.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Ego sum ipsus quem tu quaeris.—Si ita est,
							tesseram conferre si vis hospitalem.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσᾴδεθ_405">, ye are in accord with me, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> your complaint strikes a note which finds an echo in my own mind. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1113"> <title>O. T.</title> 1113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνᾴδει τῷδε τἀνδρὶ σύμμετρος</quote> (in respect of age). <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυνᾴδειν</quote> is properly said of two or more voices which harmonise; <quote xml:lang="grc">προσᾴδειν</quote> of a vocal accompaniment which harmonises with music. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 359">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 359</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσῳδὸς ἡ τύχη τὠμῷ πάθει.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_ἔργα_405">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα τὰ ἔργα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 471"> <title>O. C.</title> 471</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_θιγόντα_407">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι θίγοι ἂν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 7. 42">Thuc. 7. 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶν...εἰ ἐπικρατήσειέ τις...ῥᾳδίως ἂν αὐτὸ ληφθέν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ῥᾳδίως ἂν ληφθείη</quote>). <ref n="Soph. OC 761">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 761</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπὸ παντὸς ἂν φέρων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου δικαίου μηχάνημα ποικίλον</quote>, n.—With </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανουργίας_407">, despite its derivation, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσης</quote> must be supplied: so in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 300">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 300</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργίας</quote> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὸς ἔργου</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφ_ποεῖν_407">: from (=as a result of) which he would be likely, in the end, to effect anything not just. His objects have always something unjust in them; and he is unscrupulous in the choice of means. When the optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> (as here the implied <quote xml:lang="grc">θίγοι ἄν</quote>) stands in the antecedent clause, the optat. (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>) often stands in the relative clause: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 560"> <title>O. C.</title> 560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὴν γάρ τιν’ ἂν πρᾶξιν τύχοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξας ὁποίας ἐξαφισταίμην ἐγώ</quote>. This usage confirms L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλοι_407"> against <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλει</quote> (though the latter would be tenable: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 375"> <title>Ant.</title> 375</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_407"> here admits of two distinct explanations, though the sense is virtually the same with either. (1) It is ‘generic’ (170 n.): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> his purposes are of <emph>such a kind as</emph> can have no honest result. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1006">Cp. 1006</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν ὑγιὲς...φρονῶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 493"> <title>Ant.</title> 493</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ θυμὸς</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μηδὲν ὀρθῶς <*>v σκότῳ τεχνωμένων</quote>. I prefer this view. (2) It is ‘final’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλοι ποεῖν</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ποήσοι</quote>: ‘from which he shall not effect anything just.’ When the fut. indic. in a relative clause denotes purpose, the negative is <quote xml:lang="grc"> μή</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1412"> <title>O. T.</title> 1412</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκρίψατ’</quote> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα μήποτ’ εἰσόψεσθ’ ἔτι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_τέλος_407">, ultimately (though his <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> may be plausible at first sight): cp. <ref n="Hdt. 9. 37">Her. 9. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μέντοι ἔς γε τέλος οἱ συνήνεικε τὸ ἔχθος</quote> (‘in the end,’—though for a time he prospered).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖν_407">: for the spelling, cp. on 120: for the pres. inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλοι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 967"> <title>O. T.</title> 967</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_μείζων_411">, the son of Telamon; Ajax the son of Oileus (the leader of the western Locrians) was <quote xml:lang="grc">μείων, οὔ τι τόσος γε ὅσος Τελαμώνιος Αἴας</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.528"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 528</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔτιζῶν_411">. Soon after the death of Achilles, and either just before or just after the coming of Neoptolemus, the Atreidae had awarded the arms to Odysseus. The suicide of Ajax followed closely on the award. He died, then, either just before, or just after, the arrival of Neoptolemus at Troy. Neoptolemus implies that he left Troy for home just after the award (382). Since his indignation is feigned, it might be supposed that the interval between the award and his sailing (for Lemnos) had really been longer. But, even if that interval had been as brief as he represents it, he might still have known, before leaving Troy, that Ajax was dead. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἦ_414">...; In this formula <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> asks the question: <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλά</quote> marks surprise, as it so often marks remonstrance (‘<emph> nay</emph>, can it be so?’ or, ‘<emph>what</emph>, can that be true?’). The fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’</quote> is absent from L (see cr. n.) has led some editors to prefer the conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γάρ_414">. But it may be observed:—(<emph>a</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦ</quote> was a comparatively unfamiliar phrase, and therefore the fact that the other MSS. have it is presumptive evidence of its genuineness. (<emph>b</emph>) The preceding </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_εἶπας_414"> cannot be urged as an objection: cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 58">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 58</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς εἶπας; ἀλλ’ ἦ καὶ σοφὸς λέληθας ὤν</quote>; Eur. Itis true, however, that such a preface to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦ</quote> is unusual: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 879">Soph. <title>El.</title> 879</ref> : <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 220">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 220</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 816">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 816</ref>, <title>Helen.</title> 490, <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 425"> <title>Heracl.</title> 425</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 932"> <title>Hipp.</title> 932</ref>, [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 36. —Remark that in <ref n="Soph. OC 26"> <title>O. C.</title> 26</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> are separated, the peculiar force of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦ</quote> is not present. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὄντα_415">: <ref target="cl253">see on 253</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_τάλας_416">, ‘woe is me’ (not, ‘alas, poor Ajax’): as <ref n="Soph. OT 744"> <title>O. T.</title> 744</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_οὐχ_416">: the negative is repeated, for greater emphasis, in 418: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 5"> <title>Ant.</title> 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον οὐ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν σῶν τε κἀμῶν οὐκ ὄπωπ’ ἐγὼ κακῶν</quote>, n.<emph> —<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Τυδέως γόνος</quote> </emph>, Diomedes. Philoctetes had no personal grievance against him, but dislikes him as being a man of the same stamp as Odysseus, with whom the tenth book of the <title>Iliad</title> associates him in stealing the horses of Rhesus. In <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.230"> <title>Il.</title> 6. 230</ref> it is Diomedes who proposes to Glaucus that the latter should exchange ‘golden armour for armour of bronze.’ Lesches, in the <title>Little Iliad</title>, and Euripides, in his <title>Philoctetes</title>, made Diomedes come to Lemnos to fetch Philoctetes: see Introd. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 592">Cp. 592</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὑμπολητὸς_Λαερτίῳ_417">, ‘the son of Sisyphus, bought by Laertes,’ —because Anticleia was said to have been pregnant when Laertes married her. The word <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐμπολητός</quote> probably means that Laertes gave a large ‘bride-price’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδνα</quote>) to Anticleia's father, Autolycus. So the scholiast, <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ δοὺς χρήματα ἠγάγετο</quote>. This is simpler than to suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπολητός</quote> is merely ‘acquired’ (as a bad bargain), like <quote xml:lang="grc">λωβητὸν ἐμπόλημα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 538."> <title>Tr.</title> 538.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λαερτίου_417"> (see cr. n.) cannot be defended by understanding, (1) ‘the son of Laertes, bought from Sisyphus’; or (2) ‘the bought son of Laertes-Sisyphus,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, of a father, nominally Laertes, but really Sisyphus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔδει_418">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1363">cp. 1363</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 256"> <title>O. T.</title> 256</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_θάλλοντες_419">, full greatly prosperous: cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 272D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 272D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο... καὶ μάλ’ εὔκριτον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δ_421">; <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κ.τ.λ.</quote> The fact that the first hand in L wrote </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠ_421"> (<emph>sic</emph>) is a good reason for believing that either </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_421"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_421"> was the original reading. With <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖ</quote>, the proper punctuation would be,—<quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ αὖ παλαιὸς κἀγαθὸς φίλος τ’ ἐμός</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέστωρ ὁ Πύλιος, ἔστιν</quote>; ‘And then, again, what of Nestor,—is he alive?’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 101"> <title>Ai.</title> 101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶεν, τί γὰρ δὴ παῖς ὁ τοῦ Λαερτίου</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ σοι τύχης ἕστηκεν</quote>; and <emph>ib.</emph> 983. But the context strongly favours </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_421_2">. Philoctetes is wondering how the Atreidae and Odysseus had been allowed to work their will without hindrance. ‘How could Ajax allow it?’ ‘He was dead.’ ‘Well, but <emph>is not</emph> Nestor alive? <title>He</title> used to restrain them.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δ_421_2">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 941"> <title>O. T.</title> 941</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ); οὐχ ὁ πρέσβυς Πόλυβος ἐγκρατὴς ἔτι</quote>;— With respect to the reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ὃς_421">, we observe:—(1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_421"> might easily have been generated by the unmetrical conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_421"> which has been written in L above <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠ</quote>: (2) the ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς</quote> would be peculiarly awkward here, where the principal verb is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιός_421">, simply ‘old’: not, (as some take it,) ‘one of the good old school.’ For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_τε_421"> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 581">cp. 581</ref>, 656.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_κακά_421">, <emph>their</emph> misdeeds, at least: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 773"> <title>Tr.</title> 773</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ σοῦ κακοῦ</quote>, thy crime. The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_421"> means that, if Nestor could not ward off all troubles from the army, at any rate he was able to prevent acts of flagrant wrong on the part of such men as Odysseus and Diomedes. Placed thus between <quote xml:lang="grc">τά</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνων κακά, γε</quote> must emphasize that phrase only; it cannot here be taken with the whole sentence (‘<emph>restrained</emph>, at least...’), as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1278"> <title>O. C.</title> 1278</ref>(n.). Philoctetes alludes either to what he had seen on the voyage to Troy, or to what his occasional visitors had reported.—For the place of the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 67"> <title>Ant.</title> 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">περισσὰ πράσσειν</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξήρυκε_421">: the compound occurs only here. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρύκειν</quote> as=<emph>arcere</emph>, cp. Theocr. 7. 127 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὴ καλὰ νόσφιν ἐρύκοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀντίλοχος_425">. Pindar is our earliest authority for the story of Antilochus saving his father Nestor's life: he brings it in <emph>à propos</emph> of a son who had driven his father's chariot in the Pythian games, and won the race (<title>Pyth.</title> 6. 38 ff.). Memnon was pressing Nestor hard, and one of the horses in Nestor's chariot had been wounded by Paris. Nestor called for help to Antilochus, who diverted Memnon's attack from his father to himself, and was killed; thus winning the fame, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος ἀμφὶ τοκεῦσιν ἔμμεν πρὸς ἀρετάν</quote>. The <title>Odyssey</title> notices that Antilochus was slain by Memnon, but does not say that he fell in saving his father (4. 188). At the end of the <title>Iliad</title> Antilochus is still living (23. 785 ff.); in <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.90"> <title>Il.</title> 8. 90</ref> it is Diomedes who rescues Nestor (from Hector). Pindar's source was the <title>Aethiopis</title> of Arctînus, in which Achilles avenged Antilochus by slaying Memnon.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_γόνος_425">, the son who was at his side:—not (I think) with direct reference to the saving of Nestor's life by Antilochus,—this is more than <quote xml:lang="grc">παρῆν</quote> could suggest, without further explanation (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 373">cp. 373</ref>),—but rather in the general sense that the son was the stay and comfort of his father's old age.—The MS. reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσπερ_γόνος_425">, would clearly imply that Antilochus was Nestor's only (or last surviving) son. The <title>Iliad</title> describes Nestor as having two sons at Troy, Thrasymedes and Antilochus (17. 378); and according to the <title>Odyssey</title> (3. 413 ff.) six sons were left to Nestor after the death of Antilochus, one of these being Thrasymedes. If it be suggested that the <title>Aethiopis</title> may have represented Antilochus as the last surviving son, we may reply that this is extremely improbable, when it is remembered that several Ionian colonies claimed to have been founded by the Neleidae, descendants of Nestor who emigrated from Pylus (<title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 167). The same consideration condemns Seyffert's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_ἦν_425">. Cavallin's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_ἦν_425_2"> is free from this objection, but is somewhat weak.—See Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_ἔλεξας_426">, a correction which I published in 1869 (see cr. n.), still appears to me the most probable. Porson's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_ἐξέδειξας_426"> is founded on the schol. in L, <quote xml:lang="grc">γρ. δὔ αὐτὼ δ’ ἐξέδειξας</quote>, and may be deemed certain so far as the words <quote xml:lang="grc">δὔ αὖ τώδ’</quote> are concerned. But no one has justified the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέδειξας</quote>. We see the proper uses of the word in <ref n="Soph. OC 1021"> <title>O. C.</title> 1021</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ αὐτὸς ἐκδε<*>ξῃς ἐμοί</quote> (point them out, discover them, to me): <ref n="Soph. El. 348"> <title>El.</title> 348</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τούτων μῖσος ἐκδείξειας ἄν</quote> (‘manifest’). <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1298">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title> 1298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδὸς ἐκδεῖξαι φρένα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ σοῦ δικαίαν</quote>. But here the word is strangely inappropriate, ‘thou hast <emph>pointed out</emph>,’ instead of, ‘thou hast named.’ And </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλεξας_426">, the most natural word, is in all the MSS. Eur. Itseems very rash, then, to assume, on the strength of the schol., that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεξας</quote> is spurious, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέδειξας</quote> genuine, especially when we remember the quality of some of the variants which rest on the same authority; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, in v. 423, the schol. on <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκ’ ἐξήρυκε</quote> gives <quote xml:lang="grc">γρ. κἀξεκήρυξεν</quote>. L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_ἔλεξας_426_2">, with an erasure of two letters after <quote xml:lang="grc">δείν’</quote>, may well have arisen from <quote xml:lang="grc">δὔ αὖ τώδ’ ἄν</quote>[<quote xml:lang="grc"> δρ</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεξας</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΕΙΝ</quote> would easily have been suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΑΝ</quote> if the <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΠ</quote> had from any cause been obscured: or, again, a misreading of <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΑΝ</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΕΙΝ</quote> may have led to the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΠ</quote>. In minuscule writing the process would have been hardly less easy.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_ἔλεξας_426_3">, two things seem clear. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτως</quote>, or, as it is better written, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔτως</quote>, yields no fitting sense here. It could not mean, ‘in those few words.’ It would rather mean, ‘just as in the former cases.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 931"> <title>O. T.</title> 931</ref> n. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο...δείν’ ἔλεξας, οἷν</quote>, would be most awkward, whether rendered (<emph>a</emph>) ‘thou hast told dreadful news <emph>about</emph> two persons’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">δύο</quote> masc.), or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘thou hast told two calamities concerning persons,’ etc. (<quote xml:lang="grc">δύο</quote> neut., with <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοιν</quote> understood from <quote xml:lang="grc">ο<*>ν</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_ἄνδρε_426">: Ajax (415) and Antilochus. Prof. Campbell says that v. 415 is ‘too remote to allow of this’: but vv. 416— 420 form merely a parenthetic contrast suggested by the death of Ajax, and with v. 421 we come to the father of Antilochus. If <quote xml:lang="grc">δὔ ἄνδρε</quote> are to be Nestor and Antilochus (as Campbell holds), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλωλότοιν_426"> has to mean ‘desolate’ in the case of the living father, and ‘dead’ only in the case of the son. But surely </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵδε_426"> in 428 must include both the men mentioned in 426.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_ἠθέλης_426">, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1348"> <title>O. T.</title> 1348</ref>: so below, <ref n="Soph. OT 1239"> O. T. 1239</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν...ἐβουλόμην</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1278"> O. T. 1278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἤθελον...ἄν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 88"> <title>Ai.</title> 88</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκοπεῖν_428"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc"> προσδοκᾶν</quote>, a rare use. More often <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπεῖν</quote>=‘look for’ in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ζητεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 7. 32">Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 7. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπεῖτε παῦλάν τινα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵδε_428">, Ajax and Antilochus; perh. he thinks of Achilles (331) too. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀδυσσεὺς_κἀνταῦθ_429">: ‘while Odysseus survives in this case also,’ —outliving Ajax and Antilochus (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδε</quote>, 428), as he had already outlived Achilles (371). Once more, death has spared the worse man (436). According to other views, (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνταῦθα</quote>=‘<emph> and</emph>’ [not ‘also’] ‘in a case where’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘not only does he live, but he has survived men so much his betters.’ (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνταῦθα</quote>=‘and in such a crisis as this,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when, Achilles being dead, the Greeks at Troy could ill spare true men. (3) The schol. explains <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνταῦθ’</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς ζῶσιν</quote>: but this ignores <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, and makes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθα</quote> weak.—Some think that the phrase used by Philoctetes was intended to have a second meaning (‘here in Lemnos’) for the spectator; but this is improbable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸν_430">, <emph>illum</emph>, not <emph> ipsum:</emph> the latter would be fitting only if Odysseus had been responsible for the deaths of the others. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐδᾶσθαι_430">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1478"> <title>El.</title> 1478</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντας θανοῦσιν οὕνεκ’ ἀνταυδᾷς ἴσα</quote>, speakest of the living as if they were dead. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιστὴς_431">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 877">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 877</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν εἰς ἔριν ὀξυμερίμνοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθωσι στρεβλοῖσι παλαίσμασιν ἀντιλογοῦντες</quote> (‘when they enter the strife, contending with subtle, tortuous <emph> tricks</emph>’). Aeschin. or. 3 § 205 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαισμα τοῦτ’ ἐστὶ δικαστηρίου</quote>, a trick of the law-courts.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμποδίζονται_431">: the word seems to have been suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιστής</quote>,—alluding to a wrestler tripping up his adversary: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 262">Ar. <title> Eq.</title> 262</ref>(with ref. to the tricks by which Cleon outwits his simple victims), <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλαβών, ἀγκυρίσας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτ’ ἀποστρέψας τὸν ὦμον αὐτὸν ἐνεκολήβασας</quote> (‘you put one of your legs between his,—hook it round them,— force his shoulder back,—and fall heavily on him’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_433">, a monosyll.: <ref n="Soph. OC 964"> <title>O. C.</title> 964</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_γὰρ_433">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 249">cp. 249</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_433">, ethic dat., implying, ‘how was it that you did not find him ready to help you at that crisis?’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 81"> <title>O. C.</title> 81</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ βέβηκεν ἡμὶν ὁ ξένος;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_φίλτατα_433">, of one person, as <ref n="Eur. Ion 521">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 521</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ φίλταθ’ εὑρών</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν υιόν</quote>): but of several persons, <ref n="Soph. OC 1110">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1110</ref> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_βραχεῖ_435">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 673"> <title>El.</title> 673</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκ’ Ὀρέστης· ἐν βραχεῖ συνθεὶς λέγω</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. PB 505">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 505</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχεῖ δὲ μύθῳ πάντα συλλήβδην μάθε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_435">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τόδ’</quote>, referring to what follows: cp. n. on <title>O.C.</title> 787.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεμος_435"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote> stands as an independent sentence, unconnected with the prefatory <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἐκδιδάξω</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 612"> <title>Ant.</title> 612</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρκέσει νόμος ὅδ)· οὐδὲν ἕρπει κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκὼν_435">, ‘by choice’: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, war has a marked preference for killing good men, though, of course, it kills some bad men too. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκών</quote> does not involve a definite personification of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεμος</quote> (like that in Ar. <title>Pax</title>): we can say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ φύσις βούλεται ποιεῖν τι</quote> ( <title>An. Gen.</title> 4), without writing <quote xml:lang="grc">Φύσις</quote>. Cp. fr. 652 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς εὐγενεῖς γὰρ κἀγαθούς, ὦ παῖ, φιλεῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρης ἐναίρειν· οἱ δὲ τῇ γλώσσῃ θρασεῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγοντες ἄτας ἐκτός εἰσι τῶν κακῶν·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρης γὰρ οὐδὲν τῶν κακῶν λωτίζεται</quote>. Anacreon fr. 101 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρης δ’ οὐκ ἀγαθῶν φείδεται, ἀλλὰ κακῶν</quote>. The same thought is implied in the phrase of Andoc. , or. 3 § 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὺς μὲν Ἀθηναίων ἀπολέσαντες ἀριστίνδην</quote>, —as if the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστοι</quote> had been selected. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_γε_438">, <emph>in accordance with</emph> this very thing, = ‘on this very ground’: cp. <ref n="Isoc. 18.34">Isocr. or. 18 § 34</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄξιον οὔτε κατὰ χάριν οὔτε κατ’ ἐπιείκειαν οὔτε κατ’ ἄλλο οὐδὲν ἢ κατὰ τοὺς ὅρκους περὶ αὐτῶν ψηφίσασθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτὸς_439">, <emph>about</emph> him (gen. of connection): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 441">cp. 441</ref>: n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 307."> <title>O. C.</title> 307.</ref>—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τί … κυρεῖ</emph>. κυρέω</quote>, in ref. to a person's fortunes, can be either (1) intrans., with adv., as <ref n="Soph. El. 1424"> <title>El.</title> 1424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. Ὀρέστα, πῶς κυρεῖτε; ΟΡ. τἀν δόμοισι μὲν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς</quote>: or (2) trans., with acc., as <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 214">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ τί νῦν ἕκατι δαιμόνων κυρῶ</quote>; (‘what do I obtain?’). Here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖ_439"> seems to be intrans., while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_439"> is virtually adverbial: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1704">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1704</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπραξεν οἷον ἤθελεν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἤθελεν</quote>), ‘he has fared as he would.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίου_τούτου_441">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 572">cp. 572</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 67"> <title>O. C.</title> 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΞΕ. ἐκ τοῦ κατ’ ἀστυ βασιλέως τάδ’ ἄρχεται. ΟΙ. οὗτος δὲ τίς λόγῳ τε καὶ σθένει κρατεῖ</quote>; (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἔστιν οὗτος ὃς κρατεῖ</quote>;) —where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> continues a conversation by putting a question which the last speaker's words suggest.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_441">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, of whom do you <emph>mean</emph> to speak. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 595"> <title>O. C.</title> 595</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΙ. πέπονθα, Θησεῦ, δεινὰ πρὸς</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς κακά. ΘΗ. ἦ τὴν παλαιὰν ξυμφορὰν γένους ἐρεῖς</quote>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_441"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτου_441">, has been preferred by some; because, where a verb of speaking or asking thus takes a simple gen., the object of the verb is usu. represented, either (<emph>a</emph>) by an acc., as in <ref n="Soph. El. 317"> <title>El.</title> 317</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κασιγνήτου τί φής</quote>; or (<emph>b</emph>) by a relative clause, as above in 440 by <quote xml:lang="grc">τί νῦν κυρεῖ</quote>; But in <ref n="Soph. OC 307"> <title>O. C.</title> 307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων σοῦ</quote> (‘hearing <emph>about</emph> thee’) is an exception to the supposed rule. Further, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρεῖς</quote> is here merely a short expression for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξερήσει τί νῦν κυρεῖ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θερσίτης_442">=‘the bold one,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">θέρσος</quote> being the Aeolic form of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάρσος</quote> (Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 1190. 2), as <quote xml:lang="grc">κρέτος</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc"> κράτος</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἁλιθέρσης, Θέρσανδρος</quote>. Here he survives Achilles. But, according to the commoner legend, he died before him. Achilles had slain the leader of the Amazons, Penthesilea. Thersites thrust his spear into the eyes of the corpse, and taunted Achilles with his love for her; when the hero killed him. This was the version given by Arctînus in the <title>Aethiopis</title> (Proclus, <title>Chrestom.</title> p. 478). It was the subject of a play (prob. a satyric drama) by Chaeremon, called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχιλλεὺς Θερσιτοκτόνος</quote> (Suidas, <emph>s.v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑπάρχων</quote>, calls it simply <quote xml:lang="grc">Θερσίτης</quote>). See Nauck, <title>Frag. Trag.</title> p. 607. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_εἵλετ_443"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> This sentence deserves study as an example of Attic expression. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν εἵλετο</quote>= ‘never used to choose.’ <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7. 1. 10">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7. 1. 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπότε προσβλέψειέ τινας...εἶπεν ἄν</quote>. In this use the aor. differs from the impf. by marking a moment; as <quote xml:lang="grc">εἵλετο</quote> expresses the making of the choice, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρεῖτο</quote> would express the sentiment of preference. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_ἐῴη_443">, ‘in a case where no one was for allowing him to speak’: the optat. denotes indefinite frequency (as 289 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ μοι βάλοι</quote>). Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">προσβλέψειε</quote> in the example just cited. <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδείς</quote> is ‘generic,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> marks the occasion as being one of a class: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 170">cp. 170</ref> n. And since <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐῶ</quote>= ‘dissuade,’ ‘remonstrate,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου μηδεὶς ἐῴη</quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου πάντες μὴ‐ἐῷεν</quote>, ‘where all were protesting.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1184"> <title>Ai.</title> 1184</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφου μεληθεὶς τῷδε, κἂν μηδεὶς ἐᾷ</quote>,=<quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν πάντες μὴ‐ἐῷσιν</quote>, ‘though all the world forbid.’ (3) ‘He would never choose to speak (only) once’ = ‘he would always choose to speak often’; —a <quote xml:lang="grc">μείωσις</quote> of the same order as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἥκιστα</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα</quote>. Thus the whole sense is—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ ἂν εἵλετο πολλάκις λέγειν, ὅπου πάντες σιγᾶν κελεύοιεν</quote>. Remark that, in the negative form actually used, the aor. inf. (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπεῖν</quote>) suits <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσάπαξ</quote> better than a pres. inf. (<quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</quote>) would have done.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτον_οἶσθ_443">: for the construction, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 534">cp. 534</ref>, 544, 549, 573: <ref n="Soph. OC 1197"> <title>O. C.</title> 1197</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1242"> <title>Ant.</title> 1242</ref> f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόν_445"> should not be changed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_445">, which would be too emphatic here. He speaks in a careless tone. The following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_445">, though it was not necessary, affords no argument against <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμελλ_446">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ε<*>ναι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 448"> <title>Ant.</title> 448</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_οὐδέν_446">: the synizesis as in 948, 1037; fr. 479. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ οὐδ’ ὁ κρείσσων</quote>.—Cp. frag. adesp. 276 (Nauck) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν κακὸν <γὰρ> ῥᾳδίως ἀπόλλυται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιστέλλουσιν_446">, cherish, protect (as <ref n="Hdt. 9. 60">Her. 9. 60</ref> etc.), a sense derived from that of ‘dressing’ or ‘wrapping up’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 903">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 903</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πανοῦργα_448">: for the neut., instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς κακούργους</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1196."> <title>O. T.</title> 1196.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλιντριβῆ_448">, lit. ‘rubbed again and again,’ hence, thoroughly <emph>versed in</emph> knavery (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοισιν ἐντριβής</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 177"> <title>Ant.</title> 177</ref>). So <title>Nub.</title> 260 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν γενήσει τρίμμα, κρόταλον, παιπάλη</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 447 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρησιεπής, περίτριμμα δικῶν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναστρέφοντες_448">: alluding esp. to the story of Sisyphus cheating Pluto: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 621">cp. 621</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δὲ_448">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 442">cp. 442</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_χρήστ_448">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 972"> <title>El.</title> 972</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλεῖ γὰρ πρὸς τὰ χρηστὰ πᾶς ὁρᾶν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς χρηστάς</quote>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστέλλους_448">: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1664 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεπέμπετ’</quote> (of Oed.): <ref n="Plat. Sym. 179E">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 179E</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ Ἀχιλλέα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς μακάρων νήσους ἀπέπεμψαν</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Ion 1274">Eur. <title> Ion</title> 1274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρδην ἂν ἐξέπεμψας εἰς Ἅιδου δόμους</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>). The word is so natural after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναστρέφοντες</quote> that Nauck's alteration of it to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προυσελοῦς_448"> is strange indeed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_τίθεσθαι_451">: where am I to place these things (in a theory of divine government), <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, what am I to think of them? For this use of the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 473">cp. 473</ref>, 876: <ref n="Dem. 18.299">Dem. or. 18 § 299</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πόρρω μέντοι που τῶν ἐμοὶ πεπολιτευμένων τίθεμαι</quote> (‘rank them far below...’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_αἰνεῖν_451">: and in what respect to praise them: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 390"> <title>O. T.</title> 390</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ σὺ μάντις εἶ σαφής</quote>; and <emph>ib.</emph> 355 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅταν_451"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The simplest form of question would have been:—‘What is one to think of these things, seeing that they conflict with one's belief in beneficent gods?’ This is amplified into—‘What is one to think of these things, seeing that, while one praises the dealings of the gods, one finds (by these facts) that the gods are bad?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαινῶν_451"> is best taken in a simple temporal sense, (=‘at the same time that one praises,’) rather than as tentative (‘while one tries to praise’), or concessive (‘though one praises’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_μὲν_453">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Οἰταίου_453">, since Poeas was king of the Malians; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 4">cp. 4</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλόθεν_εἰσορῶν_454">, ‘eyeing them from a distance, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> holding aloof from them. The phrase is figurative; it is not an oxymoron, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σκότῳ ὁρᾶν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1273"> <title>O. T.</title> 1273</ref>), as though it meant ‘never seeing them.’ This <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλόθεν εἰσορᾶν</quote> is a poetical counterpart of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόρρωθεν ἀσπάζεσθαι</quote>,— familiar in Attic as meaning ‘to give a wide berth’ to an objectionable person or thing: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 499A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 499A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵων ζητεῖν μὲν τὸ ἀληθὲς...τὰ δὲ κομψά τε καὶ ἐριστικὰ ...πόρρωθεν ἀσπαζομἑνων</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 102">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσωθεν αὐτὴν</quote> (Aphrodite) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνὸς ὢν ἀσπάζομαι</quote>. Antiphilus (<emph>c.</emph> 60 A.D. ) in <title>Anthol.</title> 9. 29 (speaking of the golden age), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖτ’ ἀπὸ χέρσου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλόθεν, ὡς Ἅιδης, πόντος ἀπεβλέπετο</quote>. Cp. the phrases, tinged with a similar irony, in <ref n="Soph. OT 795"> <title>O. T.</title> 795</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 997."> O. T. 997.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυλάξομαι_454">, midd. <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτούς</quote>: cp. fr. 428 <quote xml:lang="grc">δισσὰ γὰρ φυλάσσεται</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλων τε μέμψιν κεἰς θεοὺς ἁμαρτάνειν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_456">=<quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ὅτοις</quote>, followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτους_456">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1081"> <title>Ai.</title> 1081</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου δ’ ὑβρίζειν δρᾶν θ’ ἃ βούλεται παρῆ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην νόμιζε τὴν πόλιν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειλὸς_456"> is rightly restored by Brunck for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὸς_456"> of the MSS. It alludes to Odysseus as a trickster (407) and a coward (1025). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 326"> <title>Ant.</title> 326</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δειλὰ κέρδη</quote>, where, again, L has the false reading <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὰ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινός_456">, by itself, would mean simply ‘able.’ As Arist. says, <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινότης</quote> is the faculty of finding means to an end; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν οὖν ὁ<*>σκοπὸς ᾖ καλός, ἐπαινετή ἐστιν· ἂν δὲ φαῦλος, πανουργία</quote> (<title>Eth. N.</title> 6. 13). So, in v. 440, the bad sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινοῦ</quote> is hinted by <quote xml:lang="grc">γλώσση</quote>. Campbell quotes <ref n="Isoc. 12.48">Isocr. or. 12 § 48</ref> to show that <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινός</quote> could, by itself, mean ‘a clever <quote xml:lang="grc">ρογυε</quote>’: there, however, <quote xml:lang="grc"> δεινήν</quote> (said of Sparta) means ‘formidable’ (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φοβεράν</quote> just before), and the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὴν...νομίζειν</quote> is presently repeated in <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβεῖσθαι...καὶ δεδιέναι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σκῦρος_459">: <ref target="cl240">see on 240</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαρκοῦσά_ὥστε_459"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέρπεσθαι_δόμῳ_459">, sufficient to make me content with my abode (and resigned to seeing no more of the army). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαῖρε_462"> is repeated, as <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 582">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 582</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαῖρε χαῖῤ</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 1363 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρετε χαίρετ’</quote>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡςμέγιστα_462">: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα χαῖρε</quote> (<title>Hom. hym.</title> 1. 466 etc.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεταστήσειαν_462">: <title>Helen.</title> 1442 <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέψον πρὸς ἡμᾶς καὶ μετάστησον κακῶν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_θέλεις_462">: <title>Hom. hym.</title> 2. 417 <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖα μάλ’ ἐπρη+/υνεν ἑκηβόλον, ὡς ἔθελ·, αὐτός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλοῦν_εἴκῃ_465">: a very rare instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκω τινί τι</quote> as=<emph>concedo aliquid alicui.</emph> We cannot compare <ref n="Soph. OC 172"> <title>O. C.</title> 172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκοντας ἃ δεῖ</quote>, or <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1243"> <title>Ai.</title> 1243</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκειν ἃ τοῖς πολλοῖσιν ἤρεσκεν κριταῖς</quote>, where the acc. merely denotes the things <emph>in regard to which</emph> one is to yield. Still, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.337"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 337</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶξαί τέ οἱ ἡνία</quote> (‘to give the horse rein’) seems to confirm <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκῃ</quote> here. The analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">παρείκω</quote> suggests to me that the constr. here would be somewhat softened if, instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλοῦν_465">, we might read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖν_465">: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 934 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἂν ἡμῖν παρείκωσι θεοὶ ... νομοθετεῖν</quote>. But the change, though tempting, is not necessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καιρὸς_466">, the need of the moment; for the semi-personification, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1450">cp. 1450</ref>; <ref n="Soph. El. 75"> <title>El.</title> 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς γάρ, ὅσπερ ἀνδράσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστος ἔργου παντός ἐστ’ ἐπιστάτης</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 39 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν σε καιρὸς εἰσάγῃ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλεῖ_466">, as <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1042">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1042</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλεσθ’ ἐπεισπέσωμεν; ὡς ἀκμὴ καλεῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑκάβῃ παρεῖναι</quote>. Lucian (<title>Demonactis vita</title> 65) quotes, as a familiar stage ‘tag,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς δὲ καλεῖ μηκέτι μέλλειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλοῦν_σκοπεῖν_467">, to watch for (428 n.) favourable weather: cp. <ref n="Antiph. 5.24">Antiphon or. 5 § 24</ref> (the speaker had been detained in port by adverse winds) <quote xml:lang="grc">πλοῦς ἡμῖν ἐγίγνετο, καὶ ἀνήγετο πλοῖα ἅπαντα</quote> . <ref n="Thuc. 1. 137">Thuc. 1. 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέχρι πλοῦς γένηται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἀπόπτου_467">, not at a distance (from the ship); strictly, so that the quarter in which their ship lies shall not be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄποπτος</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘seen (only) at a distance.’ Cp. Galen 3. 222 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀπόπτου θεασάμενος</quote>, and append. on <ref n="Soph. OT 762"> <title>O. T.</title> 762</ref>(p. 230, 2nd ed.). At the cave they are close to the sea, and can judge of the weather as well as at another point on the coast. But he means that they must be close to their ship, in order to sail as soon as ever the wind changes. At present it is adverse (640) for a voyage to Scyros: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it is south or south-west (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 355">cp. 355</ref>).—Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀπόπτου σκοπεῖν</quote> as=‘to watch from a place where one cannot (properly) see,’ a sort of oxymoron, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σκότῳ ὁρᾶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρός_πατρὸς_468">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόςτ_τι_468"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: cp. n. on <title> O.C.</title> 250 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός σ’ ὅ τι σοι φίλον ἐκ σέθεν ἄντομαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκέτης_470"> strengthens </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκνοῦμαι_470"> much as in <ref n="Soph. OT 760"> <title>O. T.</title> 760</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξικέτευσε τῆς ἐμῆς χειρὸς θιγών</quote>, the verb is strengthened by the added phrase, which serves to mark the attitude of formal supplication. Cp. below, <ref n="Soph. OT 930."> O. T. 930.</ref>For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκνεῖσθαι</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκετεύειν</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 932">cp. 932</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 275"> <title>O. C.</title> 275</ref> and 1011: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 588"> <title>Ai.</title> 588</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 136."> <title>El.</title> 136.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵοις_ἐνναίοντα_471">,—the disease, and the wretched dwelling: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσοισί_ἐξήκουσας_471">,—the painful provision of food, water, fuel and fire (285 —299). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 174">Cp. 174</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_με_473">, lit., regard me (451 n.) as a secondary task: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘give me a place, however lowly, in thy care.’ The thought is: ‘I should not have asked you to alter your course for me; but since you are going home at any rate, let this good deed be an accident of your voyage.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 509">Soph. <title>El.</title> 509</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθον γὰρ αὐτοῦ πρὸς τάφον, πάρεργ’ ὁδοῦ</quote> (as an incident of the journey). <ref n="Thuc. 1. 142">Thuc. 1. 142</ref>(with ref. to naval skill) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐνδέχεται... ἐκ παρέργου μελετᾶσθαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_παρέργῳ_473">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν παρέργου μέρει</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 370C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 370C</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσχέρεια_473">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 900">cp. 900</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξοιδα_474">, by the bitter experience to which he alludes in 1031 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορήματος_474">, freight, as <quote xml:lang="grc">φορέω</quote> is said of ships ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.390"> <title>Od.</title> 2. 390</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_αἰσχρὸν_476"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The objections which have been made to this verse seem idle. Philoctetes is appealing to the generous instincts of the young man. ‘To noble natures, what is (morally) shameful is hateful, and what is worthy appears glorious.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκλεές_476"> implies, ‘even if there is no applause to be gained, the <quote xml:lang="grc">γενναῖος</quote> is rewarded by the sense that he has merited true <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὔκλεια</quote>,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, that his deed is, in itself, honourable.’ Then, in vv. 477—479, Philoctetes passes to a different and a lower argument,—viz., that Neoptolemus will incur reproach if he refuse to do this act of mercy, and that, in the other case, he will have men's praises. All the difficulties which have been raised have come from failing to see (1) that the subjective sense of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκλεές_476_2"> is justified by the fact that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_γενναίοισι_476"> is an ethic dat.,—‘<emph>in the sight of</emph> the generous,’—not a dat. of interest: and (2) that the considerations urged in 475 —479 are of two distinct orders. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλιπόντι_τοῦτ_477"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἐκλίπῃς τοῦτο</quote>, if thou forsake, abandon, this deed (which is a duty laid on thee): cp. <title>I. T.</title> 750 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’, ἐκλιπὼν τὸν ὅρκον, ἀδικοίης ἐμέ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνειδος_477"> is strengthened by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_καλόν_477">, as in 842 by <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἰσχρόν</quote>, in <ref n="Soph. OC 753"> <title>O. C.</title> 753</ref> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλιον</quote>, in <ref n="Soph. OT 1035"> <title>O. T.</title> 1035</ref> by <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν</quote>. Ellendt, indeed, is with those who trace here an original ‘middle’ sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνειδος</quote> as=‘a thing said of one’ (good or evil). It would be equally reasonable to infer a neutral meaning for <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρ</quote> from <ref n="Soph. Trach. 454"> <title>Tr.</title> 454</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κὴρ πρόσεστιν οὐ καλή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖστον_478">=<quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1051"> <title>Ant.</title> 1051</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.697"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 697</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἲ γὰρ δή, βασίλεια, τόδε πλεῖστον κακὸν εἴη·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ πολὺ μεῖζον κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλω_γὼ_478">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 472"> <title>El.</title> 472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ )γώ</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴθ_480">, in entreaty; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 750">cp. 750</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1468"> <title>O. T.</title> 1468</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμέρας_μιᾶς_480">. The distance from Lemnos to Scyros is about 75 miles; and, acc. to v. 354, the voyage from Scyros to Sigeum (about 125 miles) took less than two whole days. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμησον_481">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 82">cp. 82</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπῃ_481">, L's reading, is here not less good than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅποι_481">: it goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμβαλοῦ_481"> only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγων_481"> is added as in 488, <title>O.C.</title> 910, 1342: here it expresses how passive he is content to be in the hands of Neoptolemus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντλίαν_482">, the hold of the ship, where he could be stowed away beneath the rowers. Cp. Athen. p. 37 D <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>κατα‐</emph> βαλὼν ἐμαυτὸν ὑπὸ τοὺς θαλάμους</quote> (the places of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλαμῖται</quote>, or lowest rank of rowers) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔνι μάλιστα κατωτάτω ἐκείμην</quote>. Dionysius comicus (350 B.C.) <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεσμοφόρος</quote> fr. 1. 40 describes a seaman as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀντλίας ἥκοντα</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the man was one of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλαμῖται</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 118">Her. 8. 118</ref>: Xerxes is making a long voyage in stormy weather; but he and the numerous Persian nobles with him are all on the deck (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τοῦ καταστρώματος</quote>), while only the Phoenician sailors occupy the part below (<quote xml:lang="grc">κοίλην νέα</quote>). So, too, in [Dem. ] or. 32 § 5, during a voyage of many days, all the passengers live on deck, the <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίλη ναῦς</quote> being used by the rowers only.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῷραν_πρύμνην_482">. Lucian (<title>Navig.</title> 5), speaking of a large vessel, mentions <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ κατὰ πρύμναν οἰκήσεις</quote>, but ordinarily only the <quote xml:lang="grc"> κυβερνήτης</quote> would be located at the stern, as the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῳρεύς</quote> at the prow. <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρύμνα</quote> was the later Attic form: but <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρύμνη</quote> is used by Attic poets for metre's sake, as <title>Vesp.</title> 399 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤν πως πρύμνην ἀνακρούσηται</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1451">Cp. 1451</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_482"> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπῃ</quote>) is necessary: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅποι_482"> could not stand either for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅποι βεβλημένος</quote>, or for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε ὅπου</quote>. The corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υ_482"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_482"> is one of the commonest.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλω_ἀλγυνεῖν_482">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνῶ</quote> (the relative clause, with the fut. indic., expressing purpose): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 409">cp. 409</ref>. For the fut. inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 967"> <title>O. T.</title> 967</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκεσίου_484">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1181">cp. 1181</ref>: <title>Suppl.</title> 616 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς ἱκεσίου κότον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαν προφωνῶν</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.213"> <title>Od.</title> 13. 213</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς σφείας τίσαιθ’ ἱκετήσιος</quote> (see <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 54).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόνασι_484">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 293">Eur. <title> Phoen.</title> 293</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γονυπετεῖς ἕδρας προσπίτνω σ’</quote>: and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 2.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 2.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκράτωρ_486">. As <ref n="Soph. OC 1236"> <title>O. C.</title> 1236</ref> is the only extant Attic example of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρατής</quote> as =‘weak,’ so is this the only example of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκράτωρ</quote> in that sense. Plato uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκράτωρ</quote> in the regular Attic sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρατής</quote> as =<emph>impotens sui</emph> ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 579"> <title>Rep.</title> 579</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἑαυτοῦ...ἀκράτωρ</quote>). The scholium here (if it be not rather a fusion of two distinct scholia) recognises both meanings: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσθενὴς, ἑαυτοῦ κρατεῖν μὴ δυνάμενος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_σὸν_488"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> He asks N. to convey him, either merely to the youth's own home (Scyros), or, better still, a little further, viz., to Euboea (cp. n. on 240); whence it will be easy to reach Malis (492).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_σταθμά_488">, the Euboean abode of Chalcodon, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Euboea, his realm. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1191"> <title>Tr.</title> 1191</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Οἴτης Ζηνὸς ὕψιστον πάγον</quote>. In <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.536"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 536</ref> ff. Elephenor, son of Chalcodon, figures as the leader of all the Euboeans in the Greek army, who are called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄβαντες</quote>, and represent six towns, including Carystus at the extreme south of the island, Chalcis at the middle point of its west coast, and Histiaea in the extreme north.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_Οἴτην_490">. The three names here—Oeta—Trachis—the Spercheius— mark the great features of the region. Typhrestus, at the southern end of Pindus, throws off two ranges towards the eastern sea. One runs nearly due east, and skirts the s. borders of Thessaly: this is Othrys, the lofty ‘brow’ which looks down from the north on the plain of Malis. The other—<title>Oeta</title>, the ‘sheep-land’ —runs s. of Othrys, and parallel with it at first; then, turning S. and E. , it throws out cliffs which enclose the plain of Malis on S. and W. <title>Trachis</title>—‘the rugged’— stood below those cliffs; they themselves were called ‘the Trachinian Rocks.’ ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 198">Her. 7. 198</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρεα ὑψηλὰ καὶ ἄβατα περικλῄει πᾶσαν τὴν Μηλίδα γῆν, Τρηχίνιαι πέτραι καλεόμεναι</quote>.) The <title>Spercheius</title>— ‘the vehement’—rises at the base of Typhrestus. As it runs eastward, its broad valley separates the ranges of Othrys and Oeta. It passes through the plain of Malis, and enters the Malian Gulf. Its old mouth was about five miles N. of Trachis: the present mouths are more to the south. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Τραχινίαν_δειράδα_491">, the chain of heights which bounds the plain of Malis on S. and W.,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρηχίνιαι πέτραι</quote> of Herod. (see last n.), the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔρεα Μηλίδος αἴης</quote> of Callimachus (<ref n="Call. Del. 287"> <title>Hymn. Del.</title> 287</ref>). Acc. to <ref n="Thuc. 3. 92">Thuc. 3. 92</ref> the dwellers in Malis were classed as <quote xml:lang="grc">Τραχίνιοι</quote> (highlanders, like the Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑπεράκριοι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">Παράλιοι</quote> (by the Malian Gulf), and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἱερῆς</quote> (a doubtful name).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειράδα_τὸν_491">, making an anapaest in the 4th place. Toup proposed </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεράδα_491">. As <quote xml:lang="grc">δέρη</quote> was the Attic form of <quote xml:lang="grc">δειρή</quote>, an Attic poet might possibly have ventured on <quote xml:lang="grc">δεράς</quote>. But there is no trace of such a form, while <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράς</quote> is frequent. Further, <quote xml:lang="grc">Δειράδες</quote> was the name of an Attic deme of the Leontis tribe (Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 240, 26), and <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράς</quote> would thus be familiar to Attic ears in ordinary life. Thus Toup's remedy, though attractive by its simplicity, is really a very bold one.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειράδα_491"> is sound, and that the corruption lies in the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶτὸν_491">. I conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">Τραχινίαν τε <emph>δειράδ’ ἠδ’ ἐς</emph> εὔροον</quote>. Soph. , like Aesch. and Eur. , admitted <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδέ</quote> in iambics (see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 673">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 673</ref>). The corruption might arise from the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">Δ</quote> was the second letter of two successive syllables. A scribe, copying <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΕΙΡΑΔΕΔ</quote> (or, after 403 B.C., <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΕΙΡΑΔΗΔ</quote>), might accidentally omit <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΔ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΔ</quote>). The verse would then stand, <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΡΑΧΙΝΙΑΝΤΕΔΕΙΠΑΔΕΣΕΥΡΟΟΝ</quote>. A subsequent transcriber might easily suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc"> ΔΕΙΡΑΔΕΣ</quote> (taken for <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράδες</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράδ’ ἐς</quote>) was a mere blunder for <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράδα</quote>. And, <quote xml:lang="grc">ΔΕΙΠΑΔΑ</quote> having been replaced, the copula would next be supplied, and the verse patched up, by inserting <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΑΙΤΟΝ</quote>.—For other conjectures, see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔροον_491">. Tragic iambics sometimes admit uncontracted forms in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐οος</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> Aesch. fr. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc"> διπλόοι</quote>: id. fr. 275 <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμάρροον</quote>: id. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 493">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 493</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρπνόον</quote>: on the other hand, id. fr. 293 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑπτάρους</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 852">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 852</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλατύρρους</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 917 <quote xml:lang="grc">πύρπνουν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_493"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ οὗ</quote> )...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδοικ_493">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 297"> <title>Tr.</title> 297</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβεῖν τὸν εὖ πράσσοντα, μὴ σφαλῇ ποτε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιὸν_493">=<quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιόν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστιν</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ὅτου_493">, a parenthetic clause equiv. to a simple adverb (<quote xml:lang="grc"> πάλαι</quote>) going with <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Isoc. 5.47">Isocr. or. 5 § 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖτοι γὰρ ἄρχοντες τῶν Ἑλλήνων οὐ πολὺς χρόνος</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὖ καὶ κατὰ γῆν καὶ κατὰ θάλατταν εἰς τοσαύτην μεταβολὴν ἦλθον.</quote> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 600"> <title>Ai.</title> 600</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ὁ τλάμων παλαιὸς ἀφ’ οὗ χρόνος</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνῶμαι</quote>.—In L <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιὰν</quote> is manifestly a mere blunder for <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιὸν</quote>. Those who read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαἴἂν_493"> explain it in one of two ways. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη</quote> is to be supplied with it, —<quote xml:lang="grc">βεβήκῃ</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβηκε</quote>, being read in 494. Such an ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη</quote> is impossible. (2) The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote> is to go with <quote xml:lang="grc"> βεβήκοι</quote> in 494. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 630"> <title>Tr.</title> 630</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα γὰρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ πρὼ| λέγοις ἄν</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 93">Thuc. 2. 93</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσδοκία οὐδεμία</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἄν ποτε οἱ πολέμιοι...ἐπιπλεύσειαν</quote>. But in this constr. the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> which belongs to the optative verb could not precede the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>. In <ref n="Eur. Med. 941">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 941</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδ’ ἂν εἰ πείσαιμι</quote>, the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> has a special excuse, viz., the analogy of sentences with the <emph>inf.</emph> (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν οἶμαι πεῖσαι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_βεβήκῃ_494">. <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> is ethic dat. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 50"> <title>Ant.</title> 50</ref> n.) The subjunct. is right here: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 30">cp. 30</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῇ</quote>, n. The indic. <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβηκε</quote> would also be correct ( <ref n="Dem. 19.96">Dem. or. 19 § 96</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα μὴ λελήθαμεν</quote>), but would express conviction rather than anxious fear.—For <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβηκα</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc"> οἴχομαι</quote>, of death, cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 1026">Eur. <title> Andr.</title> 1026</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβακε δ’ Ἀτρείδας ἀλόχου παλάμαις.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἱγμένοις_494">, instr. dat.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστελλον_494">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 60">cp. 60</ref> n.—The partic. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱγμένος</quote> occurs only here. In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 229"> <title>Tr.</title> 229</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵγμεθα</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόστολον_πέμψαντα_496">, having sent with his own <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλος</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having sent a ship of his own. Cp. <title>Anthol.</title> 7. 585 (on a fisherman who died by the burning of his boat at sea), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόστολος ἦλθεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς Ἀΐδην, νεκύων πορθμίδος οὐ χατέων</quote>, ‘he went to Hades <emph>in his own ship</emph>’ [because it perished along with him],—not needing to use Charon 's bark. Musaeus <title>Leandr.</title> 255 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς ἐὼν ἐρέτης, αὐτόστολος, αὐτόματος νηῦς</quote>, where, similarly, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόστολος</quote> =‘providing his own <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλος</quote>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> ‘selfwafted,’—answering to the word <emph>vector</emph> in Ovid's parallel v., <title>Idem navigium, navita, vector ero</title> (<title>Ep.</title> 18. 147).—If </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόστολον_496"> were understood as=‘setting forth in person’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">μονόστολος, ὁμόστολος</quote>), then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμψαντα_496"> would be best taken as ‘having escorted me’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 913">cp. 913</ref>, 1465), and would go closely with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκσῶσαι_496">. Nauck, interpreting <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόστολον</quote> in this second way, substitutes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεύσαντα_496"> (the conj. of Blaydes) for <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψαντα</quote>. This would certainly make the v. easier; but it is not necessary.</gloss>
