<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμους_496"> is a clearly true correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_496">. The latter could not mean, ‘to my home,’ but only, ‘for the joy of the house’ (dat. of interest). On the other hand cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 810"> <title>Ant.</title> 810</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά μ’ Ἅιδας...ἄγει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν Ἀχέροντος ἀκτάν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 1769"> <title>O. C.</title> 1769</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβας δ’ ἡμᾶς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψον</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_διακόνων_496"> we might have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠμελεῖτο</quote> or the like (‘the messengers' part was neglected’), but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποιούμενοι_496"> follows, as if he had written <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ διάκονοι</quote>. This is one of the irregularities which often arise from a change in the form of the writer's thought; it is not merely a case of constr. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σύνεσιν</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ...μειράκια...διαλεγόμενοι</quote>, Plat. <title> Lach.</title>p. 180 E). Hence it is no objection to this view that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν διακόνων</quote>, <emph>for</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ διάκονοι</quote>, would be unexampled. —Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν διακόνων</quote> as an adverbial parenthesis: ‘or (as is the way with messengers) they forgot me,’ etc. The objection to this is that, in such phrases, the sing. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_496"> is used, never the plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_496">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 77D"> <title>Phaed.</title> 77</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖς σύ τε καὶ Σιμμίας...δεδιέναι, τὸ τῶν παίδων, μὴ...ὁ ἄνεμος αὐτὴν...διαφυσᾷ</quote>: id. <title>Soph.</title> 261B <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ που, τὸ κατὰ τὴν παροιμίαν λεγόμενον, ὅ γε τοιοῦτος ἄν ποτε ἕλοι πόλιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_εἰκός_496"> expresses that such neglect might have been expected, while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶμαι_496"> conveys the belief that it was actually committed; tautology cannot be pleaded, then, as a ground for conjecturing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_496">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_σμικρῷ_496">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 875">cp. 875</ref>: <ref n="Hdt. 3. 154">Her. 3. 154</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐλαφρῷ ποιησάμενος</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Tac. Ann. 3. 54">Tac. <title>Ann.</title> 3. 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">in levi habendum</quote> </cit> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέρος_496">. The reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέρει_496"> would be tenable: cp. <ref n="Dem. 2.18">Dem. or. 2 § 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οὐδενὸς εἶναι μέρει</quote>. And it is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν μέρος</quote> is usu. adverbial (<emph>quantum in me est</emph>, or <emph>quantum ad me attinet:</emph> cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1062">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1062</ref> n.). But here <quote xml:lang="grc">μέρος</quote> gives a much finer verse.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤπειγον_496">, trans. (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1451">cp. 1451</ref>). When the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπείγω</quote> seems intrans., it is so because the acc., like <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλον</quote> here (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμον, ὁδόν</quote>) is understood: <ref n="Soph. El. 1435"> <title>El.</title> 1435</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ νοεῖς, ἔπειγε νῦν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πομπόν_ἄγγελον_500">, at once escort and, in thine own person, messenger: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, Neopt., when he brings Ph. home on board his ship, will at the same time bring the earliest tidings of Ph. 's fate. Ph. had asked his former visitors to act merely as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελοι</quote>: and they had failed to do so. Now he has found a man who will be his <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπός</quote>, and, thereby, also his first <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 79">Her. 1. 79</ref> (Cyrus) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάσας...τὸν στρατὸν ἐς τὴν Λυδίην αὐτὸς ἄγγελος Κροίσῳ ἐληλύθεε</quote>, ‘had himself brought the first news,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγελία</quote> had preceded him. See n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1511">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1511</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ θεοὶ κήρυκες</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκω_500">: after vainly appealing to others. The word is tinged with the fig. sense, ‘I have been brought by my fortune to thee,’ etc.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 377">cp. 377</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ ἐνθάδ’ ἥκων</quote>. [Dem. ] or. 45 § 85 <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ μὲν χαίρειν λέγω, οὓς δ’ ὁ πατήρ μοι παρέδωκε βοηθούς, εἰς τούτους ἥκω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὰ_κεῖται_502">, are so ordained (by the gods) as to be full of fear and peril: (for the combination of adj. and adv., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 345">cp. 345</ref>.) The infin. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθεῖν_502"> follows this phrase as it might follow <quote xml:lang="grc">κίνδυνός ἐστιν</quote> or the like ( <ref n="Plat. Crat. 436B">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 436B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σμικρὸς κίνδυνός ἐστιν ἐξαπατηθῆναι</quote>). The general sense is:—‘There is always a danger for men that, after they have been prosperous, they may be unfortunate.’ Not: ‘It is always doubtful <emph>whether</emph> men are to fare well <emph>or</emph> ill,’—like <ref n="Plat. Prot. 313A">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 313A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ᾧ πάντ’ ἐστὶ τὰ σά, ἢ εὖ ἢ κακῶς πράττειν</quote>. Of the two co-ordinated clauses, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθεῖν_θάτερα_502">, the second is that on which the emphasis falls; the first serves for contrast with it:—‘that, <emph>as</emph> they have fared well, <emph>so</emph> they may fare ill.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 616">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 616</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖς μὲν ὄνασις</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνδρῶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖς δ’ ἀπάτα</quote> (‘<emph> though</emph> to many a blessing, <emph>yet</emph> to many a false lure’): <ref n="Soph. OC 1536">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1536</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ μέν, ὀψὲ δέ</quote>, ‘<emph>though</emph> surely, <emph> yet</emph> late.’</gloss>
lemma:
παθεῖν μὲν εὖ,
							παθεῖν δὲ θάτερα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάτερα_502">: <ref n="Dem. 22.12">Dem. or. 22 § 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθὰ ἢ θάτερα, ἵνα μηδὲν εἴπω φλαυρόν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτὸς_ὄντα_504">: 1260: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 619"> <title>Ant.</title> 619</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δείνὁρᾶν_504">, to keep one's eye on dangers looming in the distance, as a steersman watches rocks ahead: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.323"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 323</ref>(the wary charioteer) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰεὶ τέρμ’ ὁρόων</quote>,— keeping his eye always on the <quote xml:lang="grc">καμπτήρ</quote>, as he drives round it. Thus the schol.'s <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐλαβεῖσθαι</quote> is true to the sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_ζῇ_505">, lives prosperously, as <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς ζῆν</quote>=to live unhappily ( <ref n="Soph. El. 354"> <title>El.</title> 354</ref>). So Pind. ( <ref n="Pind. P. 4"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 131</ref>) calls festivity <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐζῴας ἄωτον</quote>. But in <ref n="Soph. OC 1535"> Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1535</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ...οἰκῇ</quote>= ‘lives <emph>aright.</emph>’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_βίον_505">, the fortunes of one's life: the subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάθῃ_505"> is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ βίος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 207"> <title>El.</title> 207</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖρες</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἳ τὸν ἐμὸν εἷλον βίον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόδοτον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_ἆθλα_507">, ordeals consisting in <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνοι</quote>, sufferings. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 505"> <title>Tr.</title> 505</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄεθλ’ ἀγώνων</quote>. The plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλα</quote> can thus be used in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλοι</quote>: but the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλον</quote> does not occur as =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλος</quote>. In <title>Suppl.</title> 1034 <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἆθλον</quote>=‘this prize.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_507">, Porson's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσα</quote>, is probably right. It is the more natural word in such a wish: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 275">cp. 275</ref>, 315. And <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσα</quote> may have been suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν</quote>. An iambic trimeter set in lyrics might, indeed, tolerate <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσος</quote>,—as the corresponding trimeter (392) has the Doric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσα</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 864">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 864</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσσων</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 140">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 140</ref>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσσον</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 185">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 185</ref>, seem to be the only instances of these forms in Tragedy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_507_2"> is clearly better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅσσα_507"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἅτινα</quote>): for which cp. <title>O.T.</title> 425 n.—For the acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα</quote> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύχοι_507">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1298"> <title>O. T.</title> 1298</ref> n. The conject. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάχοι_507"> is unnecessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρούς_510">, odious; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 254">cp. 254</ref> n. This sense seems more suitable here than ‘bitter against thee,’ when <quote xml:lang="grc">πικροὺς...ἔχθεις</quote> would be like <quote xml:lang="grc">μισοῦντ’ ἐμίσει</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1134"> <title>Ai.</title> 1134</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_μὲν_512"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 453">cp. 453</ref> )...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πορεύσαιμ_ἂν_512"> is a respectful suggestion,—‘I, for my part, would convey him,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘If I were you, I would do so.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κακὸν_512">, the evil done by them: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 422">cp. 422</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετατιθέμενος_512">. This compound re<*>ly takes one acc. only, meaning to ‘transpose,’ ‘shift,’ a thing: and hence, either to ‘adopt’ or to ‘discard.’ Here the compound is used like the simple verb, and the force of the prep. is adverbial. <quote xml:lang="grc">τιθέμενος τὸ κείνων κακὸν τῷδε κέρδος</quote>= ‘<emph> counting</emph> their misdeeds <emph>as</emph> his gain’: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 21.333"> <title>Od.</title> 21. 333</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἐλέγχεα ταῦτα τίθεσθε</quote>; If, after the word ‘counting,’ we inserted, ‘by transference,’ this would give the force of <quote xml:lang="grc">μετά</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote> is to be shifted from the reckoning against the Atreidae to the reckoning in favour of Philoctetes. Their demerit is another reason for benefiting him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιμέμονεν_512">: the only instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμονα</quote> in Soph. (Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 686">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 686</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμονας</quote>: <ref n="Eur. IA 1495">Eur. <title> I.A.</title> 1495</ref> and <title>I. T.</title> 655 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμονε</quote>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐστόλου_516">, here prob., ‘well-equipped’; though at v. 780 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσταλής</quote> (the commoner form) = ‘expeditious.’ Cp. Apoll. <ref n="Apollon. 1.603">Rh. 1. 603</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσον ἐς ἔνδιόν κεν ἐΰστολος ὁλκὰς ἀνύσσαι</quote>.—For the double epithet, without copula, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.34">Hom. <title> Od.</title> 7. 34</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νηυσὶ θοῇσι πεποιθότες ὠκείῃσι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 710">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 710</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θοᾶν ὠκυάλων νεῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_νέμεσιν_517">. Hermann's deletion of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν</quote> is necessary, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν θεῶν</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">Λαρτίου</quote> in 401. Possibly the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> arose from a reminiscence of <ref n="Hdt. 1. 34">Her. 1. 34</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλαβε ἐκ θεοῦ νέμεσις μεγάλη Κροῖσον</quote>.— <ref n="Soph. Phil. 601">Cp. 601</ref> f., 1035 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δὲ_519">: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘beware lest, <emph>though</emph> now thou art facile, <emph> yet</emph>,’ etc.: cp. n. on 503.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐχερὴς_519">, easy-going (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 875">cp. 875</ref>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_519"> gives a slightly contemptuous tone; cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 696">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 696</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῴ γε στενάζεις καὶ φόβου πλέα τις εἶς</quote>. For its position, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 29">Soph. <title> Ai.</title> 29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μοί τις ὀπτήρ</quote>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῇς_519">, as a spectator who is not yet required to make any personal sacrifice. Not from <quote xml:lang="grc">παρίημι</quote>, as = ‘comply.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_νόσου_520"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλησθῇςξυνουσίᾳ_520">, causal dat.: sated with (wearied of) the disease, through consorting with it. It is also possible to join the verb with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνουσίᾳ</quote>, and to make the gen. depend on the latter: when the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ</quote> would be an instance like <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐχθρῶν κακά</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10"> <title>Ant.</title> 10</ref> n.). The objection is that, though <quote xml:lang="grc">πλησθῆναι</quote> can take a dat. when it means simply ‘to be filled’ ( <ref n="Thuc. 7. 75">Thuc. 7. 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυσι πᾶν τὸ στράτευμα πλησθέν</quote>), it usu. takes a gen. when it means ‘to be sated.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸς_τούτοις_520">, the same with (=consistent, in your action, with) these words. <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 298A">Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 298A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ σὺ εἶ ὁ αὐτὸς τῷ λίθῳ</quote>; If <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote> were absent, then <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς λόγοις</quote> could be a dat. of respect, ‘the same in regard to your words,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτός εἰμι τῷ βουλεύματι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 557">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 557</ref> n.). But <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote> shows that the other constr. is meant. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ὅπως_522">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 196">cp. 196</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_μέντοι_524">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 567."> <title>Ant.</title> 567.</ref>The fact that <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλὰ</quote> recurs so soon, in v. 526, has caused a corruption to be suspected in the latter place (see crit. n.): but there it has a different tone (‘come, now’). This elasticity of meaning in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> is one reason why classical poetry so readily allows it to be repeated at short intervals (cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> 645, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 647"> Ant. 647</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 651"> Ant. 651</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 238"> <title>O. C.</title> 238</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπεὶ...ἀλλ’ ἐμὲ...ἀλλ’ ἴτε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 137"> <title>El.</title> 137</ref>— 140, <ref n="Soph. El. 879"> El. 879</ref> —882: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 592"> <title>Tr.</title> 592</ref>—594). As to the tolerance of such repetition generally, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 762">cp. 762</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title> 554</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχρὰ_524">: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1395">cp. 1395</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 485"> <title>O. C.</title> 485</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_μ_524"> is better than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_ἔμ_524">: the latter would imply an ungraceful emphasis on the speaker's personal dignity.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_καίριον_524">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 38"> <title>Ai.</title> 38</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ...πρὸς καιρὸν πονῶ</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πονεῖν_524">, epexegetic of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδεέστερον</quote>, ‘in respect of toiling’: cp. <title> O.C.</title> 335 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ αὐθόμαιμοι ποῦ νεανίαι πονεῖν</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμάσθω_526">, let Philoctetes set out with us for the ship at once. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταχύς_526">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ταχέως</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 808">cp. 808</ref>, 1080. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χἡ_ναῦς_526">, the ship, on her part. If the sick man's shipmates make no difficulty, the ship will make none: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it will be easy to find room for him on board (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 481">cp. 481</ref>). Neoptolemus is on his guard against betraying elation. He speaks as if the granting of Ph. 's prayer was now a simple matter, —and one which did not greatly interest him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπαρνηθήσεται_526"> is usu. taken as passive: either (1) ‘the boon shall not be refused’: or (2) ‘he shall not be refused his wish.’ This second version is inadmissible. Clas sical Greek allows <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνοῦμαι δοῦναί τι</quote>, but not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνοῦμαι τὸν αἰτοῦντα</quote>. And with either version the change of subject would be harsh. Rather the verb is deponent, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ναῦς</quote> for subject. Prof. Ridgeway, supporting this view (<title>Trans. Camb. Philol. Soc.</title> I. p. 244), illustrates the personification of the ship from <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.131"> <title>Od.</title> 10. 131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσπασίως δ’ ἐς πόντον ἐπηρεφέας φύγε πέτρας</quote>, and <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 3.1284a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 3. 13.§ 16</ref>, where the ship Argo—endued by legend with a voice—is described as refusing to carry Heracles (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἐθέλειν αὐτὸν ἄγειν τὴν Ἀργώ</quote>).—It is true that the classical fut. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνέομαι</quote>, where it occurs, is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνήσομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 571">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 571</ref>, etc.). But there is no classical instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνηθήσομαι</quote> as fut. pass. And since the aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρνήθην</quote> is always deponent, analogy suggests that a deponent use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνηθήσομαι</quote> would have been possible. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλέγομαι</quote>, aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">διελέχθην</quote> (deponent), fut. <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλεχθήσομαι</quote> (deponent), as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλέξομαι</quote>. In later Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνηθήσομαι</quote> occurs, indeed, as pass. (St Luke xii. 9, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνηθήσεται</quote>, ‘he will be disowned’), but also as deponent (LXX. <ref n="Isaeus 31.7">Is. xxxi. 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνηθήσονται</quote>, with v.l. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνήσονται</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_528">=<emph>modo</emph>, as oft. in wishes or commands ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1109"> <title>Tr.</title> 1109</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσμόλοι μόνον</quote>, etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλοίμεσθα_528">: the optat. in the relative clause, because <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζοιεν</quote> stands in the principal clause: as 961 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιο μήπω πρὶν μάθοιμ’</quote> . <ref n="Soph. Phil. 325">Cp. 325</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. OC 778"> <title>O. C.</title> 778</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_μὲν_530"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for the epanaphora, with change from <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτατος</quote> to a synonym, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 898"> <title>Ant.</title> 898</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη μὲν... προσφιλὴς δὲ...φίλη δὲ</quote>, n. For the nom. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδιστος ἀνήρ</quote> after the voc., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 867">cp. 867</ref>, 986.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_γενοίμην_530">, a wish; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 794">cp. 794</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1457"> <title>O. C.</title> 1457</ref>: so <emph>ib.</emph> 1100 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἂν...δοίη</quote>...; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴωμεν_533"> clearly means, ‘let us be going’ (from Lemnos). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 645">Cp. 645</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρῶμεν</quote>. It expresses his joyful impatience to avail himself of N.'s offer here, and naturally follows the preceding verses. If, on the other hand, we take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴωμεν_533_2"> to mean, ‘let us go into the cave,’ we shall have no direct expression of Ph. 's eagerness to leave Lemnos: and the invitation to enter the cave will come with an awkward abruptness after the first words of gratitude. But if </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴωμεν_533_3"> means, ‘let us be going from Lemnos,’ then we must accept </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσοίκησιν_533">, unless we can substitute for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκύσαντε_533"> some partic. which could go with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_οἴκησιν_533">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκύσαντε εἰς οἴκησιν</quote> could not mean, ‘having gone into the dwelling to salute it.’ I once suggested <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε προσκύψαντ’ ἔσω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄοικον εἰς οἴκησιν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘after one look’ into it; but I now doubt whether the classical usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκύπτω</quote> would bear this. We may rather believe that Soph. hazarded the otherwise unknown word <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσοίκησις</quote>, much as in <ref n="Soph. OC 27"> Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 27</ref> he ventured on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξοικήσιμος</quote>. It implies a verb <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσοικέω</quote> (nowhere found, except as a v.l. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνοικέω</quote> in <title>Anthol.</title> 7. 320), capable of being used thus,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντρον εἰσῴκησε</quote>, ‘he entered the cave and made his dwelling there’=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντρον εἰσελθὼν ᾤκησε</quote>. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσοίκησις</quote> would be properly, the act of so making a dwelling, or the dwelling made. (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσοικίζω</quote>, to bring in as a settler, is irrelevant.) See Appendix.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκύσαντε_533_2">, a farewell salutation (as by kissing the soil), because the cave had so long given him shelter: see below on 1408.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_καὶ_533">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 13">cp. 13</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφ_ὧν_535">: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 216">Her. 1. 216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ κτηνέων. ζώουσι καὶ ἰχθύων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διέζων_535">, sustained life (under difficulties), as <ref n="Hdt. 3. 25">Her. 3. 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιηφαγέοντες διέζωον</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">διατρέφομαι, διαγίγνομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶμαι_γὰρ_536"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for I think that even the bare sight would have deterred any one but myself from enduring these things: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ἄλλος ἂν ἔτλη τάδε, εἰ θέαν μόνην ἔλαβε</quote>. The first glance at such a dwelling would have made any other man renounce the attempt to live in it. Instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καὶ</emph> ὄμμασιν μόνην θέαν λαβόντα, <emph>οὐκ</emph> ἂν τλῆναι</quote>, we have <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐδ’</emph> ὄμμασιν...τλῆναι,—οὐδ’</quote> thus serving to weld the sentence into a more compact whole.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνην_536"> need not be changed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_536">, though the latter would be more usual: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 388"> <title>O. T.</title> 388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖς κέρδεσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον δέδορκε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 361"> <title>Ant.</title> 361</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδα μόνον φεῦξιν οὐκ ἐπάξεται</quote>.—Some govern </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_536"> by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέαν_λαβόντα_536"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc"> θεασάμενον</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 223"> <title>O. C.</title> 223</ref> n.), and take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλῆναι_536"> with the partic.: ‘endure to have looked upon.’ This is forced. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τλῆναι</quote> with simple acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 71"> <title>Tr.</title> 71</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1077"> <title>O. C.</title> 1077</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔμαθον_538">, by painful steps (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρό</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1015">cp. 1015</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προὐδίδαξεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσχετον_539"> is said to N. and Ph. , who are moving towards the cave. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθωμεν_539">, absol., let us learn,—viz., what tidings the new comers are bringing. The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">μένωμεν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">μείνωμεν</quote>) would merely repeat the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσχετον</quote>.—This hortative subjunct. occurs even in the 1st pers. sing., as <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 567">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title> 567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσχετ’, αὐδὴν τῶν ἔσωθεν ἐκμάθω</quote>: id. <title>H.F.</title> 1058 <quote xml:lang="grc">σῖγα, πνοὰς μάθω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλόθρους_539">, prop., speaking a foreign tongue: here, simply = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλότριος</quote>, just as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 844"> <title>Tr.</title> 844</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλόθρου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας</quote>=merely <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλοτρίας γνώμης</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_μαθόντες_539">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having learned (their news) from them: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 370">cp. 370</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_539">=‘at a later moment,’ as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1283."> <title>Ai.</title> 1283.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴσιτον_539"> (imperat., not indic.): for the dual, after <quote xml:lang="grc"> μαθόντες</quote>, cp. <title>Laches</title> p. 187 A <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ εὑρεταὶ γεγονότε</quote>: and n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 343."> <title>O. C.</title> 343.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνέμπορον_539">, fellow-traveller, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 318"> <title>Tr.</title> 318</ref>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσαι_ποῦ_544"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for the constr., cp. n. on 443 f., <emph>ad fin.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρῶν_εἴης_544">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1285"> <title>O. T.</title> 1285</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν ἐστ’ ἀπόν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοξάζων_οὔ_545">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 255"> <title>Ant.</title> 255</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβήρης μὲν οὔ</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμισθεὶς_545">: the same constr. with the pass. in <title>H.</title> 1. 4 § 18, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὴν γῆν ὁρμισθείς</quote>,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμίσας τὴν ναῦν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμισάμενος</quote>, having brought one's ship to anchor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταὐτὸν_πέδον_545">, the same land (Lemnos); not, strictly, the same ‘spot.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_στόλῳ_547">, with no large company (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> with one ship, and only a small crew to handle it): as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 496"> <title>Tr.</title> 496</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν πολλῷ στόλῳ</quote>=‘with a numerous train.’ If <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλῳ</quote> were taken as ‘fleet,’ the phrase could hardly be, a mere equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">μιᾷ νηί</quote>, but would suggest at least a plurality of vessels. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πεπάρηθον_549"> (now called <quote xml:lang="grc">Σκόπελος</quote>), a small island near the Thessalian coast, about 12 miles E. of the south end of Magnesia. The island of Sciathus lies between it and the mainland; Euboea is only 20 miles distant to the S.W., and Scyros about 40 to the S. The E. name is well-chosen, then, to make Philoctetes feel that he is listening to a neighbour of his old home. Peparethus, though not more than some 12 miles in length, with a greatest width of 5 or 6, contained three towns. Its famous wine is ranked by Aristophanes with those of Pramnus, Chios and Thasos (fr. 301). The author of [Dem. ] or. 35 § 35 names Peparethus, along with Cos, Thasos and Mendè, as a seat of the wine-trade with the Euxine. An Alexandrian physician, Apollodorus, recommended the wine of Peparethus before all others, adding that its repute would be still higher, did it not require six years to attain perfection ( <title>H. N.</title> 14. 9). The epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔβοτρυν_549"> here is peculiarly fitting, since Pliny speaks of the island as <emph>quondam Evoenum dictam</emph> (<emph> ib.</emph> 4. 23). And so Heracleides Ponticus fr. 13 says of it, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔτη ἡ νῆσος εὔοινός ἐστι καὶ εὔδενδρος</quote>. It also produced good olives (<ref n="Ov. Met. 7. 470">Ov. <title>Met.</title> 7. 470</ref>).—In the <title>Iliad</title> the Greeks at Troy import wine from Lemnos (7. 467) and from Thrace (9. 72).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤκουσα_ὅτι_549">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1141"> <title>Ai.</title> 1141</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἀντακούσει τοῦτον ὡς τεθάψεται</quote>: <title>M.</title> 4. 2. 23 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Δαίδαλον οὐκ ἀκήκοας, ὅτι ἠναγκάζετο δουλεύειν</quote> ;— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντες_549">: and therefore he could not have been anticipated in bringing the news.— Dobree's conjecture, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συννεναυστοληκότες_549">, has been generally accepted by recent edd. If the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱνεναυστοληκότες_549"> is retained, then </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_549"> is possess. pron.: ‘that all those who had made the voyage were thy men.’ The objection to this is the want of point in the participle. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔδοξέ_μοι_551"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The constr. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστυχόντι_551"> is made somewhat awkward by the negative before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖσθαι_551">. ‘I decided to sail, not in silence, or before I had told thee, (but only when, having told thee,) I had received a due reward.’ It would have been clearer to have written either: (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξέ μοι φράσαντι τὸν πλοῦν ποεῖσθαι, προστυχόντι τῶν ἴσων</quote>: or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξέ μοι μὴ σῖγα τὸν πλοῦν ποεῖσθαι, πρὶν φράσαιμι καὶ προστύχοιμι τῶν ἴσων</quote>. The justification of the actual form is that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_φράσαιμι_551">, is felt as a more emphatic equivalent for a simple <quote xml:lang="grc">φράσαντι</quote>. For the dat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστυχόντι_551_2"> (instead of an acc.) with the inf., cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 2. 1">Xen. <title>An.</title> 2. 1</ref>§ 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξεν οὖν αὐτοῖς συσκευασαμένοις...προϊέναι</quote>. The acc. is, however, more usual, as <emph> ib.</emph> 3. 2. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξεν αὐτοῖς προφυλακὰς καταστήσαντας συγκαλεῖν τοὺς στρατιώτας</quote>, since it excludes a possible ambiguity: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 838">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 838</ref> n.— The use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστυχόντι_551_3"> (‘having obtained, met with’) is like that in <ref n="Soph. El. 1463"> Soph. <title>El.</title> 1463</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ κολαστοῦ προστυχών.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἴσων_551">: by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἴσα</quote> is meant a reasonable recompense for his trouble. This sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσος</quote> (<emph>aequus</emph>) is virtually the same as in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τοῖς ἴσοις καὶ ὁμοίοις</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 5. 79">Thuc. 5. 79</ref>), etc. Similarly the messengers in <ref n="Soph. OT 1005">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1005</ref> and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 190">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 190</ref> expressly say that they have come in the hope of being rewarded.—Others join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστυχόντι_551_4"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_551">, ‘when thou shouldst have received (the information) due.’ Nauck understands, ‘since I have met with the same fortune as thine’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, have put in at the same coast. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1168">Soph. <title>El.</title> 1168</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν σοὶ μετεῖχον τῶν ἴσων</quote>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_τοῖσιν_554"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The antecedent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote> is not <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν σαυτοῦ</quote> in 553: rather the relative clause is epexegetic. ‘Thou knowest nothing of thine own affairs, — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, of those new counsels (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ τούτων</quote>.) which,’ etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vέα_554">, in addition to the former wrong (60). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκέτ_ἐξαργούμενα_556">, deeds which are no longer allowed to remain <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργά</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in which the doers are not slack. After the <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύματα</quote> had become <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote>, by the taking of the first steps, the action might still have been sluggish. But these </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργα_556"> are </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρώμενα_556">,—advancing towards completion. So <ref n="Plut. De Lib. 2e">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 2</ref>E <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ... ἐξαργηθεῖσα</quote>, land which has been allowed to lie fallow. Arist. uses the pf. act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηργηκέναι</quote> as=‘to have become torpid’ (<title>Eth. N.</title> 1. 8: <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 5.1312a"> <title>Pol.</title> 5. 10</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 287"> <title>O. T.</title> 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐν ἀργοῖς οὐδὲ τοῦτ’ ἐπραξάμην</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_557">, ‘well’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 232">cp. 232</ref>, 336.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_προμηθίας_557">, the favour of (conferred by) thy forethought; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 764"> <title>O. T.</title> 764</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν...χάριν</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1217"> <title>Tr.</title> 1217</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσνειμαι δέ μοι | χάριν βραχεῖαν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προμηθίας_557">, the poet. form (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 943"> <title>Ant.</title> 943</ref> n.); for the sense, (‘kind thought for one,’) cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 332"> <title>O. C.</title> 332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῇ, πάτερ, προμηθίᾳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_πέφυκα_557">: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 1. 21">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5. 1. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν τούτων ἐγὼ ὑμῖν ἔχω μέν, εἰ μὴ ἀδικῶ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφιλὴς_557">, <emph>grata</emph>, well-pleasing,—gratefully remembered. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 580">Aesch. <title> Theb.</title> 580</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ τοῖσιν ἔργον καὶ θεοῖσι προσφιλές</quote>. The difficulty felt as to <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσφιλής</quote> (see crit. n.) has arisen from the assumption that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάρις_557"> here=‘gratitude.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπερ_γ_559">. It is not surprising that <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> should have been suspected here, since L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπερ ἔλεξας</quote>. But the emendations which have been suggested (see cr. n.) are improbable. If any were to be made, I should rather suggest </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπερ_κἄλεξας_559">. L seems, however, to have lost <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> in some other places (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 105">cp. 105</ref> n.): and here the particle appears defensible, if regard is had to the tone of the passage. Neoptolemus, mindful of his part, receives the (supposed) stranger's announcement with politeness, but without manifesting much concern. ‘I am really very much obliged to you for the trouble which you have taken. But perhaps you would kindly say what, precisely, it is to which you allude.’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπερ γ’</quote>=‘just those things which,’—the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_559"> merely adding a slight emphasis to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπερ_559">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεώτερον_560">, not simply <quote xml:lang="grc"> νέον</quote> (554), but ‘startling,’—ominous of some new wrong: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 51">Thuc. 4. 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν περὶ σφᾶς νεώτερον βουλεύσειν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_Ἀργείων_560"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούλευμα_560">, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις</quote>,—a plot on their part: for this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 293."> <title>O. C.</title> 293.</ref>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_560"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_560">, ‘hast for me,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, announcest to me. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 9"> <title>Ant.</title> 9</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοῖνιξ_562">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 344">cp. 344</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_κόροι_562">: Demophon,—the ruler of Athens who figures in <title>Heracleidae</title>,—and his brother Acamas, who in the same play is a mute person at his side (v. 119). These <quote xml:lang="grc">Θησείδα, ὄζω Ἀθηνῶν</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hec. 125">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 125</ref>), are plausibly represented as foes of Neoptolemus, since their father Theseus was treacherously slain in Scyros by Lycomedes ( <ref n="Paus. 1. 17. 6">Paus. 1. 17. 6</ref>). Arctinus of Miletus (<emph> c.</emph> 776 B.C.), the author of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰλίου Πέρσις</quote>, made Neoptolemus the hero of his epic, and introduced the two sons of Theseus in the episode of the wooden horse. On the Acropolis of Athens Pausanias saw the <quote xml:lang="grc">δούρειος ἵππος</quote> commemorated in bronze. ‘Menestheus and Teucer,’ he adds, ‘are peeping out of it, —and the sons of Theseus’ (1. 23. 8).— These Theseidae do not appear in <title>Il.</title>or <title>Od.</title>; nor does their father, except where Nestor speaks of having known him ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.265"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 265</ref>), and in a doubtful verse of the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυια</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.631"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 631</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_βίας_563">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 945">cp. 945</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_563"> is changed by Nauck to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόλοις_563">, because the antithesis between force and persuasion is not suitable here; ‘since Neoptolemus must assume a hostile intention in the <quote xml:lang="grc">διώκοντες</quote>.’ But why should he not suppose that the Atreidae, finding him indispensable, wish to entice him back by smooth <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote>? (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 629">Cp. 629</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισι μαλθακοῖς</quote>.) In v. 102 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἐν δόλῳ δεῖ μᾶλλον ἢ πείσαντ’ ἄγειν</quote>; the antithesis is between a false story and persuasion by honest argument. But <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> (whether true or false), as a means of prevailing, can also be contrasted with force, as in 593 f., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ λόγῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πείσαντες ἄξειν, ἢ πρὸς ἰσχύος κράτος</quote>. And that is the antithesis meant here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθ_ὁρμὴν_566">, impetuously, like <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σπουδὴν</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1. 93">Thuc. 1. 93</ref>): cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ μιᾶς ὁρμῆς</quote> (id. 7. 71). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_δρώμεν_567">. Where <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> occurs in such phrases with an imperative, it regularly belongs to the partic.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 253">cp. 253</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι μ’</quote> (n.). But here <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ταῦτα...δρώμενα</quote> could not strictly stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δρώμενα...ταῦτα</quote>. The suspicions which the text has excited are, so far, natural. Yet I think that it is sound. The irregularity seems to have arisen from the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>, prefixed to an assurance, could either (<emph> a</emph>) belong to a partic. (as in 253, 415, etc.), or (<emph>b</emph>) introduce the whole sentence, as 117 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τοῦτό γ’ ἔρξας δύο φέρει δωρήματα</quote>. Thus the Attic ear had become accustomed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> as <emph>the first word</emph> of such an assurance in either type. And so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> could be given that place in a sentence of type (<emph>a</emph>), even though the partic, did not immediately follow. That is, I do not suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ταῦτ’ ἐπίστω δρώμενα</quote> is <emph>for</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δρώμεν’ ἐπίστω ταῦτα</quote>: but rather that, instead of saying simply <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’ ἐπίστω δρώμενα</quote>, he can prefix <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>, because the associations of type (<emph>b</emph>) had blunted the feeling for what was essential in type (<emph>a</emph>),—viz., that the partic. (or partic. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>) should immediately follow <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_τάδ_568">. After <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>, it is slightly better to take these words as= ‘for this purpose’ ( <ref n="Soph. OT 766"> <title>O. T.</title> 766</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τί</quote>;), rather than as=‘in view of these facts’ (= <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ταῦτα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 426"> <title>O. T.</title> 426</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτάγγελος_568">, carrying his own message: <ref n="Soph. OC 333."> <title>O. C.</title> 333.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνός_γ_570">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> throws a slight stress on the pron., ‘oh, he’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 424">cp. 424</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_παῖς_570">, Diomedes, who, in the <title> Philoctetes</title> of Eur. , accompanied Odysseus to Lemnos (see Introd.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστελλον_570">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐστέλλοντο</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 640">cp. 640</ref>: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 147">Her. 4. 147</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστελλε ἐς ἀποικίην</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἔπλει_572">; ‘who was this other person in quest of whom Odysseus himself was sailing?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_572"> is oft. thus used after interrogatives: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 7"> <title>Ant.</title> 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί τοῦτ’ αὖ φασὶ πανδήμῳ πόλει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρυγμα θεῖναι</quote>...; (For <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ποῖον...τόνδε</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος ἦν ὅδε, πρὸς ὅν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 441">cp. 441</ref>.) Not ‘was sailing again’ (with ref. to his former voyage to Scyros, 343). If </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_572_2"> is a true correction here (as it has been deemed by almost all recent edd.), the corruption </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_572"> in the MSS. is the reverse of that which has probably occurred in <ref n="Soph. OC 1418"> <title>O. C.</title> 1418</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_572_2"> is kept, it must be explained in one of two ways. (1) Taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπλει</quote>: ‘who is this, for whom <emph>he would have been sailing?</emph>’ (=‘presumably sailed’). Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.546"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ κεν Ὀρέστης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κτεῖνεν</quote>, ‘or Orestes would have slain him,’=‘or, it may be, O. slew him.’ (2) Taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον τόνδε</quote>, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote> were understood: ‘Who might this man be, for whom he sailed?’ On this view, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> does not affect <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπλει</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ποῖον ἂν τόνδε</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος ὅδε ἂν εἴη, πρὸς ὃν ἔπλει</quote>. This is possible: though here <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ποῖον ἂν τόνδε</quote> would more naturally suggest <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος ὅδε ἂν ἦν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_575">, ethic dat.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 261">cp. 261</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πλείον_576">, the further details which N. might naturally wish to learn: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 36"> <title>O. C.</title> 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν νυν τὰ πλείον’ ἱστορεῖν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεαυτὸν_ξυλλαβὼν_576">, a phrase of colloquial tone (cp. Shaksp., ‘be packing’): <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1469">Ar. <title> Av.</title> 1469</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπίωμεν ἡμεῖς συλλαβόντες τὰ πτερά</quote>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 971">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 971</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 444">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ μὲν κομίζοις ἂν σεαυτὸν ᾖ θέλεις</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_με_578">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διεμπολᾷ_σε_578">; what bargain is he making with thee concerning me? From the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε</quote> in 573 onwards, the pretended <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπορος</quote> has spoken to N. in lower tones; while N. has taken care to pronounce v. 575 loud enough for Ph. to hear. The object of this by-play is to quicken Ph. 's interest in the coming story (603 ff.), and his anxiety to leave Lemnos. Seyffert's change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_με_578_2"> into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δὲ_578"> is no improvement. It is natural that Ph. , the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ ὑπόπτης</quote> (136), should suspect some design against himself. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπορος</quote> had suddenly assumed an air of mystery; and, on learning Ph. 's name, had urged N. to save <emph>himself</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σεαυτὸν συλλαβών</quote>). The <quote xml:lang="grc">διά</quote> in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διεμπολᾷ_578"> expresses traffic: cp. fr. 521. 7 (a woman bewailing the lot of her sex), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠθούμεθ’ ἔξω καὶ διεμπολώμεθα</quote> (as by a bargain between suitor and parents). —<ref n="Soph. Phil. 978">Cp. 978</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1036."> <title>Ant.</title> 1036.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_φῶς_581">, opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σκότον</quote> (578): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1353">cp. 1353</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 639"> <title>El.</title> 639</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ πᾶν ἀναπτύξαι πρέπει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς φῶς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1229"> <title>O. T.</title> 1229</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ φῶς φανέι</quote>: fr. 832 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐκκαλύπτων ὁ χρόνος εἰς τὸ φῶς ἄγει</quote>. Yet Nauck has ejected <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς φῶς</quote> from the text, and conjecturally substituted <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφῶς</quote>. He wishes also to replace <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξει</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῄζει</quote>. But for the fut. cp. <title>O.C.</title> 114 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἂν ἐκμάθω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνας λόγους ἐροῦσιν</quote>. So here <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξει</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλει λέξειν</quote>.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_τε_581"> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 421">cp. 421</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπέρμ_582">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 364">cp. 364</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατῷ_582">: the dat. as <ref n="Eur. Hec. 863">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 863</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαιοῖς εἰ διαβληθήσομαι</quote>, etc. In prose usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός τινα</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴς τινα</quote>: also <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά τινι</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τισι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_δεῖ_582">, <emph>quae non oporteat</emph> (generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶν_ἀντιπάσχω_582">: the emphasis is here rather on the verb than on the partic.: ‘I receive many benefits from them, in return for my services.’ The schol. has: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ ἐκείνων εὐεργετούμενος ἀντευεργετῶ αὐτούς, ὡς δύναται πένης εὐεργετεῖν, δηλονότι ὑπηρετεῖν</quote>. This makes <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶν</quote> more prominent than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπάσχω</quote>, —evidently because the schol. thought that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_πένης_582"> referred only to <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶν</quote>,—‘so far as a poor man can confer benefits.’ But that clause refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπάσχω</quote> also: the benefits which he received were important for such as he was. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 763"> <title>O. T.</title> 763</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιος...οἷ’ ἀνὴρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦλος</quote> (‘for a slave’), and <emph> ib.</emph> 1118 <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστὸς ὡς νομεὺς ἀνήρ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρηστά_θ_582">: <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλά τε</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ χρηστὰ</quote> is commoner than <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ χρηστά τε</quote> (though cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 338">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 338</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ γάρ, εὖτε πτόλις δαμασθῇ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐή, δυστυχῆ τε πράσσει</quote>): and on the other hand we find <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ...καλά</quote> (fr. 79), <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ...σοφά</quote> (fr. 99), etc. Still, <quote xml:lang="grc">χρηστά <emph>θ’</emph> </quote> seems more probable here than <quote xml:lang="grc">χρηστά <emph>γ’</emph> </quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγώ_εἰμ_585">. This synizesis is extremely rare, though that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ου</quote> is less so ( <ref n="Soph. OT 332"> <title>O. T.</title> 332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ οὔτ’</quote>, n.). Indeed there is no other certain instance in Tragedy; for in <ref n="Soph. El. 1332">Soph. <title>El.</title> 1332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐγὼ εἰς σὸν βλέφαρον πελάσω</quote> ought not to be compared. There <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐγὼ</quote> is a dactyl, by epic hiatus, as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.29"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δ’ ἐγὼ οὐ λύσω</quote>. In Comedy we have <title>Vesp.</title> 1224 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ εἴσομαι</quote>, where Burges reads <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχ’ εἴσομαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλος_μέγιστος_585">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1331"> <title>Ai.</title> 1331</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλον σ’ ἐγὼ μέγιστον Ἀργείων νέμω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_587"> appears slightly preferable to the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγον_587"> here: and either would have been written <quote xml:lang="grc">ΛΟΓΟΝ</quote> in the poet's time. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_587"> is most simply taken as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων οὕς</quote>: though, if <quote xml:lang="grc"> λόγον</quote> were retained, it might also represent (<quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἅ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_589"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Some take this verse as an exchange of veiled hints between the accomplices. But why should the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπορος</quote> fear that N. was likely to trip in his part? Rather it is merely a piece of acting, like the feigned ‘aside’ in 573, and with the same object—viz., to impress Philoctetes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_589">, referring back merely to the moment at which he began to press his question,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to 580: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1161"> <title>O. T.</title> 1161</ref> n.—For the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀντιλαβή</quote>, marking excitement, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 54">cp. 54</ref>, 466. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποιοῦ_590">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τίθου</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 54"> <title>O. T.</title> 54</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἴπερ ἄρξεις τῆσδε γῆς, ὥσπερ κρατεῖς</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_590">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1038"> <title>O. C.</title> 1038</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρῶν ἀπείλει νῦν</quote>, threaten (if you will) —but set out. So here, ‘hold me responsible if thou wilt—but answer.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_591">. So <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1130"> <title>Tr.</title> 1130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω· τέθνηκεν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 245"> <title>Ant.</title> 245</ref>(where the reluctant speaker is at last brought to the point) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὲ λέγω σοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πὶ_τοῦτον_591">. Such aphaeresis after a stop is rare: but cp. <title>I. A.</title> 719 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω· ’πὶ ταύτῃ καὶ καθέσταμεν τύχῃ</quote>: [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 157 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξω· )πὶ τούτοις τόνδ’ ὑφίσταμαι πόνον</quote>: <title> Nub.</title> 1354 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ φράσω. )πειδὴ γὰρ κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτον_591">, this man here, (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε</quote>,) Philoctetes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διώμοτοι_593">. The adj., not found elsewhere, answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">διόμνυμι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 255"> <title>Tr.</title> 255</ref>) or <quote xml:lang="grc">διόμνυμαι</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 378, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1233"> <title>Ai.</title> 1233</ref>) as=‘to swear solemnly.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_μὴν_593">, prefacing an oath, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 256"> <title>Tr.</title> 256</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">διώμοσεν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὴν...δουλώσειν</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1185 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμνυ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὴν τί δράσειν</quote>; The formula occurs first in <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.76"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 76</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμοσσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μέν</quote> (Ion. for <quote xml:lang="grc"> μήν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι ἀρήξειν</quote>. It is used also in threats, <title>O.C.</title> 816 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὴν... λυπηθεὶς ἔσει</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσαντες_594"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 102">cp. 102</ref>.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πρὸς ἰσχύος κράτος</emph>. ἰσχύς</quote> is the physical strength at the disposal of the captors; <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτος</quote>, the mastery which this strength will give them. Thus the gen. defines the source of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 212">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 212</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐ κατ’ ἰσχὺν οὐδὲ πρὸς τὸ καρτερὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρείη, δόλῳ δὲ τοὺς ὑπερσχόντας κρατεῖν</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἰσχύν</quote> expresses the available strength, and <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸ καρτερόν</quote> the triumphant exertion of it. (As to <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἰσχύος χάριν</quote> in <ref n="Eur. Med. 538">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 538</ref>, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 30.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 30.</ref>) For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_594"> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 90">cp. 90</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέον_596">, predicate: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 352">cp. 352</ref>, 601.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θατέρου_596">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ὁ ἕτερος</quote>: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 568 <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον ...σοῦ</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον ἢ σοί</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_χάριν_598">; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 698"> <title>O. T.</title> 698</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου ποτὲ</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πράγματος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_τοσῷδε_598">=<quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου τοσούτου</quote>, <emph>after</emph> so long a time: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 722">cp. 722</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1273"> <title>El.</title> 1273</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ χρόνῳ μακρῷ φιλτάταν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδὸν ἐπαξιώσας... φανῆναι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεστρέφοντο_598">, bethought them (impf.) of caring for: <ref n="Dem. 10.9">Dem. or. 10 § 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἐφροντίσατε οὐδ’ ἐπεστράφητε οὐδὲν τούτων</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 134">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 134</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸ τοῦ θανόντος τήνδ’ ἔθεσθ’ ἐπιστροφήν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγαν_οὕτω_598">, with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεστρέφοντο</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 884"> <title>El.</title> 884</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδε πιστεύεις ἄγαν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_598"> is so far from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράγματος_598">, but also because it is closely followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδ_598">, so that, when the ear caught the first words, the sense expected might naturally be, ‘Who was for this man for whom’ etc. (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 441">cp. 441</ref>). The motive has been the wish to emphasise the pron referring to Philoctetes <emph>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’</quote>)</emph>. A somewhat similar instance is <ref n="Soph. Ant. 944"> <title>Ant.</title> 944</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτλα καὶ Δανάας οὐράνιον φῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάξαι δέμας ἐν χαλκοδέτοις αὐλαῖς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶχον_ἐκβεβληκότες_600">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 590"> <title>El.</title> 590</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβαλοῦσ’ ἔχεις</quote>. The perf. part. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 701"> <title>O. T.</title> 701</ref>) is much rarer than the aor. part. in mere periphrasis. When joined to the perf. partic., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote> has usu. a separate force; as <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 3. 14">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 3. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ χρήματα ἔχομεν ἀνηρπακότες</quote> (‘have carried off, and hold’). So <emph>ib.</emph> 4. 7. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἷς καὶ τὰ ἐπιτήδεια πάντα ἀνακεκομισμένοι</quote> (had carried up, and kept).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνιον_600"> (masc.): <ref n="Soph. OC 441"> <title>O. C.</title> 441</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_πόθος_601">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 205"> <title>O. C.</title> 205</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ὁ πολύπονος ἄγει</quote>; (n.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵκετ_601">: <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.240"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ ποτ’ Ἀχιλλῆος ποθὴ ἵξεται υἷας Ἀχαιῶν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_βία_601">, constraint imposed by the gods; an unusual phrase, but suitable here, where spontaneous yearning (<quote xml:lang="grc">πόθος</quote>) is opposed to the external pressure of destiny. Cp. fr. adesp. 424 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ πρὸ μοίρας ἡ τύχη βιάζεται</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, one does not die before one's appointed time). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1140"> <title>Ant.</title> 1140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βιαίας...νόσου.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμεσις_601">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 518">cp. 518</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμύνουσιν_601">, requite, punish: <ref n="Soph. OC 1128."> <title>O. C.</title> 1128.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνομα_ὠνομάζετο_605">: <ref n="Eur. Ion 800"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 800</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα δὲ ποῖον αὐτὸν ὀνομάζει πατήρ</quote>; Symmetry with <quote xml:lang="grc">Πριάμου μὲν υἱός</quote> required <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀνομαζόμενος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 215">cp. 215</ref> n. (<quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῶν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἕλενος_606">,—distinguished as <quote xml:lang="grc">Πριαμίδης</quote> from Helenus son of Oenops, a Greek hero slain by Hector ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.707"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 707</ref>), —figures in the <title>Iliad</title> as at once a seer and a warrior. He gives counsel at critical moments to his brother Hector ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.76"> <title>Il.</title> 6. 76</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 7.44"> Il. 7. 44</ref>); with his brother Deïphobus, he leads a third of the Trojan host in the attack on the Greek camp ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.94"> <title>Il.</title> 12. 94</ref>). The story of his capture by Odysseus does not belong to the <title>Iliad</title>, but was probably included in the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰλιὰς Μικρά</quote> of Lesches (<emph> c.</emph> 700 B.C.),—the epic which contained the return of Philoctetes to Troy (see Introd.). Ovid associates this exploit with two other similar feats of Odysseus,—the capture of the horses of Rhesus, when their master, and the Trojan spy Dolon, were slain ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 10"> <title>Il.</title> 10</ref>),—and the theft of the Palladium: <ref n="Ov. Met. 13. 99"> <title>Met.</title> 13. 99</ref> <title>Conferat his Ithacus Rhesum imbellemque Dolona</title>, | <title>Priamidemque Helenum rapta cum Pallade captum.</title> In <ref n="Verg. A. 3. 346">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 3. 346</ref> ff., Helenus, then settled in Epeirus, prophesies to Aeneas.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούων_607">, with ref. to general repute, as 1313.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωβητὰ_ἔπη_607">, insulting, contumelious words: for the act. sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 538"> <title>Tr.</title> 538</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λωβητὸν ἐμπόλημα</quote>, a bargain that ruins one. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_μέσον_609"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔδειξ_609">, rather than with <quote xml:lang="grc">δέσμιον...ἄγων</quote>: cp. Pind. fr. 42 <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶν μὲν ὦν μοῖράν τε τερπνῶν ἐς μέσον χρὴ παντὶ λαῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεικνύναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_πάντα_610">, including the command to bring Neopt. from Scyros (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 346">cp. 346</ref>). This phrase serves to emphasise the statement introduced by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_610">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 506"> <title>Ant.</title> 506</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ τυραννὶς πολλά τ’ ἄλλ’ εὐδαιμονεῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄξεστιν αὐτῇ κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὶ_πέργαμ_611">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 353">cp. 353</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πέρσοιεν_611">. Helenus said, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ πέρσετε</quote>. It is certain that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μή_611"> was used with the 1st or 3rd pers. of the fut. indic. in strong denial,—having then the same force as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</quote> with the subjunctive, which was the commoner construction. There is no need, then, for changing <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ <emph>πέρσοιεν</emph> </quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ <emph>πέρσειαν</emph> </quote> (as though he had said <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ πέρσητε</quote>). In oratio obliqua after a secondary tense this fut. indic. with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</quote> could be retained (as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρσουσιν</quote>): or it could be represented by a fut. inf. (as if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη αὐτοὺς οὐ μήποτε πέρσειν</quote>). See the examples in n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 177."> <title>O. C.</title> 177.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πέρσοιεν_611_2"> would be a weaker reading. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγοιντο_613">: he said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μὴ ἄγησθε</quote>, if ye shall not bring. Blaydes places his conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγάγοιντο_613"> in the text, and also suggests </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξοιντο_613">. Either would serve; but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγοιντο_613_2"> is right also. In a conditional sentence, the pres. subj. can have either of two meanings: (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἄγησθε, καλῶς ἕξει</quote>,—‘if ye shall bring, it will be well’—a particular supposition referring to the future: or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἄγησθε, καλῶς ἔχει</quote>,—‘if ye (ever) bring, it is (always) well,’—a general supposition referring to the present. Here, of course, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγοιντο</quote> represents (1). Cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 3. 2. 13">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3. 2. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν μὲν πόλεμον αἱρῆσθε, μηκέτι ἥκετε δεῦρο ἄνευ ὅπλων...ἢν δὲ εἰρήνης δοκῆτε δεῖσθαι, ἄνευ ὅπλων ἥκετε</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 5. 3. 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν οὖν ἴῃς νῦν, πότε ἔσει οἴκοι</quote>; For similar instances of this pres. subj. (referring to the future) represented by the optative in oratio obliqua, cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.148">Dem. or. 18 § 148</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὲν τοίνυν τοῦτο...τῶν ἐκείνου συμμάχων εἰσηγοῖτό τις</quote> (representing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν εἰσηγῆταί τις</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόψεσθαι τὸ πρᾶγμα ἐνόμιζε</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας</quote>). <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6. 1. 25">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 6. 1. 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόκει δῆγον εἶναι ὅτι αἱρήσονται αὐτόν, εἴ τις ἐπιψηφίζοι</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τις ἐπιψηφίζῃ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νήσου_613">, gen. after a verb of motion: 630, <ref n="Soph. El. 324"> <title>El.</title> 324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμων...ἐντάφια... φέρουσαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 142"> <title>O. T.</title> 142</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴοιτο_μάλιστα_613"> are parenthetical, just as if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκότως μὲν ἑκούσιον λαβών</quote>: and the optat. is used as if <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπεν ὅτι δηλώσοι</quote> had preceded. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.19">Lys. or. 13 § 19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει ὅτι, ἐὰν αὐτὸν ἕλησθε περὶ τῆς εἰρήνης πρεσβευτὴν αὐτοκράτορα, ποιήσειν <note anchored="true" place="inline" xml:lang="eng">[irreg. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιήσει</quote>poih/s1ei<quote xml:lang="grc">‐σοι</quote>-s1oi</note> ὥστε μήτε τῶν τειχῶν διελεῖν μήτε ἄλλο τὴν πόλιν ἐλαττῶσαι μηδέν· οἴοιτο δὲ καὶ ἄλλο τι ἀγαθὸν...εὑρήσεσθαι</quote> </cit>. Similarly a clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> can take the optat. in oratio obliqua: <title>H.</title> 7. 1. 23 <quote xml:lang="grc">γέγων ὡς μόνοις μὲν αὐτοῖς πατρὶς Πελοπόννησος εἴη, μόνοι γὰρ αὐτόχθονες ἐν αὐτῇ οἰκοῖεν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
λέγει ὅτι, ἐὰν αὐτὸν ἕλησθε περὶ τῆς εἰρήνης
							πρεσβευτὴν αὐτοκράτορα, ποιήσειν   ὥστε μήτε τῶν τειχῶν
							διελεῖν μήτε ἄλλο τὴν πόλιν ἐλαττῶσαι μηδέν· οἴοιτο δὲ καὶ ἄλλο τι
							ἀγαθὸν...εὑρήσεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστα_613"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴοιτο_613">, indicating what he thought most likely: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 932"> <title>El.</title> 932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι μάλιστ’ ἔγωγε</quote>, and <title>O.C.</title> 1298 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_618"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τυχών_618"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ τύχοι</quote>): the place of the pron. is emphatic; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 598">cp. 598</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρα_τέμνειν_618">= <quote xml:lang="grc">κεφαλὴν ἀποτέμνειν</quote> (or poet. <quote xml:lang="grc">καρατομεῖν</quote>). The Homeric Odysseus twice uses this expression; <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.259"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 259</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκέτ’ ἔπειτ’ Ὀδυσῆι κάρη ὤμοισιν ἐπείη</quote>—if he does not chastise Thersites; and <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.102"> <title>Od.</title> 16. 102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτίκ’ ἔπειτ’ ἀπ’ ἐμεῖο κάρη τάμοι ἀλλότριος φώς</quote> —if he should not punish the suitors.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεῖτο_618">, usu. ‘commanded’ or ‘enjoined’; here rather, ‘gave leave.’ [In <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6. 6. 31"> Xen. <title>An.</title> 6. 6. 31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ στρατιά σοι ὑφεῖτο ὅ τι ἐβούλου ποιῆσαι, ἐφεῖτο</quote> is only a doubtful v. l.] </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_σπεύδειν_620">: for the superfluous art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 47"> <title>O. C.</title> 47</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐξανιστάναι</quote>) n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_καὐτῷ_620">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>=‘both,’ yet can follow <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote> because the thought is, ‘I recommend haste to you, both in your own interest and in that of your friends.’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρι_620">: a very rare addition to the gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">κήδομαι</quote>: as a general rule, however, verbs of ‘caring’ can take either the simple gen. or gen. with prep. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντίζω, μέλει</quote>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_βλάβη_622">, that utter pest. In this phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσα</quote> is justified by the figurative application; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when a man is called a <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβη</quote>, instead of saying <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πᾶς βλάβη ὤν</quote>, he who is altogether a bane, we can say <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πᾶσα βλάβη</quote>, the bane which is altogether such. The tendency is the same which appears, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, in <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει...εἶναι ταύτην</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθότητα ὀνόματος</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Crat. 443E">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 443E</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 88">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 88</ref> n.).—So Aegisthus is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πάντ’ ἄναλκις οὗτος, ἡ πᾶσα βλάβη</quote> , <ref n="Soph. El. 301.">Soph. <title>El.</title> 301.</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 927">Cp. 927</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δεῖμα</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεισθήσομαι_624">. No entreaties can recall the dead to the upper world; and no entreaties will recall him to Troy. We need not object to <quote xml:lang="grc">πεισθήσομαι</quote> that a Greek would think of the departed as glad to revisit the sunlight. The point is that the dead are deaf to the voice that would bring them back.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_624"> implies the suppressed thought, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι στελεῖ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_624">=‘at this rate’ (=‘if I go to Troy’): so oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἀνελθεῖν_624">. Nauck writes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ἐλθεῖν</quote>, taking the sense to be: ‘I shall be made to believe that I could return,’=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἔλθοιμι ἄν</quote>. But (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνελθεῖν</quote> is confirmed by the context: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 445">Ar. <title> Pax</title> 445</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς φῶς ἀνελθεῖν</quote>, etc.: and (<emph>b</emph>) it gives a more direct and forcible sense.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὑκείνου_πατήρ_624">, Sisyphus. The scholiast gives the story as it was told (probably) by the logographer Pherecydes (<emph>flor.</emph> 470 B.C.?), who is quoted in ref. to Sisyphus by the schol. on <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.153."> <title>Il.</title> 6. 153.</ref>Sisyphus had directed his wife to leave him unburied. On reaching the shades, he denounced her impiety to Pluto, and obtained leave to go back and punish her. Having thus returned to earth, he stayed there,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> (adds the scholiast) <quote xml:lang="grc">μετ’ ἀνάγκης κατῆλθεν</quote>. Theognis (v. 702) is the earliest witness:—<quote xml:lang="grc">Σισύφου Αἰολίδεω</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς τε καὶ ἐξ Ἀΐδεω πολυϊδρίῃσιν ἀνῆλθεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πείσας Περσεφόνην αἱμυλίοισι λόγοις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμφέροι_627">, be your helper: a sense derived from the idea of sharing a burden: <ref n="Soph. El. 946"> <title>El.</title> 946</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνοίσω πᾶν ὅσονπερ ἂν σθένω</quote>. Not, ‘be in accord with you’ (<emph>vobiscum conspiret</emph>, Herm. : <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 166">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ, ἐὰν μὴ τῇ γυναικὶ συμφέρῃ</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_628">: for the plur. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524">cp. 524</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_629"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖξαι_629"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεὼς_ἄγοντ_629">, leading him ashore from his ship: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 613">cp. 613</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_631"> is clearly right: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 993">cp. 993</ref>, 997, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 415."> <title>Tr.</title> 415.</ref>Welcker's <quote xml:lang="grc">οὑ</quote> (=‘whereas’) is much weaker: so, too, is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ θᾶσσον</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θᾶσσον...ἄπουν;</quote> </gloss>
