<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεῖστον_ἐχθίστης_631">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 743"> <title>O. C.</title> 743</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστον</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιστος</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπουν_632">, deprived of the use of one's feet, <quote xml:lang="grc">χωλόν</quote>: cp. <title>Metaphys.</title> 4. 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεται...ἄπουν καὶ τῷ μὴ ἔχειν ὅλως πόδας καὶ τῷ φαύλους</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_λεκτά_633">, <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> in the epanaphora <ref n="Soph. Phil. 779">cp. 779</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 806"> <title>Ant.</title> 806</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὁρίζῃ_635">. The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρίζει_635"> cannot be defended here, either with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> as=‘since,’ or with the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> as=‘while yet.’ The words clearly express the eagerness of Ph. to put a space of sea between himself and his pursuer. And he has no reason to believe that his pursuer is still distant. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐκ_πρῴρας_639">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1451">cp. 1451</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ πρύμναν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνῇ_639">, as in 764: and so 705 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανείη</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 113">Her. 2. 113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἀνίει</quote> (pres.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πνεύματα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στελοῦμεν_639">: 571 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκ_ἀλλὰ_642"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The tone of this idiomatic phrase would be nearly rendered (here, at least) by ‘nay, but.’ The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_642"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ καλὸς πλοῦς κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘This is <emph>not</emph> a case of flight from imminent peril; but (on the contrary) our pursuers also are being delayed.’ Cp. <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 277A">Plat. <title> Euthyd.</title> 277A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα σὺ οὐ μανθάνεις; ...οὔκ, ἀλλ’, ἦ δ’ ὅς, μανθάνω</quote>.—I do not think, then, that any alteration is necessary. Of the conjectures (see cr. n.) Doederlein's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶδ_642"> is perhaps the best. O. Heine's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_οὐχὶ_642">...; is also possible. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_645">, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλ’</quote> in 647: n. on 524 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρῶμεν_λαβὼν_645">. The subject to the plur. verb being <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ καὶ σύ</quote>, the sing. partic. agrees with <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote>,—a constr. harsher in form than in reality. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 202">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 202</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δευρὶ γὰρ ἐσβὰς</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτ’ ἀνεγείρας τὴν ἐμὴν ἀηδόνα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλοῦμεν αὐτούς</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 141"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 141</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίστω, κἀπολακτίσασ’ ὕπνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδώμεθ’</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Med. 564">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 564</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ξυναρτήσας γένος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὐδαιμονοῖμεν</quote> (so Elms., for <quote xml:lang="grc">‐οίην</quote> ). <ref n="Dem. 14.15">Dem. or. 14 § 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεβλέψατε πρὸς ἀλλήλους, ὡς αὐτὸς μὲν ἕκαστος οὐ ποιήσων</quote>.—Dobree's conjecture, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβόνθ_645">, was suggested by <ref n="Soph. OC 1164">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1164</ref>, where the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">μολόντ’</quote> at the end of the v.: but that should prob. be <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίπερ_ἄπο_647">: and so it will not take long to choose them out. For the use of the prep., cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 110">Thuc. 1. 110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλίγοι ἀπὸ πολλῶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεώς_ἔπι_648">. The correction of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνι_648"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπι_648"> is necessary and certain. Of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνι_648_2"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνεστι</quote>) only three explanations are possible. (1) Some hold that the gen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεώς_648"> depends on the idea of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσω</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνδον</quote> implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνι_648_3">: ‘is contained in my ship.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1274"> <title>Ai.</title> 1274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκέων... ἐγκεκλῃμένους</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 451">Eur. <title>Ph.</title> 451</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’ εἰσεδέξω τειχέων</quote>. But there the notion ‘within’ is implied far more clearly than by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνι</quote> here. (2) Or <quote xml:lang="grc">νεώς</quote> is an absolute local gen., ‘in the ship’; cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 900"> <title>El.</title> 900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάτης δ’ ὁρῶ πυρᾶς...βόστρυχον</quote>. (3) Others take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνι_648_4"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβών</quote> in 645: ‘what is there which it is not possible to obtain <emph>from</emph> my ship?’ No one of these views is tenable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάνυ_650"> is fitting enough, where he is dwelling on the value of the herb to him; and it certainly is not weaker than the substitutes which have been proposed for it (see cr. n.). Meineke (<title>Analecta Soph.</title> p. 317) makes the arbitrary assumption that <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνυ</quote> was not used by Soph. in dialogue; though it is certainly used by him in anapaests ( <ref n="Soph. OC 144">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 144</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔτ_651">: ‘Now what else...?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_651"> introduces the question, as oft., when a speaker turns to a new point: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1405">cp. 1405</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 101"> <title>Ai.</title> 101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶεν· τί γὰρ δὴ παῖς ὁ τοῦ Λαερτίου</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ σοι τύχης ἕστηκεν</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τόξων_652"> (‘I fain would fetch) any of these arrows that may have been overlooked and may have slipped away from me.’ The vaguer interpretation, ‘any appurtenance of this bow,’ is not the best here. Philoctetes, who has been afield in quest of game, carries his bow and his quiver (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 291">cp. 291</ref> n.); but he is afraid that one or more of the arrows may have been accidentally left behind in the cave. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόξα_652">, in poetry, can mean either (1) bow, (2) bow and arrows, or (3) arrows. For sense (2), cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.502"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 502</ref>: Leto picks up the arrows which had dropped from the quiver of Artemis (492 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχέες δ’ ἔκπιπτον ὀϊστοί</quote>):—<quote xml:lang="grc">συναίνυτο καμπύλα τόξα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπτῶτ’ ἄλλυδις ἄλλα μετὰ στροφάλιγγι κονίης</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">καμπύλα</quote> is the epithet of the bow only). For (3), <ref n="Eur. Ion 524">Eur. <title> Ion</title> 524</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴσω τόξα πνευμόνων λαβεῖν. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπημελημένον_652">, a rare compound, of which this perf. partic. occurs in <ref n="Hdt. 3. 129.">Her. 3. 129.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρερρύηκεν_652">, has slipped aside (as by dropping from the quiver); not, ‘has slipped from my memory.’ Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 4">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεεινὸν ἦν ἡ χιὼν ἐπιπεπτωκυῖα, ὅτῳ μὴ παρερρυείη</quote> (slip off). Plato has the word in a fig. sense, <title>Legg.</title> 781 A <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ ὑμῖν παρέρρει, πολὺ ἄμεινον ἂν ἔχοντα εἰ νόμων ἔτυχεν ἢ τὰ νῦν</quote> (escaped your care).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_μή_652">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μὴ λ.</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 67">cp. 67</ref> n.: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_652">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 81">cp. 81</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_κτλ_655"> L's reading, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γαρ άλλ’</quote> (<emph>sic</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσθ’ ἃ</quote> clearly points to the reading in the text, since </gloss>
lemma: οὐ γὰρ ἄλλ’ ἔστ’, ἀλλ’ ἃ κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_655"> might easily have been omitted by a scribe who mistook it for a repetition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλ_655">. And <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote> confirms this. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλος_655"> closely followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>, Seyffert cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.311"> <title>Od.</title> 8. 311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτὰρ οὔ τι μοι αἴτιος ἄλλος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τοκῆε δύω</quote> (cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 11. 558). Remark that this reading is further corroborated by the form of the statement. It is peculiarly Sophoclean to have three clauses, in which the second is opposed to the first, and the third repeats the sense of the first,—as here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃβαστάζω_655">= </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_655">: see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 465."> <title>Ant.</title> 465.</ref>—A's reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλαγ_ἃ_655"> is weaker, and also less likely to have generated L's. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_656"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστιν_656">, as sometimes after <quote xml:lang="grc">δυνατόν, ἐθέλω, δέομαι, πείθω</quote>, etc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 969"> <title>O. C.</title> 969</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεόν_656">. So the Arcadian Parthenopaeus swears by his spear-head (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰχμή</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν ἔχει μᾶλλον θεοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβειν πεποιθώς</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 529">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 529</ref>). Idas, one of the Argonauts, says, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔμ’ ὀφέλλει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς τόσον, ὁσσάτιόν περ ἐμὸν δόρυ</quote> (Apoll. Rhod. 1. 468). Mezentius: <title>Dextra mihi deus et telum, quod missile libro</title>, | <title>Nunc adsint</title> ( <ref n="Verg. A. 10. 773">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 10. 773</ref>). Capaneus: <title>Ades O mihi dextera tantum:</title> | <title>Tu praesens bellis et inevitable numen;</title> | <title>Te voco</title>, <emph>te solam</emph>, <emph>superum contemptor</emph>, <emph>adoro</emph> (Statius 9. 548). Here, however, Neoptolemus regards the bow as a ‘god,’ not so much because it is invincible, as because it had belonged to Heracles.—For the fig. use of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεός_656">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 27"> <title>O. T.</title> 27</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφέρῃ_659"> cannot mean, ‘what is pleasing to you’ (as Nauck takes it, ‘was genehm ist’), but only, ‘what is profitable for you.’ The latter sense, however, is quite consistent with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_659"> in 660. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_γε_660">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 221"> <title>Ant.</title> 221</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρες_660">, ‘let it go,’ ‘think no more about it.’ There is no real ground for thinking this word corrupt. <quote xml:lang="grc">παριέναι</quote> can mean <emph>omittere</emph> no less than <emph>concedere.</emph> Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1193"> <title>Ant.</title> 1193</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐδὲν παρήσω...ἔπος</quote>. <title>Legg.</title> 754 A <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τοίνυν γιγνώσκοντές γε παρῶμεν αὐτὸ ἄρρητον</quote>. <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 86</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ παρίει καλά</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσια_θέμις_662">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 432"> <title>El.</title> 432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ σοι θέμις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὅσιον</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_γ_663">, as 1215, <ref n="Soph. OT 35"> <title>O. T.</title> 35</ref>, etc. The relative, with this causal force, refers to an antecedent (<quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote>) which is understood: <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάος_663">, life, in place of imminent death.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδωκας_663">, followed by an aor. (666): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 928">cp. 928</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθόν_Οἰταίαν_663">: 490 n.—The repetition of the pron. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς</quote> has much the same rhetorical effect as the repetition of the verb (<quote xml:lang="grc">δέδωκας</quote>) would have with us. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέστησας_πέρα_666">. If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρα_666"> is genuine, the sense is:—‘When I was under the feet of my foes, thou hast lifted me up, (placing me) <emph>beyond their reach.</emph> ’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρα_666_2"> could be either prep. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν</quote>, or adv.: the former is best for contrast with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνερθεν_666">. While suffering in Lemnos, Ph. was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνερθεν τῶν ἐχθρῶν</quote>. If he is restored to his home in Greece (and he assumes that this is certain), then they can touch him no more. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα</quote> blends the thought of conveyance across the sea with the image of ‘uplifting’ which is expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέστησας</quote>. The very fact of such a blending seems in favour of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρα_666_3">. Sophocles not seldom admits a partial fusion of the figurative with the literal: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 886"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 886</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1300">Soph. O. T. 1300</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 117.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 117.</ref>—No emendation is satisfactory. If we read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν ἔνερθεν ὄντ’ ἀνέστησάς <emph>μ’ ὕπερ</emph> </quote>, we should have to suppose that the loss of the letters </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_ὕ_666"> had led to the expansion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περ_666"> into <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πέραι</quote> in L). But such a loss is not very likely. In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1301">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1301</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πέριξ</quote> prob. arose from <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ ξ</quote>[<quote xml:lang="grc">ίφει</quote>], the lost letters were the last of the verse. I had thought of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναστήσας_πάρει_666">: but prefer to retain </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρα_666_4">.—Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1090">Soph. <title> El.</title> 1090</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῴῃς μοι καθύπερθεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρὶ καὶ πλούτῳ τοσόνδ’ ἐχθρῶν, ὅσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν ὑπόχειρ ναίεις</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_667"> (nom.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέσταισοι_667">, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶθιγγάνειν_667"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνω</quote> never takes an accus. in class. Greek: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 546"> <title>Ant.</title> 546</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δοῦναι_668">. These words are not only genuine, but mark a delicate turn of phrase. Instead of saying, ‘You shall be allowed to handle the bow, on condition of returning it,’ he says, ‘You shall be allowed to handle the bow and to return it.’ The clause <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δόντι δοῦναι</quote> coheres closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνειν</quote>. The condition which <emph> qualifies</emph> the boon is thus lightly and courteously hinted,— being inserted between the words (<quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνειν, κἀξεπεύξασθαι</quote>) which express the privileges conceded. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 774">Cp. 774</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δοθήσεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλὴν σοί τε κἀμοί</quote>.—The aorist </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦναι_668"> expresses the moment of giving, and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεύξασθαι_668"> the moment of vaunting; while the pres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θιγγάνειν_668"> denotes the continuing act of touching. Cp. <ref n="Dem. 2.26">Dem. or. 2 § 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ γὰρ ῥᾷον ἔχοντας φυλάττειν ἢ κτήσασθαι πάντα πέφυκεν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_668"> is correct; it represents the nom. of the direct form, <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχει ἐπιψαῦσαι μόνος</quote>. Here, however, after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόντι_668">, it is slightly awkward. Nauck wishes to read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνῳ_668">. I should prefer to keep </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_668_2"> and insert </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ς_668"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρετῆς_668">. The direct form implied would then be, <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχει σὲ ἐπιψαῦσαι μόνον</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 474B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 474B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γὰρ δὴ οἶμαι καὶ ἐμὲ καὶ σὲ... ἡγεῖσθαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐεργετῶν_670">, by kindling the pyre for Heracles: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801">cp. 801</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_παθὼν_672">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1202"> <title>O. C.</title> 1202</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ καλὸν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν μὲν εὖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχειν παθόντα δ’ οὐκ ἐπίστασθαι τίνειν</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 40">Thuc. 2. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ πάσχοντες εὖ ἀλλὰ δρῶντες κτώμεθα τοὺς φίλους</quote>.—So, of injury, <ref n="Soph. OC 271">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 271</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παθὼν μὲν ἀντέδρων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτήματος_672">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 701"> <title>Ant.</title> 701</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ δὲ σοῦ πράσσοντος εὐτυχῶς, πάτερ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδὲν κτῆμα τιμιώτερον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωροῖς_εἴσω_674">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 624"> <title>Tr.</title> 624</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχοις ἂν ἤδη</quote>. Cavallin gives these words, as well as the following, to Philoctetes, because the invitation to enter the cave ought to come from him, whose home it is. But then the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_εἰσάξω_674"> lose their proper force; for we have to understand Ph. as saying,—‘Pray enter: — or rather —I will lead you in.’ But </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_674"> can only emphasise </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_674">: and therefore </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωροῖς_εἴσω_674_2"> must be said to Philoctetes. In these words Neoptolemus reverts to the wish which he had already expressed (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 645">645</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 651">651</ref>) that Ph. should fetch from the cave anything that he needed for the voyage.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_γὰρ_674"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσοῦν_674">: for the art. as penult. word of the v., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 231"> <title>O. T.</title> 231</ref>; <ref n="Soph. OC 265"> <title>O. C.</title> 265</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> O. C. 351</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 67"> <title>Ant.</title> 67</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78."> Ant. 78.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_νοσοῦν_674">, my sick estate: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 36">Thuc. 1. 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν δεδιὸς αὐτοῦ...τὸ δὲ θαρσοῦν</quote> (his mood of fear or courage). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξήκους_676">, as if by rumour from a far-off place: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 397">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσωθεν ἐξήκουσα κληδόνος βοήν</quote>. Above, in 378 and 472, this compound was merely a strengthened <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄπωπα_μάλα_676">. Cp. <title>Hieron</title> 1. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ τύραννοι οὐ μάλα</quote> (<emph> nequaquam</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ θεωρίας ἔχουσιν</quote>. The emphasis contrasts the sufferings known only by hearsay with those which have just been so vividly placed before their eyes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πελάταν_677">. Ixion treacherously murdered his father-in-law, <quote xml:lang="grc">Δηϊονεύς</quote>, and, when no mortal would minister the rites of purification to him, was cleansed of his crime by Zeus. He requited this grace by attempting the bed of Hera; and Zeus then commanded Hermes to bind him on a wheel of fire in the lower world.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέκτρων_Διὸς_677">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1406">cp. 1406</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βέλεσι τοῖς Ἡρακλέους</quote>. Buttmann preferred the Triclinian <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τοῦ</emph> Διός</quote>,—which is admissible (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10"> <title>Ant.</title> 10</ref> n.),—as emphasising the proper name; but </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_677"> is clearly right. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_παῖς_679">. As given in the MSS. (see cr. n.), these verses are longer than the corresponding vv. of the antistrophe, 693 f., <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ᾧστόνον...αἱματηρόν</quote>. If both </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰξίονα_679"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσμιον_679"> are to be kept here, the antistrophic verses must be expanded. But those verses appear to be sound as they stand. The question is, then, whether </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰξίονα_679_2"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσμιον_679_2"> should be omitted here. I prefer to omit </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰξίονα_679_3">, for two reasons.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσμιον_679_3"> is not, indeed, necessary to the sense. As in prose we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναβιβάζειν ἐπὶ τὸν τροχόν</quote> ( Andoc. or. 1 § 43), so, here, the sense would be adequately given by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἔβαλεν_679">. And it might fairly be suggested that <quote xml:lang="grc">δέσμιον</quote> had crept into the text from the schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἄμπυκα δὴ</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ τὸν τρόχον</quote> (which should be <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχὸν</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1065">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1065</ref> n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδεμένον</quote>. Then, omitting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσμιον_679_4">, we might keep the order of the MS. words, merely changing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_679"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀν_679">:</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰξίον_ἔβαλεν_679"> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> = ‘as men say’). But, on the other hand, poetical considerations seem in favour of </gloss>
lemma: Ἰξίον’ ἀν’ ἄμπυκα δὴ δρομάδ’ ὡς ἔβαλεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέσμιον_679_5">. It adds force to the picture of a terrible doom imposed by an irresistible power. —Other views are discussed in the Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμπυκα_679">, here, the rim of the wheel; elsewhere always ‘head-band.’ But its etymology (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμπί</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφί</quote>) might easily suggest this poet. use, esp. as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρομάδα_679"> (perh. suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχός</quote>) helps it out. The schol. seems to have read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμπυκα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄντυγα_679"> is certainly tempting, and may be right; but it does not seem necessary. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδ_682"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τόνδ’</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 597">cp. 597</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θατέρου</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_νοσφίσας_684">. A partial reminiscence of <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.690"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 690</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε τινὰ ῥέξας ἐξαίσιον οὔτε τι εἰπών</quote>, as Eustathius saw (p. 763, <ref n="Hom. Od. 2"> Od. 2</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὁμηρικὸν δέ τι καὶ παρὰ Σοφοκλεῖ ἐν Φιλοκτήτῃ τὸ οὔτε τι ῥέξας, κακὸν δηλαδή· οὕτω γὰρ νοεῖται, εἰ καὶ παντελῶς ἐκεῖ σιωπᾶται τὸ ῥεχθέν</quote>. Here the last three words prove two things,—viz., that Eustath. read <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὔτε</emph> νοσφίσας</quote>, and that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε τι ῥέξας</quote> in his citation of Sophocles was a mere slip for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἔρξας τιν’</quote>: since, if his text of our verse had really contained <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>, he could not have said, <quote xml:lang="grc">σιωπᾶται τὸ ῥεχθέν</quote>. (He has other such slips: see Appendix on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 292."> <title>Ant.</title> 292.</ref>) Schneidewin's emendation, </gloss>
lemma: οὔτ’ ἔρξας τιν’, οὔ τι νοσφίσας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_τι_684"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_684">, appears certain. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρδειν τινά τι</quote> can mean, ‘to do a wrong to a man’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρδειν τινά</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>, could not possibly mean it. Ought we, then, to write </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_684"> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρξας</quote>? Probably not. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 249"> <title>Ant.</title> 249</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὔτε</emph> του γενῇδος ἦν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> πλῆγμ’, <emph>οὐ</emph> δικέλλης ἐνβολή</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 972"> <title>O. C.</title> 972</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσφίσας_684">, robbed, defrauded. We find not only <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσφίζειν τινά τινος</quote>, but also <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσφίζειν τινά τι</quote> (as <ref n="Pind. N. 6">Pind. <title>N.</title> 6. 64</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ τ’ ἐνόσφισε</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κλᾶρος...ἄνθἐ Ὀλυμπιάδος</quote>); and this is the constr. here. The antithesis is between <quote xml:lang="grc">βια</quote> (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρξας_684">) and <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλος</quote> (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσφίσας_684_2">): Ixion had murdered his father-in-law, and had sought to steal the love of Hera. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσος_ἴσοις_685">, lit. ‘equitable towards the equitable’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσοις</quote> dat. of relation),—respecting the rights of others, as they respected his. In describing a man of peaceful and estimable character, the Greek tendency is to say, ‘he <emph>neither</emph> did <emph>nor</emph> suffered wrong’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, he was not aggressive, nor was he forced into unpleasant relations with his fellow-men by their action,—since he provoked no enmities. See, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, Lysias or. 12 § 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὶ πώποτε οὔτε ἡμεῖς οὔτε ἐκεῖνος δίκην οὔτε ἐδικασάμεθα οὔτε ἐφύγομεν, ἀλλ’ οὕτως ᾠκοῦμεν δημοκρατούμενοι ὥστε μήτε εἰς τοὺς ἄλλους ἐξαμαρτάνειν μήτε ὑπὸ τῶν ἄλλων ἀδικεῖσθαι</quote>. This is the Athenian ideal of the <quote xml:lang="grc">χρηστός, ἐπιεικής, ἀπράγμων</quote>. And this is what </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσος_ἴσοις_685_2"> expresses here. It does not imply that he dealt with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσοι</quote> in one way, and with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄδικοι</quote> in another, but merely denotes that reciprocity of fair dealing which his fairness caused. Hence the version, ‘living at peace with his fellowmen,’ is truer to the sense than (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>), ‘just among the just.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 267"> <title>Ai.</title> 267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς κοινοῖσι λυπεῖσθαι</quote>, to share the grief of friends who grieve. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσος_685"> as=<emph>aequus</emph>, said of persons, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 677"> <title>O. T.</title> 677</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσως_685"> (<emph>sic</emph>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἴσοις_685">. The objection to reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_γ_685"> is twofold. (1) The idea suggested would then be the same as in Eur. fr. 693 (quoted by Schneidewin), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς μὲν δικαίοις ἔνδικος, τοῖς δ’ αὖ κακοῖς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιος</quote>. Here, however, the point is the generally inoffensive life of Ph. ,— not the distinction between his conduct towards just and unjust men respectively. (2) The participle <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>, though not indispensable, is very desirable. It is possible that the blunder <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσως</quote> in L may be connected with the original presence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote> in the text. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυθ_686">: for the impf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 252">cp. 252</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διωλλύμην</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυθ_με_686">. A comparison with the antistrophe (701 f.) strongly confirms Erfurdt's transposition, <quote xml:lang="grc">θαῦμά μ’ ἔχει</quote>, and Dindorf's insertion of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοι</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε</quote>, since </gloss>
lemma:
ὤλλυθ’ ὧδ’ ἀναξίως· τόδε θαῦμ’
							ἔχει με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_ἔχει_686"> then corresponds with the certainly genuine words in 702, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_εἰλυόμενος_686">. The next question is how </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυθ_ἀναξίως_686"> should be reconciled with the MS. words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπει γὰρ ἄλλοτ’ ἄλλᾳ</quote> in v. 701. Hermann's change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷρπεγὰρ_686"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷρπε_δ_686"> has been generally received; it is gentler than that of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυθ_686_2"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠλέκεθ_686"> (Dindorf), or to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυτο_τῇδ_686"> (Campbell). It is less easy to decide whether </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναξίως_686"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλᾳ_686"> should be altered. Keeping <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλᾳ</quote>, Dindorf changes <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀναξίως</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτίμως</quote>, and Wecklein to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεικῶς</quote>: Linwood proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνοίκτως</quote>. We must then suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναξίως</quote> was a gloss, since such a corruption of the letters would be difficult. But it seems better to keep <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναξίως</quote>, and to suppose, with Campbell, that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλᾳ</quote> has come from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_686"> (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αχ_686">)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾷ_686">.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιπλάκτων_688">: the Doric form is clearly required in a strophe which contains <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάταν, μοίρᾳ, θνατῶν, βιοτάν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 597"> <title>Ai.</title> 597</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλίπλακτος</quote> , <ref n="Soph. El. 484"> <title>El.</title> 484</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκόπλακτος</quote>. For the active sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</ref> n. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίπλακτα ῥόθια</quote> are those which beat around the rocky promontory near his cave (1455). Hesych. defines <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥόθιον</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">κῦμα μετὰ ψόφου γινόμενον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 259"> <title>Ant.</title> 259</ref> n.—The corrupt </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύζων_688"> in L (for <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων</quote>), which violates both sense and metre, was taken by the schol. as=<quote xml:lang="grc">κλυζόμενος</quote>. (Buttmann strangely accepted this, comparing, for the gen., the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc"> λούεσθαι...ποταμοῖο</quote>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιοτὰν_κατέσχεν_690">, <emph>obtinuit</emph>, ‘kept his hold upon’ the life which might well have slipped from him. This is a common sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέχω</quote>, though a bold application of it. Not, <emph>sustinuit</emph>, ‘endured,’ as Dindorf renders. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_πρόσουρος_691">, where he was his own sole neighbour. So when a man sends no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγελία</quote> before him, he is said to arrive as his own <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄγγελος</quote>: when no herald precedes him, he is <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς κῆρυξ</quote> (n. on 500). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 866">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 866</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνος ὢν ἔφεδρος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> δισσοῖς</quote>, ‘his own sole supporter against two foes,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, there is no <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔφεδρος</quote> at his back, to fight the man who vanquishes him. Lucian <title> Timon</title> 43 <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς θυέτω καὶ εὐωχείσθω, μόνος ἑαυτῷ γείτων καὶ ὅμορος</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμορος</quote> strongly suggests that Lucian was thinking of our passage). Martial 5. 24. 8 <title>Hermes</title> (the gladiator) <emph>suppositicius sibi ipse</emph>, ‘his own substitute,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, never requiring one, because never defeated. Seneca <title>Herc. Fur.</title> act 1 sc. 1 <title>Quaeris Alcidae parem?</title> | <title>Nemo est nisi ipse.</title> Massinger, <title>Duke of Milan</title> act 4 sc. 3, ‘And, but herself, admits no parallel.’ —Remark that <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἑαυτῷ</quote> (which Meineke sought to represent by changing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_691"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_691">) is not needed, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσουρος_691">=‘near the borders,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘neighbour to the place’ (in which Ph. was), and thus represents, not <quote xml:lang="grc">γείτων</quote> simply, but <quote xml:lang="grc">γείτων τῇ χώρᾳ</quote>. For the Ionic form cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμουρος, ξύνουρος, τηλουρός</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 194">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 194</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_βάσιν_691">, without the power to walk; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 632">cp. 632</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπουν</quote>. —Bothe's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσουρον_βάσιν_691"> (‘hearing no footstep of neighbour’) is plausible at first sight. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς ἦν</quote> =‘he was alone’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1650"> <title>O. C.</title> 1650</ref> n.). But the vulgate is far more forcible. By his </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόδουλον_βάσιν_691"> Seyffert meant, ‘having no foot to serve him.’—The conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἔχων βάσιν</quote> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτινἐς_ἔγχωρον_691"> (‘having access to no neighbour’) is very weak. Those who adopt it (cp. cr. n.) join <quote xml:lang="grc">κακογείτονα</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">στόνον</quote>: see next n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακογείτονα_692">=<quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">γείτονα</quote>, a neighbour to his sufferings: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, one to be near him while he suffers. The word does not imply (as some have objected), ‘a neighbour <emph>in</emph> (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, sharing in) his sufferings.’ Nor is there any ground for saying that <quote xml:lang="grc">κακογείτων</quote> could mean only <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸς γείτων</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακογείτονα_692_2"> as epithet with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόνον_692">: <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ᾧ δὴ τὸν κακὸν γείτονα, τὸν αἱματηρὸν στόνον, ἀποκλαύσειε</quote>. And so Cavallin. Bugge, again, takes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακογείτονα_692_3"> as a subst., ‘his evil neighbour’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘his disease’),—governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">στόνον ...ἀποκλαύσειε</quote>: comparing <ref n="Soph. El. 123"> <title>El.</title> 123</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάκεις ...οἰμωγὰν</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγαμέμνονα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ᾧ_693">: in the negative statement <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ὅτῳ</quote> would be more usu.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 220"> <title>Ant.</title> 220</ref> n. For the optat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποκλαύσειεν_693"> <ref target="cl281">see on 281</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρκέσειεν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίτυπον_693">: Lucian <title>De domo</title> 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς φωνῆς ἐπανιούσης κατὰ τὸ ἀντίτυπον καὶ πρὸς αὑτὴν ἀναστρεφούσης</quote>. The force of the epithet here is proleptic,—‘so as to excite a responsive lament.’ It reminds us that the cries of Ph. were answered by Echo alone (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1459">cp. 1459</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρυβρῶτα_αἱματηρόν_693">: the epithets of the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> are given to the <quote xml:lang="grc"> στόνος</quote> prompted by it: ‘a lament for a plague that gnawed his flesh and drained his blood.’ This is not too bold for the style of tragic lyrics; and the boldness was perhaps somewhat softened to a Greek ear by the fact that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόνον_693"> was in the acc. For, though this acc. is really ‘cognate’ to <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀποκλαύσειε</quote>, yet the case itself might help to suggest that <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρυβρῶτα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματηρόν</quote> described the object of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαυθμός</quote>. With <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρυβρώς</quote> cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">διαβόρος</quote> (v. 7, n.). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 208">Cp. 208</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τρυσάνωρ</quote>. Schneidewin cites also <ref n="Aesch. Seven 348">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 348</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαχαὶ δ’ αἱματόεσσαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐπιμαστιδίων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτιτρεφεῖς βρέμονται</quote>. It seems possible that this may have been in Soph. 's mind: but it is less bold, since <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαχαὶ αἱματόεσσαι τῶν ἐπιμ</quote>. merely= <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαχαὶ τῶν αἱματοέντων ἐπιμ</quote>. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote>, etc.). A truer parallel is [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 260 <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόγαμβρον</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc"> γόον</quote>, =<quote xml:lang="grc">γόον περὶ κακοῦ λαμβροῦ</quote>. We might add <ref n="Soph. El. 752">Soph. <title>El.</title> 752</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνιον οἰμωγὴν κλύω</quote>. The conjectural insertions which have been made in these vv. are noticed in the Appendix on vv. 678 f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_τὰν_695">. The MS. text has </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_τὰν_695">,—a syllable too much. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_695"> may have been conjecturally added, to link this clause to the last; while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_695"> is not so likely to have been inserted. And </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_τὰν_695_2"> is intrinsically better here than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ὃς_695">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱμάδα_695">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν τοῦ αἵματος ῥύσιν</quote>. The word is found only here.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηκιομέναν_695"> is usu. called passive. But it is surely rather a poet. middle form. A transitive <quote xml:lang="grc"> κηκίω</quote> occurs first in postclass. Greek (<ref n="Rh. 4.600">Rh. 4.600</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρὺν ἀνακηκίει ἀτμόν</quote>), while <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 251b"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 251 B</ref> suffices to show that the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">κηκίω</quote> was familiar in There Attic. is no other example of <quote xml:lang="grc">κηκίομαι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 784">Cp. 784</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηκῖον</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> is short in Homer ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 7.262"> <title>Il.</title> 7. 262</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακήκι^ον</quote> , <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.455"> <title>Od.</title> 5. 455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κήκι^ε</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλκέων_695">, a disyll. by synizesis.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθήρου_695"> refers to the angry appearance of the ulcer, which has not been assuaged (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμερώθη</quote>) by proper treatment; cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 562">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 562</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθηρον τρίχα</quote>: Dioscorides 3. 11. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθηριωμένον ἕλκος</quote>. <title>H. N.</title> 26. 14 <emph>efferantia se ulcera.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ἐμπέσοι_699">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱμάς</quote>. This, the MS. reading, is plainly right. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπίπτω</quote> was regularly used with regard to an attack of disease: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1253"> <title>Tr.</title> 1253</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἐμπεσεῖν σπαραγμόν</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 48">Thuc. 2. 48</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λοιμὸς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τὴν Ἀθηναίων πόλιν ἐξαπιναίως ἐνέπεσε</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 49 <quote xml:lang="grc">λὺγξ τοῖς πλείοσιν ἐνέπιπτε κενή</quote>. Cp. below. 808 (the disease) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξεῖα φοιτᾷ καὶ ταχεἶ ἀπέρχεται</quote>. In the next v. Schneidewin rightly gave </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλών_699"> for the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλεῖν_699">. For the constr. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατευνάσειεν φύλλοις, ἑλὼν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 475">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 475</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρεψον</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοπόκῳ μαλλῷ λαβών</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ἐμπέσοι_699_2">, or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵ_τι_699"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμπέσοι_699">, keeping </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλεῖν_699_2">: ‘if any leaf should fall in his way, to pluck,’ or, ‘if it should be his fortune to pluck any leaf.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπίπτειν</quote> ought to be said of the wanderer, not of a stationary object which he finds. And <quote xml:lang="grc">συμπέσοι</quote> is too suggestive of a ‘coincidence’ to be a fitting word here.—Campbell, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβάδος ἔκ τε γᾶς ἑλεῖν</quote>, takes the const. to be (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἦν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις κατευνάσειεν ἑλεῖν τε</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοι τε</quote>). This is as if one said, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ εἶχεν ὅστις ἔλθοι καὶ βοηθεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορβάδος_699">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 391">cp. 391</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παμβῶτι</quote>: fr. 279 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ὠλένου γῆς φορβάδος κομίζομαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷρπε_701"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_701"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷρπε_701_2"> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 290">cp. 290</ref> n.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰλυόμενος_701">, <emph>ib.</emph> As to the reading in these verses, <ref target="cl686">see on 686</ref> f. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοτε ἀλλαχῇ</quote> occurs in <ref n="Xen. Mem. 1. 4. 12.">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1. 4. 12.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖς_ὡς_703">: like a child that cannot yet walk firmly without the help of its nurse. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 38"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 38</ref>, where the aged priestess, tottering with fear, is said to be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπαις</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθεν_704">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε ὅθεν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 3">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 3</ref>§ 17 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἡμᾶς ἀγάγῃ ὅθεν οὐχ οἶόν τε ἔσται ἐξελθεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθεν_ὑπάρχοι_704">, after <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷρπε ἄν</quote>, answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθεν ἂν ὑπάρχῃ</quote> after a primary tense: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 289">cp. 289</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐμάρεια_πόρου_704">, ‘facility of resource,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the means of supplying his needs. For <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐμάρεια</quote> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 284">cp. 284</ref>; for <quote xml:lang="grc">πόρου</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Alc. 213">Eur. <title> Alc.</title> 213</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἂν πόρος κακῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο</quote>...; He had to find food, water, fuel, and the medicinal herb (285 ff., <ref n="Eur. Alc. 649">Eur. Alc. 649</ref>).—Not, ‘ease on his path’ (ease in movement), as if the search for the herb alone were meant. Some read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρων_704"> as=‘resources’: but, in this sense, the associations of the plur. would have been too prosaic for an Attic poet. For the theory that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρον_704"> should be read, and taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷρπε</quote>, see Appendix on 695 f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξανείη_704">, remit its violence: 639 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δακέθυμος_704">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">δηξίθυμος, θυμοδακής, θυμοβόρος</quote>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερᾶς_706">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 391">cp. 391</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴρων_706"> denotes the simple act of lifting, and is thus more picturesque than <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰρόμενος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1339">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 1339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλπισί τ’ ἐκ ποταμῶν δρόσον ἄρατε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλων_706">, such as fruits, milk, etc.: from <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρων</quote> we supply a word of more general sense. The gen. is partitive ( <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 4. 20">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1. 4. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβὼν τῶν...ἵππων τε καὶ ἀνδρῶν</quote>). This is better than to repeat </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορβὰν_706"> with it (‘food consisting in other things’). Such a constr. would be awkward when <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβὰν</quote> is in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">σπόρον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_706">, relat. (14)= <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἅ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλφησταί_706">. The popular deriv., from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλφι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδ</quote> (‘meal-eating’), may possibly have been in the poet's mind here; though this inference would be stronger if he had placed the word in closer connection with <quote xml:lang="grc">σπόρον</quote>. Curtius, on the other hand, can fairly cite <ref n="Aesch. Seven 771">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 771</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν ἀλφηστᾶν ὄλβος ἄγαν παχυνθείς</quote>, in support of the sense ‘workers,’ ‘earners,’ men who eat their bread in the sweat of their brow (rt <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλφ</quote>, Lat. <emph> lab-os</emph>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέρες_706">, with epic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_706">, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1010"> <title>Tr.</title> 1010</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 869"> <title>O. T.</title> 869</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέρων</quote>. There is a reminiscence of <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.261"> <title>Od.</title> 13. 261</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέρας ἀλφηστάς</quote>, as well as of <emph>ib.</emph> 9. 89 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵτινες ἀνέρες εἶεν ἐπὶ χθονὶ σῖτον ἔδοντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτανοῖς_711"> is a purely poetical image for speed, while the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πτερόεντες ὀϊστοί</quote> more readily suggests the actual feathers on the arrow ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 567"> <title>Tr.</title> 567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κομήτην ἰόν</quote>: Aesch. fr. 135 <quote xml:lang="grc">μηχανὴν πτερώματος</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 274">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξων πτερωτὰς γλυφίδας</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰοῖς_711"> (restored by Brunck) was evidently lost through the likeness of ending in <quote xml:lang="grc">πτανοῖς</quote>. Then the gap was filled by inserting <quote xml:lang="grc">πτανῶν</quote> (to agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξων</quote>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">πτανοῖς</quote> was explained as, ‘with birds’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 168"> <title>Ai.</title> 168</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πτηνῶν ἀγέλαι</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχά_ὃς_713">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.177"> <title>Il.</title> 18. 177</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γὰρ οὐδὲ βίη Ἡρακλῆος φύγε Κῆρα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσπερ φίλτατος ἔσκε Διὶ Κρονίωνι ἄνακτι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 341"> <title>Ant.</title> 341</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_μηδ_713">, ‘<emph>one who</emph> did not’...: the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with causal force: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 170">cp. 170</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥσθη_713">, with a gen., such as follows verbs of enjoying, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολαύω, εὐωχοῦμαι</quote>, etc.: <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.780"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 780</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰρ ἐπεὶ τάρπημεν ἐδητύος ἠδὲ ποτῆτος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰνοχύτου_713">: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰνόχ. πῶμα</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἴνου κεχυμένου π</quote>.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 208">cp. 208</ref> n., <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 66">Eur. <title> Cycl.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρήναις παρ’ ὑδροχύτοις</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεκέτει_χρόνῳ_713">. The simple dat. here denotes the time <emph>within</emph> which a thing has not happened (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 769">cp. 769</ref>). For this sense <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> is usu. added. But, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν χρόνῳ μακρῷ</quote> (235), and <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ μακρῷ</quote> simply (598 n.), can alike mean ‘<emph> after</emph> a time,’ so the use of the simple dat. is extended to that sense for which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> is more specially needed,—‘<emph>within</emph> a time.’ The acc., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεκέτηχρόνον_713">, which Blaydes reads, is less suitable here. The point is that, for ten years, Ph. has not once tasted wine. A prose-writer would usu. express this by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέκα ἐτῶν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 448A">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 448A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς μέ πω ἠρώτηκε καινὸν οὐδὲν πολλῶν ἐτῶν</quote>. In our v., the acc. would rather suggest that Ph. had not had ten years continuous enjoyment of wine. Cp. <ref n="Lys. 19.60">Lys. or. 19 § 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλίγον μὲν χρόνον δύναιτ’ ἄν τις πλάσασθαι τὸν τρόπον τὸν αὑτοῦ</quote> (the dissimulation being continuous): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἑβδομήκοντα δὲ ἔτεσιν οὐδ’ ἂν εἷς λάθοι πονηρὸς ὤν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, at some moment or other <emph> within</emph> the 70 years he will be found out). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεύσσων_716">, absol., looking about him, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_γνοίη_716"> (to see) where he could perceive (stagnant water), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσενώμα_716">, he used to bend his way towards it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ὕδωρ_716"> is joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">προσενώμα</quote>, instead of standing (without <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>) as object to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνοίη_716">. The latter is oblique for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου γνῷ</quote> (delib. subjunct.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 890"> <title>Ai.</title> 890</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα μὴ λεύσσειν ὅπου</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 135"> <title>O. C.</title> 135</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἐγὼ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσων περὶ πᾶν οὔπω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δύναμαι τέμενος γνῶναι ποῦ μοί</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτε ναίει</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσενώμα_716_2"> intrans.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 168">cp. 168</ref> n.—The usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσειν</quote> in Soph. makes this constr. preferable to the other, which is possible: <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσων εἰς στατὸν ὕδωρ</quote> (fixing his gaze on it), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου γνοίη</quote>, <emph>wherever</emph> he might perceive it (oblique of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου ἂν γνῷ</quote>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στατὸν_ὕδωρ_716">, water collected in stagnant pools: cp. Arist. fr. 207 (Berl. ed. p. 1515 <emph>b</emph> 25) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσφατόν ἐστι καὶ νέον ὕδωρ τὸ ὑόμενον, ἕωλον δὲ καὶ παλαιὸν τὸ λιμναῖον</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 108">Her. 2. 108</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλατυτέροισι ἐχρέωντο τοῖσι πόμασι, ἐκ φρεάτων χρεώμενοι</quote> (‘somewhat brackish’). Odysseus remembered a spring near the cave (21), and Ph. speaks of <quote xml:lang="grc">κρῆναι</quote> (1461): but the imagination of the Chorus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τὸ μεῖζον πάντα δεινοῖ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρῶν_ἀγ_718">., Peleus and Achilles; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 384">cp. 384</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπαντήσας_718"> in prose would mean, ‘having come to meet,’ and would take a dat. A poet might feel that the gen. was sufficiently warranted by the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσω γὰρ ἐγὼ τοῦδ’ ἀνέρος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.423"> <title>Il.</title> 16. 423</ref>), etc.: indeed, the gen. differs from the dat. only by its more vivid suggestion of the idea, ‘face to face’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίον τινός</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 320">Cp. 320</ref> n. Here the phrase, ‘having come face to face’ with him, suggests not merely the good fortune of the meeting, but the intercourse,—frank on the side of Philoctetes,—which had followed it.—As Ph. and Neoptolemus are now seen to be leaving the cave, the Chorus once more speaks language designed to support N.'s plan. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύσει_εὐδαίμων_720"> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 177"> <title>Ant.</title> 177</ref>), will finish his course in happiness; =<quote xml:lang="grc">τελευτῶν εὐδαιμονήσει</quote>. (Not, I think, ‘will <emph>succeed in</emph> becoming happy,’ <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενέσθαι</quote>.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_κείνων_720"> (neut.) after those troubles: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 271">cp. 271</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντοπόρῳ_722">: epith. of <quote xml:lang="grc"> ναΐ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 250."> <title>Ai.</title> 250.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δούρατι_722">: the only example of this epic form in Soph. (for <quote xml:lang="grc">δορί</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δόρει</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1304">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1304</ref>). Aesch. has <quote xml:lang="grc">δουρίκλυτος, δουρίπληκτος</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ευρ. δούρατα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰνάλιον δόρυ</quote> (<emph>trabs</emph>), <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 411">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἄλλην</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος ηὔθυνεν δόρυ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλήθει_μηνῶν_722">, <emph>after</emph> the ten years at Lemnos: 598 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρίαν_724"> is prob. a true correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴαν_724">. There is no other instance in Soph. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote> with the 2nd syll. short (though he often shortens <quote xml:lang="grc">αι</quote> before a vowel, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1310</ref>). In Eur. there are a few such instances, but in all of them <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτριος</quote> should be restored, as by Porson in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 78">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 78</ref>(=82 Dind.). As to the sense, either word would serve here: properly, <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρίαν</quote>=ancestral; <quote xml:lang="grc"> πατρῴαν</quote>, belonging to one's father: but Tragedy does not always observe the distinction (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 398">cp. 398</ref> n.: conversely, <ref n="Soph. OC 736"> <title>O. C.</title> 736</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν πατρῴων</quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">πατρίων</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μαλιάδων_724">: the Ionic form (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 4">cp. 4</ref> n.), which the MSS. give, can hardly be kept here: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 688">cp. 688</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιπλάκτων. Μαλ. νυμφᾶν</quote> is more naturally joined with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐλὰν_724"> than (as Cavallin prefers) with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄχθας_724">: ‘his ancestral abode, haunt of the Malian nymphs,’ is a phrase which suggests the hills, woods and streams of Malis. So the nymphs of Helicon ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1109"> <title>O. T.</title> 1109</ref>), Parnassus ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1128"> <title>Ant.</title> 1128</ref>), and Lemnos (below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1454"> Ant. 1454</ref>) are associated with the rural scenery of those places. For <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> in the general sense, ‘abode,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 786"> <title>Ant.</title> 786</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρονόμοις αὐλαῖς</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 259">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 259</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκύων ἐς αὐλάν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ὄχθας_724">. Unless, with Blaydes, we change </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σπερχειοῦ_τε_724"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σπερχειοῖο_724">, the acc. is necessary here. The MSS. give </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄχθαις_724">. For other instances in which the case of the noun after <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> has prob. been corrupted, cp. nn. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 966"> <title>Ant.</title> 966</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1123"> Ant. 1123</ref> f. As to the topography, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 490">cp. 490</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἀνὴρ_727">, Heracles. The epithet has an archaeological interest. In the Homeric poems, when reference is made to the exploits of Heracles, his weapon is the bow ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.395"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 395</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.224"> <title>Od.</title> 8. 224</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.607"> Od. 11. 607</ref>). Some ancient writers, however, expressly say that the equipment of Heracles with bow, club, and lion's skin was a comparatively late invention of the poets, and that in the oldest works of art he was represented with the armour of the ordinary Homeric warrior. According to Strabo (15. 688), the innovation could be traced back to the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡράκλεια</quote>, ascribed to Peisander (<emph>circ.</emph> 650 B.C.): <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἡ τοῦ Ἡρακλέους δὲ στολὴ τοιαύτη πολὺ νεωτέρα τῆς Τρωικῆς μνήμης ἐστί, πλάσμα τῶν τὴν Ἠράκλειαν ποιησάντων, εἴτε Πείσανδρος ἦν, εἴτ’ ἄλλος τις· τὰ δ’ ἀρχαῖα ζόανα οὐχ οὕτω διεσκεύασται</quote> (implying that he had seen old images or statues in which Heracles had armour). Athenaeus (12. 512 F) quotes Megacleides (who wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ Ὁμήρου</quote>, prob. in the 4th cent. B.C.), as referring the invention to Stesichorus (<emph>c.</emph> 620 B.C.), and adding that Xanthus, an earlier lyric poet, had clad Heracles in the Homeric armour:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα πλάσαι πρῶτον Στησίχορον τὸν Ἱμεραῖον. καὶ Ξάνθος δ’ ὁ μελοποιός, πρεσβύτερος ὢν Στησιχόρου,...οὐ ταύτην αὐτῷ περιτίθησι τὴν στολήν, ἀλλὰ τὴν Ὁμηρικήν</quote>. Strabo and Megacleides, then, agree thus far,— that the invention was <emph>not older than</emph> the 7th cent. B.C.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάλκασπις_727">, which suggests the hoplite type of Heracles? The answer seems to turn on two points. (1) A compromise between the hoplite and the archer type of Heracles can sometimes be traced in ancient art. Thus a statue belonging to the east pediment of the Aeginetan temple gives Heracles a helmet (or bonnet) of lion's skin, a bow, and a <quote xml:lang="grc">θώραξ</quote> (Baumeister, <title> Denkm.</title> p. 335: cp. <emph>ib.</emph> p. 652 <emph>a</emph>). Sophocles himself makes a similar compromise when in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 510"> <title>Tr.</title> 510</ref> ff. he arms Heracles with bow, club, and two spears. (2) The Heracles of this play is associated with the legends of Oeta and Trachis. In them, as in those of Boeotia, Heracles was preeminently the warrior, who sacked Oechalia ‘with the spear’ ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 478"> <title>Tr.</title> 478</ref>), and for whom Hephaestus had wrought the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσπίς</quote> described in the Hesiodic poem. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάθει_728">. The aor. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπλα—/θην</quote> is used by Aesch. and Eur. ; and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάθη_728"> (Bergk) is tempting here: but the historic pres. seems confirmed by such examples as <ref n="Soph. OT 113">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 113</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">συμπίπτει</quote>), <emph>ib.</emph> 560 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρει</quote>). Heracles was burned alive, by his own command, on the top of Mount Oeta. As the flames rose, a storm broke forth; and, amid thunder and lightning, the hero was taken up to heaven. <ref n="Apollod. 2.7">Apollod. 2. 7. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιομένης δὲ τῆς πυρᾶς λέγεται</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέφος ὑποστὰν μετὰ βροντῆς αὐτὸν εἰς οὐρανὸν ἀναπέμψαι</quote>. <ref n="Diod. 4. 38. 4">Diod. 4. 38. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεραυνῶν ἐκ τοῦ περιέχοντος πεσόντων ἡ πυρὰ πᾶσα κατεφλέχθη</quote>. By </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θείῳ_παμφαής_728"> the poet probably meant to suggest both the flaming pyre and the splendour of the lightnings.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσι_728">. In the str., 716, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_728"> is clearly sound; and a long syllable is metrically impossible here. Nor can we save </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσι_728_2"> by transposition: both </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάθει_728_2"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θείῳ_728"> are plainly genuine. Hermann's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάθει <emph>θεὸς</emph> </quote>, presupposes that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσι_728_3"> was either a gloss, or an arbitrary substitute for a lost word; but it was more probably a corruption of the true word. Now we might certainly expect here some reference to Zeus. Oeta was sacred to him; his were the lightnings (cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 436"> <title>Tr.</title> 436</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κατ’ ἄκρον Οἰταῖον πάγον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς καταστράπτοντος</quote>); and it was as his son that Heracles entered Olympus. At this moment, above all others, there is a poetical fitness in some allusion to the hero's divine parentage, which is elsewhere made so prominent in the play (802, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 943"> Tr. 943</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1415"> Tr. 1415</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_728"> supplies this touch.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄχθων_729"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθος</quote>), not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀχθῶν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθη</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1132"> <title>Ant.</title> 1132</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_θέλεις_730">, ‘if you please,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δοκεῖ</quote> (526). But <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ βούλει</quote> usu.=‘if you prefer it’ ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 4. 41"> Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 4. 41</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόπληκτος_ἔχει_731">, <emph>attonitus haeres:</emph> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόπλ</quote>., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1189"> <title>Ant.</title> 1189</ref>: for the pass. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔχομαι</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 1140. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστιν_733">; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 753">cp. 753</ref>, 917, <ref n="Soph. OT 319."> <title>O. T.</title> 319.</ref>It does not seem likely that Soph. would have preferred to write </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστιν_733_2"> (with hiatus), though several recent editors give this: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 100">cp. 100</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_παρεστώσης_734">, not, ‘which is upon thee at this moment’ (765 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πῆμα ...τὸ νῦν παρόν</quote>), but rather, ‘which is habitual to thee’: hence the word is not superfluous. Often, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεστώς</quote>, is nearly synonymous with <quote xml:lang="grc">παρών</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1340">cp. 1340</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 633."> <title>O. T.</title> 633.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κουφίζειν_734"> is rare in Attic: in <title>Helen.</title> 1555 <quote xml:lang="grc"> κουφίζοντα</quote>, ‘treading lightly,’ seems (as Paley says) to imply an ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδας</quote>. But in this application (to illness) the phrase may have been familiar, as Hippocr. <ref n="Hp. Epid. 2. 10"> <title>Epid.</title> 2. 10</ref>(quoted by Musgrave) has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκούφισεν ὀλίγῳ</quote>, ‘he became a little better.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_θεοί_734"> (scanned as a bacchius, <quote xml:lang="grc">˘¯¯</quote>) does not receive sufficient emphasis or prominence unless it stands <emph>extra metrum.</emph> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 750">Cp. 750</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’ ὦ παῖ</quote>, and 219. <title>I. T.</title> 780 has been compared: <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΡ. ὦ θεοί. ΙΦ. τί τοὺς θεοὺς ἀνακαλεῖς ἐν τοῖς ἐμοῖς</quote>; But there, as Herm. says, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ θεοί</quote> is quite unlike the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ θεοί</quote> here: it is the rapid utterance of one who fears to betray himself, not a cry of anguish extorted by physical torment. For the absence of caesura, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101">cp. 101</ref>. Cavallin reads </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_θεοί_734_2">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_καλεῖς_734">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1129"> <title>Ai.</title> 1129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νυν ἀτίμα θεούς, θεοῖς σεσωσμένος</quote>. But the art. before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὺς_734">, in which L and A agree, seems genuine here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρῶν_741">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 544">cp. 544</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διέρχεται_743">. In 758 the disease is personified as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη</quote>, in 807 as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>: here the subject might be simply <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote> from 742.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βρύκομαι_743">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 7">cp. 7</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 987"> <title>Tr.</title> 987</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ αὖ μιαρὰ βρύκει</quote> (the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_πρόχειρον_747"> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χεροῖν_747">, if you have any sword ready in your hands. <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρόχειρος</quote> can be combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">χεροῖν</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. El. 696">Soph. <title>El.</title> 696</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόχειρον ἔγχος χειρὶ βαστάζουσ’ ἐμῇ</quote>) without seeming pleonastic, since the derived sense of the compound adj. (<emph> promptus</emph>) is prominent. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 407">Cp. 407</ref> n.: <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 200C">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 200C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μὴ προχείρους ἔχῃ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιστήμας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάταξον_πόδα_747">. The ulcered heel is to be severed from the foot. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρος ποῦς</quote> seems to mean simply, ‘the end of the foot,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> the heel (<quote xml:lang="grc">πτέρνα</quote>), the seat of the ulcer. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 824">Cp. 824</ref>. The phrase could also mean, ‘the foot at the end of the leg,’ as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.640"> <title>Il.</title> 16. 640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ κεφαλῆς εἴλυτο διαμπερὲς ἐς πόδας ἄκρους</quote> (=simply ‘from head <emph>to foot</emph>’): but this is less fitting here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴθ_παῖ_750">, an earnest entreaty: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1468"> <title>O. T.</title> 1468</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’ ὦναξ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’ ὦ γονῇ γενναῖε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεοχμὸν_ἐξαίφνης_751">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1130"> <title>Tr.</title> 1130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίως νεοσφαγής</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1283."> <title>Ant.</title> 1283.</ref>—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου</quote>, causal, with the whole sentence: 327 n.: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαυτοῦ_751"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰυγήν_751">, etc.; object. gen.—I give </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖ_751">, instead of the vulg. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ποεῖς</emph>. ποιεῖσθαι</quote> (midd.) <quote xml:lang="grc"> στόνον</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">στένειν</quote>: whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν στόνον</quote> could mean only, ‘to cause, or excite, it.’ We cannot defend <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖς</quote> here by <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.363"> <title>Il.</title> 15. 363</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιήσῃ</quote> (act.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθύρματα</quote>, which is not a mere periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθύρειν</quote>, but = ‘making playthings’ in the sand,—houses, dykes, etc. Nor can <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὰ ποιῶ</quote> be cited, which is not an equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν ποιοῦμαι</quote>, but means ‘to do dreadful things,’ referring to the outward display of horror or grief by gestures or cries. (Cp. my n. on Andoc. or. 1 § 41.) In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 75">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 75</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεῖ</quote> (midd.) is now read by most edd., L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρηις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_σοί_753">; These words clearly belong to Neopt., and mean, ‘What is the matter with thee?’ The phrase is not a usual one; but it is clear enough here, esp. as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστιν_753"> can easily be carried on. Hermann, giving <quote xml:lang="grc">τί σοί</quote> to Philoctetes, took it as meaning ‘What is that to thee?’ (<emph>quid tua refert?</emph>)—a protest against closer questioning. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπίσαγμα_755">. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπισάττειν</quote> is classical as=‘to put a load on’ a baggageanimal, or ‘to saddle’ a horse (Her. , Xen. , etc.): and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπίσαγμα</quote> was a common word, at least in later Greek, as may be inferred from the schol. on <title>Nub.</title> 450 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσαγμα τῶν ὄνων</quote>), and from its use by the LXX. (Lev. xv. 9). In the marg. of L the gl. is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐπείσοδος· ἡ προσθήκη</quote>. The second word suits <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐπίσαγμα</quote>: the first refers to the <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπείσαγμα_755">, in the sense of ‘access.’ But such a word is neither extant nor conceivable. Bergk's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπίσιγμα_755"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισίζω</quote>), ‘hounding on,’ would mean here, ‘exasperation,’—as if some Fury were stimulating the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσημα</quote>. The word was used by Soph. in his <title>Athamas</title>, acc. to an amended gloss in Hesychius ( Soph. fr. 8). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_756">=‘indeed,’ in assent; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1117"> <title>O. T.</title> 1117</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράσω_756">: aor. subj. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_ἐξεπλήσθη_758">. Ph. fears that the sight of his horrible sufferings may deter Neopt. from taking him on board. He says,—‘Do not be scared into abandoning me. For this tormentor (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὕτη_758">, the personified <quote xml:lang="grc"> νόσος</quote>) comes only now and then (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_χρόνου_758">),—when she has been sated, haply, with her roamings.’ And so—since the voyage to Greece will take less than one whole day (480)—he is not likely to have an attack while at sea. Three points deserve notice. (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_758">=‘is wont to come,’—a sense which is as fitting for it as for a regular perfect tense used in the ‘gnomic’ manner (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄπωπε</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1126"> Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1126</ref>). So in <ref n="Plat. Sym. 188A"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 188A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκει</quote> is joined to the gnomic aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδίκησεν</quote>: and in <ref n="Xen.
