Ec. 21. 3">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 21. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβαίνουσιν...ἥκουσι</quote> denotes a repeated occurrence. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_χρόνου_758_2">, ‘after an interval of time,’ implies here, as it usually does, that the interval is a considerable one: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 285">cp. 285</ref> n., where <ref n="Lys. 1.12">Lys. or. 1 § 12</ref> is cited. (3) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάνοις_758"> is contrasted with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_758_2">. The word was suggested by the fact that intermittent fevers (etc.) were called <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάνητες</quote> (Hippocr. <ref n="Hp. Epid. 1. 944"> <title>Epid.</title> 1. 944</ref>). The term implied that the intervals were irregular: cp. Erotian <title>Gloss.</title> p. 306 (quoted by Arndt) <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάνητες πυρετοὶ λέγονται οἱ μὴ κατὰ τάξιν φοιτῶντες</quote>. This may be illustrated by the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πλανᾶσθαι</quote> in <ref n="Hdt. 6. 52">Her. 6. 52</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν δὲ πλανᾶται...ἐναλλὰξ ποιεῦσα</quote> (‘if she is <emph>capricious</emph>, varying the order’— opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ ταὐτὰ αἰεὶ ποιεῦσα</quote>). So id. 7. 16. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνύπνια...τὰ ἐς ἀνθρώπους πεπλανημένα</quote> (‘the dreams which are wont at times to visit men’). It was easy, then, for the poet to imagine the fitful <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> as a personified wanderer, who, when sated with wandering, comes back to her abode:—much as Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 275">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 275</ref>) speaks of calamity ‘roaming’ among men: <quote xml:lang="grc">πλανωμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄλλοτ’ ἄλλον πημονὴ προσιζάνει</quote>. Cp. below, <ref n="Aesch. PB 808">Aesch. P. V. 808</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξεῖα φοιτᾷ καὶ ταχεἶ ἀπέρχεται</quote>. So the schol., who explains <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάνοις</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιπορίαις</quote>:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκει ἡ νόσος, ἴσως ὅτε ἐκορέσθη πλανωμένη· ὡς ἐπὶ θηρὸς δὲ ποιεῖται τὸν λόγον</quote>. This is clearly better than to understand,—‘when it has once been sated, it returns only after a long interval,—in wandering fashion, seemingly’ (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάνοις_758_2"> being then a modal dat.).—For conjectures, see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεπλήσθη_ἰώ_758">. There is no other example of such a hiatus in a tragic trimeter. (As to lyrics, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 832">cp. 832</ref>, 851.) Probably, however, the text is sound. The verse is divided between two speakers, there is a full stop after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεπλήσθη</quote>, and the second speaker begins with an interjection. Thus the hiatus has an exceptional excuse. On the other hand no emendation is probable. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_758"> (instead of the first </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰώ_758">) is certainly not so, whether it be given to Ph. or Neoptolemus. Gaisford says, ‘</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέπλης_758">’, ut videtur, conj. Elmsleius.’ This would require us to read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάνους_758">, or (keeping <quote xml:lang="grc"> πλάνοις</quote>) to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτούς</quote>. But the context strongly confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεπλήσθη</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτα_758">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1163"> <title>El.</title> 1163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς μ’ ἀπώλεσας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπώλεσας δῆτ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστηνε_φανείς_758">: the predicative adj. is assimilated to the vocative partic. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 828">Cp. 828</ref> n.: <quote xml:lang="grc">αεσξη. <title>περς.</title>ὦ πολύκλαυτε φίλοισι θανών</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1221">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1221</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ τ’ ὦ ποτ’ οὖσα καλλίνικε μυρίων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆτερ τροπαίων</quote>. Propert. 2. 15. 2 <title>Lectule deliciis facte</title> beate <emph>meis.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_πάντων_758">, ‘<emph>in</emph> all manner of troubles,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘in the course’ of them: <ref n="Soph. OT 773"> <title>O. T.</title> 773</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τύχης τοιᾶσδ’ ἰών</quote>. <title>I. T.</title> 988 <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ πόνων τ’ ἄγει</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δαίμων</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούλει_λάβωμαι_762"> ..; <ref n="Soph. El. 80"> <title>El.</title> 80</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλεις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μείνωμεν</quote>...; This idiom is a parataxis of two questions originally distinct: <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλει; λάβωμαι</quote>; Where the subjunctive stands first, as in <ref n="Dem. 14.27">Dem. or. 14 § 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θῶ βούλεσθε</quote>...; the verb of wishing might seem to be parenthetic. But such an example as <ref n="Plat. Rep. 372C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 372C</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...βούλεσθε... θεωρήσωμεν, οὐδὲν ἀποκωλύει</quote>, shows that the subjunctive had come to be felt as depending on the verb of wishing. In classical Greek no conjunction could be used to link the verbs, since <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλομαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλω</quote> took only the inf. In later Greek we still have <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλεις ποιήσω</quote>; (St Luke xviii. 41:) but also <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλω ἵνα δῷς</quote> (St Mark vi. 25). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτα_762"> has been suspected here, because it occurs in 757, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 760">Plat. Rep. 760</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 763.">Plat. Rep. 763.</ref>Nauck would remove it by rewriting the passage thus:—<quote xml:lang="grc">βούλει λάβωμαι καὶ θίγω; ΦΙΛ. μὴ τοῦτό γε</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὥσπερ ᾔτου μ’ ἀρτίως, τὰ τόξ’ ἑλών</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἀνῇ τὸ πῆμα τοῦτο τῆς νόσου</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σῷζ’ αὐτὰ καὶ φύλασσε</quote>. But here, as in 757, it is interrogative, while in 760 and 763 it is otherwise used; and this difference of usage palliates the iteration. Cp. the threefold <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> in 645, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 647">Plat. Rep. 647</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 651">Plat. Rep. 651</ref>: also <ref n="Soph. OT 517">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 517</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρον</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 519">Soph. O. T. 519</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέροντι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 520">Soph. O. T. 520</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρει</quote>, where the excuse is the same as here, viz. that, in the 1st and 3rd places the word means ‘tend,’ but in the 2nd, ‘bear.’ No weight attaches to the fact that the 1st hand in L accidentally omitted <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆτα</quote>, which the reviser added. In 772 L lacks <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> altogether; and yet that word is certainly sound. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_763">: ethic dat.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1475"> <title>O. C.</title> 1475</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕως_764"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 148"> <title>Tr.</title> 148</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 555."> <title>Ai.</title> 555.</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 917">Cp. 917</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνῇ_764">: 639 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_νόσου_765">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 363"> <title>Ai.</title> 363</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πῆμα τῆς ἄτης</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 850">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 850</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῆὐ ἀποστρέψαι νόσου</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_οὖν_766">: ‘for indeed’ (prefacing an explanation); <ref n="Soph. Ant. 489."> <title>Ant.</title> 489.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξίῃ_766">, draw to an end: <ref n="Hdt. 2. 139">Her. 2. 139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὖν ὁ χρόνος οὗτος ἐξήιε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῆξαι_768">. The subject to the inf. is <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κακόν</quote>. When the pain is subsiding (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξίῃ</quote>), the patient falls asleep; and it is only by sleep that the pain can be wholly allayed (<quote xml:lang="grc"> λῆξαι</quote>). The schol. explains <quote xml:lang="grc">λῆξαι</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ὀδύνης παύσασθαι</quote>, as if the subject were <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>: but where <quote xml:lang="grc"> λήγω</quote> is so used the gen. is commonly added, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 274"> <title>Ai.</title> 274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔληξε...τῆς νόσου</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕκηλον_εὕδειν_769">. <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> is easily supplied from 767; the omission is thus less bold than that in 801 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote>).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδετῷ_χρόνῳ_769">, within it; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 715">cp. 715</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεκέτει χρόνῳ</quote>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖνοι_769">: Odysseus and Diomedes (570). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκόντα_ἄκοντα_771">. A <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> is understood before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκόντα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 532">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 532</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πάρις γὰρ οὔτε συντελὴς πόλις</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OT 236">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 236</ref> ff. (n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 267.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 267.</ref>Dindorf changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_771"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_771">. This is, of course, admissible. When a single <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote>) connects two words, the negative force is more often, indeed, confined to the second, as in 756 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν γὰρ οὐδὲ ῥητόν</quote>. But there are also many examples in which <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> negatives the preceding word also: as <ref n="Thuc. 8. 99">Thuc. 8. 99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ Φοίνισσαι νῆες οὐδὲ ὁ Τισσαφέρνης...ἧκον</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 694">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 694</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ δ’ οὐδ’ ἀὴρ οὐδ’ οὐρανὸς ἦν</quote>. Where, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> is thus retrospective, another negative (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote>) is usu. joined to the verb: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 215">Her. 1. 215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιδήρῳ δὲ οὐδ’ ἀργύρῳ χρέωνται οὐδέν</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 6. 55">Thuc. 6. 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεσσαλοῦ μὲν οὐδ’ Ἱππάρχου οὐδεὶς παῖς γέγραπται</quote> (add id. 5. 47 cited below): <ref n="Dem. 22.4">Dem. or. 22 § 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁπλοῦν μὲν οὐδὲ δίκαιον οὐδὲν ἂν εἰπεῖν ἔχοι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτε_τέχνῃ_771">. Here again Dindorf writes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέ_771">. Note that, whether <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote> be read, it does not here balance the preceding <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote>), since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκ. μήτ’ ἄκ</quote>.=(<quote xml:lang="grc">μήθ’</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκ. μήτ’ ἄκ</quote>.: hence we might read <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>μήτ’</emph> ἄκοντα</quote>, and yet <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>μηδέ</emph> τῳ τέχνῃ</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 426B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 426B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε φάρμακα οὔτε καύσεις οὔτε τομαὶ οὐδ’ αὖ ἐπῳδαί</quote>. But it is needless to alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτε_771">.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέχνῃ_771">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 752">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 752</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοίᾳ τέχνῃ</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 5. 47">Thuc. 5. 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλα μὴ ἐξέστω ἐπιφέρειν...τέχνῃ μηδὲ μηχανῇ μηδεμιᾷ</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 5. 16">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4. 5. 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεῖτο αὐτῶν πάσῃ τέχνῃ καὶ μηχανῇ μὴ ἀπολείπεσθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόστροπον_773">: in this sense only here and in <ref n="Soph. OT 41."> <title>O. T.</title> 41.</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 470">Cp. 470</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέτης ἱκνοῦμαι</quote>: 930: <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν προστρόπαιον, τὸν ἱκέτην.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνας_γένῃ_773">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1067">cp. 1067</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 588"> <title>Ai.</title> 588</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ προδοὺς ἡμᾶς γένῃ</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Soph. 217C">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 217C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...ἀπαρνηθεὶς γένῃ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προνοίας_οὕνεκ_774">. One MS. (B) adds </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_774"> to <quote xml:lang="grc">προνοίας</quote>. Where </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕνεκα_774"> or <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἕνεκα</quote> has this sense (‘so far as’ a thing ‘is concerned’), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_774"> is certainly frequent: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 857"> <title>O. T.</title> 857</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντείας γ’...οὕνεκ’</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 387"> <title>El.</title> 387</ref> and 605 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδέ γ’ οὕνεκ’</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 22"> <title>O. C.</title> 22</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνου μὲν οὕνεκ’</quote>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> is equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>. On the other hand in <ref n="Soph. El. 787"> <title>El.</title> 787</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν τῆσδ’ ἀπειλῶν οὕνεχ’</quote>, no MS. has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπειλῶν γ’</quote>. And here the emphasis of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> is not required.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλὴν_κἀμοί_774">: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, as I receive them from thee, so to thee alone will I give them up. They shall pass between no hands save thine and mine. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 668">Cp. 668</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δόντι δοῦναι</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_τύχῃ_774">, a poet. equiv. for the familiar <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχῃ ἀγαθῇ</quote> (<emph>quod bene vertat</emph>): <ref n="Plat. Sym. 177E">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 177E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχῃ ἀγαθῇ καταρχέτω Φαῖδρος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 138">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθεῖν δ’ Ὀρέστην δεῦρο σὺν τύχῃ τινὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεύχομαί σοι</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1723">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1723</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περιπέτεσθε μάκαρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μάκαρι σὺν τύχῃ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πρόσκυσον_776">, do reverence (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 657">cp. 657</ref>) to the divine jealousy, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, propitiate it by some gesture or word showing that you fear it. To hold the bow—though only as a temporary loan— was an honour so high that it might well excite that <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόνος θεῶν</quote> which resents too great <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐτυχία</quote> in men. <ref n="Pind. I. 6">Pind. <title> I.</title> 6. 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ ἀθανάτων μὴ θρασσέτω φθόνος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι τερπνὸν ἐφάμερον</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 904">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 904</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόνος δ’ ἀπέστω· πολλὰ γὰρ τὰ πρὶν κακὰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνειχόμεσθα</quote>: id. <ref n="Aesch. PB 936"> Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 936</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ προσκυνοῦντες τὴν Ἀδράστειαν σοφοί</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέμεσιν</quote>). <ref n="Plat. Rep. 451A">Plat. <title> Rep.</title> 451A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκυνῶ δὲ Ἀδράστειαν...χάριν</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ μέλλω λέγειν</quote>. I do not write <quote xml:lang="grc">Φθόνον</quote>, since it seems unnecessary to assume a definite personification: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 436">cp. 436</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεμος</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_γενέσθαι_777"> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσκυσον</quote> as on a verb of praying. (This is simpler than to make the inf. epexegetic ‘so that,’ etc.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύπονα_777">. Ph. speaks as if his own sufferings in Lemnos, and the various trials of Heracles, were due to the bow, once Apollo's: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as if its mortal owners had been punished by jealous gods for the excessive goodfortune of possessing it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_ὅπως_777">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγένετο</quote>, in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">συνήνεγκε</quote>, turn out as they did for me. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1124"> <title>O. C.</title> 1124</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σοι θεοὶ πόροιεν ὡς ἐγὼ θέλω</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοί_779">: for the synizesis <ref n="Soph. Phil. 196">cp. 196</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένοιτο_δὲ_779">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 633">cp. 633</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτανῷν_779">: the vague phrase covers his secret prayer,—that, sharing the possession of the bow with Ph. , he may also share the victory over Troy (115).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεὐσταλής_779">, well-sped, expeditious: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 516">cp. 516</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅποι_ποτὲ_779"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: with the same ambiguity as in 529. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_Ὄκνος_782"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The MSS. have </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_ἀτελὴςεὐχή_782">. Camerarius conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν δέδοικα μὴ μάτην εὔχῃ, τέκνον</quote>, which Cavallin prints. Wecklein gives <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα δ’, ὦ παῖ, μὴ μάτην εὔχῃ τάδε</quote>,...in which <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε</quote> is his own, and <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα δ’</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ δέδοικ’</quote>) is Neue's. The conjecture in the text is my own. I differ from Camerarius in holding that the traditional </gloss>
lemma:
ἀλλὰ δέδοικ’, ὦ παῖ, μή μ’ ἀτελὴς
							εὐχή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παῖ_782"> is genuine, and from Neue in holding that the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_782"> is genuine also. The spurious word is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδοικ_782">, a gloss upon some rarer expression in the same sense, as Hermann saw; who wrote, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὔ τί σοι, παῖ, μὴ )τελὴς εὐχὴ πέλῃ</quote>. First, as to metre. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ δέδοικ’, ὦ παῖ, μή μ’ ἀτελὴς εὐχή</quote> can be read as a dochmiac dimeter, though of an unusual type (cp. J.H.Heinrich Schmidt, <title>Rhythmic and Metric</title>, p. 77). But they cannot be construed: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_782"> can be only </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_782">: and, though we read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔχῃ_ἀτελὴς_782"> (or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτελῶς_782">) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔχῃ_782"> could not mean, ‘lest thou pray vainly <emph>on my behalf.</emph>’ An iambic trimeter is required here. On this point recent edd. and critics are practically unanimous. In the whole passage from 730 to 826 the series of trimeters is otherwise unbroken, save by those brief cries of Ph. which occur ‘extra metrum’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 785">785</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 787">787</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 790">790</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 796">796</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 804">804</ref>). A solitary dochmiac dimeter is here inconceivable. The corruption of the trimeter began with the loss of the last word, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1301"> <title>Ant.</title> 1301</ref> the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">πέριξ</quote> came from <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ ξίφει</quote>. Among the words suggested are <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλῃ, τύχῃ, κυρῇ, μένῃ, τάδ’ ᾖ, τάδε, τέκνον</quote>. Of these, <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχῃ</quote> alone has any resemblance to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχή</quote>: but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_782"> might easily have dropped out after <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχηι</quote>. For the phrase cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 652"> <title>O. C.</title> 652</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μάλιστ’ ὄκνος σ’ ἔχει</quote>; Next, as to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτέλεστ_782">. An ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀτελὴς εὐχή</quote> would be too harsh: we must read <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχῃ</quote>. Again, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀτελὴς εὔχῃ</quote> could not mean, ‘lest thou pray in vain.’ In <ref n="Pind. P. 5">Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 5. 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτελὴς ...μαντεύμασιν</quote> is said of the <emph> god.</emph> On the other hand cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.570"> <title>Od.</title> 8. 570</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δέ κεν θεὸς ἢ τελέσειεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ κ’ ἀτέλεστ’ εἴη</quote>. And when <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΥΧΗΙ</quote> had become <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχή, ΑΤΕΛΕΣΤ</quote> would easily become <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτελής</quote>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> being taken for an intruded <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>.— See Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_782"> followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_782"> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 933">cp. 933</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1388"> <title>O. T.</title> 1388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ ἀποκλῇσαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1169"> <title>El.</title> 1169</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀπολείπεσθαι</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 85">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 85</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ ἀδικεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Tro. 981">Eur. <title> Tro.</title> 981</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀμαθεῖς ποίει θεάς</quote>. Most edd. now write <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀδικεῖν</quote>, etc., assuming synizesis, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">μἀδικεῖν</quote> (crasis), or <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )δικεῖν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηκῖον_784">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 696">cp. 696</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηκιομέναν</quote>, n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέον_784"> with a sinister sense: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 554">cp. 554</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέα</quote>, 560 <quote xml:lang="grc">νεώτερον</quote>, 751 <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοχμόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐργάσει_786">=<quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλεις ἐργάσεσθαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 441">cp. 441</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρεῖς</quote>, 581 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξει</quote>. The fut. is better suited than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐργάζει</quote> to the presentiment of agony (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέρπει_786">). For the latter cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1010"> <title>Tr.</title> 1010</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧπταί μου...ἥδ’ αὖθ’ ἕρπει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλας_788">, nom.; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 753"> <title>O. C.</title> 753</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τάλας ἐγώ</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχετε_788">, ‘know,’ as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 9"> <title>Ant.</title> 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις τι κεἰσήκουσας;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδαμῇ_788"> is supported by L here, and is not less fitting than <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμῶς</quote>, which Blaydes desires. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1104"> <title>O. C.</title> 1104</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κεφαλλήν_791">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 263">cp. 263</ref> f., n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_791"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχοιτο_791">, cleave to thee, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαμπερὲς_στέρνων_791">, piercing thy breast (and not merely thy foot). <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">σου</quote>, is needed here, where there is a contrast between the actual sufferer and the man to whom he wishes the plague transferred. If we read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σου_791">, the chief emphasis would fall on <quote xml:lang="grc">διαμπερὲς στέρνων</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλ_αὖθις_793">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1316"> <title>O. T.</title> 1316</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι μάλ’ αὖθις.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_στρ_793">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 264">cp. 264</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀγάμεμνον_Μενέλαε_793">. A proper name excuses an anapaest in any foot except the 6th (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1"> <title>O. C.</title> 1</ref>). The fact that this licence has been used in the 1st foot is no reason why it should not recur in the same v., if, as here, a second proper name requires it. We need not assume, then, with Hermann, that the second anapaest marks a laxity peculiar to the later period of tragedy. Blaydes conjectures, and Nauck adopts, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μενέλαέ_τε_793">—to the detriment, surely, of the verse.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ἄν_793"> with optat., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 551">cp. 551</ref> n.: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφοιτε_793">, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 503"> <title>Ai.</title> 503</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λατρείας...τρέφει</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_χρόνον_793">. Here, again, the anapaest has been impugned, on the ground that it ought to be contained in a single word. But, as a prep. and its case are excepted from this rule ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 898">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 898</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῷδε δ’ ἠγόρευε Διομήδης ἄναξ</quote>), so also are an art. and its noun. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Θάνατε_797">. So Aeschylus, too, made Philoctetes invoke Death: fr. 250 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Θάνατε παιάν, μή μ’ ἀτιμάσῃς μολεῖν·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνος γὰρ εἶ σὺ τῶν ἀνηκέστων κακῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰατρός</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1220"> <title>O. C.</title> 1220</ref>(Death as the last <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίκουρος</quote>), and <ref n="Soph. Aj. 854."> <title>Ai.</title> 854.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀεὶ_ἦμαρ_797">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 681"> <title>O. C.</title> 681</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλλει...κατ’ ἦμαρ ἀεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νάρκισσος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύνᾳ_797">, admitted in Attic verse as an equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">δύνασαι</quote> (Porson <ref n="Eur. Hec. 253"> <title>Hec.</title> 253</ref>): in prose it is postclassical. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 849">Cp. 849</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_γενναῖον_799">. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.55"> <title>Il.</title> 6. 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πέπον ὦ Μενέλαε</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1271">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1271</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Πεισθέταιῤ, ὦ μακάρἰ, ὦ σοφώτατε</quote> . <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 266">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ κάλλιστον ὦ Κυκλώπιον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_799">, hortative: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 230">cp. 230</ref>, 950. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_πυρὶ_800">: you fire, <emph>famed as</emph> Lemnian; <quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ ὃ Λήμνιον ἀνακαλοῦσι</quote>:—the volcano Mosychlus, which was always associated with Lemnos, and which had given rise to the proverb <quote xml:lang="grc">Λήμνιον πῦρ</quote>. One meaning of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακαλεῖν</quote> is ‘to call to’ a person by his name: <ref n="Thuc. 7. 70">Thuc. 7. 70</ref>§ 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακαλοῦντες ὀνομαστὶ τὸν τριήραρχον</quote>. Hence the verb is sometimes joined with appellatives, as <ref n="Thuc. 1. 3">Thuc. 1. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δαναοὺς...ἐν τοῖς ἔπεσι...ἀνακαλεῖ</quote> (Homer <emph>designates</emph> the Greeks <emph>as</emph> Danai): <ref n="Soph. El. 693">Soph. <title>El.</title> 693</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖος μὲν ἀνακαλούμενος</quote>.—Not: ‘Yon Lemnian fire, which is so famous’ (as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακαλουμένῳ</quote>, by itself, could mean ‘celebrated’): nor, ‘yon Lemnian fire which is invoked by me.’ There is thus no difficulty in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνακαλουμένῳ</quote> when rightly understood, while the proposed substitutes (cr. n.) are all unsatisfactory.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπρησον_801">: the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> is somewhat bold here: but <ref n="Soph. Phil. 769">cp. 769</ref>, 1368: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 496"> <title>Ai.</title> 496</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφῇς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 461"> <title>O. T.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λάβῃς</quote> (<emph> sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>). If we read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρί_μ_801"> |, the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπισυναλοιφή</quote> might be defended by the elision of <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’, τ’</quote>, and once <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 332"> <title>O. T.</title> 332</ref>) at the end of a verse ( <ref n="Soph. OT 29"> <title>O. T.</title> 29</ref> n.). But the fact seems to be that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συλλαβὼν_801"> in 799, which at once suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>, excuses the absence of the pron. here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_παῖδ_802">, Heracles: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 727">cp. 727</ref> f. n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῴζεις_802">, as their temporary guardian: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 766">cp. 766</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_802">; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπρῆσαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 670">cp. 670</ref>. Heracles was conveyed to the summit of Oeta by his son Hyllus, who helped to make the pyre, but refused to kindle it ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1214"> <title>Tr.</title> 1214</ref>). It was kindled, acc. to one account, by Philoctetes; acc. to another, by his father Poeas. The former version was naturally preferred where the aim of the legend was to honour Philoctetes, since thus he inherited the bow directly from Heracles: and, since Philoctetes was a more important figure than Poeas, this was the prevailing account. The other version, which made Poeas the kindler, had a recommendation of a different kind in the eyes of mythologists who aimed at a strict chronology,—viz., that the episode was thus confined to the generation before the Trojan war. Tzetzes, in his scholia on Lycophron, gives the first version in one place (on vv. 914 ff.), and the second in another (on v. 50).—Cp. <title>Met</title>, 9. 229 <title>At tu</title>, <title>Iovis inclyta proles</title>, | <title>Arboribus caesis quas ardua gesserat Oete</title> | <title>Inque pyram structis</title> , <emph>arcus pharetramque capacem</emph> | <title>Regnaque visuras iterum Troiana sagittas</title> | <title>Ferre iubes</title> Poeante satum; <emph>quo flamma ministro</emph> | <title>Subdita.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπηξίωσα_802">, brought myself to do it, here almost=<quote xml:lang="grc">έτόλμησα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1273"> <title>El.</title> 1273</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλτάταν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδὸν ἐπαξιώσας...φανῆναι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρᾶν_802"> with double acc., as 315, 918, 924, 940. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_παῖ_804">; Neopt. has no answer for the prayer, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote>. A genuine pity for the sufferer is beginning to move him; and he knows that, if the plot succeeds, this wretched man will be carried to the place which he most dreads. He remains silent.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_ὤν_804">, mentally: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 42"> <title>Ant.</title> 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ γνώμης ποτ’ εἶ</quote>; (n.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_δὴ_806">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 589">cp. 589</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὶ_κακά_806">, the ills which lie on thee: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 981"> <title>Tr.</title> 981</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπί μοι μελέῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βάρος ἄπλετον· ἐμμέμονεν φρήν</quote>. Not, ‘the ills which have <emph>come upon</emph> thee,’ as though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκοντα</quote> could be understood ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1472"> <title>O. C.</title> 1472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκει τῷδ’ <emph> ἐπ’</emph> ἀνδρὶ...τελεντή</quote>). Nor, ‘the ills in thy case.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἴσχε_807">, have good hope <emph>also</emph> (as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγος</quote>): for, as the access of the malady is sharp, so it will also be transient.—Nauck enfeebles the sense by changing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_807"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_807"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοιτᾷ_807">, of periodical visitations: <ref n="Hes. WD 103">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦσοι</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόματοι φοιτῶσι</quote>: <title>An. Hist.</title> 7. 3 (p. 583 <emph>a</emph> 26 Berl. ed.) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ</quote>... <quote xml:lang="grc">καθάρσεις φοιτῶσι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξεῖα_ταχεῖα_807"> adverbially: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 526">cp. 526</ref>, 1080. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μήν_811">. In this formula, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήν, ἀλλὰ μήν, μήν</quote> is properly adversative (‘however’): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴν ἴσην γ’ ἔτεισεν</quote>. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">μήν</quote> is like ‘nay,’ or ‘well’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the thought implied is, ‘I should prefer a promise on oath; however, I do not like to ask for it.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνορκον_θέσθαι_811">, = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκῳ πιστῶσαι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 276"> <title>O. T.</title> 276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ μ’ ἀραῖον ἔλαβες</quote>. So Oed. to Theseus, in a like case: <ref n="Soph. OC 650"> <title>O. C.</title> 650</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι σ’ ὑφ’ ὅρκου γ’ ὡς κακὸν πιστώσομαι</quote>, where see n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_812">, (be sure) that: 117 n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέμις_812"> receives a slight emphasis from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_812">: ‘it is needless for me to take an oath: even if I wished to leave thee, it is not <emph>lawful</emph> for me to do so.’ By </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέμις_812_2"> Philoctetes understands the youth's sense of duty towards a suppliant (773): the spectators know that Neopt. is thinking of the oracle (841).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὔστι_812">: so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1225"> <title>Ai.</title> 1225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μοὔστι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μοί )στι</quote> L). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμβαλλε_813"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Here Philoctetes receives this pledge in place of an oath. In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1181"> <title>Tr.</title> 1181</ref> ff. the intense anxiety of Heracles is marked by the fact that he exacts from Hyllus, first the <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιά</quote>, and then the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὅρκος</quote>:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμβαλλε χεῖρα δεξιὰν πρώτιστά μοι</quote> :—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμνυ Διὸς νῦν τοῦ με φύσαντος κάρα</quote>. When belligerents had taken oaths to a treaty, the hand-pledge followed, as the seal of mutual confidence: it was the moral sanction added to the religious. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 2. 3. 28">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 2. 3. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοσαν καὶ δεξιὰς ἔδοσαν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖσε_μ_814">. On leaving the cave with Neopt., Ph. had moved a few steps on the path leading down the cliffs to the shore. When the first attack of the disease came on (732), he stopped. The second attack (782) found him stationary in the same spot. A third is now beginning; and he begs Neopt. to take him </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖσε_814">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, up to the cave, where he will at least have the couch of leaves (33) to rest upon. Neopt. does not understand that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖσε_814_2"> means, to the cave: so Ph. adds, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνω_814">. Neopt. has meanwhile taken hold of Ph. , fearing that he may fall, or throw himself, from the cliffs (1001): his speech and manner show a fresh frenzy of agony (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραφρονεῖς_αὖ_814">), and his rolling eyes are upturned to the sky (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸνἄνω_κύκλον_814">). The mere touch of the youth's hands is torture to the sufferer (817): and Neopt. releases him the moment that he seems to be recovering self-mastery (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_φρονεῖς_814">). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_815">, as at 732 ff., 782 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_κύκλον_815">, the vault of the sky (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν κύκλον πάντα τοῦ οὐρανοῦ</quote>, <ref n="Hdt. 1. 131">Her. 1. 131</ref>): cp. <title>Au.</title> 1715 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀσμὴ δ’ ἀνωνόμαστος ἐς βάθος κύκλου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖ</quote>.—Not, ‘the orb of the sun’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡλίου κύκλος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 416"> <title>Ant.</title> 416</ref>, fr. 668). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_816">, <emph>tandem aliquando:</emph> 1041, <ref n="Soph. OT 335."> <title>O. T.</title> 335.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπό_ὀλεῖς_816">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1177">cp. 1177</ref>. Such tmesis, though frequent in tragic lyrics, is rarer in dialogue: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 432"> <title>Ant.</title> 432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δέ νιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρώμεθ’</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1047"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1047</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκ τοί με τήξεις</quote>: perh. parodied by <title>Vesp.</title> 784 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά τοί με πείθεις</quote>: id. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 295"> <title>Ach.</title> 295</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά σε χώσομεν</quote> : <title>Plut.</title> 65 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό σ’ ὀλῶ κακὸν κακῶς</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μεθίημ_818">, I <emph>do</emph> release thee: <ref n="Soph. OC 31"> <title>O. C.</title> 31</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_δὴ_818">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> nearly= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 968"> <title>O. T.</title> 968</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέον_φρονεῖς_818">, art more <emph>sane.</emph> Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 81"> <title>Ai.</title> 81</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμηνότ’ ἄνδρα...ὀκνεῖς ἰδεῖν;—φρονοῦντα γάρ νιν οὐκ ἂν ἐξέστην</quote>: and <emph> ib.</emph> 344 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνὴρ φρονεῖν ἔοικεν</quote> (when Ajax is ‘in his right mind’ again). The 1st hand in L wrote here, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὴ μεθείημι</quote> (<emph>sic</emph>): <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δὴ πλέον φρονεῖς</quote>. No MS. has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_818">, which Hermann restored. But it has not been noticed that the mis-spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθείημι</quote> in L may have been due to the fact that its archetype had <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθίημ’ εἰ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανάσιμον_819">, proleptic, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 516"> <title>Ai.</title> 516</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μητέρ’ ἄλλη μοῖρα τὸν φύσαντά τε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καθεῖλεν Ἅιδου θανασίμους οἰκήτορας</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δ’ ἀνάγκᾳ μιν φίλον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τις ἐὼν μεγαλάνωρ ἔσανεν</quote> (so as to make him a friend).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_ἔχω_819">, forthwith: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1108"> <title>Ant.</title> 1108</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’ ὡς ἔχω στείχοιμ’ ἄν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθοῦσθαι_819">, here, to <emph>be</emph> (not to become) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθός</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘to stand upright’: cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 8. 8. 10"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8. 8. 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρονται, ἐπειδὰν μηκέτι δύνωνται ὀρθούμενοι ἐξιέναι</quote> (‘on their own feet’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_χρόνου_821">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 397"> <title>O. C.</title> 397</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥξοντα βαιοῦ κοὐχὶ μυρίου χρόνου</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέ_τοι_823">, as <ref n="Soph. OC 1324"> <title>O. C.</title> 1324</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 534"> <title>Ai.</title> 534</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1212"> <title>Tr.</title> 1212</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">γέ τοι δή</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1171."> <title>O. T.</title> 1171.</ref>Here <quote xml:lang="grc">γέ τοι</quote> is like <quote xml:lang="grc">γοῦν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it gives a reason for their belief. (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 767">Cp. 767</ref>.) ‘He seems likely to fall asleep soon, since (<quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>) his head is sinking back; at any rate, a sweat is certainly breaking out,’ etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρον_ποδὸς_824">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 748">cp. 748</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φλέψ_824">, not a vein of the body, but the thin stream in which the blood issues: cp. Polyb. 34. 9 (the removal of an obstruction) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθεροῖ τὰς φλέβας τῆς πηγῆς, ὥστ’ ἀναβλύειν εὐπόρως</quote>. So Martial 10. 30. 10 <title>Lucrina vena.</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμασι_ἀντίσχοις_830">, and keep before his eyes, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάνδ_τανῦν_830">, this light which is spread before them now. By ‘this light’ I do not understand ‘a light which is no light,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘darkness,’—as if this were an oxymoron like <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέπειν σκότον</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 419"> <title>O. T.</title> 419</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σκότῳ ὁρᾶν</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 1273), for <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλὸς εἶναι</quote>. Rather <quote xml:lang="grc">τάνδ’ αἴγλαν</quote> is ‘dreamlight,’—such as illuminates the visions that come in sleep. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 354">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 354</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ὀνείρασι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτῶσά μ’ εὐφραίνοις ἄν· ἡδὺ γὰρ φίλος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κὰν νυκτὶ λεύσσειν, ὅντιν’ ἂν παρῇ χρόνον</quote>. The pron. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάνδε_830"> marks that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴγλαν_830"> has this poetical sense, —the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄναρ</quote>, not the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπαρ</quote>, of light. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 942">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 942</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καὶ σὺ νίκην τήνδε δήριος τίεις</quote>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκη</quote> which consists in yielding.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέταται_830">, referring to light, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 600">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 600</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ τέτατο φάος</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμασι_830"> might, indeed, be a dat. of interest; but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίσχοις_830"> could not mean, <emph> defendas.</emph> In <title>O.C.</title> 1651 <quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖρ’ ἀντέχοντα κρατός</quote> certainly refers to shading the eyes; but the object of the verb is that which is held before them, not that which is warded off.— Hesych. has <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴγλη· χλίδων. Σοφοκλῆς Τηρεῖ. χιτών· καὶ πέδη παρὰ Ἐπιχάρμῳ ἐν Βάκχαις</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">χλίδων</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χλιδή</quote>) meant an ‘ornament,’ esp. an armlet (<quote xml:lang="grc">ψέλιον</quote>). If <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴγλη</quote> was used for <quote xml:lang="grc">χλίδων</quote>, it was so because <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴγλη</quote> could mean ‘a gleaming object’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφή</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">θρέμμα</quote>). The same explanation applies to <quote xml:lang="grc">χιτών</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πέδη</quote>,—‘a glistering tunic,’ ‘a bright chain.’ Cp. the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">γλήνεα</quote>, prop. ‘bright objects,’ then ‘trinkets’ or the like ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.192"> <title>Il.</title> 24. 192</ref>). The meanings of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴγλη</quote> given in Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 354 add nothing, for our purpose, to Hesychius. We cannot, then, accept Welcker's version of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴγλαν_830_2"> here:—‘keep upon his eyes this bandage (<emph>fasciam</emph>) that is bound upon them now’ (<title>Rhein. Mus.</title> p. 125, 1828). —No alteration, either of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίσχοις_830_2"> or of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάνδ_αἴγλαν_830">, seems probable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴθι_ἴθι_832">. The hiatus is defensible because the words are virtually interjections; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, there is a slight pause after the first <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1276"> <title>Ant.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ φεῦ, ὦ πόνοι</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1328 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτω ἴτω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_στάσει_833">, in a fig. sense, combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ...βάσει</quote> (‘what your attitude is to be,’—‘what steps you are to take’), as oft. in expressions of perplexity; cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1079">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1079</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾷ βῶ, πᾷ στῶ, πᾷ κάμψω</quote>; <ref n="Eur. Alc. 864">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 864</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ βῶ; πᾷ στῶ; τί λέγω; τί δὲ μή</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_μοιτἀντεῦθεν_834">, and in the corresponding v. of the antistrophe (850), </gloss>
lemma:
ποῖ δὲ βάσει πῶς δέ μοι
							τἀντεῦθεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνό_λάθρᾳ_834">. The want of a verb for <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δέ μοι τἀντεῦθεν</quote> suggests some corruption: we cannot well take <quote xml:lang="grc">βάσει</quote> with both clauses by changing <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δέ</quote> (as Hermann proposed) to <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς τε</quote>. Nor, again, is it satisfactory to expand v. 850 by adding <quote xml:lang="grc"> τούτου</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνδρὸς</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">λάθρᾳ</quote>, or by repeating <quote xml:lang="grc"> λάθρᾳ</quote> itself. Wecklein, leaving <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνό μοι κεῖνο λάθρᾳ</quote> untouched, writes here <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ δὲ τἀνθένδε βάσει</quote> (omitting <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δέ μοι τἀντεῦθεν</quote>). But then,—granting that a dittographia was the cause of error,—it is hard to see how <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> could have crept in between <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δέ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀντεῦθεν</quote>. I prefer to read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_βάσει_834"> here, and to insert <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> (this with Hermann) after the first <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνο</quote> in 850. The MS. reading may have arisen thus. A transcriber, whose eye chanced to pass over <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι τἀνθένδε</quote>, wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ δὲ βάσει</quote>. Then, perceiving that he had missed two words, he preferred to begin anew, and wrote the whole verse right, but either forgot, or failed to mark clearly, that his original <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ δὲ βάσει</quote> should be deleted. (A similar case occurs in L's text of the metrical <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑπόθεσις</quote> to this play: see p. 3.) A successor, finding <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ δὲ βάσει ποῖ δέ μοι τἀνθένδε βάσει</quote>, deemed it obvious that the <emph> second</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">βάσει</quote> should be omitted. The verse thus became, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ δὲ βάσει ποῖ δέ μοι τἀνθένδε</quote>. But the metrical context showed that a long final syllable was needed; and nothing seemed easier than to correct <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθένδε</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀντεῦθεν</quote>. Lastly, as a verb such as <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξεις</quote> seemed to be understood with <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀντεῦθεν φροντίδος</quote>, the second <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</quote> was altered to <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς</quote>.—Join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_834"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φροντίδος_834"> (partit. gen.): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 170"> <title>O. C.</title> 170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ τις φροντίδος ἔλθῃ;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀνθένδε_834">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 895">cp. 895</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷς_ἤδη_835">, ‘thou seest now’ (how matters stand),—said with a glance or gesture towards the sleeping Philoctetes. There is a certain awkwardness in these words, since, coming so soon after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα ποῦ στάσει</quote>, they might naturally mean, ‘thou art already taking heed.’ Herwerden and Wecklein conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷς_εὕδει_835">. This may be right. But the cautious vagueness of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς ἤδη</quote> is perhaps a little in its favour. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_μενοῦμεν_836"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ὥστε</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_836"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτό</quote>): for the epexegetic inf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 62">cp. 62</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καιρός_837">, occasion, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντωνγνώμαν_ἴσχων_837">=<quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γιγνώσκων</quote>, taking cognisance of all things,—discerning, in every case, whether the circumstances warrant prompt action. For <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμην ἔχειν</quote> as = <quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκειν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 214"> <title>El.</title> 214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γνώμαν ἴσχεις, ἐξ οἵων, κ.τ.λ.</quote> The general sense is the same as in <ref n="Soph. El. 75"> <title>El.</title> 75</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">νὼ δ’ ἔξιμεν· καιρὸς γάρ, ὅσπερ ἀνδράσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστος ἔργου παντός ἐστ’ ἐπιστάτης</quote>. Though we need not write <quote xml:lang="grc">Καιρός</quote>, still <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρός</quote> is virtually personified both by <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμαν ἴσχων</quote> and by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρνυται</quote>. Pausanias (5. 14. 7) saw two altars at the entrance to the Olympian stadium; one was to Hermes <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐναγώνιος</quote>,—the other to <quote xml:lang="grc"> Καιρός</quote>, who enabled athletes to seize the critical moment in a struggle. Cp. <title>Anthol.</title> 10. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γε λέγων τὸν Καιρὸν ἔφης θεόν, εὖ γε Μένανδρε</quote>.—Blaydes takes <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς γνώμαν ἴσχων</quote> as=‘opportunity combined with judgment,’ and joins <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτος</quote> (‘superiority in all cases’). The order of the words seems against this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_πολὺ_837">. No curtailment of v. 854 (<quote xml:lang="grc">μάλα τοι ἄπορα πυκινοῖς ἐνιδεῖν πάθη</quote>) is probable. The addition of <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύ τι</quote> makes v. 838 equal to v. 854: and the remedy, however uncertain, is at least not violent. See Appendix on vv. 852 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὰ_πόδα_837">, ‘then and there,’ <emph> extemplo</emph>,—by a prompt stroke of action. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Soph. 242A">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 242A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή ποτε διὰ ταῦτά σοι μανικὸς εἶναι δόξω, παρὰ πόδα μεταβαλὼν ἐμαυτὸν ἄνω καὶ κάτω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μὲν_839"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘It is true that <emph>he</emph> would be unconscious of our flight: but <title>I</title> know that it would be useless to sail without him.’ The stately hexameters—in contrast with the lighter rhythms of the Chorus—suit the authoritative tone in which Neoptolemus declares the purport of the oracle. As vv. 844 ff. show, he speaks in a louder voice than the Chorus deem safe.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θήραν_ἔχομεν_839">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 564"> <title>Ai.</title> 564</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσμενῶν θήραν ἔχων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 566"> <title>O. T.</title> 566</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἔρευναν τοῦ θανόντος ἔσχετε</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδε_τοῦτον_841">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1331">cp. 1331</ref>, 1434 f., 1437.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_στέφανος_841">, fig.: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 660">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 660</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς στέφανον ἀνθαιρήσεται</quote>, no one will take the palm (for misery) in her stead. Helenus had declared that the victory would belong jointly to Philoctetes and Neoptolemus, as the latter says at 1335. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κομπεῖν_δ_842"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> It will be a disgrace to them, when they go back to Troy, to boast of their task as accomplished, when it will be, in fact, only half done, if they bring the bow without its master. And the discredit of such a result will be aggravated by the deception used towards Philoctetes. The words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτελῆ_ψεύδεσιν_842"> are closely connected; ‘an incomplete result, combined with falsehood,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> not only incomplete, but obtained by falsehood. This seems better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν ψεύδεσιν</quote> as merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδῶς</quote>, ‘to boast falsely.’—Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 641"> <title>El.</title> 641</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δὺν φθόνῳ</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 933"> <title>Ai.</title> 933</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐλίῳ σὺν πάθει</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OT 585"> <title>O. T.</title> 585</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν φόβοισι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_τέκνον_843">. The Chorus reply,—‘If an oracle has said that Ph. must be brought to Troy, the god himself will provide for the fulfilment of that decree. Meanwhile, <emph>thy</emph> part is to secure the bow.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 724">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 724</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν γὰρ ἂν θεὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρείαν ἐρευνᾷ ῥᾳδίως αὐτὸς φανεῖ. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀψεται_843">, look to it, provide for it: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1165"> <title>Ai.</title> 1165</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάπετόν τιν’ ἰδεῖν</quote>: Theocr. 15. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρη δίφρον, Εὐνόα, αὐτᾷ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_844"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς</quote>, by attraction to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_844">: for the double acc. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμείβῃ_844">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 991."> <title>O. C.</title> 991.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντων_847"> masc.: in sickness all men's sleep,—if, indeed, it can be called sleep at all,—is quick of vision (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεύσσειν_847">, epexeg. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐδρακὴς_847">). Words appropriate to eyesight are here used to denote perception generally. The slightest sound will stir consciousness in the sick sleeper. For a somewhat similar use of language cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 104">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὕδουσα γὰρ φρὴν ὄμμασιν λαμπρύνεται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_δύνᾳ_849"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The connection of thought is:—‘A sick man is very easily awakened. But the bow must be carried off without awakening him (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάθρᾳ_849">).’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύνᾳ_849">=<quote xml:lang="grc">δύνασαι</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 798">cp. 798</ref>. (Not Doric for <quote xml:lang="grc">δύνῃ</quote>, as some have thought: <quote xml:lang="grc">ῃ</quote> was not changed in the Doric subjunct.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνο_κεῖνο_849">, with the same kind of emphasis as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸ τοῦτο</quote> in 77. The Chorus are unmoved by what N. has said (841). They repeat that the bow should be taken, and Ph. left behind. As to the conjectural insertion of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_849">, <ref target="cl834">see on 834</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_849">, ethic (763).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτι_μάκιστον_849"> (Doric for <quote xml:lang="grc">μήκιστον</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξιδοῦ_849">, lit., ‘look forth to the furthest possible point,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘use all possible precaution,’—a fresh warning not to disturb the sleeper by the slightest noise, but to depart while there is yet time. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.342"> <title>Il.</title> 20. 342</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγ’ ἔξιδεν ὀφθαλμοῖσιν</quote>, he strained his sight (in eager search): <emph>ib.</emph> 23. 477 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε τοι ὀξύτατον κεφαλῆς ἐκδέρκεται ὄσσε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπᾳ_849"> is preferable to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_849"> where the particular mode of effecting the object is in question; and it is supported by the corrector of L (cr. n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθα_πάθη_852">. I read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃν_852"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν</quote>), with Hermann, for the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_852"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_852"> of the MSS. ‘If thou holdest this purpose—thou knowest what purpose I speak of—in relation to this man (Philoctetes), truly there are desperate troubles (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπορα πάθη</quote>, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι</quote>) for shrewd men to foresee’ (lit., ‘to see <emph>in</emph>’ such a deed). The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμαν_852"> is the purpose of Neoptolemus to take Philoctetes on board the ship—ostensibly for conveyance to Greece—and then carry him to Troy. The allusive phrase, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθαγὰρ_αὐδῶμαι_852">, is used, because they are afraid of breathing a word which might betray the secret to the sick man, if he should awake while they were speaking. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύταν_852"> emphatically opposes this plan to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνο_852">—the course which they themselves recommend. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτῳ_852"> is a dat. of relation, nearly=<quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ τούτου</quote>: cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 598D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 598D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπολαμβάνειν δεῖ τῷ τοιούτῳ ὅτι εὐηθής τις ἄνθρωπος</quote> (‘in the case of such a person’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυκινοῖς_852">: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ξριτιας Σίς</quote>. fr. 1. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνός τις καὶ σοφὸς γνώμην ἀνήρ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνιδεῖν_852">, oft. used of seeing a difficulty or danger in a proposed course of action: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 89">Her. 1. 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴρετο Κροῖσον ὅ τι οἱ ἐνορῴη ἐν τοῖσι ποιευμένοισι</quote> (what harm he foresaw for him in what was being done). <ref n="Hdt. 1. 120">Id. 1. 120</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ φοβερόν τι ἐνωρῶμεν, πᾶν ἂν σοὶ προεφράζομεν</quote>. The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄποραπάθη_852"> are the horrors of the disease,—the fury which would burst forth in Philoctetes when he learned that they were taking him to Troy,—and the curses which he would invoke from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ἱκέσιος</quote> on his betrayers.—For other views of this passage, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖρος_855">, a fair wind, meaning here an opportune moment: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸς ἐπιτήδειος</quote>. The metaphor is a fitting one for sailors. When <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὖρος</quote> is fig., it more oft.= ‘a prosperous course’ ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 815"> <title>Tr.</title> 815</ref>).—This is better than to take the word literally, as if the wind, which had been adverse (640), had just changed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἀρωγὰν_856">, because his bow is in N.'s hands (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 931">cp. 931</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νύχιος_856">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σκότιος</quote>, in the darkness of sleep. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλεὴς_ἐσθλός_859">. If these words are right, they can mean only, ‘sleep in the heat is sound,’—a parenthetic comment on the preceding <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτέταται νύχιος</quote>. In the excitement of the Chorus, it is perhaps not strange that they should use a phrase scarcely consistent with their own <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνος ἄϋπνος</quote> (848). Cp. Theocr. 7. 21 <quote xml:lang="grc">μεσαμέριον</quote> .. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνίκα δὴ καὶ σαῦρος ἐφ’ αἱμασίαισι καθεύδει</quote>. We certainly cannot render (with Cavallin), ‘a <emph>warm</emph> sleep (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> a sound one, in which a gentle warmth pervades the body) is favourable to our plan.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλεής_859"> occurs only here, though Hesych. has <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλεός</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεεινός</quote>. It is, however, a correct formation from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλέα</quote>: and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλέας_859"> (gen.) is not a probable correction. The easy emendation </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδεὴς_859"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Δ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">Λ</quote>) would give the sense, ‘'tis a secure (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> tranquil), sound sleep.’ This may be right; but I have preferred to keep the MS. reading. The addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_859"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλεὴς_859"> might seem desirable in such a parenthesis: cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.308">Dem. or. 18 § 308</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἄλλο τι δύσκολον γέγονε</quote>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ δὲ τὰ ἀνθρώπινα</quote>,) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτ’ ἐπὶ τούτῳ τῷ καιρῷ κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλεὴς_ἐσθλός_859_2"> as a mere gloss. But a marginal commentator might have been expected to use more prosaic language,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μεσημβρινὸς ὕπνος βαθύς</quote>. Dobree, reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδεὴς_859_2">, supposed the following process. (1) In v. 864 Soph. wrote </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδεὴς_ἐσθλός_859">. This was supplanted by a gloss, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνος_φοβῶνκράτιστος_859">, which now stands there. (2) Then the displaced </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδεὴς_ἐσθλός_859_2"> was corrupted into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεὴς ὕπνος ἐσθλός</quote>, and inserted in the text after <quote xml:lang="grc">νύχιος</quote>. This hypothesis is very ingenious, but it seems much too complex to be probable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_τινος_860">. The conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐ</emph> φρενὸς</quote>, has found much favour; but, in a picture of utter helplessness, is not the vulg. more forcible? <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1161">Cp. 1161</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκέτι μηδενὸς κρατύνων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀΐδᾳ_κείμενος_861">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 972"> <title>O. T.</title> 972</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται παρ’ Ἅιδῃ Πόλνβος</quote>. This mode of writing is preferable to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρακείμενος_861"> because <quote xml:lang="grc">παρακεῖσθαί τινι</quote>=‘to lie beside one,’ or ‘before one,’ with ref. to things which are ready to one's hand, or at one's disposal. But when the sense is, ‘to be lodged or deposited with one,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖσθαι παρά τινι</quote> is used. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_βλέπ_862">. For the double imperat. in excited utterance, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 981">cp. 981</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 121"> <title>O. C.</title> 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσδέρκου, λεῦσσεδή</quote>. Seyffert's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_862"> is much better here than the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷ_862">, ‘he sees as the dead see,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, not at all. After <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνόμματος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">νύχιος</quote>, this would be weak.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_φθέγγει_862">. ‘See whether thy words are seasonable’ means here, ‘We fear that thy counsel (839 ff.) is unseasonable.’ We miss our <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρός</quote>, if we stay here with Philoctetes, instead of escaping with the bow. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φρ_863">, as far as my thought can grasp the question, =<quote xml:lang="grc">καθ’ ὅσον ἐγὼ κατανοῶ τὸ πρᾶγμα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Tim. 29A">Plat. <title> Tim.</title> 29A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λόγῳ καὶ φρονήσει περιληπτόν</quote>. The acc. is one of ‘respect’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν μέρος</quote>, etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνος_κράτιστος_863">, ‘the enterprise not fraught with fear is best’ (Whitelaw): a sententious utterance, like <quote xml:lang="grc">βράχιστα γὰρ κράτιστα τἀν ποσὶν κακά</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1327"> <title>Ant.</title> 1327</ref>). They mean that it is best to depart noiselessly with the bow, and so avoid the risks involved in taking Philoctetes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_φοβῶν_863"> is left vague by the proverb-like brevity of the phrase: it means, ‘which does not disturb the sleeping Philoctetes.’ The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνος_863"> is also in keeping with the gnomic form,—implying that there will be <emph>least</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνος</quote> in such a course; as if it were, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνος ἐλάχιστος κράτιστος</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγῆς ἀκίνδυνον γέρας</quote> (meaning that <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγή</quote>, though it wins <emph> no</emph> positive <quote xml:lang="grc">γέρας</quote>, risks nothing): ‘Discretion is the better part of valour,’ etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_φρενῶν_865">: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1021">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1021</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέστην φρενῶν</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀφεστάναι</quote>, cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 1457 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ ἀποστῆναι χαλεπὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσεος</quote>. The words convey a hurried reproof and warning,—‘do not lose your wits’ (through fear). All their presence of mind is needed, since Philoctetes is awaking. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φέγγος_867">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_τ_867"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> For a voc. thus combined with a nom. (and art.), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 986">cp. 986</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 861"> <title>Ai.</title> 861</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κλειναί τ’ Ἀθῆναι καὶ τὸ σύντροφον γένος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδων_ἄπιστον_867">, not credited by my hopes,— such that my hopes could not have believed it possible. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1067">Cp. 1067</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 847"> <title>Ant.</title> 847</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλων ἄκλαυτος</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κλαιομένη ὑπὸ φίλων</quote>), and n.: <ref n="Soph. El. 1214"> <title>El.</title> 1214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτιμος...τοῦ τεθνηκότος</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τιμωμένη ὑπὸ τοῦ τ.</quote>). So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων ἄπιστον</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πιστευόμενον ὑπὸ τῶν ἐλπίδων</quote>. This is better than to take it as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων πίστιν οὐκ ἔχον</quote>, in the sense, ‘not having the <emph>pledge</emph>, assurance, given by hopes,’ ‘not warranted’ by them (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήνεμος χειμώνων</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OC 677"> <title>O. C.</title> 677</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκούρημα_867">, as having guarded the place while he slept. So a watch-dog is called <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρός</quote> in <title> Vesp.</title> 970: cp. below, 1328. For the periphrasis cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 417"> <title>El.</title> 417</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc"> πατρὸς...ὁμιλίαν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 606">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 606</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν Φεραίων εὐμενὴς παρουσία</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_869"> is governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηύχης_869">, not by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλῆναι_869">, which interprets it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_869"> might go with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλῆναι_869_2"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι τλαίης ἄν</quote>), but is better taken, as its position suggests, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηύχης_869_2">. The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ἐξηύχησ’</quote> warrants the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τλῆναι</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τλήσεσθαι</quote>. See Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖναι_869"> (depending on <quote xml:lang="grc"> τλῆναι</quote>) governs </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀμὰ_πήματα_869">, to ‘wait for’ them, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to wait till they were better: cp. Aesch. fr. 35 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγὼν γὰρ ἄνδρας οὐ μένει λελειμμένους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνωφελοῦντά_μοι_869">, helping to do me good, with dat. instead of the usual acc.; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 560"> <title>Ant.</title> 560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς θανοῦσιν ὠφελεῖν</quote> (n.). It is possible, but less simple, to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πήματα</quote>) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνωφ.</quote>, ‘helping me to assuage them.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_872">: ‘the Atreidae, at any rate (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>), did not thus.’ Here <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">γοῦν</quote>) justifies his wonder at the youth's constancy. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 907">Cp. 907</ref>: 1389: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 321"> <title>Ant.</title> 321</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐφόρως_872"> is the best correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐπόρως_872"> (see cr. n.). Cp. Hippocr. <ref n="Hp. Aph. 1242"> <title>Aph.</title> 1242</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφορώτατα φέρειν</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1244 <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσφόρως φέρειν</quote> (as <ref n="Soph. OT 783">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 783</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσφόρως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔνειδος ηγον</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀξ_εὐγενῶν_874">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 384">cp. 384</ref>: 719.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἔθου_874">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 498">cp. 498</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σμικρῷ ποιούμενοι</quote> (n.); and for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τίθεσθαι</quote>, 451, 473.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέμων_874">: cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.308">Dem. or. 18 § 308</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλάττει πηνίκ’ ἔσεσθε μεστοὶ τοῦ συνεχῶς λέγοντος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λήθη_878">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 211">Eur. <title> Or.</title> 211</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλον ὔπνου θέλγητρον, ἐπίκουρον νόσου</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πότνια λήθη τῶν κακῶν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_878">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύ_ποτέ_879">. Philoctetes has awakened to find that the acute pains have ceased (768); but, after the violent attack of the disease, a sense of faintness (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόπος_879">) remains. He has been lying on his back (822). He now asks Neoptolemus to assist him in rising to his feet: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ού_κατάστησον_879">: where <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote> means that he does not wish the Chorus to approach him at present. He is afraid that disgust might render them unwilling to take him on board (890). In his crippled state,— now aggravated by exhaustion,—the mere act of rising was a serious exertion. At v. 886 Neoptolemus gives the aid of his hands to the recumbent sufferer, at the same time asking him to make an effort,</gloss>
lemma: ού μ’ αὐτὸς ἆρον, σύ με κατάστησον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_σαυτόν_879">: which is not, of course, contrasted with <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ μ’ αὐτὸς ἆρον</quote>, as if N. meant that Ph. must rise <emph>without</emph> help: that would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">συ δ’ αὐτὸς αἶρε σαυτόν</quote>. At the same time, N. says that, if Ph. prefers it, the sailors will lift him up and carry him. Ph. replies, ‘No, thank you—help me to rise, as you propose’ (889). N. assents (893), saying, ‘Stand up, and take hold of me yourself’ (as I am holding you). And v. 894 marks the moment at which Ph. slowly rises, leaning on N. Then there is naturally a pause, in order that Ph. may rest after this effort, and may feel whether he is yet strong enough to attempt walking. It is this pause which is foreshadowed by the words, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_ποτέ_879"> (880). And it is in this pause that the remorse of Neoptolemus gains the mastery.</gloss>
lemma:
ἵν’, ἡνίκ’ ἂν κόπος μ’
							ἀπαλλάξῃ ποτέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσχωμεν_881">, intrans. (the use of this verb in 349 is a different one); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πλεῖν_881"> defines the act in regard to which delay is forbidden.
