Cp. <title>M.</title> 3. 6. 10 <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ πολέμου συμβουλεύειν τήν γε πρώτην ἐπισχήσομεν</quote>. For the art. prefixed to the inf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 118">cp. 118</ref>: 1241 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς σε κωλύσει τὸ δρᾶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μέν_882">: here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_882"> slightly emphasises the verb, but does not oppose it to any other thought: the <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν <emph> δ’</emph> </quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 886">886</ref> should not be regarded as answering to it. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1278">Cp. 1278</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 82"> <title>O. T.</title> 82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰκάσαι μέν, ἡδύς</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 769 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἵξεται μέν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνώδυνον_882"> masc., to be taken adverbially with both participles (‘living and breathing, free from pain’): not neut., with <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέποντα</quote> only, as if the sense were, ‘showing the absence of pain by thy looks.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλέποντα_882">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντα</quote> (though here with special reference to his recent slumber, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 856">cp. 856</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνόμματος</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 962"> <title>Ai.</title> 962</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ βλέποντα μὴ )πόθουν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντ’ ἂν οἰμώξειαν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμπνέοντ_882">: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 671"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 671</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνων εἴ τις ἐστὶν ἐμπνέων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὄντος_884">. Here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβόλαια_884"> are the signs observable by one who watched Ph. sleeping after the attack of the disease, when he seemed like one <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀΐδᾳ πάρα κείμενος</quote> (861). The chief of such signs would be, a deathly pallor, and the absence (as a spectator might think) of respiration.—By </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰςπαρούσας_ξυμφορὰς_884"> are meant the agonies of disease to which he is subject, and which he had endured just before his sleep. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς παρούσας</quote> might be the part. of the imperf., <quote xml:lang="grc">αἳ παρῆσαν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1192">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1192</ref> n.), but is more forcible if taken as pres.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">αἳ πάρεισιν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 734">cp. 734</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς παρεστώσης νόσου</quote>. Thus the meaning is:—‘Thy symptoms (in sleep), <emph>judged in the light of</emph> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_884">) the sufferings which afflict thee, seemed like those of a dead man.’ Such a sleep, following on such paroxysms, might well have been mistaken for death. For <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> as= ‘in view of,’ cp. <ref n="Thuc. 7. 47">Thuc. 7. 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐβουλεύοντο πρός τε τὴν γεγενημένην ξυμφορὰν καὶ πρὸς τὴν παροῦσαν ἐν τῷ στρατοπέδῳ κατὰ πάντα ἀρρωστίαν</quote>.—Not: ‘In view of <emph>thy plight just now</emph> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, while sleeping), thy symptoms seemed like those of a dead man.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_παρξυμφοράς_884"> would then mean merely the <emph>condition</emph> of the sleeper, as distinguished from the <quote xml:lang="grc">συμβόλαια</quote> or outward <emph>signs</emph> thereof. But, since the inference was drawn wholly from the outward signs, the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸςτὰς_ξυμφορὰς_884"> would lose their natural force, and mean no more than <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ παρόντα συμβόλαια σκοποῦντι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβόλαια_884_2">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σύμβολα</quote>: the only Attic example of this sense; which occurs, however, in <ref n="Hdt. 5. 92">Her. 5. 92</ref>§ 7, <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστὸν γάρ οἱ ἦν τὸ συμβόλαιον</quote> (the token, or proof, <quote xml:lang="grc">μαρτύριον</quote>). In <ref n="Eur. Ion 411">Eur. <title> Ion</title> 411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ τε νῷν συμβόλαια πρόσθεν ἦν</quote>, the meaning is ‘dealings,’ ‘intercourse’ (the regular Attic sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">συμβόλαια</quote> being that of ‘covenants’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_σαυτόν_886">. The reflexive pron. is not necessarily emphatic when thus used with an active verb: cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 747">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 747</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτ’ ἐμοὶ ζῆν κέρδος, ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐν τάχει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρριψ’ ἐμαυτὴν τῆσδ’ ἀπὸ στύφλου πέτρας</quote>...; At v. 879 Ph. asked N. to assist him; and now—after a kindly greeting—N. proceeds to do so. His hands are now stretched forth to Ph. , ready to raise him, and the words <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ αἶρε σαυτόν</quote> prepare Ph. for the effort.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_φίλον_886">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, he need not make even this effort, but can be lifted from the ground.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_γὰρ_886">: since Neoptolemus and Philoctetes are agreed upon the voyage, the sailors will not grudge the trouble of carrying their master's friend. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰνῶ_παῖ_889">. ‘Thanks, my son’ (lit., ‘I commend what you say’). The phrase implies a courteous recognition of the proposal that the sailors should carry him: but, as is shown by <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καί</emph> μ’ ἔπαιῤ ὥσπερ νοεῖς</quote>, it is not a direct way of refusing the offer, like ‘<title>No</title>, thank you.’ The formula </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰνῶ_τάδε_889"> regularly means, as here, ‘I commend your words’ ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 786">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 786</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 908">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 908</ref>). Eur. Itis known, indeed, that Soph. used <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνῶ</quote> like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαινῶ</quote>, as a civil form of refusal, in his <title>Alcmaeon</title> (Hesych. s. v. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνῶ</quote>): cp. <ref n="Hes. WD 641">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 641</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νἦ ὀλίγην αἰνεῖν, μεγάλῃ δ’ ἐνὶ φορτία θέσθαι</quote>. But here <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνῶ τάδε</quote> is better taken in its simple and usual sense. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔασον_890">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1257">cp. 1257</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀσμῇ_890">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 876">cp. 876</ref>, 1032.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνναίειν_890"> (epexeg. inf.) can be said of companionship in a brief voyage, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ναίειν</quote> is oft. no more than ‘to be in’ a place: <ref n="Soph. OC 117"> <title>O. C.</title> 117</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσται_τάδ_893">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1773"> <title>O. C.</title> 1773</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσω καὶ τάδε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵστω_893">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίστω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 143"> <title>O. T.</title> 143</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 147."> O. T. 147.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὐτὸς_ἀντέχου_893">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as I am supporting thee, so, on thy part, cling to me. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 121">Her. 2. 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνου τῆς χειρὸς ἀντέχεσθαι</quote>. For the omission of the gen., cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1120">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1120</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε, τοῦ δόρατος ἀφελκύσωμαι τοὔλυτρον</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχ’, ἀντέχου, παῖ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύνηθες_ἔθος_894">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 502"> <title>Ant.</title> 502</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κλέος...εὐκλεέστερον</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δρῷμ_895">. Schaefer's insertion of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> is not indispensable. The simple optat. is grammatically possible. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> is clearly right, because the question here is a practical one (not the less so, because no answer is expected); <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the speaker is really deliberating what he shall do next: it does not refer merely to abstract possibility, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τεάν, Ζεῦ, δύνασιν τίς...κατάσχοι</quote>; ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 605"> <title>Ant.</title> 605</ref>). Cp. <title>O. C.</title>, Appendix on v. 170. The case is precisely similar to that in 1393 (where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> is certain), <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτ’ ἂν ἡμεῖς δρῷμεν</quote>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῷμ_895">. Contracted verbs had two ways of forming the act. optat. pres.: (1) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">δρά‐ο‐ι‐μι</quote>, contr. <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῷμι</quote>, the mode proper to verbs with a thematic vowel: (2) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ιη</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">δρα‐ο‐ίη‐ν</quote>, contr. <quote xml:lang="grc"> δρῴην</quote>, where, though the thematic vowel <emph>o</emph> is kept, the endings follow the analogy of the verbs which have no such vowel (‘verbs in <quote xml:lang="grc">μι</quote>’). The only Homeric examples of (2) are <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.692"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 692</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλοίη</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 9. 320 <quote xml:lang="grc">φοροίη</quote>. But in the 5th cent. B.C. this second formation was already predominant in For Attic. the sing. number the first formation had become rare, though Attic poets could still use it whenever it was metrically convenient: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> 1044 (and <ref n="Soph. OT 1470"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1470</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῖμ’</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1235">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1235</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσοῖ</quote>: Aesch <ref n="Aesch. PB 978">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 978</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσοῖμ’ ἄν</quote>. Some instances of the 3rd sing. occur also in Attic prose: as <ref n="Thuc. 2. 79">Thuc. 2. 79</ref>(and 100) <quote xml:lang="grc"> δοκοῖ</quote>: <title>Legg.</title> 664 E <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδῷ</quote>, etc. (Cp. Curtius, <title>Greek Verb</title>, ch. XIV., p. 335 Eng. tr.) In dual and plur. the prevalence of the second formation appears to have been less decisive; and the 3rd pers. plur. always retained the first formation (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> δρῷεν</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῴησαν</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐνθένδε_γε_895">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 834">cp. 834</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 476"> <title>O. C.</title> 476</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ἔνθεν</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_895"> at the end of the v., as 438, <ref n="Soph. OC 265"> <title>O. C.</title> 265</ref>, etc. The emphasis is fitting here. He has reached the furthest point to which the deception can be carried, since it must be revealed by the presence of Odysseus at the ship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέβης_896">, ‘digressed’ from the matter in hand: cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.211">Dem. or. 18 § 211</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπανελθεῖν οὖν, ὁπόθεν ἐνταῦθ’ ἐξέβην, βούλομαι</quote> . <title>I. T.</title> 781 (Orestes to Iphigeneia, whom he has interrupted by an unguarded exclamation) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν· πέραινε δ)· ἐξέβην γὰρ ἄλλοσε</quote> (‘my thoughts had wandered’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_896"> is better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_896">. The latter is more suitable in such a phrase as <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ λόγων ἀμηχανῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθω</quote>; <ref n="Soph. El. 1174."> <title>El.</title> 1174.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_χρὴ_897"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: he does not know in what words he can break the truth to Ph. ,—that they are going to Troy. After an obscure hint in vv. 912 f., he at last speaks bluntly (915). Cp. <title>Hipp. ma.</title> 297 D <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔτι ἔχω, ὦ Ἱππία, ὅποι τράπωμαι, ἀλλ’ ἀπορῶ· σὺ δὲ ἔχεις τι λέγειν</quote>;—Nauck wishes for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρέφειν_897">, which would imply an artful ‘twisting’ of speech; cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1128">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 1128</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ· τί δράσω; πρὸς τίνας στρεφθῶ λόγους</quote>; But </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέπειν_897"> better suits the ingenuous perplexity of one who simply doubts what course he ought to take. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπορεῖς_σύ_898">; Remembering the behaviour of his former visitors when it came to the all-important point (310), Ph. is alarmed at the first trace of embarrassment in Neoptolemus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδε_πάθους_899"> (partit. gen.)=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθάδε τῆς ἀπορίας</quote>: at such an advanced point in it that I do not know what to say next (897).—Not, as Wunder took it, ‘at such a point that I <emph>must</emph> speak’ (referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ λέγε</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δή_900">...; as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 668"> <title>Tr.</title> 668</ref>: and with <quote xml:lang="grc">που</quote> added, <ref n="Soph. OT 1472"> <title>O. T.</title> 1472</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 381."> <title>Ant.</title> 381.</ref>—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_900"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπεισεν_900"> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 656">cp. 656</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναύτην_900">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυβάτην</quote> (246), <emph>vectorem:</emph> so <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 719">Aesch. <title> Pers.</title> 719</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεζὸς ἢ ναύτης</quote>, <title> C.</title> 3. 4. 30 <emph>navita.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαντα_δυσχέρεια_902">: for the neut. plur. as subject, with sing. subst. as predicate, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 883"> <title>O. C.</title> 883</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐχ ὕβρις τάδ’</quote>; <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.433"> <title>Od.</title> 24. 433</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λώβη γὰρ τάδε γ’ ἐστί</quote>: Stobaeus <title>Flor.</title> 5. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβος τὰ θεῖα τοῖσι σώφροσιν βροτῶν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 4.7">Lys. or. 4 § 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ταῦτ’ ἐστὶ πρόνοια;</quote> </cit> <ref n="Dem. 19.72">Dem. or. 19 § 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δὲ ταῦτα γέλως.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_φύσιν_902"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιπών_902">, whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γενναῖον</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ ἐξιστάμενον ἐκ τῆς αὑτοῦ φύσεως</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 51">cp. 51</ref> n.). Fraud was foreign to his nature (88).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_προσεικότα_902">, such things as do not befit him: for the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 170">cp. 170</ref>, 409, 444, 909. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_φυτεύσαντος_904">, nothing that deviates from his example. The father (Achilles) is the <quote xml:lang="grc">παράδειγμα</quote> which regulates the son's conduct,—as in <title>Eth. N.</title> 3. 6 the <quote xml:lang="grc"> σπουδαῖος</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ κανὼν καὶ μέτρον</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν καλῶν</quote>). Thus the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω</quote> is justified: it expresses a departure from the lines of the pattern. Cp. <title>Legg.</title> 876 E <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦναι τὰ παραδείγματα τοῖς δικασταῖς τοῦ μήποτε βαίνειν ἔξω τῆς δίκης</quote>. Musgrave quotes Libanius 1. 574 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τῆς πόλεως ἤθους καὶ τῆς ἐμῆς πολιτείας ἔξω τὸ πρᾶγμα εἶναι δοκεῖ</quote>. The boldness of the expression <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω τοῦ φυτεύσαντος</quote> finds some analogy in the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά τινα</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ τρόπον τινός</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Parm. 126C">Plat. <title>Parm.</title> 126C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ τὸν πάππον...πρὸς τῇ ἱππικῇ διατρίβει</quote> (following his example). So Alciphron can say <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ παῖς ἐξεμάξατο τὸν διδάσκαλον</quote> (took the stamp of his teacher), instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν τοῦ διδασκάλου χαρακτῆρα</quote> (3. 64). I cannot, then, think with Nauck that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυτεύσαντος_904"> is spurious. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσεικότος_904"> would be but a tame substitute. A reference to the youth's inherited generosity seems fitting here: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 874">cp. 874</ref>, 1310. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμφυτευθέντος_904"> (Tournier) is ingenious, but less forcible than <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ φυτεύσαντος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσθλὸν_904">: Blaydes would take this as= ‘of noble birth,’ in order that Ph. may not praise himself. A similar feeling has prompted conjectures (cr. n.). But by <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐσθλόν</quote> Ph. means that the kindness of N. is not disgraced by its recipient. The situation is one in which he can say this with perfect dignity and propriety. So he refers to himself elsewhere as the comrade (1131) and benefactor (670) of Heracles; as a zealous ally of the Greek chiefs (1027); as one who has shown rare courage under his trials (535), and who will not fail in gratitude to his deliverer (1370). In like manner Oedipus reminds his Attic hosts that he is no unworthy guest ( <ref n="Soph. OC 287">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 287</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 625">Soph. O. C. 625</ref> f.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_906">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 589">cp. 589</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_907"> (872 n.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνοἷς_δρᾷς_907"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρὸς φανεῖ</quote>): in respect of thy deeds (<emph>thus far</emph>) thou certainly wilt not be found <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρός</quote>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνοἷς_αὐδᾷς_907">, but in respect of what thou <emph>sayest—i.e.</emph>, in respect of the <emph>future</emph> conduct which thy words foreshadow,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀκνῶ_907"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ αἰσχρὸς φανῇς</quote>).—For the emphasis given to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρᾷς_907"> by place and pause, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 989">cp. 989</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote>), 1009 (<quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 555"> <title>Ant.</title> 555</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ μὲν γὰρ εἵλου ζῆν, ἐγὼ δὲ κατθανεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράσω_908">, delib. aor. subjunc.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 757">cp. 757</ref>.—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ <emph>μὴ</emph> δεῖ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 903">cp. 903</ref>. He has been base, first, as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_ἐπῶν_908"> —telling the falsehood that he was sailing to Greece: next, as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύπτωνἃ_δεῖ_908">— hiding the truth, that Ph. must go to Troy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁνὴρ_ὅδ_910">: the transition to the 3rd pers., marking bitter indignation, is like that in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1238"> <title>Tr.</title> 1238</ref>, where Heracles fears disobedience in Hyllus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_γὼ_910">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1086"> <title>O. T.</title> 1086</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ ἐγὼ μάντις εἰμὶ καὶ κατὰ γνώμαν ἴδρις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 472"> <title>El.</title> 472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ )γὼ παράφρων μάντις ἔφυν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γνώμας λειπομένα σοφᾶς</quote>.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_910"> (which Naber alters to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμων_910">) cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 546"> <title>El.</title> 546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβούλου καὶ κακοῦ γνώμην</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 687"> <title>O. T.</title> 687</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθὸς ὢν γνώμην</quote>. The dat. in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1374"> <title>Ai.</title> 1374</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη σοφὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φῦναι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_στελεῖν_910">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1045"> <title>Ai.</title> 1045</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ δὴ τόνδε πλοῦν ἐστείλαμεν</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">στέλλω</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">πλοῦν</quote> in 571, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 640."> Ai. 640.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιπὼν_912"> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πλοῦν στελῶ</quote>) after <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκλιπών</quote>, as 1383 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνοιτ’</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">καταισχύνει</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 841"> <title>O. C.</title> 841</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προβᾶθ’ ὧδε, βᾶτε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπω_912">, convey: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1368">cp. 1368</ref>, 1399, 1465. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπων_912"> (prob. a mere error caused by <quote xml:lang="grc">λιπὼν</quote>) would require us to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πλοῦν στέλλω</quote> (subjunct.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_912">, emphatic, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 458"> <title>Tr.</title> 458</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ πυθέσθαι, τοῦτό μ’ ἀλγύνειεν ἄν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 504"> <title>O. C.</title> 504</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 407."> <title>O. T.</title> 407.</ref>Remark the repetition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_πάλαι_912"> so soon after 906. So <ref n="Soph. Ant. 613"> <title>Ant.</title> 613</ref> and 618 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἕρπει</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 614 and 625 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_κρύψω_915">: for the double acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 957"> <title>El.</title> 957</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν γάρ σε δεῖ κρύπτειν μ’ ἔτι</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 625">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 625</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτοι με κρύψῃς τοῦθ’</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκρύπτομαί τινά τι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_915"> merely prefaces the statement: <ref n="Soph. OT 277."> <title>O. T.</title> 277.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_917">; I insert </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_917">, which might easily have dropped out. Such a hiatus as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_917_2"> is not Sophoclean. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 100">Cp. 100</ref> n. After a voc., we elsewhere find <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> thus used in a question: <ref n="Soph. OC 332"> <title>O. C.</title> 332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνον, τί δ’ ἦλθες</quote>; <emph>ib.</emph> 1459 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτερ, τί δ’ ἐστὶ τἀξίωμ’ ἐφ’ ᾧ καλεῖς</quote>; The objection to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_917_3"> (‘what hast thou said <emph>of me?</emph>’) is that it does not suit the sense here (‘what purpose hast thou declared in regard to me?’). And </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_917_4"> would be weak.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_μάθῃς_917">, without <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄν</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, 764. Soph. affords some 14 instances of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἄν</quote> with subjunct. (as 332, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1332">Soph. 1332</ref>), and 7 instances (besides this) of simple <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν</quote> with subjunct.,— <ref n="Soph. Ant. 619">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 619</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 608">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 608</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 946">Soph. Tr. 946</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Aj. 742"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 742</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 965">Soph. Ai. 965</ref>; fr. 583. 2, fr. 596.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖον_μάθημα_917">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 42"> <title>Ant.</title> 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖόν τι κινδύνευμα</quote>; For the verb with its cognate noun, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 150">cp. 150</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> μέλον...μέλημα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρᾶσαι_917"> with double acc.: 803 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῶσαι_κακοῦ_919">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1162"> <title>Ant.</title> 1162</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώσας μὲν ἐχθρῶν...χθόνα</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_σοὶ_919">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1335">cp. 1335</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ταῦτ_921">...; For <quote xml:lang="grc"> καί</quote> in preface to an indignant question, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀληθῆ_921">, predicative adj., with adverbial force, and so here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθῶς</quote> (a word not extant in Soph. ). In <ref n="Plat. Lach. 186A">Plat. <title>Lach.</title> 186A</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο μὲν ἀληθῆ λέγεις</quote> (as also in <title>Menon</title> 98 B etc.), Krüger and others take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθῆ</quote> as an adv.,=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀληθῶς</quote>: but the sense there is, ‘you are right as to that,’—<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote> being acc. of respect, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθῆ</quote> acc. governed by <quote xml:lang="grc"> λέγεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατεῖ_τούτων_921">, <emph>controls</emph> these things (like <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατεῖν τῶν πραγμάτων</quote>, Dem. or. 1. § 26), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ordains that they must be so. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ξένε_923">, a form which he has not used since 219. He has hitherto addressed N. as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τέκνον</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖ</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 932">Cp. 932</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τε_925">: so <ref n="Soph. OC 1418."> <title>O. C.</title> 1418.</ref>Other places where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> is omitted after <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷός τε</quote> are <ref n="Soph. OC 1136"> <title>O. C.</title> 1136</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 742"> <title>Tr.</title> 742</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 24."> <title>O. T.</title> 24.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_τέλει_925">: 385 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῦρ_927">, the symbol of a ruthless destroyer. Neoptolemus is leaving utter desolation behind him. The image is one which Lemnos itself might well suggest (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 800">cp. 800</ref> n.). The combination of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῦρ_927_2"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖμα_927"> (‘monster’) curiously recalls a passage in the <title>Lysistrata</title> (which appeared two years before this play), 1014 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν ἐστι θηρίον γυναικὸς ἀμαχώτερον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ πῦρ, οὐδ’ ὧδ’ ἀναιδὴς οὐδεμία πόρδαλις</quote>. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ</quote> is a figure for warlike rage, as <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.371"> <title>Il.</title> 20. 371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ δ’ ἐγὼ ἀντίος εἶμι, καὶ εἰ πυρὶ χεῖρας ἔοικε</quote>: or, generally, for an irresistible bane, as Eur. fr. 432 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ πυρὸς γὰρ ἄλλο πῦρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον ἐβλάστομεν γυναῖ</quote> |<quote xml:lang="grc">κες πολὺ δυσμαχώτερον</quote>. Cp. <title>C.</title> 4. 4. 42 <title>Dirus per urbes Afer ut Italas</title>, | <title>Ceu flamma per taedas</title> etc. Tennyson: ‘The children born of thee are fire and sword.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_δεῖμα_927">, utter monster. As <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πᾶσα βλάβη</quote> (622), said of a man, is equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πᾶς βλάβη</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>), so here <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δεῖμα</quote> is equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς δεῖμα</quote>. The latter would describe the man as effaced; the former describes the <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖμα</quote> as perfect; and thus the sense is not affected by the assimilation of the adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς</quote> to the subst. But we cannot compare <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 787">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 787</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς πᾶν ἐσμὲν κακον <*>, κἀξ ἡμῶν ἐστιν ἅπαντα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔριδες, νείκη, στάσις, ἀργαλέα κ.τ.λ.</quote>, since there the sense is, ‘<emph>every sort of</emph> ill,’ not, ‘utter ill.’ For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖμα_927_2"> cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 700">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 700</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρσας δείματα θηρῶν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανουργίας_τέχνημα_927">, a work of art <emph> in</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργία</quote> (defining gen.),—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a man in whom <quote xml:lang="grc"> πανουργία</quote> assumes its subtlest form; not, a work of art produced <emph> by</emph> (personified) <quote xml:lang="grc">Πανουργία</quote> (like Shakespeare's, <cit> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘Confusion now hath made his masterpiece,’</quote> <bibl n="Shak. MAC 2.3"> <title>Macb.</title> 2. 3. 71</bibl> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέχνημα_927"> could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνίτης</quote>, ‘contriver’ of <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργία</quote>, as Nauck implies by comparing <ref n="Hor. Epod. 17. 35">Hor. <title>Epod.</title> 17. 35</ref>(of Canidia) <emph>cales venenis</emph> officina <title>Colchicis</title>. For the neut. noun, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλημα, κρότημα, λάλημα, μίσημα, παιπάλημα</quote>, etc. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 320"> <title>Ant.</title> 320</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰργάσω_928">, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἠπάτηκας</quote>. A perf. follows an aor. in 676, 1172: an aor. follows a perf. in 666. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_προστρόπαιον_930">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 773">cp. 773</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_βίον_931">. This verse deserves notice as one of those which indicate the sensitiveness of the Athenian ear to accent. For if <quote xml:lang="grc">βίον</quote> could have been mistaken for <quote xml:lang="grc">βιόν</quote>, the effect would have been as unhappy as when the actor pronounced <quote xml:lang="grc"> γαλήν’</quote> too much like <quote xml:lang="grc">γαλῆν</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 304">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 304</ref>).—<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1282">Cp. 1282</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_πατρῴων_933">, the gods of Achilles and Peleus. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 756"> <title>O. C.</title> 756</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_ἀφέλῃ_933">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote>, cp. on 782 n. Either the act. or the midd. is admissible. But a strong reason for preferring the midd. is that Soph. uses it in three other places; and if in 376 there was a metrical motive for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρήσοιτο</quote>, there was none in 1303 for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφείλου</quote>, or in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 100">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 100</ref> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρείσθων</quote>. On the other hand, he nowhere uses the active <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρεῖν</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OT 1522">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1522</ref>, where L has the true <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλῃ</quote>, some later MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλῃς</quote>: and probably <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀφέλῃς</quote> in L here is merely a like error. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφωνεῖ_934">: for the 3rd pers., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 910">cp. 910</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_μήποθ_934">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> here is probably ‘generic,’ as in 253 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι</quote>, and 415 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηκέτ’ ὄντα ...νόει</quote>. ‘He looks away, like <emph>one who</emph> will never give it up.’ [Another view is that it is the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> of ‘strong assurance’; see 1329, <ref n="Soph. OC 656"> <title>O. C.</title> 656</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδ’ ἐγώ σε μή τινα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθένδ’ ἀπάξοντ’ ἄνδρα</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as we could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα αὐτὸν μήποτε μεθήσοντα</quote>, so in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷ πάλιν ὡς μήποτε μεθήσων</quote> the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> emphasises the speaker's conviction of N.'s <emph>resolve.</emph> This is possible, but seems less natural.] For the omission of the object to <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθήσων</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801">cp. 801</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote> ).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷ_πάλιν_934">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.415"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 415</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν τρέπεν ὄσσε φαεινώ</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 343">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 343</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσωπον ἔμπαλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφοντα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιμένες_936">, bays or creeks, near the cave,—not necessarily implying anchorage: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 302">cp. 302</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τις ὅρμος ἐστίν</quote> (n.). So in <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.432"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 432</ref> the <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμὴν πολυβενθής</quote> is merely the bay, while the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὅρμος</quote> is the anchorage within it (<emph>ib.</emph> 435).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβλῆτες_936"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκραι</quote>, promontories: in Homer always an adj. (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκταί</quote>, etc.). It is curious to note that, just in that part of his epic for which he would naturally have consulted this play, Quintus Smyrnaeus reproduces this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">προβλής</quote> (10. 175 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ νυ τόν γε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργουσιν προβλῆτες</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνουσίαι_θηρῶν_936">: for the periphrasis <ref n="Soph. Phil. 868">cp. 868</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρρῶγες_936">, only here: a poet. substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορρῶγες</quote> ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 6. 3">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 6. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρα ἀπορρώξ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_938">, subj.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1341"> <title>Ant.</title> 1341</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς πότερον ἴδω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνακλαίομαι_938">, lament aloud. Antiphon <quote xml:lang="grc">α. δ</quote>. § 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς...ἀτυχίας ἀνακλαύσασθαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παροῦσι_938">, present with me as ye are, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_εἰωθόσιν_938"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖναι</quote>), ye, who are wont to be so. In freely rendering these words, ‘my wonted companions,’ we must remember that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παροῦσι_938_2"> is not a subst. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">θεαταῖς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">μάρτυσι</quote>): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, we could not say, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ εἰωθότες παρόντες</quote>, meaning, ‘my wonted companions.’ That would be possible only if <quote xml:lang="grc">παρών</quote> had acquired a definitely substantival use (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρχων</quote>). Thus in <ref n="Thuc. 7. 75">Thuc. 7. 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ζῶντες καταλειπόμενοι</quote> is not ‘the living remnant,’ but ‘those who were left behind alive’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντες κατελείποντο</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμόσας_941">, by giving his promise (527), though no formal oath had been exacted (811).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθείς_941">, having <emph>added</emph> the pledge of the hand (813) to his word. So fr. 428 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκου δὲ προστεθέντος</quote> (added to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ψιλὸς λόγος</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 651"> <title>O. C.</title> 651</ref> n.) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιμελεστέρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὴ κατέστη</quote>. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προθείς_941"> is weaker, and strange as a substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">προτείνας</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1292">cp. 1292</ref>): it is not adequately defended by <ref n="Eur. Hec. 66">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύπουν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλυσιν ἄρθρων προτιθεῖσα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 4">Xen. <title> An.</title> 3. 2. 4</ref>(the Persian king) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς ὀμόσας ἡμῖν, αὐτὸς δεξιὰς δούς, αὐτὸς ἐξαπατήσας συνέλαβε τοὺς στρατηγούς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερὰ_943"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄντα</quote>, sacred as the bow is: because it had been given by Apollo to Heracles, himself now a god (728). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 198">Cp. 198</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ θεῶν ἀμάχητα βέλη</quote>: 657.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_Ἡρακλέους_943">, gen. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Ζηνὸς Ἡρακλῆς</quote>, the bow, once, of Heracles son of Zeus. I do not take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερὰ_943_2"> with this gen., because, though the bow may fitly be called ‘sacred,’ it cannot be called ‘sacred to Heracles’ without straining the natural sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερός τινος</quote>. Rather </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦΖ_Ἡρ_943">. is an indignant development of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερά</quote>:—‘he has stolen my bow,—a sacred one,—the bow of Heracles.’—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_Ἡρακλ_943">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 623"> <title>O. C.</title> 623</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χὡ Διὸς Φοῖβος</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 172"> <title>Ai.</title> 172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταυροπόλα Διὸς Ἄρτεμις</quote> (without art.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φήνασθαι_944">: the aor. midd. of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">φαίνω</quote> occurs nowhere else; nor is there any other place where any part of the simple midd. <quote xml:lang="grc"> φαίνομαι</quote> is trans., ‘to show.’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεφηνάμην</quote> is frequent.) The poet prob. meant </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φήνασθαι_944_2"> here to be a little more than <quote xml:lang="grc">φῆναι</quote>,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘to show for his own glory,’ ‘to display.’ The object to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φήνασθαι_944_3"> is <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τόξα</quote> only. It would be awkward to understand (with Nauck) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ καὶ τὰ τόξα</quote>: and the display of the captive is implied in the next vv.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλει_944">, and reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχ_ὡς_944"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_οἶδ_944"> in 946, understands:—‘He wishes to boast (<quote xml:lang="grc">φήνασθαι</quote>, <emph>gloriose de se praedicare</emph>) among the Argives that (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_944"> 945) he is bringing me by force, a strong man whom he has taken, and not as it were a dead man whom he is slaying’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐχ ὡς ἐναίρων νεκρόν</quote>). But the awkwardness of this conjectural </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχ_ὡς_944_2"> is intolerable, when </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_944_2"> in 945 is to mean ‘that.’ Further, it is clearly essential to the force of the passage that there should be a full stop (or colon) at </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλει_944_2">. Verse 945 is an indignant amplification of 941, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς Τροίαν μ’ ἄγει</quote>. ‘He is taking me by force, I say, as if he had captured (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἑλὼν_944">) a strong man,’ etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_οἶδ_946">. Neoptolemus knows, of course, that Ph. is feeble. But these words mean that, in taking Ph. to Troy, N. does not realise what he is doing; he will not gain a triumph, but merely extinguish a flickering life. As this speech wavers between curses and prayers, so it vacillates between denunciation of the youth's cruel guile (926 f.), and something like pity for his thoughtless folly. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1010">Cp. 1010</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναίρων_νεκρὸν_946">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1030"> <title>Ant.</title> 1030</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν θανόντ’ ἐπικτανεῖν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καπνοῦ_σκιάν_946">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1170"> <title>Ant.</title> 1170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄλλ’ ἐγὼ καπνοῦ σκιᾶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν πριαίμην</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴδωλον_ἄλλως_946">: <ref n="Soph. OC 109"> <title>O. C.</title> 109</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτίρατ’ ἀνδρὸς Οἰδίπου τόδ’ ἄθλιον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδωλον· οὐ γὰρ δὴ τό γ’ ἀρχαῖον δέμας</quote> (n.). The adv. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλως_946"> means (1) ‘otherwise,’ <ref n="Soph. OC 492"> <title>O. C.</title> 492</ref>: (2) ‘besides,’ ‘moreover,’ <ref n="Soph. OT 1114"> <title>O. T.</title> 1114</ref>: (3) ‘otherwise than well,’ and so, ‘vainly,’ <ref n="Soph. OT 333"> <title>O. T.</title> 333</ref>,—as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτερος</quote> oft.=<quote xml:lang="grc">κακός</quote>: (4) with a subst. implying disparagement, ‘<emph>merely</emph>’; <title>Nub.</title> 1203 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριθμός, πρόβατ’ ἄλλως</quote>, ‘ciphers—very sheep’: <ref n="Dem. 19.24">Dem. or. 19 § 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἀντιλέγοντες ὄχλος ἄλλως καὶ βασκανία κατεφαίνετο</quote>, ‘the opposition was pronounced to be mere obstructiveness and spite’ (where see Shilleto). This sense comes through that of ‘vainly,’ ‘uselessly.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_οὐδ_948">: for the synizesis <ref n="Soph. Phil. 446">cp. 446</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_950">, though only conjectural (cr. n.), is confirmed by the fact that elsewhere also the hortative is combined with the limiting <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>: see 1040 f.: <ref n="Soph. OC 238"> <title>O. C.</title> 238</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπεὶ...ἀλλ’ ἐμὲ</quote> (me, at least). The loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόδος</quote> here may have been due to a reminiscence of 932.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_νῦν_950">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 411"> <title>El.</title> 411</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγένεσθέ γ’ ἀλλὰ νῦν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_γενοῦ_950">: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 5. 17">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 5. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἑαυτῷ ἐγένετο</quote>, he recovered himself (after an outbreak of passion). So <ref n="Hdt. 1. 119">Her. 1. 119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ἐξεπλάγη ἐντός τε ἑωυτοῦ γίνεται</quote>, ‘he did not lose his presence of mind, but mastered his feelings.’ The simple gen. of the reflex. pron. is similarly used, <ref n="Soph. OC 660">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 660</ref>(n.): <ref n="Dem. 2.30">Dem. or. 2 § 30</ref> (which confirms </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_950"> here): <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ δὴ...ὑμῶν αὐτῶν ἔτι καὶ νῦν γενομένους κ.τ.λ.</quote>—The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_σαυτοῦ_950"> here has been supported by <title> Vesp.</title> 642 <quote xml:lang="grc">σκορδινᾶται κἄστιν οὐκ ἐν αὑτοῦ</quote>. But there I should read either <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθ’ αὑτοῦ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν αὑτῷ</quote>. In <ref n="Plat. Charm. 155D"> Plat. <title>Charm.</title> 155D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτ’ ἐν ἐμαυτοῦ ἦν</quote>, other readings are <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἐμαυτοῦ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐμαυτῷ</quote>; the last is prob. right. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σιωπᾷς_951">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1271"> <title>O. C.</title> 1271</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί σιγᾷς;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_εἰμ_951">, am as dead: <ref n="Soph. El. 677"> <title>El.</title> 677</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 393"> <title>O. C.</title> 393</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτ’ οὐκέτ’ εἰμί, τηνικαῦτ’ ἄρ’ εἴμ’ ἀνήρ</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δίπυλον_952">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">διπύλου</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc"> σχῆμα‐πέτρας</quote> forms one notion: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote>.—The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχῆμα_952">, in such a periphrasis, usu. denotes stateliness (as in <ref n="Eur. Alc. 911"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 911</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σχῆμα δόμων</quote>, and so <ref n="Eur. Hec. 619">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 619</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σχήματ’ οἴκων</quote>): here it marks the distinctness of the form present to his thoughts (like <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα...θηρὸς</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1568">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1568</ref>). Alike in bodily and in mental suffering, the outlines of surrounding objects become vividly stamped upon the mind. Cp. Byron, <title>Prisoner of Chillon</title> (stanza x): ‘But then by dull degrees came back | My senses to their wonted track; | I saw the dungeon walls and floor | Close slowly round me as before.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_πάλιν_952">, a rarer phrase than <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις πάλιν</quote> (342), or <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις αὖ</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 854">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 854</ref>): in <ref n="Soph. OC 1418">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1418</ref> we should read <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις ἂν πάλιν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_σὲ_952"> here: but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρός_σε_952"> is surely required by the sense. There is no emphasis on the pron. (as if the cave were contrasted with some other abode). The stress is on <quote xml:lang="grc">ψιλός</quote>: his former life in the cave, when he had the bow, is contrasted with the life now before him.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψιλός_952">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1029"> <title>O. C.</title> 1029</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ψιλὸν οὐδ’ ἄσκευον</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐανοῦμαι_954">: <ref n="Soph. El. 819"> <title>El.</title> 819</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλος αὐανῶ βίον</quote>. This is one of the rare instances in which a true reading, lost to the text of L, has been preserved by the schol.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 40"> <title>Ant.</title> 40</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 235."> Ant. 235.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτηνὸν_955"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 288">cp. 288</ref> )...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρειβάτην_955"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 937">cp. 937</ref>): the epithets are not merely ornamental; they suggest the distance of the prey, and so the helplessness of the unarmed man.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοισίδ_955">, if right, is the only example of this Ionic form in Soph. ; nor is there any in In <ref n="Eur. Med. 1295">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1295</ref>, where the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδέ γ’</quote>, Canter gave <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιδ’</quote>, which Elms. wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">τοισίδ’</quote> (comparing <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιόνδ’</quote>): Wecklein there, as here, conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδ’ ἔτ’</quote> (<title>Ars Soph. em.</title> p. 33); though here, in his ed., he keeps <quote xml:lang="grc">τοισίδ’</quote>. The question here is,—Does L's <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιδ’</quote>, corrected by the 1st hand from <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιν</quote>, point rather to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοισίδ_955_2"> or to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσδ_ἔτ_955">? To the former, I think. If <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδ’ ἔτ’</quote> had been the original reading, the unusual form <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιδ’</quote> would hardly have supplanted it. The accent proves nothing, for the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">τοίσδεσσι</quote> used to be written <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδεσσι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέξω_δαῖτα_957"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> τούτοις</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_ἐφερβ_957">.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1362"> <title>O. T.</title> 1362</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμογενὴς δ’ ἀφ’ ὧν ἔφυν</quote>. <title>M.</title> 1. 2. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλέγεσθαι παρ’ ὧν ἂν λάβοιεν τὸν μισθόν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις παρ’ ὧν</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπό_957"> is right. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπό_957"> would also be right, but tamer. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_μ_958">, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄμ_958">, because the contrast between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐθήρων_958"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηράσουσι_958"> suffices. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 47">Cp. 47</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥύσιον_959"> is what one ‘draws to oneself,’ as spoil, or by way of security ( <ref n="Soph. OC 858"> <title>O. C.</title> 858</ref> n.), or in reprisal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνον_τείσω_959">=I shall pay (to the beasts) my life-blood, <emph>taken by them in reprisal</emph> for life-blood (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνου_959">, gen. of the price or equivalent). Cp. Polyb. 4. 53 <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥύσια κατήγγειλαν τοῖς Ῥοδίοις</quote>, ‘formally threatened them with reprisals’ (for bloodshed).—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τείσω</emph>, ἔτεισα</quote>, was the Attic spelling in the poet's time, as inscrr. prove: <ref n="Soph. OT 810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</ref>(2nd ed.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δοκοῦντος_959">, partic. of the imperf. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἐδόκει</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 835"> <title>O. T.</title> 835</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τοῦ παρόντος</quote> n.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1565"> <title>O. C.</title> 1565</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1192."> <title>Ant.</title> 1192.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_κακόν_959">, not, ‘to have no evil sentiment’ (like the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤπια εἰδώς</quote>, etc.), but simply, ‘to know no evil’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 301"> <title>Ant.</title> 301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσέβειαν εἰδέναι</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄλοιο_961"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήπω_961">. The mere fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιο</quote> comes first means that the curse does pass his lips,—though it is instantly qualified by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήπω_961_2">. Hence the effect of the Greek is not like this—‘I say not yet, Mayest thou perish’: but rather;—‘Perish !—no, not yet,’ etc. Just so in <ref n="Eur. Med. 83">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιτο μὲν μή· δεσπότης γάρ ἐστ’ ἐμός</quote>, ‘curse him—I may not,’ etc. (In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 383">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιντο μή τι πάντες κ.τ.λ.</quote>, the context is different.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_μάθοιμ_961">: the optat. is due to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιο</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 325">cp. 325</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 655"> <title>Tr.</title> 655</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σταίη</quote> | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἀνύσειε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_πάλιν_961">. Nauck, referring to Porson's note on <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1464">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1464</ref>(=1450 Dind.)—as to which, see Appendix—says that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_961"> cannot be right; and on that assumption various emendations have been proposed. The defence of the metre turns on the distinction between two classes of monosyllables: (1) those which count as belonging to the words after them, viz., the article; prepositions; <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ, ἤ, καί, μή, οὐ, ὡς</quote>; and the interrogatives, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς, πῶς, ποῦ, ποῖ, πῇ</quote>: (2) those which count as belonging to the words before them, viz., all enclitics, and such other words as cannot begin a sentence. Since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_961"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_961_2"> are both of the first class, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_πάλιν_961_2"> is metrically equivalent to a quadrisyllable like <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱρούμενον</quote>, and therefore the rule against a final cretic does not apply. On the other hand such an ending as <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν μάθοιμ’ οὖν καὶ πάλιν</quote> would be wrong, because <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> is a monosyllable of the second class.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_961_3"> closely with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλιν_961">: cp. <ref n="Plat. Menex. 249E">Plat. <title>Menex.</title> 249E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα καὶ αὖθίς σοι...ἀπαγγέλλω</quote>. This seems better than to take it with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετοίσεις_961"> (‘if thou wilt <emph>indeed</emph> change’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλινμετοίσεις_961"> is pleonastic, since Ph. does not now suppose that N.'s purpose was ever honest: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1270">cp. 1270</ref> : <ref n="Thuc. 2. 13">Thuc. 2. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐλάσσω ἀντικαταστῆσαι πάλιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶμεν_963">; subjunct.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_σοὶ_963"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 314"> <title>O. T.</title> 314</ref> n.: <title>I. T.</title> 1057 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τἄμ’ ἐν ὑμῖν ἐστιν ἢ καλῶς ἔχειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ μηδὲν εἶναι καὶ στερηθῆναι πάτρας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσχωρεῖν_963">: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιχωρεῖν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 219"> <title>Ant.</title> 219</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 222">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 222</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ δὲ ξένον μὲν κάρτα προσχωρεῖν πόλει</quote> (‘comply’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_μὲν_965">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> emphasising the pron. (without an answering <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέπτωκε_965">: cp. Philippides <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργυρίου Ἀφανισμός</quote> 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔλεος ἐμπέπτωκέ τις μοι τῶν ὅλων</quote>. Soph. has used the acc. with this verb in <ref n="Soph. OC 942">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 942</ref> (n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πρῶτον_965">: <ref n="Soph. El. 1049">Soph. <title>El.</title> 1049</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι δέδοκται ταῦτα κοὐ vεωστί μοι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλἐησον_967">: cp. on 307 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῇς_ὄνειδος_967">, allow men to have ground for reproaching thee: a poet. modification of the more usual constr., <quote xml:lang="grc">παρῇς σεαυτὸν βροτοῖς ὀνειδίζειν</quote> (as <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 101">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 101</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεὶς ἀποκρίνασθαι τοῖς... σοφωτέροις</quote>). So oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνειδος καταλείπειν. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκλέψας_967">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαπατήσας</quote>, as in 55 (n.): not, ‘having stolen me out of Lemnos.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήποτ_969">, though it belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιπεῖν_969">, can be prefixed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤφελον_969"> because the whole phrase is felt as a wish: so <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.548"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 548</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὴ μὴ ὄφελον νικᾷν</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 997"> <title>Tr.</title> 997</ref> the inf. has its due precedence: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν μή ποτ’ ἐγὼ προσιδεῖν ὁ τάλας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤφελον ὄσσοις. </quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σκῦρον_969">: 240 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δοὺς_972">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ αἰσχρά</quote>, having left the base deeds to others, whom they befit (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_εἰκός_972">, <emph> sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦναι αὐτά</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 405">Cp. 405</ref>—409. As the chief emphasis here is on the character of N. (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_σύ_972"> ), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοισι_972"> is naturally contrasted with <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote>, rather than with <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν ἀνδρῶν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοὺς_972">= <quote xml:lang="grc">δοὺς σεαυτόν</quote>, ‘yielding to others’ (than the <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοὶ ἄνδρες</quote>),—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to Philoctetes himself. The objection here is the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">δούς</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 21">Eur. <title> Phoen.</title> 21</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ ἡδονῇ δούς</quote>, is the only extant example of this usage in the classical period, and there it denotes self-abandonment to impulse; a tone which was apparently associated with it by Alciphron also, when he wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμῳ δοὺς φέρεσθαι</quote> (3. 47), <emph>me in pedes coniciens.</emph> (2) Reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοις_δοὺς_972">: ‘having allowed thyself to be overruled by others’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, by Ph. ). But this phrase implies relations of confidence and friendship (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 84">cp. 84</ref>): it does not suit the stern and cold admonition which these verses convey. (3) With Dindorf's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_972"> (which he does not explain) the obvious sense would be, ‘having given others their due,’—an anticipation of <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμά μοι μεθεὶς ὅπλα</quote>. The objection to this is that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοισι_972_2"> then becomes strange, since Ph. is no longer contrasted with bad advisers, but is merely the recipient of the bow.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶ_πάλιν_972">; Neoptolemus was in the act of approaching Philoctetes: Odysseus places himself between them. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1398"> <title>O. C.</title> 1398</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν τ’ ἴθ’ ὡς τάχος πάλιν</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1724 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν, φίλα, συθῶμεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπραμαι_978">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 579">cp. 579</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διεμπολᾷ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅμ_αὐτοῖς_983">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς τόξοις</quote>. So in 1059 <quote xml:lang="grc"> τούτων</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὅπλα</quote> in 1056.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στελοῦσί_σε_983">, <emph>sc.</emph> the two attendants of Odysseus, who have entered along with him (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 985">cp. 985</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδε</quote>, and 1003). It should be remembered that, to the spectators, there could be nothing obscure in <quote xml:lang="grc">στελοῦσι</quote>, since Odysseus would glance or point at the men. There is no need, then, for the conjectures (cr. n.) which have sought either to introduce <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδε</quote> or to remove <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐτοῖς</quote>. Greek idiom readily tolerated either change or ellipse of subject: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1065"> <title>O. C.</title> 1065</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλώσεται</quote>.—We cannot well refer </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῖς_983"> either to the attendants of Od., or to Neoptolemus and the Chorus. Odysseus would rather say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν</quote>. If it is objected that the bow cannot be said <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχειν</quote>, the answer is that <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχειν ἅμ’ αὐτοῖς</quote> is merely a way of saying <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχειν ἅμα τοῖς τὰ τόξα φέρουσι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τολμήστατε_984">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> τολμηέστατε</quote>, superl. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμήεις</quote>. Odysseus says in <ref n="Hom. Od. 17.284"> <title>Od.</title> 17. 284</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμήεις μοι θυμός, ἐπεὶ κακὰ πολλὰ πέπονθα</quote>. The contracted form has been much suspected here; Nauck pronounces it corrupt, because (1) tragic dialogue nowhere admits adjectives in <quote xml:lang="grc">άεις, ήεις, όεις</quote>, and (2) the contr. <quote xml:lang="grc">ήστατος</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ηέστατος</quote> is unexampled. As to (1), we may observe that in <ref n="Soph. OT 1279"> <title>O. T.</title> 1279</ref> it is almost certain that Soph. used <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματόεις</quote>: Porson there conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματοῦσσ’</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματος</quote>): Heath, with greater probability, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματοῦς</quote>. As to (2), it is true that there is no other instance of this contr. in a superlative: but there are epic examples of the same contr. in the positive: <ref n="Hom. Od. 7.110">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 7. 110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνῆσσαι</quote>, restored by Bekker from <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνῆσαι</quote> (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνήεσσαι, τεχνιτίδες</quote>): <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.475">Hom. <title>Il.</title> 18. 475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ χρυσὸν τιμῆντα καὶ ἄργυρον</quote> (where no emend. is probable). I do not add <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.605"> Hom. <title>Il.</title> 9. 605</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέθ’ ὁμῶς τιμῇς ἔσεαι</quote>: for, though we cannot read <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμῶς τιμῆς</quote>, I would suggest that the change of one letter will restore the true reading, viz. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμῆς τιμῆς</quote>: and C. LobeckA. , while conceding that Florian Lobeck (<title>Quaest. Ion.</title> p. 8) used ‘too great severity’ in condemning <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμήστατος</quote>, agrees with him in pointing out that such a contraction as <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνῇς</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνήεις</quote> cannot be safely inferred from <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνῆσσα</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνήεσσα</quote> (<title>Pathol.</title> 1. 343). This consideration is a fresh argument against Hermann's conj. <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλαζῇς</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1279"> <title>O. T.</title> 1279</ref>: and it also reminds us that <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμήστατος</quote> does not imply <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμῇς</quote>. The example of Oppian (<title>Cyneg.</title> 2. 140 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆντα χαλινά</quote>) shows that late poets did not shrink from this contraction. In Pindar we find <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγλάεντα</quote> (<title>P.</title> 2. 10), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκάεντας</quote> (<title>O.</title> 9. 77), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργάεντα</quote> (<title>O.</title> 13. 69), <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνάεντα</quote> (<title>O.</title> 2. 93), with synizesis of <quote xml:lang="grc">αε</quote>; though recent edd. no longer write <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγλᾶντα</quote>, etc. On the whole, I believe that Soph. would have felt that he had sufficient poetical warrant for <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμήστατε</quote>. No emendation seems possible which is at once tolerable in itself, and such as to account for the tradition. <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμίστατε</quote> was a worthless conjecture. Such forms as <quote xml:lang="grc">κλεπτίστατος, φαρμακίστατος</quote> always imply a positive in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐ης</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ος</quote>, and occur only in Comedy or in late prose. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵδ_985">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1003">cp. 1003</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἕρπῃς_985">: the coalescence of final </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="η_985"> with an <emph>aspirated</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ε_985"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_985"> is extremely rare: <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 828">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 828</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ἑτέρωσε</quote> (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 64">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 64</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 736">Ar. <title>Lys.</title> 736</ref>): Philemon <quote xml:lang="grc">Παρεισίων</quote> 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἁμάρτημα τί</quote>;—The addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_985"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρπῃς_985"> is plausible: but the placid answer is perhaps more effective without it: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 105">cp. 105</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_Ἡφαιστότευκτον_985">. As to the place of the second adj., see note on <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχρυσον</quote> in 393. The ‘flame wrought by Hephaestus’ is the flame which he causes to break forth from the summit of the volcano Mosychlus (800 n.): cp. Antimachus fr. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡφαίστου φλογὶ εἴκελον, ἥν ῥα τιτύσκει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων ἀκροτάταις ὄρεος κορυφῇσι Μοσύχλου</quote>. We need not suppose, with the schol., that the epithet refers directly to Hephaestus working at his forge within the mountain. When hurled by Zeus from Olympus, Hephaestus fell on Lemnos, and was there tended by the <quote xml:lang="grc">Σίντιες</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.593"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 593</ref>). The isle was sacred to him,—<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡφαίστῳ...γαιάων πολὺ φιλτάτη</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.284"> <title>Od.</title> 8. 284</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">κραναὸν πέδον Ἡφαίστοιο</quote> (Perieget. Dionys. 522): <title>Vulcania Lemnos</title> (Valerius Flaccus 4. 440: cp. <title>Fasti</title> 3. 82). The chief seat of his worship was the town of Hephaestia, situated on the northern inlet (now the bay of Purnia).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἀπάξεται_988">: <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> with fut. ind. is oft. thus used, where indignation is implied: cp. n. on 376: <ref n="Soph. El. 1210"> <title>El.</title> 1210</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.15">Lys. or. 12 § 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐλεοῦντες...τὰ τείχη, εἰ πεσεῖται, οὐδὲ κηδόμενοι τῶν νεῶν, εἰ... παραδοθήσονται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἐλεοῦντες...τὰ τείχη, εἰ πεσεῖται, οὐδὲ
							κηδόμενοι τῶν νεῶν, εἰ... παραδοθήσονται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_σῶν_988">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ σά</quote>, the precincts of Lemnos and her <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγχώριοι θεοί</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_989">. Philoctetes has appealed to the local deities of Lemnos. Odysseus retorts that Zeus is above them all, and that Zeus (by his oracle) has given the behest which is now being executed.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_εἰδῇς_989">, here like ‘let me tell thee,’— with a dictatorial tone. Schneidewin cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.111"> <title>Od.</title> 2. 111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δ’ ὧδε μνηστῆρες ὑποκρίνονθ’, <ref n="Hom. Od. 1"> οδ. ι</ref>(/να εἰδῇς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς σῷ θυμῷ, εἰδῶσι δὲ πάντες Ἀχαιοί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῖσος_991">: cf. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 760"> <title>Ant.</title> 760</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγετε τὸ μῖσος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἐξανευρίσκεις_991">, ‘dost <emph>indeed</emph> invent’ (not, ‘dost invent <emph>besides</emph>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τοῖς ἔργοις</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 234">cp. 234</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦ τὸ καὶ λαβεῖν</quote>: and so in a question expressing surprise, <ref n="Soph. OT 1129"> <title>O. T.</title> 1129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον ἄνδρα καὶ λέγεις</quote>;—The compound <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανευρίσκω</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαφοράω</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1648"> <title>O. C.</title> 1648</ref>) is otherwise strange to classical Greek, but appears to have been common later: Wyttenbach, in his Index to Plutarch (p. 595), quotes eight instances of it from the <title> Moralia.</title> The inf. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_991"> is epexeg. (‘for thyself to say’): we cannot compare <ref n="Soph. OT 120"> <title>O. T.</title> 120</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἓν γὰρ πόλλ’ ἂν ἐξεύροι μαθεῖν</quote> (n.). Perhaps it should be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_991">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προτείνων_992">, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆψιν προτείνειν</quote>, putting the gods forward as authority for thy deed: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψευδεῖς_τίθης_992">, thou makest them false,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> responsible for thy fraud. (Not, ‘makest them false prophets,’ because Ph. will never go to Troy.) For the art. with the repeated word, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 277">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ θεοὺς τιμῶντες ε<*>τα τοὺς θεοὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μοίραις ποεῖσθε μηδαμῶς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀληθεῖς_993">, because their oracle will be fulfilled: Ph. will be brought to Troy. Thus Hom. Od.parries the thrust given by <quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδεῖς τίθης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_ὁδὸς_993">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1501"> <title>El.</title> 1501</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἀντιφωνεῖς, ἡ δ’ ὁδὸς βραδύνεται</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_φήμ_994">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_φημί_994">. So Gernhard. The MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ φημ’ ἔγωγε.—φημί</quote>. Now, <quote xml:lang="grc">φημί</quote> could stand thus alone, if it were the answer to a question, <quote xml:lang="grc">φὴς ἢ οὐ φής</quote>; but not here, where two persons are opposed to each other. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 840"> <title>O. C.</title> 840</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. χαλᾶν λέγω σοι. ΚΡ. σοὶ δ’ ἔγωγ’ ὁδοιπορεῖν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πειστέον_τάδε_994">=<quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ πείθεσθαι τάδε</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1252">Cp. 1252</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμᾶς_μὲν_995">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 965">cp. 965</ref>. These words show the speaker's bitter sense that, while he is not inferior to his enemy in point of birth, he has been superior to him in such deeds as become an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεύθερος</quote>. It was Odysseus, not Philoctetes, who had gone to Troy, only ‘when brought under the yoke’ (1025). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἀρίστοισιν_997"> is far better here than <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἀριστεῦσιν</quote>, in which the idea of rank partly obscures that of personal prowess.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασκάψαι_997">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1421"> <title>O. C.</title> 1421</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτραν κατασκάψαντι</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1318 <quote xml:lang="grc"> κατασκαφῇ...δῃώσειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέποτέ_γ_999">: <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 109"> Ar. <title>Pax</title> 109</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ τὸν Διόνυσον οὐδέποτε ζῶντός γ’ ἐμοῦ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_κακόν_999">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 615"> <title>El.</title> 615</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν ἂν εἰς πᾶν ἔργον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_βάθρον_999">= <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε γῆς αἰπεινῆς βάθρον</quote> (952), this pedestal of (=consisting in) a steep land, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the island, with its sheer cliffs, on which he is standing. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 859"> <title>Ai.</title> 859</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*> γῆς ἱρὸν οἰκείας πέδον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Σαλαμῖνος, ὦ πατρῷον ἑστίας βάθρον</quote> (cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 135), where, as here, the whole island is the <quote xml:lang="grc">βάθρον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐργασείεις_1001">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1232"> <title>Tr.</title> 1232</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐργασείων</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 326"> <title>Ai.</title> 326</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δρασείων</quote>: fr. 900 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκουσείων</quote>. The only other examples in Tragedy are <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1208">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1208</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δρασείετον</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Her. 628">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 628</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φευξείω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέτρᾳ_1002">, locative (rather than instrum.) dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱμάξω_1002">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1266"> <title>O. T.</title> 1266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ δὲ γῇ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκειτο.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέτρας_1002">, from the rock, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεσών_1002">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 613">cp. 613</ref>, 630. Such a use of the simple gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πίπτειν</quote> would be somewhat harsh, were there nothing in the context to explain it; but here the adv. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνωθεν_1002">, ‘from above,’ placed between <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρας</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πεσών</quote>, prevents any obscurity. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωθεν</quote> cannot be a prep. governing <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρας</quote>, since it could mean only, ‘above the rock.’) Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 782"> <title>Tr.</title> 782</ref>(where Lichas is hurled from the cliff) <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατὸς διασπαρέντος αἵματός θ’ ὁμοῦ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυλλάβετον_αὐτόν_1003"> is far the best correction of L's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυλλάβετ_αὐτόν_1003">. The addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1003"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυλλάβετε_1003"> was a feeble makeshift, and cannot be excused by assuming that the attendants had taken the initiative, so that their master merely says, ‘<title>Yes</title>, seize him.’ The use of the dual—‘Seize him, you two men’— is the more natural here, since each grasps one of his arms. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1437"> <title>O. C.</title> 1437</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθεσθε δ’ ἤδη, χαίρετόν τ’</quote> (as here, in 1054, we have the plur.): so in <title> Plut.</title> 76 the imperat. dual <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούετον</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθεσθε</quote> in 75.
