Other Attic examples of the imperat. dual are <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 107">Ar. <title> Av.</title> 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπατον</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 294C">Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 294C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιδείξατον</quote>. In Homer it is frequent ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.322"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 322</ref>; 7. 279; 8. 186, <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.191"> Il. 191</ref>; 20. 115; 23. 443; <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.60"> <title>Od.</title> 4. 60</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τῷδ_1003">: for this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> with dat. (<emph>penes eum</emph>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 66"> <title>O. C.</title> 66</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )πὶ τῷ πλήθει λόγος</quote>; <ref n="Soph. El. 1431."> <title>El.</title> 1431.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_χεῖρες_1004">: his arms have been seized by the two attendants, one of whom stands on each side of him: but we are not to infer from 1016 (<quote xml:lang="grc">συνδήσας</quote>) that he was actually bound. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1054">Cp. 1054</ref>. Heracles in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1089"> <title>Tr.</title> 1089</ref> uses a similar apostrophe; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ χέρες, χέρες κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_χρείᾳ_1004">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, denoting circumstance, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 185">cp. 185</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνθηρώμεναι_1004">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 432"> <title>Ant.</title> 432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δέ νιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρώμεθ’ εὐθύς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φρονῶν_1006">. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ὑγιές</quote> was a common one in Attic, and is often used by Eur. , though never by Aesch. , and only here by It Soph. is thrice combined with <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονεῖν</quote> by Eur. ,—twice to denote malevolence; fr. 496 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν δοκοῦσιν ὑγιὲς ἀνδράσιν φρονεῖν</quote>: fr. 821 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὑγιὲς οὐδὲν φασὶ μητρυιὰς φρονεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νόθοισι παισίν</quote>: and in <title>Androm.</title> 448 to describe the dishonesty of Spartans,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλικτὰ κοὐδὲν ὑγιὲς ἀλλὰ πᾶν πέριξ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονοῦντες.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_ὑγιές_1006">, the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the man is <emph>of the kind</emph> to have no sound thoughts: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 409">cp. 409</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν δίκαιον</quote>, n. This use of <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέν</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote>) here would probably sound the more natural, since the same combination oft. occurred in phrases with the <emph>inf.</emph>: as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 200">Soph. <title> Ph.</title> 200</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονὴ δέ τις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναιξὶ μηδὲν ὑγιὲς ἀλλήλαις λέγειν</quote>: fr. 660 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλῳ δ’ ἀρέσκει μηδὲν ὑγιὲς ἐκ φρενῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοντι πείθειν τοὺς πέλας τόλμῃ κακῇ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 50">Ar. <title>Plut.</title> 50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μηδὲν ἀσκεῖν ὑγιές</quote> </cit> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεύθερον_1006">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθέριον</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 63"> <title>Tr.</title> 63</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δούλη μέν, εἴρηκεν δ’ ἐλεύθερον λόγον</quote>: fr. 855 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ σῶμα δοῦλον, ἀλλ’ ὁ νοῦς ἐλεύθερος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_ὑπῆλθες_1007">: Odysseus had ‘stolen upon’ Ph. before, when he contrived that he should be left on Lemnos: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 264">cp. 264</ref>, 407 ff. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ’ αὖ μ’</quote> is the best and simplest correction of L's <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷά μ’</quote>. But Nauck is too hasty in saying that <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵως μ’</quote> is impossible, because the adv. was always <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα</quote>. In <title>Vesp.</title> 1362 f., certainly, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ αὐτὸν τωθάσω νεανικῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵως ποθ’ οὗτος ἐμὲ πρὸ τῶν μυστηρίων</quote>, the <emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵοις</quote> is tenable: but in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 923"> <title>Ai.</title> 923</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷος ὢν οἵως ἔχεις</quote> is beyond fair suspicion. And in Apoll. Rhod. 4. 786 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵως</quote> is much more probable than <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπῆλθες_1007">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 386"> <title>O. T.</title> 386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λάθρᾳ μ’ ὑπελθών</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποτρέχειν</quote>. Ovid <title>Ars amat.</title> 1. 742 <title>Si tibi laudanti credidit</title>, <emph> ipse</emph> subit (‘supplants thee’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόβλημα_1008">, a screen: <ref n="Plat. Soph. 261A">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 261A</ref> (a sophist is <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσθήρευτος</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">φαίνεται γὰρ οὖν προβλημάτων γέμειν</quote> (to have a large supply of outworks), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἐπειδάν τι προβάλῃ, τοῦτο πρότερον ἀναγκαῖον διαμάχεσθαι πρὶν ἐπ’ αὐτὸν ἐκεῖνον ἀφικέσθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάξιον_σοῦ_1009">, too good for thee: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἴσος</quote> as=‘greater,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅμο<*>ος</quote> as=‘more important’: <ref n="Soph. OT 810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</ref> n. Wakefield cp. <title>Phorm.</title> 2. 2. 28 <emph>te indignas seque dignas contumelias</emph> | <title>Numquam cessavit dicere hodie.</title> For the emphatic place of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_1009">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 907">cp. 907</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾷς</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_πλὴν_1009"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘had no ideas’ beyond obedience to orders: a freq. phrase in Comedy ( <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 19">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 19</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 740">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 740</ref>, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_νῦν_1011">, already, though the time for remorse has been short.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_1011">= <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote> (causal dat.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ.—)παθον</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">)φάνη</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457"> <title>Ant.</title> 457</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_βλέπους_1013">, peering forth through (the obscurity of) the secret places from which it watches. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 914"> <title>Tr.</title> 914</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαθραῖον ὄμμ’ ἐπεσκιασμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρούρουν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 490"> <title>El.</title> 490</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινοῖς κρυπτομένα λόχοις</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐρινύς</quote>). <ref n="Plat. Rep. 519A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 519A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ οὔπω ἐννενόηκας, τῶν λεγομένων πονηρῶν μέν, σοφῶν δέ, ὡς δριμὺ μὲν βλέπει τὸ ψυχάριον καὶ ὀξέως διορᾷ ἐφ’ ἃ τέτραπται</quote>...; The words are illustrated by the keenness with which Odysseus had seized, and used, the weak side of the youth's character,—his desire for glory (113—120).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφυᾶ_1013">. When </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εα_1013"> is preceded by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ε_1013">, the contr. is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εᾶ_1013">, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδεᾶ</quote>: when by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_1013"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υ_1013">, it is alternatively </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ιᾶ_1013"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῆ_υᾶ_1013"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υῆ_1013">. Of the alternative forms, those with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾶ_1013"> were the standard Attic down to about 350 B.C.: thus <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐφυᾶ</quote> is attested by an Attic inscription of 356 B.C. Afterwards the forms with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῆ_1013"> prevailed. Cp. Meisterhans, p. 66, who cites Moeris p. 316: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑγιᾶ Ἀττικῶς, ὑγιῆ Ἑλληνικῶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐδίδαξεν_1013"> implies <emph>gradual</emph> teaching: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 538">cp. 538</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> προὔμαθον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 163"> <title>Ai.</title> 163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας προδιδάσκειν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνδήσας_1016"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγειν_1016">. He anticipates such an indignity from the fact that the two attendants are still holding his arms (1005).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐβάλου_1016"> is an exceptional use of the midd. in this sense: but cp. <ref n="Hdt. 6. 101">Her. 6. 101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππους...ἐξεβάλλοντο</quote> (put them ashore). The word is much stronger than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέθηκ’</quote> (5), or <quote xml:lang="grc">προθέντες</quote> (268): like <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔρριψαν</quote> (265), it implies ruthless scorn: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 830">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 830</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιφθῶ κυσὶν πρόβλητος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπολιν_1016">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1357"> <title>O. C.</title> 1357</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄθηκας ἄπολιν</quote>. To have no <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote> was to be an outcast from human society. It is the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφρήτωρ, ἀθέμιστος, ἀνέστιος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.63"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 63</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὶ_1020">: for the synizesis, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1036">cp. 1036</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 964"> <title>O. C.</title> 964</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶ_1020">, and takes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_πολλοῖς_1020"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γελώμενος_1020"> (‘mocked, in addition to my woes’). This punctuation, he argues, is necessary to the sense; for Ph. means that life itself (a joy to others) is a pain to him; whereas, if </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_πολλοῖς_1020_2"> be joined with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶ_1020_2">, Ph. will merely say that his pain consists in the <emph>misery</emph> of his life. Cavallin adopts this view. The answer to Seyffert's dilemma is, I think, that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_πολλοῖς_1020_3"> is not merely an adverbial qualification of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶ_1020_3"> (‘live miserably’), but is here equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς πολλοῖς συνών</quote> (‘live,—in company with many woes’). There is no objection to a comma after <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶ</quote>, provided that there be one after <quote xml:lang="grc">τάλας</quote> also; but it seems unnecessary. For <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 268">cp. 268</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_στρατηγῶν_1020">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 943">cp. 943</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλοπῇ_ζυγεὶς_1025">, brought under the yoke (of military service) by stratagem and compulsion. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζυγεὶς_1025">, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 841">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 841</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνος δ’ Ὀδυσσεύς, ὅσπερ οὐχ ἑκὼν ἔπλει</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζευχθεὶς ἕτοιμος ἦν ἐμοὶ σειραφόρος</quote>. Odysseus was in Ithaca when he was called to the war, and feigned madness. Palamedes, the envoy of the Greeks, found him ploughing with an ox and an ass yoked together, and placed the infant Telemachus in front of the plough; when Odysseus betrayed his sanity by stopping. As in the case of Solomon's judgment, the typically shrewd man relied on his conviction that art could be surprised by nature. Cp. Lycophron 815 ff., where Cassandra says to Odysseus, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σχέτλἰ, ὥς σοι κρεῖσσον ἦν μίμνειν πάτρᾳ</quote> (in Ithaca) | <quote xml:lang="grc">βοηλατοῦντι</quote>,... | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλασταῖσι λύσσης μηχαναῖς οἰστρημένῳ</quote>. Tzetzes <emph>ad loc.</emph>, and Hyginus <ref n="Hyg. Fab. 95">Hyg. <title>Fab.</title> 95</ref>, tell the story. In Ovid <ref n="Ov. Met. 13. 34"> <title>Met.</title> 13. 34</ref>Ajax contrasts himself with Odysseus:—<title>An quod in arma prior nulloque sub indice veni</title> | <title>Arma neganda mihi? Potiorque videbitur ille</title> | <title>Ultima qui cepit</title>, <emph>detrectavitque furore</emph> | <title>Militiam ficto: donec sollertior isto</title>, | <title>Sed sibi inutilior</title> , <emph>timidi commenta retexit</emph> | <title>Naupliades animi</title> , <emph>vitataque traxit in arma?</emph> Sophocles wrote an <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀδυσσεὺς Μαινόμενος</quote> on this theme. According to <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.115"> <title>Od.</title> 24. 115</ref> ff. Agamemnon and Menelaus brought Odysseus from Ithaca to Troy by persuasion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑπτὰ_ναυσὶ_1027">, the ‘sociative’ use of the dat., to denote attendant circumstance: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 704"> <title>El.</title> 704</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκτος ἐξ Αἰτωλίας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξανθαῖσι πώλοις</quote>. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 11">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 2. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντων...Περσῶν...παμπληθεῖ στόλῳ</quote>. The poet follows <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.718"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 718</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δὲ Φιλοκτήτης ἦρχεν, τόξων ἐὺ εἰδώς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑπτὰ νεῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβαλον_1028">=<quote xml:lang="grc">προὔβαλον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1333"> <title>Ai.</title> 1333</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθαπτον...βαλεῖν</quote> (and <emph>ib.</emph> 1309).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_φής_1028"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: Blaydes (who compares <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 801">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 801</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς μὲν γάρ φαμεν ὑμᾶς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς δ’ ἡμᾶς</quote>) asks how Philoctetes could know this. We can only suppose that, before he was put on shore at Lemnos, the decision was announced to him by the Atreidae, who laid the responsibility on Odysseus. It was he who actually put Philoctetes ashore; and, when doing so, he may have cast the blame on his superiors,—as he does in v. 6. The occasional visitors to Lemnos (307) cannot well have been Ph. 's informants, since the Atreidae and Odysseus would not court notoriety for their deed (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 257">cp. 257</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγετε_1029">, take me away,—referring to the use of physical force; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάγεσθε_1029">, carry me with you,—referring to their ulterior purpose. For the midd., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 613">cp. 613</ref>, 988.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_εἰμι_1029">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 951">cp. 951</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηχ_ὑμῖν_1029">, dat. of relation, meaning here, ‘so far as it rested with you to kill me.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 429"> <title>O. C.</title> 429</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάστατος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖν ἐπέμφθην</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1128"> <title>Ai.</title> 1128</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεὸς γὰρ ἐκσῴζει με, τῷδε δ’ οἴχομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_1031">, ‘in thy sight,’ ethic dat.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 40"> <title>O. T.</title> 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτιστον πᾶσιν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 904"> <title>Ant.</title> 904</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσώδης_1031">. This word might suggest that it was the presence of <emph>Ph. in the same ship</emph> which the Greeks found insupportable. But the poet cannot have meant that. Chrysè was imagined by him as close to Lemnos (fr. 352); and Ph. would have been put on board one of his own ships (1027). <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσώδης</quote> must refer, then, to his presence at the sacrifices in Chrysè, which his cries interrupted (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 8">cp. 8</ref>, n.). Sophocles probably took this touch from the <title>Cypria</title>—the epic prelude to the <title>Iliad</title>—in which it was said that Ph. was bitten at Tenedos, where the Greek warriors were feasting, and then <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τὴν δυσοσμίαν ἐν Λήμνῳ κατελείφθη</quote> (Proclus <title>Chrest.</title> p. 475 ed. Gaisford).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ὁμοῦ_1031"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The MSS. have </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ἐμοῦ_1031">. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔξεσθ_1031"> only two senses are possible: (1) ‘<emph>vow</emph>’ to sacrifice. The pres. inf. could stand: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 933">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 933</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὔξω θεοῖς δείσας ἂν ὧδ’ ἔρδειν τάδε</quote>. But here the question is of actual sacrificing, not of vowing to do so at a future time. (2) ‘How will ye <emph>boast</emph> that ye sacrifice?’—a way of saying, ‘how will ye be able’ to do so. But such a phrase would be peculiarly awkward when the other sense of <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὔξεσθε</quote> would necessarily be suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς, αἴθειν, σπένδειν</quote>. Thus the context condemns </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔξεσθ_1031_2">. With regard to the conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξεσθ_1031"> it should be noted that its probability is confirmed by that of the further conjecture, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_1031"> instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοῦ_1031">. The traditional </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔξεσθ_ἐμοῦ_1031"> might, indeed, have arisen from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξεστ_ἐμοῦ_1031">, but would have been a still easier corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξεσθὁμοῦ_1031">. Given </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_1031">, the proximity of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖς_1031"> would suggest to a scribe that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεσθ’</quote> must be a blunder for <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔξεσθ’</quote>. The corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεσθ’</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔξεσθ’</quote> occurred earlier, we may infer, than that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote>. And this inference is supported by the fact that a tradition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ</quote> as a current <emph>v. l.</emph> is preserved in <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote>, while the only trace of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεσθ’</quote> appears to be a correction (prob. conjectural) in V.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξεσθ_1031_2"> it has been objected that the fut. is required. But Ph. is ironically repeating what the Greek chiefs said long ago, and is supposing that he is once more their comrade. ‘When I have once sailed with you, how can ye sacrifice?’ With <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὁμοῦ πλεύσαντος</emph>, ἐμοῦ</quote> is easily understood: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 803">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 803</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσαῦτ’ ἐπισκήψαντος</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ</quote>): <ref n="Plat. Parm. 137C">Plat. <title> Parm.</title> 137C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ γὰρ λέγεις τὸν νεώτατον λέγων. ἀλλ’ ἐρώτα ὡς ἀποκρινονμένου</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄλοισθ_δ_1035">: Blaydes cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 887">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 887</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς ἄρ’ ἐξόλοιο· κἀξολεῖ γ’ ἔτι</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc">γέ τοι</quote> vulg.].—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>θεοῖσιν</emph>, ¯˘</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1020">cp. 1020</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλει_γ_1037">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the verb, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 660">cp. 660</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_οὔποτ_1037">: for this synizesis, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 446">cp. 446</ref> n.—Philoctetes has not found the gods kindly: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 254">cp. 254</ref>, 452, 1020. But the very fact that Odysseus and his comrade have taken the trouble to visit Lemnos shows that at least the gods have some care for justice. Maimed as Ph. is (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρὸς_ἀθλίου_1037">), he is not one whom those pitiless warriors would have sought, had not the gods driven the sense of need for him like a goad into their souls. The Greeks must be failing at Troy; and their failure is the proof that the gods are just.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_1037">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 104">cp. 104</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέντρον_ἐμοῦ_1037">, the sting of need <emph> for</emph> me. The objective gen. is like that after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθυμία</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντρον</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶστρος</quote>, was constantly associated with that idea. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 573E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 573E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ὑπὸ κέντρων ἐλαυνομένους τῶν ... ἐπιθυμιῶν ... οἰστρᾶν</quote> (‘rage’). <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 39">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντροις ἔρωτος</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1303 <quote xml:lang="grc">δηχθεῖσα κέντροις</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀφροδίτης</quote>). So an objective gen. can follow <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶστρος</quote> when it means <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰστρώδης ἐπιθυμία</quote>: <title>Anthol.</title> II. 389. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σέ γ’ ἀπειρεσίων οἶστρος ἕλῃ κτεάνων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοί_ἐπόψιοι_1040">, gods who look upon the deeds of men, noting the good and the evil. The name <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόψιος</quote> was specially given to Zeus,—primarily in reference to the fact that, as <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὕψιστος</quote>, he was so often worshipped on mountain summits,—as on Parnassus, Cithaeron, Parnes, Hymettus, Ida, the heights near Cenaeum ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 238"> <title>Tr.</title> 238</ref> n.), etc. Hence his epithets <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάκριος, ἀκραῖος</quote>, and in Boeotia <quote xml:lang="grc">καραῖος</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴα_γῆ_1040">, in connection with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπόψιοι_1040">, is peculiarly appropriate for Ph. , in whose country Zeus was worshipped on Oeta (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 728">cp. 728</ref> n.). The secondary sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόψιος</quote>—‘watching over’ human life—is associated with the first by Callimachus in his <cit> <bibl n="Call. Jov. 82">Hymn to Zeus, 82 ff.:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶκας δὲ πτολίεθρα φυλασσέμεν· ἵζεο δ’ αὐτὸς | ἄκρῃς ἐν πτολίεσσιν, ἐπόψιος οἵ τε δίκῃσι | λαὸν ὑπὸ σκολιῇς, οἵ τ’ ἔμπαλιν ἰθύνουσιν</quote> </cit>. Apoll. Rhod. 2. 1125 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντόμεθα πρὸς Ζηνὸς ἐποψίου</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 1182 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς αὐτὸς τὰ ἕκαστ’ ἐπιδέρκεται</quote> (as <ref n="Soph. El. 175">Soph. <title>El.</title> 175</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ὃς ἐφορᾷ πάντα καὶ κρατύνει</quote>). As the vindicator of right, Zeus was also called <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαιόσυνος, ἀλάστωρ, τιμωρός</quote>. Acc. to Hesych. s. v. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπόψιος</quote>, the epithet was also given to Apollo. But, next to Zeus, the deity whom <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόψιος</quote> most directly suggests is Helios <quote xml:lang="grc">πανόπτης,—θεῶν σκοπὸς ἠδὲ καὶ ἀνδρῶν</quote> (<title>Hom. hym.</title> 5. 62). </gloss>

data.quote:
δῶκας δὲ πτολίεθρα φυλασσέμεν· ἵζεο δ’ αὐτὸς |
							ἄκρῃς ἐν πτολίεσσιν, ἐπόψιος οἵ τε δίκῃσι | λαὸν ὑπὸ σκολιῇς, οἵ τ’
							ἔμπαλιν ἰθύνουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τείσασθε_1041">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 959">cp. 959</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_χρόνῳ_1041">: so in <ref n="Soph. El. 1013"> <title>El.</title> 1013</ref>: in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 201"> <title>Tr.</title> 201</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ σὺν χρόνῳ</quote>. Cp. above, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 950"> Tr. 950</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄμ_1042">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘me, on the other part’: for this <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 53"> <title>O. C.</title> 53</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσ’ ο<*>δα κἀγὼ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_vόσου_1044">: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.18"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔνθα πεφυγμένος ἦεν ἀέθλων</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 488"> <title>Ant.</title> 488</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρύς_1045">: 368 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχ_ὑπείκ_1045">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 472"> <title>Ant.</title> 472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκειν δ’ οὐκ ἐπίσταται κακοῖς</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρείκοι_1048">, impers., here=<quote xml:lang="grc">σχολὴ εἴη</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 1">Thuc. 3. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσβολαὶ...ἐγίγνοντο... ὅπῃ παρείκοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑνὸς_λόγου_1048">, ‘I have the power (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, leisure) to say only one thing.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 409"> <title>O. T.</title> 409</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσ’ ἀντιλέξαι· τοῦδε γὰρ κἀγὼ κρατῶ</quote>.—Not, ‘lay hold upon’ one saying, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘take my stand upon’ it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1049">, prefacing the statement: 915.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιούτων_1049">, ‘such or such’ a man,— ‘any given kind’ of person:—euphemistic for <quote xml:lang="grc">δολίων</quote>, or the like. Such a colloquial use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτος</quote> seems quite intelligible, since it could be interpreted by an expressive tone of the voice, or by a slight gesture. (Not, ‘such as thou hast described.’) It would be grievous to change </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιούτων_1049_2"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανούργων_1049">, as Nauck proposes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρίσις_1049">, lit., trial, competition ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 266"> <title>Tr.</title> 266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τόξου κρίσιν</quote>): the usual word would be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγών</quote>, but euphony would not permit it here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μου_1049">: see n. on 47. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νικᾶν_1052">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 109">cp. 109</ref>, 134.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_μέντοι_1052">: 93 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_σέ_1052">, with regard to thee: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 731"> <title>Ant.</title> 731</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβεῖν εἰς τοὺς κακούς</quote>. Odysseus is resigned to Ph. carrying his point by staying in Lemnos.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκστήσομαι_1052">, ‘make way for’ (and so, here, ‘defer to’): <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 353">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 353</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφημεῖν χρὴ κἀξίστασθαι τοῖς ἡμετέροισι χοροῖσιν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄφετε_αὐτόν_1054"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1054"> confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκστήσομαι</quote>. ‘I will yield; for (I now say) ‘loose him.’’ Hence we may render, ‘<title>Yes</title>, loose him.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1004">Cp. 1004</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_σοῦ_1054">. If we wrote </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_σου_1054">, then the stress would fall on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσχρῄζομεν_1054"> ‘(We shall leave thee here.) Nor do we <emph>need</emph> thee.’ This is possible. But it seems to extort a little too much from the verb: and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_1054"> is also recommended by the contrast with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_γὅπλ_1054"> in 1056. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Τεῦκρος_1057">: <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.313"> <title>Il.</title> 13. 313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τεῦκρός θ’, ὅς ἄριστος Ἀχαιῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοξοσύνῃ, ἀγαθὸς δὲ καὶ σταδίῃ ὑσμίνῃ</quote>. The words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδἐπιστήμην_1057"> express that skill with the bow was not a regular attribute of the Homeric warrior—whose ordinary weapon was the spear—but the special accomplishment of a few, such as Teucer, Meriones, Philoctetes. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1120"> <title>Ai.</title> 1120</ref>, where Menelaus tauntingly calls Teucer <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ τοξότης.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ἡμῖν_1057">. The addition of <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote>, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστι_1057">, is unusual: but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 243e"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 243 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖτος παρά σοι μάλα πλησίον ἀεὶ πάρεστιν</quote> </cit>: where Thompson rightly rejects Cobet's proposal (<title>Var. Lect.</title> p. 119) to delete <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστιν</quote> and write <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρα σοι</quote>. It should be noticed that, both there and here, a slightly different shade of meaning is given by the presence of the prep.: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι Τεῦκρος ἡμῖν</quote>=simply, ‘Teucer is with us’: but <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι Τεῦκρος παρ’ ἡμῖν</quote>=‘Teucer is available, being with us,’—‘Teucer is at hand to serve us.’—So in Plato <emph>l.c.</emph>, ‘he is at your command,—quite near you.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖναι παρά τινι</quote>, though rare, is parallel with <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δίκη συνεῖναι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 610"> <title>El.</title> 610</ref> f.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνεῖναι ἔν τινι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 115"> <title>O. C.</title> 115</ref> f.), and similar to <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρος τινὸς προτίθεσθαι</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 418). </gloss>

data.quote: οὖτος παρά σοι μάλα πλησίον ἀεὶ πάρεστιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγώ_θ_1058">. After <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι μὲν Τεῦκρος</quote>, the regular constr. would have been <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρειμι δὲ ἐγώ</quote>. But, having omitted to repeat the verb, the poet has written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ θ’</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’</quote> would now have been awkward. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1162"> <title>Ant.</title> 1162</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώσας μὲν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβών τε</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_ἐπιθύνειν_1058">. The Ionic and Epic form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰθύνω</quote>, though unknown to Comedy or classical prose, occurs in our MSS. of Aesch. and Eur. ,—and not in lyrics only. Some edd. now always give <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐθύνω</quote> in Trag.; unnecessarily, I think.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_1058">, when </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_1058"> precedes the other. Two answers are possible. I place first that which seems to me right. (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_1058_2"> belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάκιον_1058"> only, and not to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατύνειν_1058">. Thus there is no incongruity between <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote>, since only <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote> belongs to an inf. This may be seen by supposing an equivalent phrase substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ κάκιον οὐδέν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι ὅμοια σοὶ τούτων ἂν κρατύνειν, μηδὲ χεῖρον ἐπιθύνειν</quote>. Schneidewin cp. <ref n="Plat. Prot. 319B">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 319B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθεν δὲ αὐτὸ ἡγοῦμαι οὐ διδακτὸν εἶναι, μηδ’ ὑπ’ ἀνθρώπων παρασκευαστὸν ἀνθρώποις, δίκαιός εἰμι εἰπεῖν</quote>: where, if <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> belonged to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>, the immediately following <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote> would be extremely harsh; while there is no such harshness if <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> belongs to <quote xml:lang="grc">διδακτὸν</quote> only, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ‐διδακτὸν</quote> being equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδύνατον διδάσκεσθαι</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_1058_3"> belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατύνειν_1058_2">, and, in using </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_1058_2"> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’</quote> before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιθύνειν_1058">, the writer has merely used the other alternative which </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶμαι_1058"> left to him. Now, idiom is partly governed by association, and can even be influenced by false analogy. The sequence of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_1058"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέ_1058"> was most familiar to the Attic ear in a constr. which opposed their clauses to each other (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θᾶσσον οἴσεις μηδ’ ἀπιστήσεις ἐμοί</quote>;). It seems unlikely, then, that an Attic writer would wantonly have used </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μηδέ_1058"> instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_οὐδέ_1058"> in a short sentence where the two negatives were simply coordinate.— <ref n="Eur. Andr. 586">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 586</ref>(quoted by Schneidewin) is not apposite: <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν εὖ, κακῶς δ’ οὔ, μηδ’ ἀποκτείνειν βίᾳ</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι</quote> is understood with <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν</quote>, and again with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ</quote>: ‘they are thine to benefit, (but <emph> not</emph> to injure,)—and not to slay’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote> contrasts <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀποκτ</quote>. with <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν εὖ</quote>, and the words <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς δ’ οὔ</quote> form a parenthesis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠδ_ἐπευθύνειν_1058">, is specious, but not necessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_Αῆμνον_1060">: the art. here is like our possessive pron. used with a scornful tone: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 381">cp. 381</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 324"> <title>Ant.</title> 324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόμψευέ νυν τὴν δόξαν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέρας_1061">, the bow, which can be fitly so called because Ph. received it as a reward for good service (670).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_χρῆν_1061">. It is possible to write </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_ἐχρῆν_1061">, as though <quote xml:lang="grc"> σὲ</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>) were elided: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 339">cp. 339</ref>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν σὲ χρῆν</quote> is here much better, and is favoured by the fact that Soph. has <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆν</quote> in nine other places (430, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1363">Soph. 1363</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1184"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1184</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1185">Soph. O. T. 1185</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 529">Soph. <title>El.</title> 529</ref> , <ref n="Soph. El. 579">Soph. El. 579</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1505">Soph. El. 1505</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1133">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 1133</ref>: fr. 104. 5), but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχρῆν</quote> only once, viz. in fr. 104. 6, where metre prompted it. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχρῆν</quote>, though a product of false analogy (since <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆν</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ ἦν</quote>), was, of course, equally correct in Attic: it is attested by metre in <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 11">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 11</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 135">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 135</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 364">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 364</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1177">Ar. Av. 1177</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1201">Ar. Av. 1201</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 152">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 152</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 935">Ar. Ran. 935</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 598">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 598</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 19">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 19</ref>: fr. 110 and 304.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1061"> must be considered as belonging to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀργείοις_1061">, this v. has no caesura either in the 3rd or in the 4th foot: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101">cp. 101</ref>, 1369. It may seem strange that the poet did not write <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλοις ἐν Ἀργείοισι κοσμηθεὶς φανεῖ</quote>, as Mekler proposes. But the halting rhythm of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπλοισι_κοσμηθεὶς_1061">, etc., seems to express the anguish with which Ph. dwells on this bitter thought,—that his bow is to win glory for his enemy. A similar effect of rhythm occurs in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 44</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γὰρ νοεῖς θάπτειν σφ’, ἀπόρρητον πόλει</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἀντιφώνει_1065">: the acc., as with <quote xml:lang="grc">προσφωνῶ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείβομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 991"> <title>O. C.</title> 991</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἓν γάρ μ’ ἄμειψαι μοῦνον</quote>): so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 764"> <title>Ai.</title> 764</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ἐννέπει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_1065">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 939"> <title>Ant.</title> 939</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγομαι δὴ κοὐκέτι μέλλω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφθεγκτός_1067">: see n. on 867 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπιστον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_1067">, without more ado: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 315"> <title>Ant.</title> 315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπεῖν τι δώσεις, ἢ στραφεὶς οὕτως ἴω</quote>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενναῖός_ὤν_1068">, noble, generous, though thou art,—and therefore naturally disposed to pity him. (Not, ‘loyal to thy duty,’—and so capable of pitying him without yielding to him.)— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_διαφθερεῖς_1068">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, spoil the good fortune which has enabled us to secure the bow. He fears that N. may give the bow back.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_μὴ_1068"> with fut. ind., as an object clause, would be regular if a verb of ‘taking care’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλάσσομαι</quote>) had preceded. But here a final clause with the subjunct. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μὴ διαφθείρῃς</quote>) would be usual. Cp. Andoc. or. 1 § 43 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη χρῆναι λύειν...τὸ ψήφισμα..., ὅπως μὴ πρότερον νὺξ ἔσται πρὶν πυθέσθαι</quote>. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 2. 1. 21">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2. 1. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ δἰ ἓν ἄλλο τρέφονται ἢ ὅπως μαχοῦνται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναυκράτωρ_1072">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ναύαρχος</quote>: elsewhere=<quote xml:lang="grc">ναυσὶ κρατῶν</quote>, ‘having naval superiority’ ( Her. and Thuc. , always in plur.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούσομαι_1074">, have it said of me: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 378">cp. 378</ref>, 382. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ὅσον_1076">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 83">cp. 83</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_στείλωσι_1076">, make ready the things in the ship,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, set the tackle, etc., in order. The only difference between <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐκ νεώς</quote> here and <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐν νηί</quote> is that the former suggests the notion of the quarter —at some distance from the speaker— where the preparations are to be made. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Lach. 184A">Plat. <title>Lach.</title> 184A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν δὲ γέλως καὶ κρότος ὑπὸ τῶν ἐκ τῆς ὁλκάδος</quote>:—‘the people off there in the merchant-ship.’ <ref n="Thuc. 6. 32">Thuc. 6. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συνεπηύχοντο δὲ καὶ ὁ ἄλλος ὅμιλος ὁ ἐκ τῆς γῆς</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> carries the mental eye from the scene on board the ships to the scene ashore). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείλωσι_1076">, as <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.287">Hom. <title> Od.</title> 2. 287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῆα θοὴν στελέω</quote> (fit out).—On reaching Lemnos, the sailors—if they followed Homeric practice—would have unshipped the mast (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστός</quote>), and laid it down so that its top should rest on the mast-holder (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστοδόκη</quote>) at the stern. Cp. <title>Hom. hym.</title> 2. 278 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐπὶ γαῖαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβῆτ’, οὐδὲ καθ’ ὅπλα μελαίνης νηὸς ἔθεσθε</quote>; They have now to raise the mast,—make it fast by the fore-stays (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρότονοι</quote>),—and hoist the sails. (Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.416"> <title>Od.</title> 2. 416</ref> ff.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖς_εὐξώμεθα_1076">. When all was ready for sailing, a prayer was recited, and libations poured. Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 32">Thuc. 6. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχὰς δὲ τὰς νομιζομένας πρὸ τῆς ἀναγωγῆς</quote>... <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐποιοῦντο</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.430">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 2. 430</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δησάμενοι δ’ ἄρα ὅπλα θοὴν ἀνὰ νῆα μέλαιναν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στήσαντο κρητῆρας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμώμεθον_1079">: pres.subjunct. Only two other instances of a 1st pers. dual occur in texts of the classical period: (1) <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.485"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 485</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τρίποδος περιδώμεθον ἠὲ λέβητος</quote>. Here, while the greater MS. authority supports the dual, one MS. gives <quote xml:lang="grc">περιδώμεθα</quote>: and the hiatus can be defended by the ‘bucolic diaeresis,’ just as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.484"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 484</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷόν κ’ ἠὲ φέροιεν Ἀχαιοὶ ἤ κεν ἄγοιεν</quote>. (2) <ref n="Soph. El. 950"> <title>El.</title> 950</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λελείμμεθον</quote>: where again one of the minor MSS. has <quote xml:lang="grc">λελείμμεθα</quote>. Elmsley denied the existence of such a 1st pers. dual, because it is so rare, and is nowhere required by metre. Bieler (<title>De duali numero</title>, p. 18) pushes this unsafe argument further by pointing out how often Homer and the dramatists abstained from this form where they might have used it. Leaf (on <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.485"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 485</ref>) thinks that it can be explained only as due to the analogy of the 2nd dual (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">‐μεθον</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">‐μεθα</quote> :: <quote xml:lang="grc">‐σθον</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">‐σθε</quote>). But even so, analogy might have produced this form before the time of the dramatists: we cannot assume that it was merely a figment of later grammarians. I should therefore keep <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμώμεθον</quote> here and <quote xml:lang="grc">λελείμμεθον</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 950"> <title>El.</title> 950</ref>; though in <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.485"> <title>Il.</title> 23. 485</ref>, considering all the facts, I should prefer <quote xml:lang="grc">περιδώμεθα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμᾶσθαι_1080">, infin. for imperat. (57): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταχεῖς_1080"> with adverbial force (526). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γύαλον_1081">, ‘hollow’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1491"> <title>O. C.</title> 1491</ref> ff., n.), is here properly the chamber itself, while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίλας_πέτρας_1081"> (possessive gen.) is the cavernous rock which contains it. Cp. <title> Helen.</title> 189 <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρινα μύχατα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γύαλα</quote>, ‘inmost recesses of the rocks.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμὸν_παγετῶδες_1081">. Contrast this with the description by Odysseus, 17 ff. Cp. <ref n="Hes. WD 640">Hes. <title> Op.</title> 640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄσκρη, χεῖμα κακῇ, θέρει ἀργαλέῃ, οὐδέ ποτ’ ἐσθλῇ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνῄσκοντι_συνείσει_1085">, thou wilt be conscious of my death,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, wilt be the only witness of it. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 92"> <title>El.</title> 92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δὲ παννυχίδων ἤδη στυγεραὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνίσασ’ εὐναὶ μογερῶν οἴκων</quote>: and so oft. The MSS. have </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνοίσει_1085">. This has been rendered: (1) ‘thou wilt be <emph>a fit place for me</emph>’ to die in,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, good enough. Now, the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφέρομαι</quote> does, indeed, mean ‘to agree with’ one,—in opinions, or tastes: <ref n="Soph. OC 641"> <title>O. C.</title> 641</ref> n.: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 114">Her. 4. 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ὦν δυναίμεθα ἐκείνῃσι συμφέρεσθαι</quote> (‘live in harmony with them’). But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνοίσει_1085_2"> here could not mean simply, <emph>conveniet mihi morienti.</emph> (2) ‘Thou wilt be profitable to me,’—by giving me a grave. So the first schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολλυμένῳ μοι σύμφορον ἔσει καὶ ὠφέλιμον, καὶ δέξει με ἀποθανόντα</quote>. This version confounds </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνοίσει_1085_3"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνοίσεις_1085">. (3) ‘Thou wilt be with me,’ —simply. This last is impossible. <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφέρομαι</quote> never means, or could mean, merely <quote xml:lang="grc">σύνειμι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">συνδιάγω</quote>. Dindorf, who quotes a schol. for this, has not perceived that this schol.,—the second, prefaced by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ οὕτω</quote>,—is explaining, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνοίσει_1085_4">, but, manifestly, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνείσει_1085">:—<quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν ἐμοὶ ἔσει καὶ ὄψει με ἀποθανόντα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔλιον_1087">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 19">cp. 19</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπας_ἐμοῦ_1087">. Ph. addresses the cave as if it were a living companion, long condemned to endure his presence. (With </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πληρέστατον_1087"> cp. what he says of Neopt. in v. 876, <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῆς τε καὶ δυσοσμίας γέμων</quote>.) Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπας <emph>τᾶς ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</emph> </quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς ἐμᾶς</quote>) is fitting,—‘the anguish on my part,’—so painful for thee to witness. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 292">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 292</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθυμήματα...τἀπὸ σοῦ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίπτ_αὖ_1089"> (= 1105 <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνδρῶν</quote>), Bothe's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτ’ αὖ</quote>, has been generally received. As Dind. remarks, Aesch. has twice used this epic <quote xml:lang="grc">τίπτε</quote> in lyrics ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 975">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 975</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 554">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 554</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἆμαρ_1089">, daily provision. Cp. <ref n="Isoc. 11.39">Isocr. or. 11 § 39</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλῆται καὶ τῶν καθ’ ἡμέραν ἐνδεεῖς</quote>. Eur. uses this phrase as an adv. (‘every day,’ <ref n="Eur. Ion 123">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 123</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 182">Soph. <title>El.</title> 182</ref>), like <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καθ’ ἡμέραν</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1126"> Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 1126</ref> etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σιτονόμου_ἐλπίδος_1091">. As <quote xml:lang="grc">σιτονόμος</quote> (found only here)=<quote xml:lang="grc">σῖτον νέμων</quote>, affording food, <quote xml:lang="grc">σιτονόμος ἐλπίς</quote>=‘a hope concerning the provision of food.’ Hence the phrase is not really parallel with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστυνόμοι ὀργαί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 355"> <title>Ant.</title> 355</ref>), ‘dispositions which regulate cities.’ It is more like <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ τρυσάνωρ</quote> in 208 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_πόθεν_1091">: for the double question, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 243">cp. 243</ref>, and n. on 220.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλειαι_ἄνω_1091"> is my emendation of the corrupt <quote xml:lang="grc">† <emph>εἴθ αἰθέρος</emph> † ἄνω</quote>. The word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴθ_1091"> would be possible only if, in 1094, we read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_ἕλοιεν_1091"> for the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕλωσί_μ_1091">. But the general sense of the passage forbids this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλῶσιν_1091"> (conjectured by Erfurdt and others, and found (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλῶσί μ’</quote>) in one MS.) is a certain correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕλωσί_μ_1091_2">: as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσχω_1091"> (Heath) is of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσχύω_1091">. He is not here praying to be caught up by winds, or slain by birds, but saying—in continuation of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ποτε τεύξομαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σιτονόμου πόθεν ἐλπίδος</quote>—that now the birds will fly unharmed over his head. That </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰθέρος_1091">, no less than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴθ_1091_2">, is spurious, is made almost certain by two distinct considerations. (1) The antistrophic v., 1113, <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^<01>δοι— μα—ν δε^</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νι—ν</quote>, is a dochmiac. <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἰθέρος</quote> resolves the second long syll. of the bacchius (= the final syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδοίμαν</quote>); not an unexampled licence, but still a most rare one. (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτωκάδες_1091"> is sound, but could not be used, without art. or subst., to denote ‘timid <emph>birds.</emph>’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰθίρος_1091"> has probably supplanted that subst.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴθ_αἰθέρος_1091"> was an attempt to supply, from the context, words which had been wholly or partly lost. Now suppose that the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΕΑΕΙΑΙ Δ ΑΝΩ</quote> had been partly obliterated, so as to leave only <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΙΑΙ ΑΝΩ</quote>. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυτόνου πνεύματος</quote> would readily suggest that AI was a vestige of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθέρος</quote>. And the very fact that the schol. accepts <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθε ἕλωσί με</quote> as possible shows how, in post-classical times, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλωσι</quote> might have elicited <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθ’</quote> from the letters EI. The birds which will now fly harmless over his head are such as those which his bow used to slay,—<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ὑποπτέρους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλλον πελείας</quote> (288).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυτόνου_πνεύματος_1091">, shrill-sounding breeze: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.17"> <title>Il.</title> 14. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιγέων ἀνέμων αἰψηρὰ κέλευθα</quote>. The epithet is perh. intended to suggest also the <quote xml:lang="grc">πτερῶν ῥοῖβδος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1004"> <title>Ant.</title> 1004</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκέτ_ἴσχω_1091">, I do not restrain them, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, do not arrest their career (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλῶσιν</quote>) by my arrows. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1153">Cp. 1153</ref> ff. For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσχω</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 242"> <title>El.</title> 242</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτίμους ἴσχουσα πτέρυγας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυτόνων γόων</quote>,—where L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσχύουσα</quote>, by the same error as here. The MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἰσχύω_1091"> raises the question whether we should read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλῶς_γὰρἴσχω_1091">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ οὐ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1217">cp. 1217</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 161"> <title>Tr.</title> 161</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔτ’ οὐκ ὤν</quote>. But the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλωσί μ’</quote> would have arisen from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλῶσιν</quote> more easily than from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλῶσ’ ἔτ’</quote>. It is more probable that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1091"> was an interpolation here, as it is in L's text of <ref n="Soph. OC 1766"> <title>O. C.</title> 1766</ref> and <ref n="Soph. Aj. 706."> <title>Ai.</title> 706.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύ_μείζονος_1095">. In this passage I adhere to the MS. text, merely writing, with Wecklein, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_1095"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_1095">. The words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοθεν_τᾷδ_1095"> cannot be metrically reconciled with the corresponding words in the antistrophe (1118 f.), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχ_ἐμᾶς_1095">. Dindorf assumes that the latter words are sound, and that the fault is in the strophe. He therefore writes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοθεν <emph>ἁ τύχα ἅδ’</emph> ἀπὸ μείζονος</quote>, and thus obtains a dactylic tetrameter, answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχ’ ὑπὸ χειρὸς ἐμᾶς· στυγερὰν ἔχε</quote>. This alteration is, however, extremely bold, since it eliminates </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_1095"> without attempting to account for it. On Dindorf's view, I should prefer to conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔ σ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἄλλου</emph> ἔχει τύχα ἅδ’ ἀπὸ μείζονος</quote>. The traditional <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχᾳ τᾷδ’</quote> would thus be explained; it would have arisen from the ambiguous <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔχει</quote>, after <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔ σ’</quote> had become <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐκ</quote>. But, on the whole, it appears safer to suppose that the fault is in the antistrophe. A very slight change will bring the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχ’ ὑπὸ χειρὸς ἐμᾶς</quote> into agreement with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοθεν ἔχει τύχᾳ τᾷδ’</quote>. We have only to write, with Bergk, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχεν ὑπὸ χειρὸς ἀμ</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾶς</quote>. (Wecklein obtains the same metrical result by conjecturing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχε παλάμαις ἐμαῖ</quote> |<quote xml:lang="grc">σιν</quote>.)—See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηξίωσας_1095">, hast thought it right (to have it so). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 944"> <title>O. T.</title> 944</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιῶ θανεῖν</quote> : <ref n="Plat. Rep. 337D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 337D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ἀξιοῖς παθεῖν;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_τᾷδ_1095">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 272"> <title>Ai.</title> 272</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷσιν εἴχετ’ ἐν κακοῖς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_μείζονος_1095">, explaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοθεν_1095">: for this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1533"> <title>O. C.</title> 1533</ref> ff. n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὸν_1099">: cp. fr. 323 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν παρὸν θέσθαι καλῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός τις αὑτῷ τὴν βλάβην προσθῇ φέρων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονῆσαι_1099">, to come to a sound mind (ingressive aor.). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1259">Cp. 1259</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσωφρόνησας</quote>. So the aor. partic. in <ref n="Soph. OT 649"> <title>O. T.</title> 649</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ θελήσας φρονήσας τ’</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δαίμονος_1099"> depends not on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵλον_1099"> alone (as if it were <quote xml:lang="grc">προέκρινας</quote>), but on the idea of comparison suggested by the whole phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵλου_κάκιοναἰνεῖν_1099">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1357"> <title>Ai.</title> 1357</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικᾷ γὰρ ἁρετἡ με τῆς ἔχθρας πολύ</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ νικᾷ με</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ κρείσσων παρ’ ἐμοί ἐστι</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰνεῖν_1099"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">στέργειν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 2">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θῆσσαν τράπεζαν αἰνέσαι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δαίμονος_1099_2">, the MS. reading, is metrically impossible. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ λῴονος</quote> must represent <quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘˘¯</quote> (= 1121 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ ἐμοὶ</quote>). But the first syllable of <quote xml:lang="grc">λῴονος</quote> is necessarily long. A shortening of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ωι_1099"> before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ο_1099"> cannot be justified by the similar shortening of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1099"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οι_1099">, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">δείλαιος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310"> <title>Ant.</title> 1310</ref> n.) or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰωνούς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1058"> <title>El.</title> 1058</ref>). Musgrave compares <quote xml:lang="grc">ζωῆς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δῃώσας</quote> from Eur. ; but in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1108">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1108</ref> we must read <quote xml:lang="grc">ζόης</quote>, and in <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 995">Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 995</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διώσας</quote>. In the few places where <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote> appears to have the 2nd syll. short, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτριος</quote> is a certain correction (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 724">cp. 724</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦπλέονος_1099">? It occurs in the first schol. on this v.:—<quote xml:lang="grc">πλείονος δὲ δαίμονος λέγει τοῦ λυσιτελεστέρου καὶ συμφόρου</quote>. Hermann, Dindorf and Wecklein are among those who accept it. In its favour two points may be noticed. (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</quote>, when it means <quote xml:lang="grc"> μοῖρα</quote>, is sometimes quite impersonal; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> e.g.</foreign>, fr. 587 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σπεῖρε πολλοῖς τὸν παρόντα δαίμονα</quote> (‘spread not thy present trouble abroad’—by speech). (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ <emph>πλέονος</emph> δαίμονος</quote> would be suggested by such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον ἔχειν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it might be possible to say <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πλείω δαίμον’ ἔχω</quote>, or the like, though not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πλείων δαίμων με σῴζει</quote>. And so the bold phrase seems just conceivable here, where the idea is, ‘Instead of the better portion, thou hast chosen the worse.’ Omitting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_1099">, Bothe would read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωΐονος_1099"> (cp. Simonides Amorg. 7. 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">λωΐων γυνή</quote>), and Wunder </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωϊτέρου_1099">. But, for Soph. , neither seems probable.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤδη_1103">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕστερον_εἰσοπίσω_1103">: the redundant diction marks strong feeling, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 858"> <title>Ai.</title> 858</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανύστατον δὴ κοὔποτ’ α<*>θις ὕστερον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕστερον_1103">: for the short syll. at the end of the verse, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 184">cp. 184</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφέρων_1108">, bringing home. The act. denotes the simple act of ‘carrying towards’ the cave; the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">προσφερόμενος</quote> would have further expressed that the food was for his own use. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 708">Cp. 708</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 6"> <title>O. C.</title> 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέροντα</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φερόμενον</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἴσχων_1109">. The <emph> <emph>only</emph> </emph> food which Ph. could obtain was that which his bow procured (287). And here the loss of the bow is uppermost in his thoughts. Hence the emphatic repetition :—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ φορβὰν ἔτι προσφέρων, <emph>οὐ</emph> </quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">προσφέρων</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτανῶνἀπ_ὅπλων_1109">. The general word, <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσφέρων</quote>, is understood again with the adverbial phrase which specialises it. Thus the rhetorical effect is much as if he had said, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀγρεύων ὄρνιθας, οὐ τοξεύων</quote>. The object to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσχων_1109"> is <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὅπλα</quote>) understood. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1058">Cp. 1058</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιον οὐδὲν ἂν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων κρατύνειν μηδ’ ἐπιθύνειν χερί</quote>. Hartung objects that it is the <emph> bow</emph> which is held, whereas </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτανῶν_1109"> suits only the <emph>arrows</emph>: hence he writes, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πτανῶν <emph>ὅπλ’</emph> ἐμῶν <emph>τόξων</emph> </quote> (for the final spondee <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1151">cp. 1151</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκάν</quote>). The simple answer is that, at the moment of shooting, the archer holds both bow and arrow: and the epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κραταιαῖς_1109"> suits precisely that moment, since it suggests the effort of drawing the bow. Brunck was clearly wrong in supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβάν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσχων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσκοπα_1111">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπροσδόκητα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1315"> <title>El.</title> 1315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργασαι δέ μ’ ἄσκοπα</quote>.—The dative with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέδυ_1111"> would not be unusual if the sense were, ‘came into my thoughts’: <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.398"> <title>Od.</title> 10. 398</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσιν δ’ ἱμερόεις ὑπέδυ γόος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 298"> <title>Tr.</title> 298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ γὰρ οἶκτος εἰσέβη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 372"> <title>O. C.</title> 372</ref> n. But here the sense is, ‘beguiled,’ for which we should have expected the acc., as after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρχομαι, ὑποπίπτω</quote>. The explanation may be that the sense, ‘beguiled,’ is here derived from the sense, ‘insinuated themselves into my mind.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδοίμαν_1113">: for the midd., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 351">cp. 351</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότμος_δαιμόνων_1116">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 157"> <title>Ant.</title> 157</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> θεῶν...συντυχίαις</quote>, n. Two constructions are possible: I prefer the first. (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_1116"> (nom.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_ἔσχεν_1116">, ‘these things have come upon thee as a doom from heaven.’ (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότμος_σετάδε_1116"> (cogn. acc.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχεν_1116">, ‘fate hath put this constraint on thee.’ We can say <quote xml:lang="grc"> βιάζομαι</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναγκάζω</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τινά τι</quote>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω τινά τι</quote> would be harsher. There is a like ambiguity in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 750">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 750</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς τάδ’ οὐ νόσος φρενῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχε παῖδ’ ἐμόν</quote>; and there, too, <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’</quote> seems best taken as nom. For the sense of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχεν_1116_2">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 331">cp. 331</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_γε_1116">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> with the repeated <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 790."> <title>Ant.</title> 790.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμᾶς_1116">, Bergk's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμᾶς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1095">cp. 1095</ref> ff., n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγερὰν_1119">, pass., abhorred, dreadful: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1214"> <title>Ai.</title> 1214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγερῷ δαίμονι. —ἔχε</quote>, ‘direct,’ like a missile: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.263"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεδίονδ’ ἔχον ὠκέας ἵππους.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἄλλοις_1119">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 468"> <title>Tr.</title> 468</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δ’ ἐγὼ φράζω κακὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄλλον εἶναι, πρὸς δ’ ἔμ’ ἀψευδεῖν ἀεί</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.295"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 295</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοισιν δὴ ταῦτ’ ἐπιτέλλεο, μὴ γὰρ ἐμοί γε</quote>. There is no reference to Odysseus, whom they presently defend (1143), or to any definite person. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἐμοὶ_1121"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The sense is:—‘Do not blame <emph>us</emph>: so far from being thy foes, we are sincerely anxious to win thy friendship.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_1121">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ φιλότητ’ ἀπώσῃ</quote>. The constr. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλει_1121"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀπώσῃ</quote> is like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">σκοπῶ</quote> (meaning, ‘to take care’) with <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μή</quote>. Others make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἀπώσῃ_1121"> imperative: ‘<emph>do not</emph> reject,’ etc. Then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_1121_2"> becomes awkward, since it can hardly refer to the coming deprecation, nor can it well mean ‘thy welfare.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολιᾶς_1123">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">πολιοῦ</quote>, since the words <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντου θινὸς</quote> form a single notion: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> n. The gen. goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφημένος_1123">: cp. <ref n="Pind. N. 4">Pind. <title> N.</title> 4. 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς...ἐφεζόμενοι</quote>: <ref>Ap. Rhod. 3. 1000</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">νηὸς...ἐφεζομένη</quote>. Some take the gen. as partitive, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_1123">: but the latter clearly means here, ‘I ween’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 382"> <title>Ai.</title> 382</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ που πολὺν γέλωθ’ ὑφ’ ἡδονῆς ἄγεις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γελᾷ_μου_1123">. The antistrophic verse (1148 <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρος οὐρεσιβώτας</quote>) shows the true metre; and a substitution of ˘¯¯ for ¯˘¯ is impossible here. If, on the other hand, the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγ_1123"> of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγγελᾷ_1123"> had been accidentally lost, the insertion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μου_1123"> is just such an expedient as might have occurred to a post-classical corrector. There is no classical example of a gen. after the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">γελᾶν</quote>, though Lucian has that construction ( <ref n="Lucian Dem.Enc. 16"> <title>Dem. Enc.</title> 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γελᾶν ἔπεισί μοι τοῦ τὰς ὀφρῦς συνάγοντος</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_μελέου_1126">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 344"> <title>O. C.</title> 344</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ δυστήνου κακά.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφάν_1126">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 931">cp. 931</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_που_1130">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1123">cp. 1123</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί που</quote> n.— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεινὸν ὁρᾷς</quote>, lookest piteously, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with a look expressing sorrow for thyself, and pity for him. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 527"> <title>Tr.</title> 527</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεινὸν</quote>: <title>Scut.</title> 426 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινὸν ὁρῶν ὄσσοισι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἄρθμιον_1130">, the ally, friend, of Heracles. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρθμιον_1130"> seems a certain correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλιον_1130">. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρθμιος</quote> (expressing the bond of alliance or friendship) was a poet. synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.427"> <title>Od.</title> 16. 427</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἡμῖν ἄρθμιοι ἦσαν</quote>: Theognis 1312 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷσπερ νῦν ἄρθμιος ἠδὲ φίλος</quote>. Cp. <title>Hom. hym.</title> 3. 524 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀρθμῷ καὶ φιλότητι</quote>: and the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρίηρες ἑταῖροι</quote>. The adj. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἡράκλειον_1130"> represents the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέους</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄρθμιος</quote> with the art. can be treated as a subst. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεῖος, ἐπιτήδειος</quote>, etc.): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 267"> <title>O. T.</title> 267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ ααβδακείῳ παιδί</quote> (n.). Prof. Campbell reads <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλον <ἔμ)> ὧδέ σοι</quote>, adopting <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλον</quote> from the margin of L, and conjecturally adding <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμ’</quote>. He renders: ‘me thus destined no more to use thee in the Heraclean exercise,’—taking the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡράκλειος ἆθλος</quote> to be archery. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλου_μεταλλαγᾷ_1134"> is Hermann's emendation of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μεταλλαγᾷ_1134">, which is shorter by a syllable than the antistrophic v., 1157 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμᾶς σαρκὸς αἰόλας</quote>. It is the simplest and most probable correction. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1134"> here denotes an attendant circumstance (cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 931">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 931</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ οὐκέθ’ αὑτὸς ἦν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν στροφαῖσιν ὀμμάτων ἐφθαρμένος</quote>): and the gen. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεταλλαγᾷ_1134"> denotes the ownership <emph>to</emph> which the change is made: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 18">Thuc. 6. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπραγμοσύνης μεταβολῇ</quote>, a change <emph>to</emph> inactivity. Thus the phrase is equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταλλάξαν ἄλλον πολυμήχανον ἄνδρα, ἐρέσσει</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ αὐ<*>ῦ</quote>): ‘having got a new master—a man of many wiles—thou art wielded (by him).’ For the idiomatic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλου_1134"> cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 424">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγας ὅδ’ ἄλλος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρέσσει_1134"> means that the new owner's hands can deal with the bow as they will. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρέσσω</quote> (‘row,’ then fig., ‘ply’), cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 158"> <title>Ant.</title> 158</ref> n. The word is here a poet. synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">νωμάω</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.594"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 594</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγχος ἐνώμα</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 512"> <title>Tr.</title> 512</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξα καὶ λόγχας ῥόπαλόν τε τινάσσων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἀγκάλαις_1134"> (which others have modified, see cr. n.), is liable to this primary objection, that <quote xml:lang="grc">μετ’ ἀγκάλαις</quote> could not here stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ χερσίν</quote>. Such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀγκάλαις ἔχειν</quote> are used only of what is carried ‘in the arms.’ Odysseus does not hug the bow. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶν_μὲν_1136">: for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 279">cp. 279</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγνόν_τε_1136">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1058">cp. 1058</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῶτ_ἐχθοδοπόν_1136"> is a periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρόν</quote> (subst.), hence <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐχθοδοπόν</quote> can follow <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνόν</quote> without seeming weak.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_1136"> is Dindorf's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀδυσσεύς_1136">, instead of which we require a spondee or trochee (=<quote xml:lang="grc">αἶα</quote> 1162). Cp. the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1198"> <title>O. T.</title> 1198</ref> and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 995"> <title>Tr.</title> 995</ref>: and <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 225">Ar. <title> Ach.</title> 225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις, ὦ Ζεῦ πάτερ καὶ θεοί, τοῖσιν ἐχθροῖσιν ἐσπείσατο</quote>. But he might still more fittingly have quoted <ref n="Dem. 19.113">Dem. or. 19 § 113</ref>, where, as here, the indignant invocation closes the sentence: —<quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὺς ἔφη τοὺς θορυβοῦντας εἶναι, ὀλίγους δὲ τοὺς στρατευομένους, ὅταν δέῃ</quote>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">μέμνησθε δήπου</quote> ,) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός, οἶμαι, θαυμάσιος στρατιώτης, ὦ Ζεῦ</quote>. At v. 1181 Ph. appeals to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀραῖος Ζεύς</quote>. Reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_1136_2">, it is best to adopt Bothe's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_1136"> for the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_1136">, and to make </gloss>
