<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνατέλλονθ_1136"> intrans.: ‘countless ills, arising from (effected by) shameful arts.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_1136_3">, the most attractive correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀδυσσεύς</quote> is Arndt's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδείς_1136">, which would require us to take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνατέλλονθ_1136_2"> as acc. sing. masc., with transitive sense, and to keep </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_1136_2">: ‘causing countless ills to spring up..., more than any other man ever contrived against me.’ Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.380"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 380</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς κακὰ πόλλ’ ἔρρεξεν, ὅσ’ οὐ σύμπαντες οἱ ἄλλοι</quote>. But this is far less forcible. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρός_εἰπεῖν_1140">. Arndt thus amends the MS. </gloss>
lemma: ἀνδρός τοι τὰ μὲν ἔνδικ’ αἰὲν εἰπεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρός_εἰπεῖν_1140_2">. The change involved is very slight,</gloss>
lemma:
ἀνδρός τοι τὸ μὲν εὖ
							δίκαιον εἰπεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_1140"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ν_1140"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υ_1140">, and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ε_1140"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ο_1140">. The sense is:— ‘The part of a (true) man is ever to assert what is right, but to do so without adding invectives.’ That is, Philoctetes is justified in expressing his sense of the wrong done to him; but not in reviling Odysseus. Odysseus was merely the agent of the Greek army, and acted for the public good. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1158"> <title>O. T.</title> 1158</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ λέγων γε τοὔνδικον</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Tro. 970">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 970</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τήνδε δείξω μὴ λέγουσαν ἔνδικα</quote>. Nauck objects that with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὲν_1140"> we ought to have the pres. inf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_1140">. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὲν_εἰπεῖν_1140">=‘to assert <emph>on each occasion</emph>,’—the aor. inf. marking the moment of the assertion. The combination of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰέν</quote> with the aor. is therefore no less correct than (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) in <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.263"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς αἰεὶ Ἀχιλῆα κιχήσατο κῦμα ῥόοιο</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρός_εἰπεῖν_1140_3">, is Hermann's:—‘It is the part of a man to say that what is expedient (<emph>quod utile est</emph>) is just’:—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, Philoctetes, if he is a true man, ought to remember that the act of taking him to Troy is for the public good (</gloss>
lemma:
ἀνδρός τοι
							τὸ μὲν εὖ δίκαιον εἰπεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_εὖ_1140">); and ought therefore to admit that it is just. But we may object:—(1) This sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_εὖ_1140_2"> is too obscure. (2) The Chorus may properly remonstrate with Philoctetes on his invectives against Odysseus; but they could scarcely require him to allow that his treatment had been <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαιον</quote>. (3) The antithesis between the first clause and the second (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰπόντος_δὲ_1140"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>) thus loses its force; for a man who conceded the justice of the act would not revile the agent.—Other versions of the vulgate, and other emendations, will be found in the Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰπόντος_ὀδύναν_1141">. The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπόντος</quote> depends, like <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνδρός</quote>, on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> understood. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπόντα</quote> would be equally correct, but would be subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῶσαι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 552">Cp. 552</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προστυχόντι</quote>, where similarly the acc. could stand.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξῶσαι_1141">, like a sting: cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 423 <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀξείρας τὸ κέντρον εἷτ’ ἐπ’ αὐτὸν ἵεσο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλώσσας_ὀδύναν_1141">, lit., ‘pain arising from (given by) the tongue,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, galling speech: not ‘garrulity,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">γλωσσαλγία</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνος_δ_1143">. Odysseus acted by the public command for the public good. He himself has used a similar plea (109).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷς_ταχθεὶς_1143">, appointed to the task as one <emph>out of</emph> many, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as their agent. For the prep., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 647">cp. 647</ref> n. Though v. 6 might suggest </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπὸ_1143">, change is needless.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφημοσύνᾳ_1143">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφετμῇ, ἐντολῇ</quote>: a Homeric and Pindaric word.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_1143"> is a clearly true correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδ_1143">. Blaydes, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">τάνδ’ ἐφημοσύναν</quote>, joins it with <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχθείς</quote> (‘intrusted with this commission’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_φίλους_1143">, ‘towards’ his friends,—in their interest. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς</quote> has been suspected (see cr. n.); but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς φίλους</quote> is better than <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς φίλοις</quote> here, where two aspects of the same act are contrasted. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 679"> <title>Ai.</title> 679</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τ’ ἐχθρὸς ἡμῖν ἐς τοσόνδ’ ἐχθαρτέος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς καὶ φιλήσων αὖθις· ἔς τε τὸν φίλον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσαῦθ’ ὑπουργεῖν ὠφελεῖν βουλήσομαι κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαροπῶν_1146">. The rt <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρω, χαρά, χάρις</quote>) is akin to the Sanskrit <emph>ghar</emph> (<emph>har</emph>), ‘glow,’ ‘shine’ (Curt. <title> Etym.</title> § 185). <quote xml:lang="grc">χαροπός</quote>, ‘bright-eyed,’ was used esp. to denote the fierce light in the eyes of wild animals: <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.611"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 611</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαροποί τε λέοντες</quote>. So in <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1065">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 1065</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">χαροποῖσι πιθήκοις</quote> alludes <*>o the Spartans, the adj. implies ‘truculent.’ In men, according to <title>Physiogn.</title> 3, the <quote xml:lang="grc">χαροπὸν ὄμμα</quote> is characteristic of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρεῖος</quote>, and also of the <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφυής</quote>. Though not descriptive of colour, <quote xml:lang="grc">χαροπός</quote> is sometimes associated, or even identified, with <quote xml:lang="grc"> γλαυκός</quote> (Theocr. 20. 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμματά μοι γλαυκᾶς χαροπώτερα πολλὸν Ἀθάνας</quote>): cp. <ref n="Stat. Germ. 4"> <title>Germ.</title> 4</ref> <emph>truces et caerulei oculi.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐρεσιβώτας_1148">, acc. plur., ‘finding food on the hills’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 937">cp. 937</ref>, 955: <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.299"> <title>Il.</title> 12. 299</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέων ὀρεσίτροφος</quote>: <title>Scut.</title> 407 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγὸς ὀρεσσινόμου</quote>: and so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρειλεχής, ὀρειvόμος, ὀρεσκῷος, οὐρεσίφοιτος</quote>, etc. If we took the adj. as nom. sing., with <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρος</quote>, it would mean, ‘affording pasture on the hills’: as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 614"> <title>Ai.</title> 614</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρενὸς οἰοβώτας</quote>, ‘feeding lonely thoughts.’ But the first view seems to agree better with usage: and in such a compound the ending <quote xml:lang="grc">‐βώτης</quote> could represent either <quote xml:lang="grc">βόσκων</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">βοσκόμενος</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγᾷ_αὐλίων_1148"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελᾶτ_1148">, of which the only tenable rendering is Hermann's:—‘No more, in your flight, will ye <emph>draw m<*> after you</emph> from my cave.’ On this w<*> remark:—(1) The use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελᾶτ_1148_2">, though possible, is strange. When <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζειν</quote> i<*> trans., the place <emph>to which</emph> the object i<*> brought is almost always expressed, eithe<*> by a dat., or by a prep. and case: or, i<*> not expressed, it is at least clearly im<*> plied; as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.92"> <title>Il.</title> 21. 92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ὀΐω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σὰ<*> χεῖρας φεύξεσθαι, ἐπεί γ’ ἐπέλασσέ γ<*> δαίμων</quote>: where the context implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμ<*></quote> far more clearly than <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγᾷ</quote> here implie<*> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῖν αὐτοῖς</quote>. Comparing <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.766"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 766</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ<*> μάλιστ’ εἴωθε κακῇς ὀδύνῃσι πελάζειν</quote>, and <ref n="Pind. O. 1"> Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 77</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐμὲ...κράτει...πέλασον</quote>, w<*> might surmise that, to a Greek ear, <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγ<*> μ’ οὐκέτ’ ἀπ’ αὐλίων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote> would rathe<*> suggest this sense,—‘Ye will no longe<*> force me to flight from my cave.’ (2) But apart from the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote>, there i<*> a further difficulty. Verse 1149 shoul<*> correspond with v. 1126, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἐμὰν μελέο<*> τροφάν</quote>. These are glyconic verses. A<*> iambus, <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγᾷ</quote>, could not begin such<*> verse, unless its first syll. served merel<*> as anacrusis. If we transpose <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγᾷ</quote> b<*> keep <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’ οὐκέτ’</quote>, then we have anothe<*> impossibility, viz. a sentence beginnin<*> with <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>. Other versions of the vulgate which have been proposed are examined in the Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_οὐκέτ_1148"> is corrupted from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκέτ_1148">. Auratus, keeping <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote>, understood (like Wunder), ‘No longer approach, <emph>in order to fly</emph> from my cave,’—an impossible sense for the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγᾷ</quote>: though <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote> as imperat. might be defended by the verse of an unknown poet in <ref n="Plut. De Cohib. 457d">Plut. <title> Mor.</title> 457</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">βαῖνε λὰξ ἐπὶ τραχήλου, βαῖνε καὶ πέλα χθονί</quote>. Canter read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλᾶτ_1148">, ‘no longer rush.’ For this imperat. (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάω</quote>) cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 819">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 819</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλα</quote>), and Eur. fr. 779 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλα δὲ μήτε</quote> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> But I feel certain that the true reading is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πηδᾶτ_1148">, which I proposed in the <title>Journ. of Philology</title> vol. II. p. 80 (1869). <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΕΔΑΤ’</quote> (as it would have been written by Sophocles) would most easily become <quote xml:lang="grc"> ΠΕΔΑΤ’</quote>. The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πηδᾶτ_1148_2"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελᾶτ_1148_3"> would have facilitated that of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκέτ_1148_2"> into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_οὐκέτ_1148_2">, since <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶτ’</quote> would naturally be taken as fut. indic. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζω</quote>, not as imperat. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγᾷ_1148"> last, instead of first, in the v. It is not essential to the correspondence of glyconic verses in strophe and antistrophe that the dactyl should occur in the same place: thus v. 1124 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντου θινὸς ἐφήμενος</quote> answers to 1147, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθνη θηρῶν οὓς ὅδ’ ἔχει</quote>.—See Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἀνέδην_1153"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The reading of the MSS. here (see cr. n.) presents two great difficulties. (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέδην_1153"> yields no possible sense when joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρύκεται</quote>. That adv. (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίημι</quote>, ‘to let go’) means, ‘without restraint,’ ‘with free course’ (<emph>immissis habenis</emph>), as in <title>Suppl.</title> 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγειν ἀνέδην διὰ κῦμ’ ἅλιον</quote>. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρύκεται_1153">, as the whole usage of the verb shows, must mean either ‘is detained,’ or else, ‘is warded off.’ Hence the following versions of the MS. text are impossible:—(<emph>a</emph>) ‘this place is <emph>remissly guarded</emph> ’<emph>;</emph> (<emph>b</emph>) ‘this place is <emph>held</emph> by you <emph>in freedom</emph>’ (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρύκεται· κατέχεται</quote>). Seyffert understands, ‘this place <emph>detains</emph> you with it <emph>in freedom</emph>’: but, even if we could make the verb midd., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέδην</quote> could not represent <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνετούς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνειμένους</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρύκεται_1153_2"> ought to be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρ_οὐκέτι_1153">. The error would have been an easy one if the apostrophe after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄῤ</quote> had been lost, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χῶρος_1153"> has no verb. That the initial </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_1153"> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄῤ</quote> would have been no obstacle, may be seen from the converse case in <ref n="Soph. OC 550"> <title>O. C.</title> 550</ref>, where the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεστάλη</quote>, corrupted from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ἁστάλη</quote>. Many other false readings have arisen from two words being made into one (or <emph>vice versa</emph>), often with a further corruption of the letters; as <ref n="Soph. OC 775"> <title>O. C.</title> 775</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσαύτη</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς αὕτη</quote>; <emph>ib.</emph> 1482 <quote xml:lang="grc">συντύχοιμι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ τύχοιμι</quote>. The parenthesis, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δὲ χῶρος ἄρ’ οὐκέτι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβητός, οὐκέθ’ ὑμῖν</quote>, is naturally placed, because the emphatic word of the whole sentence is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέδην_1153_2">, and the parenthesis justifies it: ‘Without restraint—and there is nothing here now, it seems, to restrain you—go on your way.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρα_1153"> expresses his new and bitter sense of helplessness. With regard to the repeated </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκέτι_1153">, it should be noted that such pathetic iteration is peculiarly frequent in this <quote xml:lang="grc">κομμός</quote>: see 1095 <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ τοι, σύ τοι</quote>: 1102 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὢ τλάμων, τλάμων ἄρ’ ἐγώ</quote>: 1128 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τόξον φίλον, ὦ φίλων κ.τ.λ.</quote>: 1165 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ γνῶθ’, εὖ γνῶθ’</quote>: 1186 <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων, δαίμων</quote>: 1197 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέποτ’, οὐδέποτ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοβητός_οὐκέθ_1153"> (for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτι φοβητός</quote>), is the best mode of restoring the metre (=1131 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις τὸν Ἡράκλειον</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 156">Cp. 156</ref> where <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ προσπεσών με λάθῃ</quote> has become in the MSS. <quote xml:lang="grc">μή με λάθῃ προσπεσών</quote> (n.).—See Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_καλὸν_1155">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 292">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 292</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν ἐστιν εὔξασθαι καλόν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίφονον_1155">, taking blood for blood: <ref n="Soph. El. 248"> <title>El.</title> 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιφόνους δίκας.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_χάριν_1155">, ‘at your pleasure’: see <ref n="Soph. Ant. 30"> <title>Ant.</title> 30</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰόλας_1155">, discoloured, spotted, by the disease. When this word refers to light or colour, the primary notion of rapid movement is usu. present,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the sense is ‘glancing,’ ‘gleaming’ (as in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">σάκος αἰόλον</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 7.222"> <title>Il.</title> 7. 222</ref>, with Leaf's n.), or ‘sheeny’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">δράκων</quote>, II <title>Tr.</title>). But it could also mean ‘variegated’ simply, as in Callim. <ref n="Call. Dian. 91"> <title>Dian.</title> 91</ref>(of a speckled hound).—Some take it here as=‘quivering’ (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.509"> <title>Il.</title> 22. 509</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰόλαι εὐλαί</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_λείψω_1158">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 817">cp. 817</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τρέφεται_1160">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀνέμων τρέφεται</quote> (schol.). With <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφεσθαι</quote>, the prep. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1160"> usu. denotes the surroundings of the <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφή</quote>, as Plat. <title>Theaet.</title>p. 175 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐλευθερίᾳ...τεθραμμένου</quote>: but it can also denote, as here, the aliment; id. Plat. <title>Tim.</title>p. 81 C <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθραμμένης...ἐν γάλακτι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκέτι_1161">: the generic <quote xml:lang="grc"> μή</quote> (being <emph>one who</emph> commands not...), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 170">cp. 170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή του κηδομένου.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδενὸς_1161"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσα_1161">: the relative clause here takes the place of a partitive gen.: cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 8. 1. 20">Xen. <title> Cyr.</title> 8. 1. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤν τις ἀπῇ οἷς παρεῖναι καθήκῃ</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 957">Cp. 957</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπει_1161"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀναπέμπει</quote> like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵησι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">προΐησι</quote> etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιόδωρος_1161">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 391">cp. 391</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ξένον_1163">, if thou hast any regard for a friendly stranger, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνοίᾳ_πελάταν_1163">, who draws near to thee with all good will, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλασσον_1163"> (intrans.), draw near to him:—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, meet his advances half way, instead of repelling him. For the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">σς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 390"> <title>Ai.</title> 390</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλέσσας</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 926 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανύσσειν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλασσον_1163_2">, between <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένον</quote> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐν_ππελάταν_1163">, is warrantable, since the latter words suggest a reason for the prayer, <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλασσον</quote>. Bolder collocations of words occur elsewhere in Soph. : <emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 1427">Soph. O. C. 1427</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς δὲ τολμήσει κλύων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τοῦδ’ ἕπεσθαι τἀνδρός</quote>; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1251.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1251.</ref>The word <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάταν</quote> gives a certain tone of deference, since <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάτης</quote> was familiar in Attic as = ‘dependent’ ( <title>Euthyphr.</title> p. 4 C).— Other versions are:—(1) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι σέβει, ξένον πέλασσον</quote>, ‘if anything is sacred to thee, <emph>approach</emph> the stranger’: (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι σέβει ξένον, πέλασσον...πελάταν</quote>, ‘approach him who approaches thee.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζειν</quote> (intrans.) could not take an acc. of the person approached: see Append. on 1149 ff. (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι σέβει, ξένον πέλασσον</quote> (trans.), bring the stranger near thee (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘allow him to approach thee’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_σοὶ_1165"> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1003">cp. 1003</ref>) is Seyffert's correction of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτισοι_1165"> (or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_1165">), which could not mean, ‘that it is <emph>for</emph> thee,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘in thy power.’ The objection to reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτι_σόν_1165"> is that this would mean rather, ‘that it is thy <emph>part</emph>’ (or ‘duty’): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 721"> <title>O. C.</title> 721</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόσκειν_1167">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to feed with thine own flesh: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 313">cp. 313</ref>. For the omission of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_1167">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 806"> <title>Ant.</title> 806</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1275."> <title>O. C.</title> 1275.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδαὴς_ἔχειν_1167"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, while it cannot be taught to bear the countless woes that attend upon it. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote> here=<emph> sustinere</emph>, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 537"> <title>O. C.</title> 537</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαθον ἄλαστ’ ἔχειν</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. Ant. 421."> <title>Ant.</title> 421.</ref>It is needless to read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀχεῖν_1167">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_ξυνοικεῖ_1167">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1237"> <title>O. C.</title> 1237</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆρας ἄφιλον, <ref n="Soph. OC i"> ο. ξ. ι</ref>(/να πρόπαντα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ κακῶν ξυνοικεῖ</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 1134. The context here slightly favours </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_1167"> as against </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_1167">, though the latter is possible. The only source of obscurity here is that in the first clause (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτρὰ γὰρ βόσκειν</quote>) the <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρ</quote> is the disease itself, while in the second (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδαὴς δ’</quote>) it is identified with the patient. The sense is, ‘thy disease is dreadful, and no length of time could inure thee to the countless other ills that accompany it’ (hunger, hardship, solitude). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιὸν_ἄλγημ_1170">, the pain which the proposal that he should return to Troy has caused to him from the first moment that he heard of it: see vv. 622, 917, 999.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέμνασας_1170"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801">cp. 801</ref>.—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ λῷστε κ.τ.λ.</quote>: their words grieve him the more, because they have otherwise shown him so much sympathy (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1121">cp. 1121</ref>, 1163 f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐντόπων_1170">, those mentioned in 307 ff.: for <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 100"> <title>Ant.</title> 100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιστον</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν προτέρων φάος</quote>. The adj. here=merely ‘present in a place’ (at a given moment), as in 211, <ref n="Soph. OC 1457"> <title>O. C.</title> 1457</ref>: not ‘resident,’ as in <ref n="Soph. OC 841."> <title>O. C.</title> 841.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλεσας_1172">. A return to Troy is more dreadful to him than death (999), and the mere suggestion of it has pierced him to the heart. This verb can denote the infliction, not only of physical (817), but also of mental anguish: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 831"> <title>El.</title> 831</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. ἀπολεῖς. ΧΟ. πῶς</quote>; | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. εἰ τῶν φανερῶς οἰχομένων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς Ἀΐδαν ἐλπίδ’ ὑποίσεις, κατ’ ἐμοῦ τακομένας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἐπεμβάσει</quote>. (But in 1388 below <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλεῖς</quote> is not similar.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴργασαι_1172">: perf. following aor., as 676, 929. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔλεξας_1173">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 270"> <title>Ai.</title> 270</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς τοῦτ’ ἔλεξας</quote> ;—(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὤλεσάς με</quote>), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἤλπισας_1173">, if thou hast indeed conceived the hope: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίσαι</quote> in 629.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξειν_1173"> with double acc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 811"> <title>Ant.</title> 811</ref>(n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπό_λείπετ_1177">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 817">cp. 817</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_1177">, ‘then,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘if ye persist’ (as the present tense </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοῶ_1177"> implies). This is better here than <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλα_πράσσειν_1178">. The </gloss>
lemma: φίλα μοι … παρήγγειλας ἑκόντι τε πράσσειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1178"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκόντι_1178"> has been suspected (see cr. n.). But analogous instances occur, where conjunctions, which might have been omitted, couple dissimilar clauses: as <ref n="Plat. Prot. 336A">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 336A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεκρίνατο διὰ βραχέων τε καὶ αὐτὰ τὰ ἐρωτώμενα</quote> : <ref n="Thuc. 1. 67">Thuc. 1. 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἡσύχαζον ἀνδρῶν τε σφίσιν ἐνόντων καὶ ἅμα...δεδιότες</quote>. Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκόντι <emph>τε</emph> </quote> would probably seem all the more natural to a Greek ear, since <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλομένῳ μοί ἐστι τοῦτο</quote> was so familiar an equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">προσφιλές μοί ἐστι τοῦτο</quote>. Cavallin's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκόντα_1178"> (acc. neut. plur.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1178_2"> cannot be justified by <ref n="Soph. OT 1229">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1229</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκόντα</quote> is merely a bold way of saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ ἃ ἑκών τις ἐποίησε</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναὸς_1181"> (partit. gen.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵνἡμῖν_τέτακται_1181">, to that part of the ship where (=whither) it has been appointed for us to go. The Chorus are common seamen, who have to take their places on the rowing benches or at other posts. The moment of sailing is now at hand (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1076">cp. 1076</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀραίου_Διός_1182">, Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἱκέσιος</quote> (484 n.) in another aspect,—as the god who hears the imprecation of the rejected suppliant. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀραῖος</quote> does not occur elsewhere as an epithet of Zeus, but among his titles were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ</quote> (Cramer <title>Anecd. Ox.</title> 1. 62), <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρός</quote> (Clemens <title>Protrept.</title> p. 24) and <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαμναῖος</quote> ( <title>De Mundo</title> 7).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλθῃς_1182">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέλθῃς</quote>: 48 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετρίαζε_1182"> in full (making the choriambic verse hypercatalectic): Brunck wrote </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετρίαζ_1182">. For the sense, cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 603E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 603E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετριάσει δέ πως πρὸς λύπην</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίμων_1187">: for the nom., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 891"> <title>Ant.</title> 891</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τύμβος</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_πούς_1188">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 786">cp. 786</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_τεύξω_1188"> ;=<quote xml:lang="grc">τί σε ποιήσω, τί σοι χρήσομαι</quote> ; ‘what shall I do with thee,’—how endure the pain,—now that my doom is otherwise so much worse?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετόπιν_1188">, used by <ref n="Rh. 4.1764">Rh. 4. 1764</ref>, occurs nowhere else in class. Greek, but is related to the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">μετόπισθεν</quote> (used by Eur. fr. 449) as the Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">κατόπιν</quote> to the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">κατόπισθεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλθετ_ἐπήλυδες_1190">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1222">cp. 1222</ref>: <title>Suppl.</title> 388 <quote xml:lang="grc">παλίσσυτος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στεῖχ’</quote> : <title>Legg.</title> 879 D <quote xml:lang="grc">νεήλυδος ἀφιγμένου</quote>. The adj. here=‘coming <emph>back</emph>’ (answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπανέρχομαι</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπέρχομαι</quote>): yet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_1190"> need not be regarded as redundant (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίως</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοσφαγής</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1130"> <title>Tr.</title> 1130</ref>); for they had once before been on the point of departing (1070). Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπηλυς</quote> always=<emph>advena.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ῥέξοντες_1191">, to do what, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμᾳ_πάρος_1191">, with a purpose different from (that of) the former course, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_1191"> (by attract. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔφαινες_1191">, which thou didst prescribe? He had told them to go away and leave him (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό νύν με λείπετ’ ἤδη</quote>, 1177). They ask if they are now to contravene that order, and if so, what they are to do. For the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν πάρος</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλοκότῳ</quote>, cp. <title>M.</title> 4. 4. 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα τῶν δικαίων</quote>. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">προφαίνειν</quote> can be used of any utterance ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 324"> <title>Tr.</title> 324</ref>); but, as it is said of oracles ( <ref n="Soph. OT 790"> <title>O. T.</title> 790</ref> n.), so it is peculiarly applicable to commands.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥέξοντος_1191"> is not the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote>, which is quite possible (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 801">cp. 801</ref> n.), but the fact that Greek idiom would require <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὡς</emph> τί ῥέξοντος</quote>. In the very rare instances where this <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> is omitted, the fut. partic. refers to the subject of the principal verb, as <ref n="Eur. Hec. 631"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 631</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕλαν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτάμεθ’, ἅλιον ἐπ’ οἶδμα ναυστολήσων</quote> (cp. Paley in <title>Journ. Phil.</title> vol. VIII. p. 80). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτοι_νεμεσητόν_1193">, since the feeling of <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμεσις</quote> is justified only when fair allowance has been made for human weakness. ( Andoc. or. 1. 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ γὰρ ἀνθρωπίνως περὶ τῶν πραγμάτων ἐκλογίζεσθαι, ὥσπερ ἂν αὐτὸν ὄντα ἐν τῇ συμφορᾷ</quote>.) Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.523"> <title>Il.</title> 9. 523</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν δ’</quote> (before the amend was made) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτι νεμεσσητὸν κεχολῶσθαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλύοντα_1193">: 174 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειμερίῳ_1193">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1460">cp. 1460</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 206"> <title>Ai.</title> 206</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Α<*>ας θολερῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται χειμῶνι νοσήσας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_θροεῖν_1193">, referring to his abrupt dismissal of them (1177). <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> (‘e'en’) expresses the relation of cause and effect. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>παρὰ</emph> νοῦν</quote> like <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ δίκην</quote> etc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 550"> <title>O. T.</title> 550</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ νοῦ χωρίς</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἀστεροπητὴς_1198">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.580"> <title>Il.</title> 1. 580</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀλύμπιος ἀστεροπητής</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 200"> <title>O. T.</title> 200</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τᾶν πυρφόρων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστραπᾶν κράτη νέμων</quote>. This is a repetition, in stronger words, of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἢν χρῇ με πᾶν παθεῖν κακόν</quote> (999). To brave the lightnings of Zeus is to face death in its most appalling form: so Ares says that he will avenge his son, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ πέρ μοι καὶ μοῖρα Διὸς πληγέντι κεραυνῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖσθαι ὁμοῦ νεκύεσσι</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.117"> <title>Il.</title> 15. 117</ref>). And Dido: <title>Vel pater omnipotens adigat me fulmine ad umbras</title> | ... <title>Ante</title> , <title>Pudor</title>, <emph>quam te violo</emph> ( <ref n="Verg. A. 4. 25"> <title>Aen.</title> 4. 25</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροντᾶς_αὐγαῖς_1198">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 1043">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 1043</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ταῦτ’ ἐπ’ ἐμοὶ ῥιπτέσθω μὲν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὸς ἀμφήκης βόστρυχος</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1083 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλικες δ’ ἐκλάμπουσι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στεροπῆς ζάπυροι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶσι_φλογίζων_1198">, lit., ‘shall <emph>be in the course of</emph> consuming,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in <emph>the very act</emph> of doing so:—as if he should behold Zeus in heaven, with the thunderbolt already brandished in his uplifted right hand. The peculiar vividness of the phrase depends on the somewhat rare use of the pres. part. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι</quote>—a use quite distinct from that of the fut. part. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχεται κατηγορήσων μου</quote> ( <title>Euthyphro</title> 2 C)=simply, ‘he is <emph>going to</emph> accuse me’; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι ἐπιχειρῶν σοι ἐπιδείξασθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 100"> <title>Phaedo</title> 100</ref>B)=‘I am <emph>proceeding with</emph> an attempt to show you’: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 122">Her. 1. 122</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤιε ταύτην αἰνέων διὰ παντός</quote>: <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 69</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι...ἐννέπων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρρέτω_Ἴλιον_1200">: not a curse on Troy itself, but a way of saying that he cares not how the Trojan war may end.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_ἐκείνῳ_1200">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 764">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 764</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν θανόντων...ὑπ’ Ἰλίῳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδ_ἄρθρον_1200">, this limb (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρθρα</quote> in 1207), my foot: <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδὸς</quote> is here a defining genitive, and the phrase is a periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἐμὸν πόδα</quote>, with a certain added pathos,—‘this poor lame foot.’ But in <ref n="Soph. OT 718"> <title>O. T.</title> 718</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρθρα ποδοῖν</quote> are the ankles.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπῶσαι_1200">, act., as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 446"> <title>Ai.</title> 446</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς τοῦδ’ ἀπώσαντες κράτη</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 600">cp. 600</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβεβληκότες</quote>. (But the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπώσῃ</quote> in 1122, of repelling advances.) He speaks as if the tortured limb were a mute suppliant that might well have moved their pity: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1188">cp. 1188</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ποὺς πούς</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1203">, appealing (230).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρέξατε_1203">, extend it to me, concede it: cp. <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 56</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπεῖν τίνι τοῦτο Μοῖρα τέλος ἔμπεδον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρεξε</quote>: a poet. use, like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγυαλίζω</quote>. (Distinguish the sense in <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.328"> <title>Il.</title> 12. 328</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠέ τῳ εὖχος ὀρέξομεν ἠέ τις ἡμῖν</quote>, ‘give <emph>glory.</emph>’) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_1204">: for the fut., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 441">cp. 441</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴποθεν_1204"> <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">προπέμψαι ἔχετε</quote> ,=‘from <emph>any</emph> quarter.’ So in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 886"> <title>Ai.</title> 886</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ ποθι...λεύσσων</quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσων, εἴ που</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> λεύσσει</quote>). The elliptical use of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τις</quote> is frequent ( <ref n="Thuc. 4. 26">Thuc. 4. 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσάγειν σῖτόν τε...καὶ εἴ τι ἄλλο βρῶμα</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένυν_1204">, axe: <ref n="Soph. El. 485"> <title>El.</title> 485</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφάκης γένυς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 249"> <title>Ant.</title> 249</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπέμψατε_1204">. This use of the verb is somewhat strange at first sight, and has led to conjectures (see cr. n.). But it seems to be justified by the context. The group of fifteen men is standing before him, and he sees that they are not regularly armed; but, as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ποθεν_1204"> shows, he hopes that some one of their number may have some weapon. <quote xml:lang="grc">προπέμψατε</quote> means strictly, ‘pass forward,’ from hand to hand. Cp. Ar. fr. 427 <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε παῖ ταχέως κατὰ χειρὸς ὕδωρ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">παράπεμπε τὸ χειρόμακτρον</quote>,—‘pass’ it round. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ῥέξῃς_1206">...; So <ref n="Soph. OC 398"> <title>O. C.</title> 398</ref>(Ismene having said that Creon will come) <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΙ. ὅπως τί δράσῃ</quote>; cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 1724: <ref n="Soph. El. 390"> <title>El.</title> 390</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΡ. ὅπως πάθῃς τί χρῆμα;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλάμαν_1206">, ‘deed of violence’; a sense in which the sing. does not seem to occur elsewhere, though the plur. often=‘violent hands’ ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.128"> <title>Il.</title> 3. 128</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ Ἄρηος παλαμάων</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρᾶτ_1206">, seems to me certain. For the interchange of <quote xml:lang="grc">χ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κ</quote>, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύχομαι</quote> corrupted from <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύκομαι</quote> in 745 (cr. n.). Here the error may have been facilitated by a recollection of 618 <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τέμνειν</quote>. The sense is, ‘hew all the flesh (from my bones), and sever limb from limb,’—a frenzied exaggeration of his prayer in 748, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάταξον εἰς ἄκρον πόδα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάμησον ὡς τάχιστα· μὴ φείσῃ βίου</quote>. Sophocles knew the History of Herodotus (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 337"> <title>O. C.</title> 337</ref> n.). Is it not possible that the poet's diction here may have been influenced by a reminiscence of the passage describing the ghastly suicide of the insane Cleomenes (6. 75)? Cleomenes, like Philoctetes, ‘asked for a sword,’— which the terrified Helot gave him. Then, <quote xml:lang="grc">παραλαβὼν τὸν σίδηρον ἄρχετο ἐκ κνημέων ἑωυτὸν λωβώμενος· ἐπιτάμνων γὰρ κατὰ μῆκος τὰς σάρκας</quote> (cp. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Χρῶτα_πάντα_1206">) <quote xml:lang="grc">προέβαινε ἐκ τῶν κνημέων ἐς τοὺς μηρούς, ἐκ δὲ τῶν μηρῶν ἔς τε τὰ ἰσχία καὶ τὰς λαπάρας, ἐς ὃ ἐς τὴν γαστέρα ἀπίκετο, καὶ ταύτην καταχορδεύων ἀπέθανε</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρᾶτ_1206_2"> be kept, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_1206"> must be taken in one of two ways. (1) As acc. masc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτ’</quote>. Cp. Ionfr. 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν αὑτοῦ κρᾶτα</quote>: Eur. fr. 243 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν σὸν κρᾶτ’</quote>. But with Sophocles <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτα</quote> is elsewhere neut.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1001">cp. 1001</ref>, 1457. (2) As adverbial neut. pl., ‘utterly.’ In either case the sense is weak. We cannot take <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα καὶ ἄρθρα</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πάντα ἄρθρα</quote>. (In <ref n="Aesch. PB 51">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 51</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκα τοῖσδε κοὐδὲν ἀντειπεῖν ἔχω</quote>, the comma should stand after <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδε</quote>, not after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκα</quote>.) A transposition is, indeed, possible—<quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτα καὶ ἄρθρ’ ἀπὸ πάντα</quote>. But, even then, there is the difficulty that he cuts off his own head before mangling his limbs. This, surely, is more than the figure of ‘prothysteron’ will comfortably excuse. Prof. Campbell compares <ref n="Soph. Aj. 238">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 238</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεφαλὴν καὶ γλῶσσαν ἄκραν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιπτεῖ θερίσας</quote>: but Ajax is not decapitating himself. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονᾷ_1209">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 117"> <title>Ant.</title> 117</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ποτε_1209">; the verb understood is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">φονᾷ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 381"> <title>Ant.</title> 381</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτ’</quote>; ‘What means this?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατέρα_ματεύων_1209">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">φονῶ</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">φονᾷ νόος</quote> had preceded: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 159"> <title>O. T.</title> 159</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἔτι_1212">. Hermann's deletion of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστ’</quote> before </gloss>
lemma: οὐ γὰρ ἐν φάει γ’ ἔτι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1212"> is probable on metrical grounds; and the interpolation might easily have arisen, as he says, from a superscript gloss <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>. On the other hand it is simpler and better to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> than (as Hermann prefers) <quote xml:lang="grc">ματεύων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_πόλις_1213">: Trachis (491): for the nom., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1186">cp. 1186</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ἂν_1214"> with optat. in a wish; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 531">cp. 531</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλιός_ἀνήρ_1214">. This, the reading of the MSS., is confirmed, as against Dindorf's conjecture (see cr. n.), by a point which seems to have escaped notice. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1214"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_1214"> marks the causal force of the relat. pron. (as in 663); and this indicates that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλιος_1214"> means, not merely ‘unhappy,’ but ‘wretchedly foolish’ (as in <title>O.T.</title> 372). The reflective emphasis which <quote xml:lang="grc">γ’</quote> adds to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλιος</quote> is thus exactly in place,— ‘misguided <emph>indeed</emph> that I was.’ A comma after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσίδοιμί_ς_1214"> makes this clearer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερὰν_λιβάδ_1215">, the Spercheius (492), neighbour to the haunts of the Malian nymphs (725). All rivers were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροί</quote>, but here the epithet has a special force, which </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθροῖς_1215"> brings out: he had voluntarily withdrawn himself from the realm of friendly deities. Cp. his appeal in 1040 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὦ πατρῴα γῆ θεοί τ’ ἐπόψιοι. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτ_εἰμι_1215">: for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’</quote>, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ οὐχ οἵα τε</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_1218"> as a prep. with the gen. (schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύς</quote>). The dat. is the usual case ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1007"> <title>O. T.</title> 1007</ref>). There are, however, two other passages in which the gen. is a well-attested reading. (<emph>a</emph>) <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 6. 24">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4. 6. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν δὲ ὁμοῦ εἶναι τοὺς πολλοὺς ἀλλήλων</quote>: three of the best MSS. support the gen., while others give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλήλοις</quote>. (<emph>b</emph>) Menander fr. incert. 204. The schol. on <ref n="Rh. 2.121">Rh. 2. 121</ref> quotes it as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ δὲ τῷ τίκτειν παρεγένεθ’ ἡ κόρη</quote>: but the mutilated form of it found in Suidas, Photius and Harpocration has <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τίκτειν ὁμοῦ</quote>. It is noteworthy that the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ</quote> in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύς</quote> (as distinguished from the sense ‘along with’) is said by the schol. on Apollonius to be distinctively AndAttic. , when it bore this sense, the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύς, πέλας</quote>, etc., might easily permit it to be sometimes construed with the gen. See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχων_1218"> is suspected by Nauck, who says, ‘one would rather have expected the aorist (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπελθών</quote>).’ But the pres. partic. is quite right: ‘moving on my way, I should now have been near my ship.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχοντα_1218"> following <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχων</quote>. This is a real blemish, though a small one. But it does not follow that it is corrupt. There are several proofs that Sophocles, writing rather for hearers than for readers, was not always careful to avoid such iteration of commonplace words. The emphasis here falls on the contrasted qualifications (<quote xml:lang="grc">νεὼς ὁμοῦ</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας</quote>), not on the participles themselves. A recurrence which, in print, catches the eye would hardly have offended the ear. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 87">Cp. 87</ref>, 88 <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν</quote> <emph>bis</emph>, with n.,—265 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρίᾳ</quote>, 267 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρίῳ</quote>: 1268 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγων, λόγοις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἰόντ_1218">, repeating the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας στείχοντα</quote>. The words are certainly unnecessary; but they are nothing worse. For a like redundancy, cp. Lysias or. 16 § 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς μὲν ἱππεύουσιν ἀσφάλειαν εἶναι δεῖν νομίζοντας, τοῖς δ’ ὁπλίταις κίνδυνον ἡγουμένους</quote>, where the second participle merely repeats the sense of the first, and might have been omitted. We could, indeed, take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰόντ_1218"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἰόντε</quote> (for the elision of the dual, cp. <ref n="Hes. WD 199">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτον προλιπόντ’ ἀνθρώπους</quote>), placing commas after <quote xml:lang="grc">γόνον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰόντ’</quote>. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχοντα</quote> would refer to both men. ‘I see Hom. Od.and N. approaching, on their way hither to us.’ But this is less natural.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχων_σοι_1219">. The ethic dat. implies, ‘thou would'st have seen me depart’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 81"> <title>O. C.</title> 81</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ βέβηκεν ἡμὶν ὁ ξένος</quote>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεύσσομεν_1221">: for the plur. following the sing. (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_1221">) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1394">cp. 1394</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734"> <title>Ant.</title> 734</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_φράσειας_1222">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.456"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 456</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν δὴ τόνδ’ ἄνδρα μάχης ἐρύσαιο μετελθών</quote>..; the formula is more courteous than <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> with fut. ind. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</ref> n.). He seeks to restrain himself.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλίντροπος_1222">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1190">cp. 1190</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέλευθον_1222">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1212"> <title>Ant.</title> 1212</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆυρα δυστυχεστάτην</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευθον ἕρπω...;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_ταχύς_1222">; for <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 268">cp. 268</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύσων_1224">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 691"> Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 691</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦσαι τὰς πρότερον ἁμαρτίας</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 46">Thuc. 3. 46</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταγνῶναι καὶ...τὴν ἁμαρτίαν καταλῦσαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινόν_φωνεῖς_1225">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> in such comment, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1035"> <title>O. T.</title> 1035</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν γ’ ὄνειδος σπαργάνων ἀνειλόμην</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1127"> <title>Ai.</title> 1127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείναντα; δεινόν γ’ εἶπας, εἰ καὶ ζῇς θανών</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 341."> <title>El.</title> 341.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_πιθόμενος_1226">. This passage (down to 1234) well illustrates the dramatic use of interruption in stichomuthia. The spectators are now to learn that the repentance of Neoptolemus is complete. Obeying his superiors (1226), he did a base deed (1228); he will restore the bow (1230, 1232); for he has no right to it (1234). Each point is thrown into relief by the excited interpellations of Odysseus. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 210">Cp. 210</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_πρέπον_1227"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἃ οὔ σοι πρέπον ἦν πρᾶξαι</quote>. Cp. <title>O.T.</title> 862. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλών_1228">. The partic. answers the question asked by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖον_1228">: ‘what unbecoming deed didst thou do?’ ‘(I did such a deed) <emph>by capturing,</emph>’ etc. Thus we understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπραξα ἔργον οὐ πρέπον μοι</quote>. The verb which N. would naturally have used, if Od.had allowed him to finish his sentence, would have been <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥμαρτον</quote>, to which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν</quote> in 1226 would have been cogn. acc.: but, after the interruption, the verb is best supplied from v. 1227. Thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_1228"> remains actually an acc. of respect, ‘(the sin), by which.’ Blaydes suggests <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εἷλον</emph> ἄνδρα καὶ δόλοις</quote>. It is true that in stichomuthia an interrupted speaker usually ends with a finite verb (as <ref n="Soph. OT 560"> <title>O. T.</title> 560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρει</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 646"> <title>O. C.</title> 646</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήσω</quote>). But in this context </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλών_1228_2"> is more forcible than <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷλον</quote>, since then it is Od.himself who supplies the description of the deed as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πρέπον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέον_1229">: for the sinister sense, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 784">cp. 784</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_φόβος_1231">. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>, in foreboding, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1163"> <title>Ai.</title> 1163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσται μεγάλης ἔριδός τις ἀγών</quote>: for its place, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 104">cp. 104</ref>, 519, 1039. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1231"> (‘how!’) as in <ref n="Soph. El. 1112"> <title>El.</title> 1112</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ ξέν); ὥς μ’ ὑπέρχεται φόβος</quote>.—Seyffert's conject., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_1231"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 928"> <title>El.</title> 928</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαῦμά τοί μ’ ὑπέρχεται</quote>), seems less fitting after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>. With regard to Nauck's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπήλυθεν_1231">, it may be noted that neither Aesch. nor Soph. admits <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλυθον</quote> in dialogue, though Eur. does so ( <ref n="Soph. El. 598">Soph. <title> El.</title> 598</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ἔλαβον_1232">: for the tribrach (not contained in one word), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1247">cp. 1247</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 26"> <title>O. C.</title> 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅστις ὁ τόπος</quote>: and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 537."> <title>O. T.</title> 537.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_λέξεις_1233">; for the fut., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1204">cp. 1204</ref> n. —The interrogative </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_που_1233">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δή</quote> (900) and <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δήπου</quote>, was freq. in Attic ( <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 522">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 522</ref>, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερα_δὴ_1235"> seems clearly right (see cr. n.): the <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> gives indignant emphasis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὲ_1235"> is also possible (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 917">cp. 917</ref>); but it is weaker, and gives a less good rhythm. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερα_1235"> in a simple question (like Lat. <emph>an</emph>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 333."> <title>O. C.</title> 333.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κερτομῶν_1235">, of bitter jest; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 956"> <title>Ant.</title> 956</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_κερτόμησις_1236">. The quiet force of the answer would be rather spoiled by adding </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_1236"> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 105">cp. 105</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναπολεῖν_1238">, to plough anew; hence, fig., ‘to go over the same ground’ again. <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτὰ...τρὶς τετράκι τ’ ἀμπολεῖν</quote>. In this sense Attic prose preferred <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναπολεῖν</quote> : <ref n="Plat. Phileb. 60A">Plat. <title>Phileb.</title> 60A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ δ’ ἡ παροιμία δοκεῖ ἔχειν, τὸ καὶ δὶς καὶ τρὶς τό γε καλῶς ἔχον ἐπαναπολεῖν λόγῳ δεῖν</quote>: <title>Legg.</title> 723 E <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναπολήσωμεν</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τριπόλιστον οἶκτον</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 858"> <title>Ant.</title> 858</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχὴν_1239">, adv., placed before the negative word; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 92"> <title>Ant.</title> 92</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_1239"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐβουλόμην_1239"> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 427">cp. 427</ref>, 1278: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.22">Lys. or. 12 § 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ἐβουλόμην ἂν αὐτοὺς ἀληθῆ λέγειν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἐγὼ δ’ ἐβουλόμην ἂν αὐτοὺς ἀληθῆ λέγειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_νῦν_1240">. Though in <ref n="Soph. OT 658"> <title>O. T.</title> 658</ref> and <ref n="Soph. El. 616"> <title>El.</title> 616</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ <emph> νυν</emph> ἐπίστω</quote>, the temporal <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> seems fitter in this curt response.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκηκοὼς_1240"> is much better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκήκοας_1240">. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 480"> <title>Ai.</title> 480</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἀκήκοας λόγον</quote> is fitting at the end of a speech: cp. above 241 n. But in a brief statement of resolve, such as this, the compact unity given by the participial construction suits the placid firmness of the speaker's tone. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 253">Cp. 253</ref>, 567. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_1241">: for the art., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 118">cp. 118</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_τάδε_1241">; for this use of the fut. partic. with art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 297."> <title>O. T.</title> 297.</ref>Dindorf is not quite accurate in saying that, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσται, οὑπικωλύων</quote> ‘would have sufficed,’ and that the poet preferred the fut. partic. only for the sake of correspondence with <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλύσει</quote>. The fut. partic. was required by Greek idiom, whether the principal verb was to be past, pres., or future. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 2. 4. 5">Xen. <title> An.</title> 2. 4. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἡγησόμενος οὐδεὶς ἔσται</quote>, ‘there will be no one <emph>to lead</emph> us’ ( Xen. could not have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἡγούμενος</quote>). For the place of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_1241"> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 139"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνον ὁ κτανών</quote>. The compound </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπικωλύσων_1241"> comes after the simple </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κωλύσει_1241"> as in <ref n="Soph. OT 566">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 566</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέσχομεν</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχετε</quote> , <emph>ib.</emph> 575 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκμάνθαν’</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθεῖν</quote>. Cp. above, <ref n="Soph. OT 249">Soph. O. T. 249</ref>: and for the converse, <ref n="Soph. OT 911">Soph. O. T. 911</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_1241">: for the double acc. (a rare constr. with <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλύω</quote>), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lysis 207e"> <title>Lys.</title>p. 207 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ γε...καὶ μάλα πολλὰ κωλύουσιν</quote> </cit> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ γονεῖς</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τοῖς_1243">. Attic usage recommends <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς</quote>, in preference to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσδ_1243"> here: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 741"> <title>O. C.</title> 741</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς δε Καδμείων λεὼς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλεῖ δικαίως, ἐκ δὲ τῶν μάλιστ’ ἐγώ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρασείεις_1245">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1001">cp. 1001</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφά_1245"> is right, as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαια_1245"> shows: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφόν_1245"> would be intolerable here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_ἔλαβες_1247">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">γ’</quote> with causal force (<emph>quae ceperis</emph>): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 663">cp. 663</ref>. For the tribrach, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1232">cp. 1232</ref>. Odysseus, ignoring the moral question, asserts a right of property in the bow, because his <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλαί</quote> (as he euphemistically calls them) have won it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_ἁμαρτίαν_1248">. The <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀντιλαβή</quote> marks a rising tone of excitement (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 54">cp. 54</ref> n.). These words sum up N.'s resolve, and his mentor turns from expostulations to threats.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναλαβεῖν_1248">, ‘retrieve.’ So <ref n="Eur. Ion 426">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 426</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς πρὶν ἀναλαβεῖν ἁμαρτίας</quote>. This sense comes through that of ‘recovering’ (since the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμαρτία</quote> may be regarded as a loss of character),—not through the notion of ‘taking back’ a false move (for which the word was <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνατίθεσθαι</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 5. 121">Her. 5. 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο τὸ τρῶμα ἀνέλαβον</quote>: id. 8. 109 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναλαμβάνειν τὴν προτέρην κακότητα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_δικαίῳ_1251">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, having it on my side, as an ally: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν θεῷ</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1125"> <title>Ai.</title> 1125</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν τῷ δικαίῳ γὰπ μέγ’ ἔξεστιν φρονεῖν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_φόβον_1251">, I do not fear the terror (=terrible thing) of which thou speakest,—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, the wrath of the army. For this objective sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβος</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1651"> <title>O. C.</title> 1651</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δεινοῦ τινος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβου φανέντος</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σὸν_1251">, <ref n="Soph. El. 1110"> <title>El.</title> 1110</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα τὴν σὴν κληδόν’</quote>: fr. 169 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα τὴν σὴν πεῖραν· ἓν δ’ ἐπίσταμαι</quote>.—I prefer this version to the other which is possible:— ‘I do not <emph>feel</emph> the <emph>fear</emph> which thy words suggest.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τοι_1252"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, is the reply to a lost verse, in which Odysseus said that he would enforce his will with his own hand. Throughout this passage it is Odysseus who threatens, while Neoptolemus stands on the defensive. To Odysseus must belong <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τἄρα Τρωσίν, ἀλλὰ σοὶ μαχούμεθα</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc"> χεῖρα...ἐπιψαύουσαν</quote>: while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστω τὸ μέλλον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ κἀμέ...κοὐ μέλλοντ’ ἔτι</quote> are the answers of Neoptolemus. Hence, if we reject the hypothesis of a lost verse, only three resources remain.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῇ_χειρὶ_1252"> in 1252 referred to words of Odysseus which either included <quote xml:lang="grc">χείρ</quote>, or at least foretold his <emph>personal</emph> interference more explicitly than is done by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαχούμεθα_1252">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_φόβον_1252">, with some such retort as, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν τῷ δικαίῳ χεὶρ ἐμή σ’ ἀναγκάσει</quote>. Or v. 1252, </gloss>
lemma: ξὺν τῷ δικαίῳ τὸν σὸν οὐ ταρβῶ φόβον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1252"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_τοι_1252"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, may have answered such a verse as, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδ’ ἄλυπος τῆς ἐμῆς ἔσει χερός</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τοι_1252_2">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 47"> <title>O. C.</title> 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀλλ’</emph> οὐδ’ ἐμοί τοι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_1252"> refers to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῇ_χειρὶ_1252_2">: as he does not fear the Greek army (1250), so neither does he fear the violence of Odysseus. ‘But neither do I obey thy hand (=yield to thy threat of force), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_1252">, so as to do thy bidding.’ —For the constr. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείθομαι_1252"> with dat. and inf., cp. <ref n="Plat. Prot. 338A">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 338A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πείθεσθέ μοι ῥαβδοῦχον...ἑλέσθαι</quote>: for the art. with the inf., <ref n="Plat. Prot. 118">Plat. Prot. 118</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1105">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίας δ’ ἐξίσταμαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δρᾶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστω_1254">. Wecklein reads <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἴτω</quote>, which is the fitter word where bold indifference to possible consequences is declared (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 120">cp. 120</ref> n., and <ref n="Soph. OT 1458"> <title>O. T.</title> 1458</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ μὲν ἡμῶν μοῖῤ, ὅποιπερ εἶς’, <ref n="Soph. OT i"> ο. τ. ι</ref>)/τω</quote>). But the calmer word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστω</quote> is more dignified and more effective here. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1205"> <title>O. C.</title> 1205</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστω δ’ οὖν ὅπως ὑμῖν φίλον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐ_ἔτι_1256">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 567">cp. 567</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_1257">, ‘however.’ Odysseus, who is not naturally <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσοργος</quote> (377), has quickly recovered his self-control. He recalls his threat of violence—speaking as if he had not heard N.'s reply. He now leaves the scene—in the hope that his parting threat will suffice—but remains near, to watch unseen. At the crisis he again interposes (1293),—as in v. 974.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλθών_1257">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1033"> <title>El.</title> 1033</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθοῦσα μητρὶ ταῦτα πάντ’ ἔξειπε σῇ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσωφρόνησας_1259">: for the aor., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1099">cp. 1099</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονῆσαι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλαυμάτων_1259">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 931"> <title>Ant.</title> 931</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιν ἄγουσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαύμαθ’ ὑπάρξει</quote>. The familiar use of <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίων</quote> in threats (<emph>ib.</emph> 754) made it natural to use the subst. as =‘troubles’: hence the confusion of metaphor would not be felt. For like phrases with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόδα_1259">, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 619."> <title>Ant.</title> 619.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φιλοκτήτην_λέγω_1261">: for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 32"> <title>Ant.</title> 32</ref>(n.). Matthiae's ground for proposing to read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φιλοκτήτης_1261"> (as nom. for voc., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 432">cp. 432</ref>) was that the accus. seems awkward when it refers to a person who is accosted: but we may properly compare <ref n="Soph. Aj. 71"> <title>Ai.</title> 71</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος, σὲ</quote>... | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">προσμολεῖν καλῶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴαντα φωνῶ· στεῖχε δωμάτων πάρος</quote>: for, though the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνῶ</quote> (‘I call to’) is different from that of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote>, yet the objection to the accus. would be the same. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμείψας_1262">, of leaving a place (as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 659"> <title>Tr.</title> 659</ref>): but it can also denote ‘entering,’ as <ref n="Hdt. 5. 72">Her. 5. 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν τὰς θύρας αὐτὸν ἀμεῖψαι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 945">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 945</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάξαι</quote> (of leaving), n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετρήρεις_1262">: here no more than <quote xml:lang="grc">πετρίνας</quote>. Cp. the phrase of Eur. in <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 889">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 889</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβήρεις ἕδρας</quote>, ‘seat on a tomb.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_αὖ_1263">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1500"> <title>O. C.</title> 1500</ref>(Theseus entering) <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς αὖ παρ’ ὑμῶν κοινὸς ἠχεῖται</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος...;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄντροις_1263">, poet. plur., like <quote xml:lang="grc">δώματα, αὐλαί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 945"> <title>Ant.</title> 945</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">θρόνοι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 425"> <title>O. C.</title> 425</ref>), etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵσταται_1263">: cp. <title>I. T.</title> 1307 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἀμφὶ δῶμα θεᾶς τόδ’ ἵστησιν βοήν;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκαλεῖσθε_1263">: the midd. here differs from the act. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 597"> <title>O. T.</title> 597</ref> n.) only by suggesting that their own interests are involved.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεχρημένοι_1263">. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">κέχρημαι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> χράομαι</quote>) in classical prose always means either, ‘to have used,’ or ‘to have been used.’ In poetry it means also, ‘to stand in need of.’ The partic. occurs only in poetry, as <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.13"> <title>Od.</title> 1. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστου κεχρημένον</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 1199">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πώματος κεχρημένοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤμοι_χρῆμα_1265">. Philoctetes, in the recesses of his cave, did not recognise the voice that called to him, and expected to see only the sailors, —who were still in front of the cave when he entered it (1217), and whom he regards as friends (1171). It is when he comes to the mouth of the cave, and sees Neoptolemus—the stealer of his bow— —that he exclaims <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι, κακὸν τὸ χρῆμα</quote>. (For this use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῆμα_1265">, familiar in Attic, cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 799 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα τὸ χρῆμα</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 834 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτε τὸ χρῆμ’</quote>;)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῶν_κακά_1265">; Bergk's correction </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vέα_1265"> is confirmed by the <quote xml:lang="grc"> κακα</quote> in the text of L. Probably <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote> was merely a conjecture made to suit </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγα_1265">,—a corruption which doubtless arose from the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_1265"> (‘perchance,’ <ref n="Soph. OC 969"> <title>O. C.</title> 969</ref>) just before it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμποντες_1265">, ‘ushering in,’ ‘heralding’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1286"> <title>Ant.</title> 1286</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ κακάγγελτά μοι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">προπέμψας ἄχη</quote>, ‘O thou herald of evil, bitter tidings.’ (The use of <quote xml:lang="grc"> προπέμψατε</quote> in 1205 is different.) His fear is that Neoptolemus has come to execute the threat of taking him to Troy by force (983). That is, indeed, the only evil that could now be added to his lot. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_λόγων_1268">, through them: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 88">cp. 88</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_1268">: for the repetition, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 88">cp. 88</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῦτος_1271"> is explained by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστός_1271"> etc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 435"> <title>O. T.</title> 435</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς τοιοίδ’ ἔφυμεν, ὡς μὲν σοὶ δοκεῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μῷροι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 62"> <title>O. C.</title> 62</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτά σοι ταῦτ’ ἐστίν, ὦ ξέν’, οὐ λόγοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμώμεν’</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστός_1271_2">, inspiring confidence: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 71">cp. 71</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μὴν_1273">: the same formula occurs in <ref n="Soph. El. 817"> <title>El.</title> 817</ref>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴν</quote> seems here better than the <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_1273"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῦε_1275">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1751"> <title>O. C.</title> 1751</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃν_γε_1275">. Dobree ( II. <title>Adv.</title> 47) would alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1275"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_1275">, comparing <ref n="Eur. Ba. 655">Eur. <title> Bacch.</title> 655</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">σοφὸς σοφὸς σύ</quote>), where <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote>, lost in the MSS., was restored by Porson from <title>Chr. Patiens</title> 1529. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1275_2"> is right. ‘All thy <emph>words</emph> will be in vain (though I cannot resist force, if that be used).’ He knows what their <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote> are worth (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1268">cp. 1268</ref> f., 1271). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_λέγω_1277">: ‘yes, (I am so resolved,) and more strongly than my words express.’ Though <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδογμένον</quote> might be supplied with </gloss>
lemma: καὶ πέρα γ’, ἴσθ’, ἢ λέγω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθ_1277">, it is better to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοκται</quote>. The simple <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἴσθι</quote> i(s sometimes, like <quote xml:lang="grc">σάφ’ ἴσθι</quote>, parenthetic: <ref n="Soph. OT 1022"> <title>O. T.</title> 1022</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρόν ποτ’, <ref n="Soph. OT i"> ο. τ. ι</ref>)/σθι, τῶν ἐμῶν χειρῶν λαβών</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_λέγω_1277">, cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 1082"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 1082</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπώλεσέν με, κἄτι μᾶλλον ἢ λέγω</quote>: id. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 667">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 667</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παντάλαινα, κἄτι μᾶλλον ἢ λέγω</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἄν_1278">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 427">cp. 427</ref>, 1239: and for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μέν</quote>, 882 n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸςκαιρὸν_1278">=<quote xml:lang="grc">καιρίως</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 325"> <title>O. T.</title> 325</ref> n.).— </gloss>
