<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπαυμαι_1278">: for the perf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 76">cp. 76</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλα</quote>. Similarly <quote xml:lang="grc"> πεπαύσομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 91"> <title>Ant.</title> 91</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_γὰρ_1278">: for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>, marking assent, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 639."> <title>Ant.</title> 639.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτήσει_1281">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1370">cp. 1370</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1360"> <title>Ai.</title> 1360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτᾶσθαι φίλους</quote>: and for the constr. here, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 267">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ θεῖον δυσμενὲς κεκτήμεθα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_γ_1282">. When </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_1282"> refers, with causal force, to a <emph>definite</emph> antecedent ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1184"> <title>O. T.</title> 1184</ref>), the addition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1282"> to it is comparatively rare in Soph. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 810">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 810</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ γε</quote> is not similar): while <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς γε</quote> is frequent (663).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεστέρηκας_1282">, with acc. of the thing only: 931. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλθών_1284"> implies, ‘after robbing me, thou wilt not even leave me in peace.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1276"> <title>Ai.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρρύσατ’ ἐλθὼν μοῦνος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχθιστος_γεγώς_1284">, having proved thyself a most hateful son of a noble sire. Achilles was <quote xml:lang="grc"> φίλτατος</quote> to Ph. (242): the son has become <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔχθιστος</quote> by his theft of the bow.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴσχιστος_1284"> was approved by Porson, and has received weighty support from recent critics. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 906">Cp. 906</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρὸς φανοῦμαι</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. Phil. 585">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 585</ref>(=594 Porson) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴσχιστον</quote> is a <emph>v. l.</emph> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχθιστον</quote>: in <ref n="Soph. OT 1519">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1519</ref> at least one late MS. has <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴσχιστος</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχθιστος</quote>: and in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1059"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1059</ref>Triclinius gave <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθίστῳ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχίστῳ</quote>. But, as it seems to me, we should rather lose than gain by forsaking the MSS. here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρα_1288"> seems the true correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἆρα</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄρα</quote>. The expected answer to a question asked by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα</quote> may be either ‘yes’ ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 405"> <title>Ant.</title> 405</ref>), or ‘no’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1304"> <title>Ai.</title> 1304</ref>): here it suits the suspense between fear and hope. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_οὐ_1288"> is unsuitable; it would mean, ‘Is it not clear that I am being deceived again?’ When <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐ</quote> is used, the answer ‘yes’ is always inevitable, and the tone of the query is usually triumphant (see <ref n="Soph. OT 540"> <title>O. T.</title> 540</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 823"> O. T. 823</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 828"> O. T. 828</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 791"> <title>O. C.</title> 791</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 883"> O. C. 883</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1034"> <title>Ai.</title> 1034</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 614"> <title>El.</title> 614</ref>). The other conjecture, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γὰρ_1288">, is also inappropriate; that would mean, ‘what, am I not being deceived again?’ (as if a second fraud had been expected. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 246">Cp. 246</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1017"> <title>O. T.</title> 1017</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1348"> <title>Ai.</title> 1348</ref>). The intrusion of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα</quote> in the MSS. here may have been due to the scribe's reminiscence of passages in which the question </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_εἶπας_1288"> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> (246, <ref n="Soph. OT 1017"> <title>O. T.</title> 1017</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώμος_1289">, ‘I swear, ‘No’’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόφημι</quote>, ‘I say ‘No,’’ <ref n="Soph. OC 317"> <title>O. C.</title> 317</ref>): <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 424">Ar. <title> Eq.</title> 424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς θεοὺς ἀπώμνυν</quote>. For the aor., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1314">cp. 1314</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 536"> <title>Ai.</title> 536</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπήνεσ’</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 693 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφριξ’</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 668"> <title>El.</title> 668</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεξάμην</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1276">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέπτυς’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνοῦ_1289">: cp. <title>Suppl.</title> 652 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς ἵκτορας ἁγνοῦ</quote>. The fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνόν</quote> is oft. an epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβας</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. OT 830"> <title>O. T.</title> 830</ref>) is no adequate reason for writing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνὸν_ὑψίστου_1289"> here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέσται_1291">, ‘shall be forthcoming’ (in fulfilment of thy word,—cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 726"> <title>O. C.</title> 726</ref>): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανερόν_1291">, ‘before thine eyes’; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 910"> <title>O. C.</title> 910</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργεῖς_1291"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_γ_1293">: <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the verb: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 660">cp. 660</ref>, 1037. Odysseus darts forward from his place of concealment (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1257">cp. 1257</ref> n.),—his voice being heard before he is seen (1295). At v. 974 he was just in time to prevent the bow being restored; now he is too late.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ξυνίστορες_1293">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 542"> <title>Ant.</title> 542</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν τοὔργον, Ἅιδης χοἱ κάτω ξυνίστορες</quote>: <title>Suppl.</title> 1174 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς δὲ ξυνίστωρ οἵ τ’ ἐν οὐρανῷ θεοί</quote>. For the invocation of the gods in a protest, cp. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 87">Thuc. 4. 87</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάρτυρας μὲν θεοὺς... ποιήσομαι ὡς ἐπ’ ἀγαθῷ ἥκων οὐ πείθω</quote>: and id. 1. 78, <ref n="Thuc. 2. 71.">Thuc. 2. 71.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέρ_τ_1293">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> irregularly placed, as in 185: <ref n="Soph. OC 33"> <title>O. C.</title> 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ὑπέρ τ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτῆς θ’ ὁρώσης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέκνον_1295">: a mode of address which he has not used since v. 997 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖ</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 923">Cp. 923</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξένε</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_φώνημα_1295">; In this agitated and rapid utterance, it seems best to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">φώνημα</quote>, and to take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπῃσθόμην_1295"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ὀδυσσέως_1295">, rather than to suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">φώνημα</quote> is governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῃσθόμην</quote> and understood again with the proper name. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστελῶ_1297">, here, ‘convey away’; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 983">cp. 983</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στελοῦσι</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξουσιν</quote> in 985). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_χαίρων_1299">: a regular formula in threats,—most forcible when, as here, the verb is left to be supplied from the last speaker's words: so <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1592">Eur. <title> Or.</title> 1592</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΠ....ἀρκέσω δ’ ἐγὼ λέγων</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΕ. ἀλλ’ οὔ τι χαίρων, ἤν γε μὴ φυγῇς πτεροῖς</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 36">Her. 3. 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ δὲ ὤλεσας Κῦρον πειθόμενόν σοι. ἀλλ’ οὔ τι χαίρων, ἐπεὶ κ.τ.λ.</quote> Sometimes, again, the verb is expressed: as in <ref n="Soph. OT 363">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 363</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 843">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 843</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὔ τι χαίρων αὔτ’ ἐρεῖς</quote>: id. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 563">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 563</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὔτι</quote> (so Bentley for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρων ταῦτα τολμήσει λέγειν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθωθῇ_1299">, be directed straight: fr. 430. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθοῦται κανών</quote>. Cp. the fig. sense in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 675"> <title>Ant.</title> 675</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν... ὀρθουμένων</quote>, ‘lives whose course is fair.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆ_1300">, in reproof, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1147"> <title>O. T.</title> 1147</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆ, μὴ κόλαζε</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1087">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1087</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆ, ποῖ ποτ’ ἤγαγές με</quote>; the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆ ἆ</quote> also occurs in trimeters, as <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1598">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1598</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> οπ.ἔσται τάδ’. ΜΕ. ἆ ἆ, μηδαμῶς δράσῃς τάδε</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεθῇς_1300"> is altered to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφῇς_1300"> by Nauck, who thinks that <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες</quote> in 1301 caused the error. But two points claim notice. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθῇς</quote>, ‘permit to escape from thy hand,’ ‘allow to fly,’ is a more forcible word than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφῇς</quote> (‘discharge’) when, as here, the archer is at the very point of shooting. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 784"> <title>O. T.</title> 784</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ μεθέντι τὸν λόγον</quote>, ‘who had let that taunt escape him’ (with n. there). <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 4. 3">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 4. 3</ref>§ 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">παλτὰ...οἶς καὶ μεθιέντες καὶ ἔχοντες χρῴμεθ’ ἄν</quote> (‘darts which will serve us, whether our hands release or retain them’). (2) It is no objection to <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθῇς</quote> that <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες</quote> in 1301 has a different application. Cp. n. on 762 (<quote xml:lang="grc">δῆτα</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέθες_χεῖρα_1301">: the second acc. defines the part: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.240"> <title>Il.</title> 11. 240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ ἄορι πλῆξ’ αὐχένα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 831"> <title>Tr.</title> 831</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γάρ σφε</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρίει</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρά</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_κτλ_1302"> At first sight it might appear simplest to suppose that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφείλου_1302"> governs a double accus., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_κτανεῖν_1302"> being epexegetic (‘thou hast robbed me of the man, so that I should not slay him’). But other passages show that there was an idiomatic use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιροῦμαι</quote> with the inf., in which it was nearly equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλύω</quote>. Pindar <title> I.</title> 1. 60 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα δ’ ἐξειπεῖν</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρεῖται βραχὺ μέτρον ἔχων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος</quote>, ‘hinders from uttering.’ <ref n="Eur. Andr. 913">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 913</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄκτεινας, ἤ τις συμφορά σ’ ἀφείλετο</quote>; (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κτεῖναι</quote>,—‘prevented thee’). <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1145">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1145</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δεσπότου τάχος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφείλετ’ αὐτὴν παῖδα μὴ δοῦναι τάφῳ</quote>, ‘her master's haste deprived her of the power to bury her son.’ So, here, the true construction seems to be, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_πολέμιον_1302">; ‘why hast thou robbed me of the chance of slaying a foe?’ In admitting, but not requiring, <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with the inf., this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιροῦμαι</quote> is like other verbs of hindering. </gloss>
lemma: τί ἀφείλου με μὴ κτανεῖν ἄνδρα πολέμιον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολέμιον_1302">: for the tribrach in the 5th place, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 719">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 719</ref> n. This is the rarest form of it (the last word of the verse being a ‘paeon quartus’): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1327">cp. 1327</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 780">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 780</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ἄτιμος ἡ τάλαινα βαρύκοτος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθρόν_τ_1302">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1323">cp. 1323</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιον δυσμενῆ θ’</quote>. He has avowed his hostility to the whole Greek army (1200), and can properly call Odysseus <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιος</quote>,—as Menelaus gives that name to Ajax ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1132"> <title>Ai.</title> 1132</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γε_1305">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 84"> <title>Ant.</title> 84</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_στρατοῦ_1305">: for the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10"> <title>Ant.</title> 10</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψευδοκήρυκας_1305">, ‘lying heralds.’ The word alludes more particularly (as is indicated by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνδὲ_θρασεῖς_1305">) to the protest which Odysseus has just made ‘in the name of the whole army’ (1293 f.), and to his threat at vv. 1257 f.:—it is not merely an equivalent for ‘false envoy’ (because he had executed his mandate by fraud). In Attic Tragedy the <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρυξ</quote> was especially associated with unsuccessful bluster. Examples are afforded by the herald of Aegyptus ( <title> Suppl.</title> 836 ff.), the herald of Creon ( <title>Suppl.</title> 399 ff.), and the herald of Eurystheus (id. <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 55"> <title>Heracl.</title> 55</ref> ff.). Menelaus plays a similar part when he forbids the burial of Ajax ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1047"> <title>Ai.</title> 1047</ref>), and he is then attended by a <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρυξ</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 1115 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ταῦτα πλείους δεῦρο κήρυκας λαβών κ.τ.λ.</quote>). With <quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδοκῆρυξ</quote> cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ψευδόμαντις</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1097"> <title>O. C.</title> 1097</ref>): for the allusive plur., <ref n="Soph. OT 366"> <title>O. T.</title> 366</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_αἰχμήν_1305">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 266"> <title>Tr.</title> 266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τόξου κρίσιν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶεν_1308">, ‘so far so good’: cp. <title>Helen.</title> 761 <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΛ. εἶεν· τὰ μὲν δὴ δεῦρ’ ἀεὶ καλῶς ἔχει</quote>: similarly it marks a pause between statement and comment ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1308"> <title>O. C.</title> 1308</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτου_1308">, causal gen.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 698"> <title>O. T.</title> 698</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου ποτὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆνιν τοσήνδε πράγματος στήσας ἔχεις</quote>. The MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_1308">, though defensible, seems less suitable here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἐμέ_1308">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 522">cp. 522</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δ_1310">: the elision gives quasi-caesura: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 276">cp. 276</ref> and 101 n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἧς_1310">, since the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσις</quote>, or inherited strain of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γένος</quote>, can stand in poetry for the <quote xml:lang="grc">γένος</quote> itself.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβλαστες_1310">: for the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ε_1310"> before <quote xml:lang="grc">βλ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 440"> <title>El.</title> 440</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πασῶν ἔβλαστε</quote>, fr. 119 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ δὲ βλάστοι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 717"> <title>O. T.</title> 717</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδὸς δὲ βλάστας</quote>: also <title>O.C.</title> 972, Eur. fr. 432, fr. adesp. 376. So Eur. fr. 698 <quote xml:lang="grc">πτώχ’ ἀμφίβλητα σώματος</quote>. On the other hand, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">περιβλέπω</quote> is regularly long (<title>O.C.</title> 996 n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχὶ_πατρός_1310">, explaining what precedes: (‘thou hast shown, I say, that thou dost not spring from) Sisyphus.’ It is simpler to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστών</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔβλαστες</quote>) than <quote xml:lang="grc"> φύσιν</quote>. The gen. is influenced by the prep. before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧς</quote>: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρός_1310">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 3">cp. 3</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετὰ_θ_1310">. The <quote xml:lang="grc"> θ’</quote>, though wanting in L, seems genuine. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τε... δέ</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τε...τε</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1096"> <title>Ant.</title> 1096</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεθνηκότων_1310"> might be governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">μετά</quote>, but really depends rather on the unexpressed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστα ἀκούει</quote>. The poet may have been thinking of <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.482"> <title>Od.</title> 11. 482</ref> ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">σεῖο δ’ Ἀχιλλεῦ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτις ἀνὴρ προπάροιθε μακάρτατος οὔτ’ ἄρ’ ὀπίσσω·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν μὲν γάρ σε ζωὸν ἐτίομεν ἶσα θεοῖσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖοι, νῦν δ’ αὖτε μέγα κρατέεις νεκύεσσιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥσθην_1314">: for the aor., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1289">cp. 1289</ref> n.: for the acc., <ref n="Soph. OT 236"> <title>O. T.</title> 236</ref>(n.): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 136"> <title>Ai.</title> 136</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ μὲν εὖ πράσσοντ’ ἐπιχαίρω</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1339">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς γὰρ εὐσεβεῖς θεοὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκοντας οὐ χαίρουσι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμὸν_1314"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὸν</quote> is the best correction. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα τὸν ἀμὸν</quote> occurs in <ref n="Soph. El. 279"> <title>El.</title> 279</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 588"> El. 588</ref> , <ref n="Soph. El. 1496"> El. 1496</ref>, and in the first two of those passages <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμὸν</quote> in L has been made from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὸν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1118">Cp. 1118</ref> above (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμᾶς</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμᾶς</quote>).—With <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσθην πατέρα <emph>τε</emph> τὸν ἐμὸν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσθην <emph> γε</emph> πατέρα τὸν ἐμὸν</quote>, the rhythm is enfeebled by two consecutive tribrachs,—without the justification given in 1029 by the pause after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγετε</quote>. And </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1314"> would be somewhat weak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_τυχεῖν_1315">: for the double gen., cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 7. 33">Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 7. 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ δὲ δὴ πάντων οἰόμεθα τεύξεσθαι ἐπαίνου</quote> (‘in a case where we expect to win praise from all men’): and <ref n="Soph. OC 1170">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1170</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφίεμαι_1315">, ‘desire,’ with inf., as <ref n="Thuc. 6. 6">Thuc. 6. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφιέμενοι ...τῆς πάσης ἄρξειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_δοθείσας_1316">: for this order (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">σοθείσας τύχας</quote>), cp. <ref n="Thuc. 7. 23">Thuc. 7. 23</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ πρὸ τοῦ στόματος νῆες ναυμαχοῦσαι</quote>: <title>O.T.</title> 1245 (n.): for </gloss>
lemma: τὰς μὲν ἐκ θεῶν τύχας δοθείσας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1316">, <title>O.T.</title> 590 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκουσίοισιν_1318">, since, though Ph. could not have avoided the woes of the past ten years, their prolongation is now his own choice. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 215">Soph. <title>El.</title> 215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκείας εἰς ἄτας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπίπτεις. ἑκούσιος</quote> is in Attic either of two or of three terminations: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 727">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 727</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἑκουσίας</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 1123 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκουσία</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 8. 27">Thuc. 8. 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθ’ ἑκουσίαν</quote> (yet id. 7. 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκούσιος...στρατεία</quote>): <ref n="Plat. Rep. 603C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 603C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βιαίους ἢ ἑκουσίας πράξεις</quote>. But of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούσιος</quote> the fem. in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ία, ‐ιαι</quote> seems not to occur: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 861 E <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβαι... ἀκούσιοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγκεινται_1318">, ‘are intent upon,’ meaning here, ‘persist in enduring,’ though a release is offered to them. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 91">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 91</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσπερ ἐγκείμεσθ’ ἀεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θρήνοισι καὶ γόοισι καὶ δακρύμασι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αἰθέρ’ ἐκτενοῦμεν· ἐμπέφυκε γὰρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναιξὶ τέρψις τῶν παρεστώτων κακῶν</quote>: where the sense is, ‘to which I give my days.’ So id. <title>I. T.</title> 144 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδεθ’ ὡς θρήνοις ἔγκειμαι</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 182"> <title>Ion</title> 182</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς δ’ ἔγκειμαι μόχθοις</quote>. But sometimes Eur. uses this verb as simply =<quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι ἐν</quote>: <title>Helen.</title> 269 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλαῖς συμφοραῖς ἐγκείμεθα</quote> (‘are plunged in’): and so <emph>ib.</emph> 924. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠγρίωσαι_1321"> expresses the temper which fiercely rejects friendly remonstrance (whereas in 226 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπηγριωμένον</quote> referred to aspect): cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.575"> <title>Od.</title> 8. 575</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠμὲν ὅσοι χαλεποί τε καὶ ἄγριοι οὐδὲ δίκαιοι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠὲ φιλόξεινοι</quote>. So in <ref n="Plat. Rep. 410D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 410D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγριότης</quote> is associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληρότης</quote>.— For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_τε_1321">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1363">cp. 1363</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1397"> <title>O. C.</title> 1397</ref> n. Here, as often, the clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> expresses the contrary of that with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> (‘<emph>so far from</emph> accepting advice, you resent it’): <ref n="Hdt. 1. 63">Her. 1. 63</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅκως μήτε ἁλισθεῖεν ἔτι οἱ Ἀθηναῖοι, διεσκεδασμένοι τε εἶεν</quote>: cp. id. 1. 119 quoted above on v. 950. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνοίᾳ_1322">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 233"> <title>El.</title> 233</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν εὐνοίᾳ γ’ αὐδῶ</quote>. Schneidewin's view that L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔνοιάν_λέγων_1322">, arose from <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εὔσοιαν</emph> λέγων</quote> (cp. <title>O.C.</title> 390 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσοίας χάριν</quote>), is more ingenious than probable: rather <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote> was a mere gloss, explaining the object of the <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔνοια.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσμενῆ_θ_1322">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1303">cp. 1303</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζῆνα_ὅρκιον_1324">, Zeus, the guardian of oaths,—who punishes men who break them. In the <quote xml:lang="grc"> βουλευτήριον</quote> at Olympia there was a statue of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ὅρκιος</quote>, with a thunderbolt in each hand ( <ref n="Paus. 5. 24. 9">Paus. 5. 24. 9</ref>). Cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1767 <quote xml:lang="grc">χὡ πάντ’ ἀΐων Διὸς Ὅρκος</quote> (n.). <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1025">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1025</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ὅρκιόν σοι Ζῆνα καὶ πέδον χθονὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμνυμι</quote>: id. <ref n="Eur. Med. 208">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 208</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν Ζηνὸς ὁρκίαν Θέμιν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γράφου_ἔσω_1325">: so <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 450">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 450</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτ’ ἀκούων ἐν φρεσὶν γράφου</quote>. More often this metaphor is developed by the word <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτος</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 789">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 789</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 683">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 683</ref>, fr. 537), or a derivative of it ( <title>Suppl.</title> 179 <quote xml:lang="grc"> δελτουμένας</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 275"> <title>Eum.</title> 275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> δελτογράφῳ...φρενί</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γράφου_1325">. The midd. <quote xml:lang="grc"> γράφομαι</quote> is used in prose also (apart from its legal sense, ‘to indict’) of writing down something for one's own use: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 82">Her. 2. 82</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 8. 135">Her. 8. 135</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">συγγραψάμενον</quote>), <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 142">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 142</ref> So <quote xml:lang="grc">δ. ἀπογράφομαι</quote>, of taking an inventory (<ref n="Lys. 12.8">Lys. or. 12, § 8</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_γὰρ_1326">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> prefacing a statement, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1337">cp. 1337</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 277"> <title>O. T.</title> 277</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τύχης_1326">: cp. fr. 198 <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὖν μάχωμαι θνητὸς ὢν θείᾳ τύχῃ</quote>; Philoctetes has shown no consciousness that his misfortune was anything more than an ordinary accident (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 267">cp. 267</ref>, 632). He now learns that it was ordained by the gods,—in order that he might not reach Troy before the time appointed for that city's fall (197 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σηκὸν_1327"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ὑπαίθριον τέμενος</quote>, the sacred precinct, open to the sky. This form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαλυφής</quote> is similarly used by <title>De Anim.</title> 2. 9 (Berl. ed. 422 <emph>a</emph> 1), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ὀσφραντικὸν αἰσθητήριον ἀκάλυφες</quote> (better <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαλυφὲς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>,— opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν ἐπικάλυμμα</quote>. Here it is opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόστεγος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">στεγανός</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc"> σηκός</quote>, in ref. to sacred places, properly means, as here, an enclosure without any roofed building (cp. <ref n="Hdt. 4. 62">Her. 4. 62</ref>), though poets sometimes use it as a general term for ‘shrine’: <ref n="Eur. Ion 300">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 300</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σηκοὺς...Τροφωνίου</quote> (his cave): [Eur. ] <title> Rhes.</title> 501 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς Ἀθάνας σηκόν</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαλυφῆ</quote> at the end of the v., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1302">cp. 1302</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύφιος_ὄφις_1327">. The epic version speaks merely of an <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοόφρων ὕδρος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.723"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 723</ref>). But the Attic poet feels that the mysterious significance of the event is enhanced, if the serpent which inflicted the bite is conceived as the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> of the shrine. Clearly Sophocles does not identify Chrysè with any form of Athena; Chrysè is, for him, a lesser deity: yet the associations of the Erechtheum have suggested the word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκουρῶν_1327">. The sacred serpent in that temple,—representative of Erichthonius, and guardian of Athena Polias,—was regularly called <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρὸς ὄφις</quote>. Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρὸν ὄφιν· τὸν τῆς Πολιάδος φύλακα δράκοντα</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 758">Ar. <title> Lys.</title> 758</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ δύναμαι )γωγ’ οὐδὲ κοιμᾶσθ’ ἐν πόλει</quote> (in the acropolis), | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὗ τὸν ὄφιν εἶδον τὸν οἰκουρόν ποτε</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 41">Her. 8. 41</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγουσι Ἀθηναῖοι ὄφιν μέγαν φύλακα τῆς ἀκροπόλιος ἐνδιαιτᾶσθαι ἐν τῷ ἱρῷ</quote>.—For the verb <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρεῖν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 343.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 343.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῦλαν_1329">, subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυχεῖν_1329">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 275">cp. 275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ’ αὐτοῖς τύχοι</quote>. This is better than to make <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦλαν</quote> the object of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τυχεῖν</quote> as =‘obtain’: since the acc. after <quote xml:lang="grc"> τυγχάνω</quote> is elsewhere a neut. pron. or adj., or art. with inf. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 778"> <title>Ant.</title> 778</ref> n.). The correction of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντυχεῖν_1329"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_τυχεῖν_1329"> seems certain. In <ref n="Aesch. PB 667">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 667</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ μὴ θέλοι, πυρωπὸν ἐκ Διὸς μολεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεραυνόν, ὃς πᾶν ἐξαϊστώσοι γένος</quote>, the future sense of the simple aor. inf. is sufficiently marked by the context.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήποτ_1329">. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc"> μή</quote> here is due to the notion of ‘feeling confident,’ not to the imperat.: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτόν γ’ οἶδα, μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτ’ ἄλλο πέρσαι μηδέν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕως_ἂν_1330"> is a certain emendation of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἂν_1330">, which would mean ‘in whatever way,’ ‘however’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1369"> <title>Ai.</title> 1369</ref>). But, ‘however the sun may rise,’ etc., could not stand for, ‘so long as the sun continues to rise.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄν</quote> never means, or could mean, ‘while.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> scanned as one syllable (by synizesis), cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.727"> <title>Il.</title> 17. 727</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.148."> <title>Od.</title> 2. 148.</ref>Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1361"> <title>O. C.</title> 1361</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕωσπερ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ</quote> MSS.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ζῶ</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1117"> <title>Ai.</title> 1117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> MSS.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ᾖς οἶός περ εἶ. </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸς_ἥλιος_1330">. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 143">Her. 8. 143</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν τε ἀπάγγελλε Μαρδονίῳ ὡς Ἀθηναῖοι λέγουσι, ἔστ’ ἂν ἥλιος τὴν αὐτὴν ὁδὸν ἴῃ τῇ περ καὶ νῦν ἔρχεται, μήκοτε ὁμολογήσειν ἡμέας Ξέρξῃ</quote>. ‘While <emph>the same</emph> sun rises,’ etc.,=‘while the sun rises as he now does.’ It is possible that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸς_1330"> may be a reminiscence of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν αὐτὴν ὁδόν</quote> in Her. ; at any rate it is decidedly more forcible here than Brunck's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτος_1330">, which he illustrates from <ref n="Plut. Arist. 10">Plut. <title>Arist.</title> 10</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἥλιον δείξας· ἄχρις ἂν οὖτος ταύτην πορεύηται τὴν πορείαν, Ἀθηναῖοι πολεμήσουσι Πέρσαις</quote>. The gesture implied by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτος_1330_2"> would be superfluous here, since in saying </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτῃ_τῇδε_1330">, he points to the east and to the west. (For the combination of these pronouns, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 841">cp. 841</ref> n.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴρῃ_1330">: there seems to be no other classical instance of this intrans. use; but cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίσχω, ἀνέχω</quote> (of sunrise). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκὼν_αὐτὸς_1332">, a pleonasm (used also by <ref n="Soph. Phil. 476">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 476</ref>), like, ‘of thine <emph>own</emph> free will.’ The oracle had made the consent of Ph. a condition (612): the use of fraud was an unauthorised device of Odysseus (103). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντυχὼν_1333"> with gen., instead of the usual dat., as in <ref n="Hdt. 4. 140">Her. 4. 140</ref>, quoted on v. 320, where see n. The gen. here (like that with <quote xml:lang="grc"> συντυχών</quote> there) has a special warrant, since the idea is that of ‘obtaining their aid.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_Ἀσκληπιδῶν_1333">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.731"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 731</ref>(referring to the warriors from Tricca, Ithomè, and Oechalia in Thessaly), <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ αὖθ’ ἡγείσθην Ἀσκληπιόο δύο παῖδε</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰητῆρ’ ἀγαθώ, Ποδαλείριος ἠδὲ Μαχάων</quote>.— The form <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἀσκληπίδης</quote>, for <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀσκληπιάδης</quote>, occurs nowhere else, and is wrongly formed from <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἀσκληπιός</quote>. The rule for masc. patronymics is as follows:—(1) Stems in <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ιο‐</quote> take the suffix <quote xml:lang="grc">‐δα—‐</quote>, when <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote> becomes <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ιο‐</quote> becomes <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐ια‐</quote>: as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεά‐δη‐ς</quote>, from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργέα—‐ς, Μενοιτιά‐δη‐ς</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">Μενοίτιο‐ς</quote>. (2) All other stems take <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ιδα—</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">Τανταλ‐ίδη‐ς</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">Τάνταλο‐ς</quote>. But the first formation is sometimes used by poets instead of the second, for metre's sake: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">Χαλκωδοντιάδης</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.541"> <title>Il.</title> 2. 541</ref>) for <quote xml:lang="grc">Χαλκωδοντίδης, Τελαμωνιάδης</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 9. 623) for <quote xml:lang="grc"> Τελαμωνίδης</quote>. And the converse licence is attested by Etym. Magn. p. 210. 11 (quoted by Herm. ): <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ ποιηταὶ πολλάκις ἀποβάλλουσι τὸ α, οἷον, Ἐριχθονιάδης</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐριχθόνιο‐ς</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἐριχθονίδης</quote>. [The writer wrongly adds <quote xml:lang="grc">Τελαμωνιάδης, Τελαμωνίδης</quote>, as if the latter were the irregular form.] <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐριχθονίδαι</quote> occurs in <title>C. I.</title> 1. 411. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀσκληπίδης</quote>, then, though incorrect, may well be genuine.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσου_μαλαχθῇς_1334">: the gen. as after verbs denoting cessation or respite: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 274"> <title>Ai.</title> 274</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνέπνευσε τῆς νόσου</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 43">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 43</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα κουφισθῇ νόσου</quote>: so </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωφᾶν_1334">, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_τόξοις_1334">, with its aid: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 8">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 2. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν τοῖς ὅπλοις...δίκην ἐπιθεῖναι αὐτοῖς</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote> with dat., in ref. to arms, is oft. no more than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχων_1334"> with acc.; <emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.251"> Il. 11. 251</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στῆ δ’ <emph> εὐρὰ</emph>ξ σὺν δουρί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρσυς_φανῇς_1334">: the phrase suggests the glory of the exploit; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1064">cp. 1064</ref>: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 11">Thuc. 2. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιστον...πολλοὺς ὄντας ἑνὶ κόσμῳ χρωμένους φαίνεσθαι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ταῦτα_1336"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The report of the prophecy given by the pretended <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπορος</quote> in 603—613 was true as far as it went, but designedly incomplete. Neoptolemus, we must suppose, derived his knowledge from the Atreidae or Odysseus (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 114">cp. 114</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐφάσκετ’</quote>): who, however, had omitted to tell him that the aid of the bow was indispensable to his own success (112 ff.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1337"> as in 1326.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_ἔστιν_1337"> (‘we have a man,’ etc.), rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν <emph>ἐστιν</emph> </quote> (when the verb would be merely auxiliary to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλούς</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἕλενος_1337">: <ref target="cl606">see on 606</ref> ff.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀριστόμαντις_1337"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστος μάντις</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθόμαντις</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1241">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1241</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόμαντις</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. N. 1">Pind. <title> N.</title> 1. 92</ref>), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ταῦτα_1339">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <emph>if</emph> Ph. consents to come (1332): if he does not, then Troy cannot be taken (611). The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖ_1339"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρὴ_1339"> is unnecessary, since <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> can equally well denote what is ordained by fate: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 998">cp. 998</ref>: 1397: <ref n="Soph. OT 825."> <title>O. T.</title> 825.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέρους_1340">: so <ref n="Verg. A. 3. 8">Verg. <title> Aen.</title> 3. 8</ref>, <emph>vix prima inceperat aestas.</emph> The general tradition was that Troy fell about the end of May (late in the Attic month Thargelion): see <title>Camill.</title> 19 (where Ephorus and other writers are quoted for the statement), and Clemens <ref n="Clem. Al. Strom. 1. 21"> <title>Strom.</title> 1. 21</ref>, p. 139 (where <quote xml:lang="grc">Διονύσιος ὁ Ἀργεῖος</quote> is cited: cp. Müller <title>Frag. Hist.</title> III. p. 26). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσαν_1340">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 386">cp. 386</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίδωσ_ἑαυτόν_1340">, offers himself, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνειν_1340">, (for us) to slay: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 618">cp. 618</ref>: <title>Nub.</title> 440 <quote xml:lang="grc">τουτὶ τό γ’ ἐμὸν σῶμ’ αὐτοῖσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέχω τύπτειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλὴ_πίκτησις_1344">. The further gain (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίκτησις</quote>) is the fame which he will win, in addition to being cured. This is indicated by the place of the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλήνων_ἕνα_1344"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κριθέντἄριστον_1344"> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕνα</quote> with the superl., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 563"> <title>O. C.</title> 563</ref> f., n.). Then the clause relating to the cure is co-ordinated with the clause relating to victory; see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1112."> <title>Ant.</title> 1112.</ref>We should say rather, ‘that, <emph> while</emph> you are cured, you should <emph>also</emph> win fame.’ For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_μὲν_1344"> followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶτα_1344">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 61"> <title>Ant.</title> 61</ref> n.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶτα_1344_2"> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>), <ref n="Soph. El. 261"> <title>El.</title> 261</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῶτα μὲν...εἶτα</quote>. Ellendt says, ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">κτῆσιν</quote> interpretatus schol. errat’: but the schol.'s words are, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ )πίκτησις· ἡ κτῆσις τῆς δόξης</quote>: which is exactly right. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ω_αἰών_1348">: for the nom., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1186">cp. 1186</ref>, 1213. <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἰών</quote> is here tinged with the notion of ‘fortune,’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 179">cp. 179</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνω_1348">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1068"> <title>Ant.</title> 1068</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄνω</quote> (the living): <ref n="Soph. El. 1167"> <title>El.</title> 1167</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡνίκ’ ἦσθ’ ἄνω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφῆκας_μολεῖν_1348">: for the inf. (which was unnecessary) cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 194">Her. 1. 194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πλοῖον... ἀπιεῖσι κατὰ τὸν ποταμὸν φέρεσθαι</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰκάθω_1352">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 651"> <title>O. C.</title> 651</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_εἶμι_1352">, into the public gaze; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 581">cp. 581</ref> : <ref n="Xen. Ages. 9. 1">Xen. <title>Ages.</title> 9. 1</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φῶς</quote> (‘publicity’) = <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἀεὶ ἐμφανὴς ε<*>ναι</quote>, as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σπανίως ὁρᾶσθαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_προσήγορος_1352">; the dat., as in <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 146A">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 146A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλους τε καὶ προσηγόρους ἀλλήλοις</quote>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδενὸς προσήγορος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1437.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1437.</ref>Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 16">Thuc. 6. 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ δυστυχοῦντες οὐ προσαγορευόμεθα</quote>: and the prosperous Creon's words in <ref n="Soph. OT 596"> <title>O. T.</title> 596</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν με πᾶς ἀσπάζεται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_κύκλοι_1354">. Although <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πάντα ἀμφ’ ἐμοί</quote> could stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἀμφ’ ἐμοὶ πάντα</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 659"> <title>Ant.</title> 659</ref> n.), the interposed </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδόντες_1354"> here requires that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφ_ἐμοὶ_1354"> should be taken with it: literally, ‘ye that, in my case, have seen all,’— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘ye that have seen all the wrong done to me’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ περὶ ἐμὲ πάθη</quote>, schol.). Of the two readings, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφ’ ἐμοὶ</quote> (L) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> (A), the first has the better MS. authority; and though the second is more euphonious, that fact does not warrant a preference. As used by Soph. , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφί</quote> with gen. =‘concerning’ (554): with dat., either ‘concerning’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 684">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 684</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀμφὶ μὲν τούτοισιν εὖ σχήσει</quote>), or ‘around’ ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1223">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1223</ref> etc.). The sense ‘concerning’ is fittest here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κύκλοι_1354"> has been explained as ‘years’ (schol.), ‘the orbs of heaven’ (Brunck, Buttmann). Camerarius saw the true meaning. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 704"> <title>O. C.</title> 704</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ γὰρ αἰὲν ὁρῶν κύκλος</quote> (‘eye’) | <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσει νιν Μορίου Διός</quote> . <ref n="Soph. OT 1270"> <title>O. T.</title> 1270</ref> ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαισεν ἄρθρα τῶν αὑτοῦ κύκλων</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῶν τοιαῦθ’ ὁθούνεκ’ οὐκ ὄψοιντονιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ οἷ’ ἔπασχεν οὔθ’ ὁποἶ ἔδρα κακά</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_τἄλγος_1358"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> ‘How can I return to the Atreidae? <title>For</title> it is not merely a question of forgetting the past; I dread the future.’ He does not mean that he has ceased to resent the former wrongs, but only that his present resolve is influenced <emph>less</emph> by resentment than by fear. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_κακούς_1360">: ‘for when a man's mind has (once) become a mother of evil deeds, it trains him to be evil in everything else,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in all subsequent deeds. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_1360"> as proleptic predicate, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475"> <title>Ant.</title> 475</ref> n.; <ref n="Eur. Med. 296">Eur. <title> Med.</title> 296</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδας περισσῶς ἐκδιδάσκεσθαι σοφούς</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_1360_2"> and the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακά_1360"> demands care. I prefer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_1360_3">, for these reasons. (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακά_1360_2">, if retained, would naturally suggest this sense:—‘When a man's mind has once given birth to evil (counsels), it trains <emph>the rest</emph> also (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, his actions) to be evil.’ But the antithesis here is between the earlier and the later bad deeds; not between bad counsels and bad deeds. (2) The effect of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_1360_4"> is to indicate that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄλλα_1360"> stands in antithesis, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶν_1360"> only, but with the whole preceding clause, and thus to suggest its true sense, viz., ‘in all that follows.’ (3) It is true that the image, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτηρ_γένηται_1360">, is then no longer consistently maintained; but this very failure to persevere with a metaphor is Sophoclean (cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 866"> <title>O. T.</title> 866</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτηργένηται_1360"> is a poetical equivalent for, ‘make a <emph>beginning</emph> of.’ Hence the poet felt that he did not require <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἅπαξ</quote>, though we should naturally add ‘once.’ The same delicate economy may be observed in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 584"> <title>Ant.</title> 584</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς γὰρ ἂν σεισθῇ θεόθεν δόμος, ἄτας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἐλλείπει</quote>: ‘For when a house hath (once) been shaken from heaven, there the curse fails nevermore.’ The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_1360_5"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακά_1360_3"> might easily have been caused by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄλλα_1360_2">.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δ_1362">. The formula <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ... δέ</quote> means ‘<emph>and...also</emph>,’ with an emphasis on the intervening word. This is the only instance in Soph. : it occurs, however, in <ref n="Aesch. PB 973">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 973</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σὲ δ’ ἐν τούτοις λέγω</quote> ), <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 65">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 65</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1117">Soph. <title>El.</title> 1117</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σὺ δ’ αὐθάδης ἔφυς</quote>): <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 250">Ar. <title>Pax</title> 250</ref>: and oft. in Attic prose. The usual account of it is that the <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>=‘also,’ while <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>=‘and.’ This suits those instances in which, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc"> καὶ...δέ</quote> is preceded by a full stop, or by a pause; but it is less natural where <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ...δέ</quote> links a new clause to a preceding one in the same sentence; as in <ref n="Thuc. 4. 24">Thuc. 4. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μάλιστα ἐνῆγον</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς Συρακοσίους</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Λοκροὶ τῶν Ρηγίνων κατὰ ἔχθραν, καὶ αὐτοὶ δὲ ἐσεβεβλήκεσαν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: id. 9. 71 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μὴ παντάπασιν ἱπποκρατῶνται, καὶ χρήματα δὲ ἅμα αὐτόθεν τε ξυλλέξωνται καὶ παρ’ Ἀθηναίων ἔλθῃ, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Examples of the latter class clearly suggest that in the combination <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ...δέ, καί</quote> was the conjunction, while <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>, ‘on the other hand,’ added the force of ‘also.’ Cp. the well-known use of <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> with the pron. after a voc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀντιγόνη, σὺ δ’ ἐνθάδε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλασσε</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 507">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 507</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαυμάσας_ἔχω_1362">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> τεθαύμακα</quote> (emphatic): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 257c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 257 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λόγον δέ σου πάλαι θαυμάσας ἔχω</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 258b"> <emph>ib.</emph> 258 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθαυμακότες</quote> </cit>. This constr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμάζω</quote> with gen. of pers. and acc. of thing is common ( <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 89">Plat. <title> Phaedo</title> 89</ref>A, etc.): the gen. is properly possessive (‘I wonder at this <emph>in</emph> you’). We find also the gen. with a dependent clause in place of the acc. ( <title>H.</title> 2. 3. 53 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν...θαυμάζω εἰ μὴ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc"> βοηθήσετε</quote>); and the gen. alone (<cit> <bibl n="Lys. 7.23">Lys. or. 7 § 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τούτου μὲν οὐ θαυμάζω</quote> </cit> ).</gloss>

data.quote: τὸν λόγον δέ σου πάλαι θαυμάσας ἔχω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_1362">: this advice of thine that I should go to Troy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῆν_1363">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 418">cp. 418</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδει</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτε_τε_1363"> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1321">cp. 1321</ref> n.—<quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ γε</quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ ἐκεῖνοι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref>(n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵτινες</quote>): the plur. is implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροίαν</quote>: cp. <emph> ib.</emph> 941.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθύβρισαν_1363"> with gen., as <ref n="Soph. OC 960."> <title>O. C.</title> 960.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_γέρας_1365">, the arms, wrought by Hephaestus, which were a gift of honour to Achilles (cp. note on <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβας ὑπέρτατον</quote> in 402). It would strain the words to render them, ‘a gift of honour (bequeathed to thee) from thy sire.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἳ_ἔκριναν_1365">.] It can hardly be doubted that these words are spurious. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναγκάζεις_1366"> with double acc.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 280."> <title>O. T.</title> 280.</ref>The present tense, expressing endeavour, is quite compatible with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶ_1366"> (‘dost thou intend to go..., and art thou trying to force...?’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_1366"> (L) = <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἰέναι ξυμμαχήσοντα</quote>. The occurrence of the same form in 1362 is no argument for <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 88">cp. 88</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνώμοσας_1367"> has been needlessly changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνῄνεσας</quote>, a weaker word. The sense here is, ‘thou didst make a compact with me, confirmed by thine oath’ (813, 941). This is but a slight deflection,—surely permissible for poetry,—from the ordinary sense, ‘to take an oath along with another person.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμψον_1368"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801">cp. 801</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔα_κακῶς_1369"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The absence of caesura has the effect of allowing the words to fall from the speaker's lips with a certain deliberate emphasis: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101">cp. 101</ref> n. By </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόλλυσθαι_1369"> he means the failure and ruin at Troy with which the gods will visit the Greeks: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1035">cp. 1035</ref> ff. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶς_κακούς_1369"> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 166">cp. 166</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆν_δὲ_1370">: epanaphora (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 200"> <title>Ant.</title> 200</ref> n.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> will be <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλῆ</quote> because he will have rescued Philoctetes, and also forsaken the Atreidae. Here he thinks of his father as still living: cp. n. on 1209 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστεύσαντα_1374">, ‘in reliance’ upon the divine oracle, and upon the report of it given by N. (1336—1343). Though <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστεύω</quote> sometimes=‘obey’ ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1228"> <title>Tr.</title> 1228</ref>), it is unnecessary to suppose that sense here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδε_ποδί_1374"> (dat. of circumstance) have a compressed dramatic force. ‘What,—go to Agamemnon,—when I bear about with me this plague which caused him to cast me forth?’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_οὖν_1378">: <ref n="Soph. OT 705"> <title>O. T.</title> 705</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_βάσιν_1378">: the phrase recalls those in which Ph. himself had spoken of the ulcered limb as if it had a being distinct from his own (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 786">786</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1188">1188</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1202"> 1202</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 750"> <title>O. C.</title> 750</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί σε κηδεύουσα καὶ τὸ σὸν κάρα.—υ</quote> was short in <quote xml:lang="grc"> πύον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυος</quote> (as in Lat. <emph> puter</emph>), though long in <quote xml:lang="grc">πύθω</quote> (as in <emph> puteo</emph>, <emph>putidus</emph>): Empedocles 336 <quote xml:lang="grc">πύον, ἔπλετο λευκόν</quote>: Andromachus (flor. <emph> circ.</emph> 50 A.D. ) <emph>ap.</emph> Galen p. 876 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μογερῶν στέρνων ἀπολύεται ἔμπυον ἰλύν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀποσώσοντας_1378"> is a necessary correction here (cp. cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἶνον_αἰνέσας_1380">. Though one sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἶνος</quote> was a story with a moral ( <ref n="Hes. WD 200">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 200</ref>), it could hardly have been used as it is here, in the sense of ‘advice,’ unless the meaning had been helped out by the cognate verb. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 555">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 555</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνω δὲ κρύπτειν τάσδε συνθήκας ἐμάς</quote> (‘I recommend’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_τελούμενα_1381"> (pres. part.), ‘what I see is in the way of being accomplished with the best results’ for us: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, what promises such results, if it be done. The pres. part., implying that the action is already in train, suits the speaker's hopeful tone. I should not, then, take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελούμενα_1381"> as <emph>fut.</emph> part. with pass. sense; esp. as there is no clear example in Attic of <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦμαι</quote> as fut. pass., while <quote xml:lang="grc">τελούμενος</quote> as pres. part. pass. is frequent (<emph> e.g. </emph> <ref n="Soph. OT 797"> O. T. 797</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1344"> <title>El.</title> 1344</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_λέξας_1382"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The question of Ph. is, ‘Art thou not ashamed before heaven of pretending that a return to Troy is for <emph>my</emph> good?’ (For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταισχύνει_1382"> with acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1424.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1424.</ref>) Now, if we retain in 1383 the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελούμενος_1382">, Neoptolemus replies,—‘Why should one be ashamed, when he is <emph> receiving</emph> a benefit?’ This would be a sentiment like that of Odysseus in 111, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν τι δρᾷς εἰς κέρδος, οὐκ ὀκνεῖν πρέπει</quote>. But the rejoinder of Ph. shows that N. cannot have so spoken; for Ph. asks,—‘Dost thou mean a benefit <emph>to the Atreidae</emph>, or <emph>to me</emph>?’ N.'s words, then, must have been to this effect,—‘Why should one be ashamed, when he is <emph> conferring</emph> a benefit?’ If, therefore, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελούμενος_1382_2"> is to be kept, it must be midd., not pass., ‘benefiting.’ There are some instances of rare midd. forms in Soph. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ποθουμένᾳ...φρενί</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc"> ποθούσῃ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 103">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 103</ref>): but they usually occur in contexts which exclude the pass. sense. Here, a midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠφελούμενος</quote> would be too ambiguous. The pass. sense of that form was familiar, whereas the midd. sense is unexampled.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελουμένους_1382"> is the most attractive at first sight. But, if </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεούς_1382"> be left in 1382, then <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠφελουμένους</quote> could refer to nothing else: and such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ...δαίμονι...σύμμαχος πέλω</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 244"> <title>O. T.</title> 244</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ θεῷ βοηθῶν</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Apol. 23B">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 23B</ref>), certainly do not warrant a description of the gods as ‘benefited’ when they are obeyed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελουμένων_1382"> (gen. absol.), ‘when people are being benefited,’ would be too vague.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελούμενος_1382_3"> is not confined to the termination. Buttmann's conjecture, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠφελῶν_φίλους_1382">, gives precisely what is required; since <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλους</quote>, in N.'s mouth, might well suggest Ph. 's reply in 1384, <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεις δ’ Ἀτρείδαις κ.τ.λ.</quote> The origin of the corruption may have been the resemblance of the syllables <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΕΛ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΙΛ</quote>, leading a careless scribe to erase the second of them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγεις_δ_1384"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘Is this benefit of which thou speakest for the Atreidae, or one that concerns me?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἐμοὶ_1384"> =‘in my case’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 414"> <title>O. C.</title> 414</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτ’ ἐφ’ ἡμῖν Φοῖβος εἰρηκὼς κυρεῖ</quote>; The prep. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> might govern </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀτρείδαις_1384"> also (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 829"> <title>O. T.</title> 829</ref> n.), but is better taken with the pron. only. L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_1384">, is possibly right (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 885"> <title>O. C.</title> 885</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ οὐχ ὕβρις τάδ’</quote>;): but I prefer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_1384"> in this direct reference to the last speaker's phrase (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ὠφελῶν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_που_1385">. The particle conveys the assurance with a shade of friendly irony (‘<emph>thy</emph> good, I should rather think’)— which marks surprise at Ph. 's question. Seyffert overlooks this when he objects to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_1385"> here as having ‘dissimulationis aliquid.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_1386"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος εἶ</quote> ), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_γε_1386">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 663">cp. 663</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τᾶν_1387">: a familiar, but not homely, form of address: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1145"> <title>O. T.</title> 1145</ref> n. Cp. Curtius <title>Etym.</title>, 5th ed. (1886), vol. II. p. 336 (Eng. tr.): ‘If...<quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τᾶν</quote> (also <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ )τᾶν</quote>) has anything to do with <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>της</quote>, Lange's theory that <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐτᾶν</quote> is an expansion of the st. <quote xml:lang="grc">vετα</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγιστάν</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστος</quote>, is the most probable one.’ (In earlier editions he had inclined to the theory that <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶν</quote> is an old form of <quote xml:lang="grc">τύ, τύνη</quote>.) Others favour the view that <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶν</quote> comes from <quote xml:lang="grc">τάλαν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρασύνεσθαι_κακοῖς_1387">, to become too bold, to show contumacy, amid troubles. The dat. is not causal, but rather a dat. of circumstance, expressing the idea, ‘in time of misfortune.’ Cp. the use of the dat. with regard to festivals ( <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1519">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 1519</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεσμοφορίοις νηστεύομεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 691">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 691</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεῖς_με_1388">, ‘wilt work my ruin by these persuasions,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, if thou prevail on me to go to Troy.’ The addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_1388"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γιγνώσκω_1388"> has been suggested by the common idiom, <quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκω σε ὅτι ὀλεῖς</quote>: the sense is not, ‘I now see through thee’; and the dat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσδετοῖς_λ_1388"> should therefore be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὀλεῖς</quote>, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_ἔγωγε_1389">, ‘I, <emph>at least</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>), will not ruin thee’—though possibly thou mayest ruin thyself. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 872">Cp. 872</ref> n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν Ἀτρεῖδαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_οὐκ_1390">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 585">cp. 585</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέποθ_γ_1392">. In saying </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σώσους_1392">, N. meant, ‘they will restore thee to health, and to honour.’ Ph. replies, ‘Never,—if I must visit Troy of my own free will.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_1392"> expresses the condition: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 602"> <title>O. C.</title> 602</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δῆτά σ’ ἂν πεμψαίαθ’, ὥστ’ οἰκεῖν δίχα</quote>; The comma is better placed after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέποθ’</quote> than after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκόντα γ’</quote>, since the latter thus gains emphasis. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1332">Cp. 1332</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδεῖν_1392"> is right: the variant </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλεῖν_1392"> arose from the likeness of <quote xml:lang="grc">Δ</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">Λ</quote>, helped, perhaps, by a reminiscence of vv. 347, 998, 1347. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολεῖν_1392"> would be feebler. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἰδεῖν</quote> (‘to set eyes upon,’ ‘visit’), cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 824"> <title>O. T.</title> 824</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ...μοι φυγόντι μἤστι τοὺς ἐμοὺς ἰδεῖν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῷμεν_1393">: for the form, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 895">cp. 895</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λόγοις_1393">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 60">cp. 60</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσειν_δυνησόμεσθα_1394">. The fut. inf. is probably sound. It is made easier by the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc"> δύναμαι</quote> is used in the fut. tense; not because the fut. indic. can be regarded as attracting the inf. into the same tense; but because, ‘we <emph>shall not be</emph> able to persuade,’ implies, ‘we cannot hope to persuade.’ Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 28">Thuc. 3. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνόντες δὲ οἱ ἐν τοῖς πράγμασιν οὔτ’ ἀποκωλύσειν δυνατοὶ ὄντες, εἴ τ’ ἀπομονωθήσονται τῆς ξυμβάσεως, κινδυνεύσοντες κ.τ.λ.</quote> (where the MSS. agree in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀποκωλύσειν</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκωλύειν</quote> is merely a conjecture). If </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσειν_1394"> were to be altered, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεῖσαι_1394"> would be more probable than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείθειν_1394">. See Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυνησόμεσθα_λέγω_1394">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1221">cp. 1221</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1395">, causal (‘for’), referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">τί...ἂν...δρῷμεν</quote>, which implies, ‘It is vain to do more.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥᾷστ_μὲν_1395"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the sentence is a compressed form of <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥᾷστα ἐμοί ἐστιν, αὐτῷ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν... λῆξαι, σὲ δὲ ζῆν, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάσχειν_1397"> denotes the continuance of the sufferings: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθεῖν_1397">, the sum of those sufferings, regarded as a doom. So <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσαντι παθεῖν</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 313">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 313</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 95">Cp. 95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐξαμαρτεῖν...νικᾶν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖ_1397">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1339">cp. 1339</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔνεσας_1398">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνῄνεσας</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 122">cp. 122</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὠμολόγησας.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θιγών_1398">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 813">cp. 813</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπειν_1398">. Here the inf. merely defines the action to which the pron. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_1398"> refers: the <emph> fut.</emph> inf. was therefore unnecessary: and the pres. inf. has been used, rather than the aor., because ‘sending’ is thought of as a process, not as a momentary act. Similarly the pres. (or aor.) inf. is sometimes used, rather than the fut., when the notion of fut. time is sufficiently expressed by the principal verb: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 13">Thuc. 3. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε οὐκ εἰκὸς αὐτοὺς περιουσίαν νεῶν ἔχειν, ἢν</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς...ἐπεσβάλητε</quote>: and id. 1. 81 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως εἰκὸς Ἀθηναίους...μήτε...δουλεῦσαι κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βράδυνε_1400">, here intrans., as in <title> Suppl.</title> 730 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ βραδύνοιμεν βοῇ</quote>, <ref n="Plat. Rep. 528D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 528D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπεύδων...μᾶλλον βραδύνω</quote>, etc. Others take it transitively (‘delay us,’ or ‘delay the matter’). So <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχύνω</quote> also is either trans. or intrans.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεθρήνηται_1400">, impersonal.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόοις_1400"> is better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λογοις_1400">, which may have arisen through the scribe's eye wandering to v. 1393. The very name of Troy renews the memory of his sorrows; and lamentation has been his portion too long. He would fain turn to thoughts of home. Some supply <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροία</quote> as subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθρήνηται</quote>: this seems less fitting here. If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεθρύληται_1400"> were read, then, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροία</quote> would be the subject; ‘its name has been heard often enough in my laments’ (<emph>satis decantata est</emph>...). But this <emph>v. l.</emph> seems to have arisen merely from the corruption <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθρήληται</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_στείχωμεν_1402">. In a trochaic tetrameter the end of the fourth foot regularly coincides with the end of a word. This verse breaks the rule. The only other exception is <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 165">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 165</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτά μοι διπλῆ μέριμν’ ἄφραστός ἐστιν ἐν φρεσίν</quote>, where Porson wished to place <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλῆ</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">φρεσίν</quote>, and Hermann, to read <quote xml:lang="grc">μέριμνα φραστός</quote>. Hermann holds that the breach of rule here is excused by the pause after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχωμεν_1402">. This I believe to be the true explanation. As <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχωμεν</quote> is the signal that the prayer of Ph. has at last been granted, it demands emphasis. The unusual rhythm—which would be too harsh in a continuous verse—here serves to accentuate the joyful surprise of Philoctetes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ἔπος_1402">. Porson's fine instinct refrained from any such attempt; he felt that, if the verse was to be amended, only one remedy was tolerable,—viz., to strike out </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δοκεῖ_1402">, and leave an iambic trimeter. In favour of this view, it might be said that a scribe, or an actor, who wished to make v. 1402 into a tetrameter, might have been led to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δοκεῖ</quote> by a reminiscence of 526 and 645: though we cannot concede to Burges that the spuriousness of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δοκεῖ</quote> is bewrayed by the lack of the usual <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> before it. The absence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> merely renders <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δοκεῖ</quote> a little more abrupt.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντέρειδε_1403">, plant firmly (on the ground). Lucian (perhaps with a reminiscence of this v.) uses the word of one who refuses to move,—<quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ πόδε ἀντερείδων πρὸς τοὔδαφος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">Κατάπλους</quote> § 4). Cp. Anthol. 12. 84 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ γαῖαν,...ἴχνος ἐρειδόμενος</quote>. This is better than to render, ‘lean thy steps on mine.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἐὰν_1405"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <title>Nub.</title> 1445 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’, ἢν ἔχων τὸν ἥττω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγον σε νικήσω</quote>...; (also <quote xml:lang="grc">τί γάρ, ἤν κ.τ.λ.</quote> <emph>ib.</emph> 351).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_παρὼν_1405">: for the interruption of the sentence, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 210">cp. 210</ref> n., 1226. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσωφέλησιν_1406">: a compound found only here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρξεις_1406">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 786"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 786</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πήματ’ ἔρξαντες</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελάζειν_1406"> the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆς πάτρας. ΝΕ. ἀλλ’ εἰ δρᾷς ταῦθ’, ὥσπερ αὐδᾷς</quote>. This is probably an interpolation, which may have arisen in the first instance from <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆς πάτρας</quote>, a gloss explanatory of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζειν</quote>, the rest being then added, to supply a supposed defect of metre. Some of the attempts which have been made to expand the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ δρᾷς ταῦθ’, ὥσπερ αὐδᾷς</quote>, will be found in the Appendix. Seyffert's is the best,— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ σὺ δὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα δράσεις, ὥσπερ αὐδᾷς</quote>: but no one of them is very probable. To the objection that, without these words, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεῖχε_1406"> becomes too abrupt, we may perhaps reply that the decision of Neoptolemus has really been taken; these last misgivings which flit across his mind are not causes of serious hesitation. Thus the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">στεῖχε</quote>—implying his inward contentment with the answer, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴρξω πελάζειν</quote>—is in truer harmony with the context than a form of words which would suggest that his consent depended, even now, on an explicit assurance.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκύσας_χθόνα_1406">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 533">cp. 533</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 156">Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 156</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν γῆν πρόσκυσον καὶ τοὺς θεούς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήπω_γε_1409"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> This is one of the instances in which the appearance of the ‘deus ex machina’ is not preceded by any notice in the text. Similar cases are those of Athena in <title>I. T.</title> 1435 and <title>Suppl.</title> 1183, and the Dioscuri in <title>Helen.</title> 1642. It may be inferred that in these instances the apparition was a sudden one,—effected, perhaps, by the actor coming out upon a high platform (<quote xml:lang="grc">θεολογεῖον</quote>) at the back of the scene. When, on the other hand, the approach of the deity is described in the text (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <title>Androm.</title> 1227 ff.), he was probably lowered, or raised, by machinery. (Cp. MA. üller, <title>Griech. Bühnenalterthüm.</title>, pp. 151 ff.) The nine anapaests here are spoken as Heracles moves forward.— With regard to the dramatic fitness of this interposition, see Introd. § 11. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσκειν_δ_1411">: inf. as imperat.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 57">cp. 57</ref>. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">φάσκειν</quote>, as= ‘deem,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 462"> <title>O. T.</title> 462</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκοῇ_κλύειν_1411">. If we had simply, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδήν τε κλύειν, λεύσσειν τ’ ὄψιν</quote>, the misplacing of the first <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> would be of a common kind (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref> n.): the further peculiarity here is that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκοῇ τε κλύειν</quote> suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀφθαλμοῖς τε λεύσσειν</quote>, as if the object of both verbs were the person. </gloss>
