<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_χάριν_1413">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 485"> <title>Tr.</title> 485</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνου τε καὶ σὴν ἐξ ἴσου κοινὴν χάριν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 762">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 762</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφ’ ἀξίως νιν σοῦ τε τήν τ’ ἐμὴν χάριν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_φράσων_1415">. The son of Zeus comes from heaven to declare, with his own lips, that Zeus ordains the return of Philoctetes to Troy. Thus a wholly new motive is brought to bear on Ph. , who hitherto knew merely (at second hand) what Helenus had prophesied (1336 ff.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_στέλλει_1415">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 4.296">Ap. Rh. 4. 296</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στέλλεσθαι τήνδ’ οἶμον</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μέν_1418"> would properly have been answered in 1421 by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα δὲ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοὶ τοῦτο ἐπαγγέλλομαι</quote>): but meanwhile the thought of sequence in the topics yields to that of parallelism between the two cases; and so in 1421 we have simply <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοί, κ.τ.λ.</quote>—The phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_τύχας_1418"> might naturally seem the prelude to a fuller recital: but the meaning is simply this:—‘I have to tell thee that, after many sufferings, I have been received among the gods; and for thee, too, suffering is to end in glory.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθάνατον_ἀρετὴν_1420">, ‘deathless glory.’ The difficulties felt regarding <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετήν</quote> have arisen from the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὁρᾶν_1420">, which imply some visible sign, and thus suggest that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατος ἀρετή</quote> means something more than ‘undying <emph>fame of</emph> prowess.’ But no emendation is probable (see cr. n.). And the soundness of the text will appear from two considerations.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχον_1420">, (‘ingressive’ aor.,) ‘came to have,’ ‘won’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1229"> <title>Ant.</title> 1229</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 465"> <title>Ai.</title> 465</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχε στέφανον εὐκλείας.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὁρᾶν_1420_2">: a laurel-wreath perhaps sufficed as symbol of the apotheosis: see n. on 728. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_1421"> is explained by the next v.; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote> in 1440.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1421">, not merely ‘after’ (720), but ‘as a result of,’ ‘through.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκλεᾶ_θέσθαι_1421">, make it glorious: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 532">cp. 532</ref>. (Not, ‘lay down, end, thy life in glory, as Ellendt takes it.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῶτον_τε_1424">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> here is not corr<*>ative to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ δ’</quote> in 1431: it is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1058">cp. 1058</ref> n. So in 1426 ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">Πάρ<*> μέν...πέρσεις τε</quote>. Possibly </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1424"> ought to <*> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> in one of the two places, or in both: but, in each case, the <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> may be a trace of the somewhat careless writing which appears in this speech.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκρεὶς_1424">, as Menelaus was for the <quote xml:lang="grc">μονομαλ<*></quote> with Paris ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 3"> <title>Il.</title> 3</ref>), and Ajax for that with Hector ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 7"> <title>Il.</title> 7</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ηάριν_1426">: the slaying of Paris by Ph. was told by Lesches in the <title>Little Iliad</title>, and must have come into the <quote xml:lang="grc">Φιλοκτήτης ἐν Τροίᾳ</quote> of Sophocles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴτιος_κακῶν_1426">: cp. Alcman fr. 31 <quote xml:lang="grc">Δύσπαρις αἰνόπαρις, κακὸν Ἑλλάδι <*>τιανείρῃ</quote>. Attius <title>Philocteta</title> fr. 18 <title>Pari </title><<title> *>yspari</title>, <emph>si impar esses tibi</emph>, <emph>ego nunc non <*>ssem miser.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκῦλά_τ_1428"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> These spoils constitute the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριστεὶα</quote>. For the custom of hanging up such <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>λα</quote> in temples or houses, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 577">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 577</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 278">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 278</ref> : <title>I. T.</title> 74: <ref n="Verg. A. 1.247">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> I. 247 ff.</ref>, <ref n="Verg. A. 3.286">3. 286 ff.</ref>, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλαβὼν_1428"> is a more probable correction than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλαχὼν</quote> of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκβαλὼν_1428">. The force of the compound is, ‘having received from the hands of the army,’—as a reward due to him. So <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκλα<*>βάνειν</quote> is said of receiving what is surrendered under a treaty (<ref n="Isoc. 5.100">Isocr. or. 5 § 100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν Ἀσίαν... παρὰ τῶν Ἑλλήνων ἐν ταῖς συνθήκαις ἐξέλαβεν</quote>): or of receiving ‘in full’ ( <title>Legg.</title> 958 D <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκας...ἐκλαβόντι</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 123">Her. 8. 123</ref> where, after Salamis, the <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατηγοί</quote> meet at the Isthmus, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριστήια δώσοντες τῷ ἀξιωτάτῳ</quote>, and vote by laying <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆφοι</quote> on Poseidon's altar.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμψεις_1428"> seems here to mean ‘convey’ (implying triumphal pomp): though elsewhere this use seems restricted to the escorting of persons (1368, 1399). It may, however, mean merely ‘send’ in advance (as in the Heracles <title>Trach.</title>sends his captives before him with the <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρυξ</quote>).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ποίαντι_1428">: Ph. thus learns that his father is indeed alive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_λάβῃς_1431"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: ‘and whatsoever spoils thou receivest from that army (the Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατεύματος</quote> of 1429), (from those spoils) take memorials of my bow (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a thank-offering for its work) to my pyre.’ A portion of the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῦλα</quote> is to be dedicated to Heracles on Mount Oeta; where in historical times such relics were doubtless shown at the so-called <title>Pyra</title> ( <ref n="Liv. 36. 30">Liv. 36. 30</ref>: Introd. § 1).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδε_στρατοῦ_1431">, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάβῃς_1431">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 580">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 580</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ κομίζεται</quote>, <emph> ib.</emph> 1022 <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρόν ποτ’, <ref n="Soph. OT i">σοπη. ο. τ. ι</ref>)/σθι, τῶν ἐμῶν χειρῶν λαβών</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1163 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδεξάμην δέ του</quote>.—For other views, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ταῦτ_1433"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Heracles now addresses Neoptolemus in a parenthesis which extends down to 1437: then, at the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_δ_1433">, he again turns to Philoctetes. Two views of these words are possible. I prefer the first.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1433"> refers to the general tenor of the preceding verses, from 1423 onwards, —viz., that Ph. is to go to Troy with N., and there triumph. ‘And to <emph>thee</emph> (as well as to Ph. ) I give these counsels’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, it concerns thee, too, to note that he must accompany thee to Troy. In </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σοὶ_1433"> the <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> =‘<emph>and</emph>’: but the emphasis which falls on <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote> makes it equivalent to ‘thee also.’ If <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> meant ‘also,’ the asyndeton would be too harsh. The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1433_2"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταὔτ_1433"> seems needless. A modified form of this view refers </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1433_3"> only to v. 1431, as if Heracles meant that Neoptolemus also must bring spoils to the pyre: but this seems less fitting.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1433_4"> refers to what follows: the aor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῄνεσα_1433"> is then like <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπώμοσα</quote> in 1289 (n.): and the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1433"> after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> merely introduces the statement (1049). I do not share Buttmann's feeling that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶσοὶ_1433"> ought then to be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_δὲ_1433">: but the whole context appears to render the first view more natural.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πεδίον_1433">, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 69">cp. 69</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συννόμω_1436">, ‘having the same pasture’—here, ‘seeking their prey on the same ground.’ Cp. <title>Hist. An.</title> 6. 18 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ταῦροι ... ὄντες σύννομοι</quote>. This primary sense of the adj. is here blended with the derived sense, ‘partners.’ The image is Homeric: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.297"> <title>Il.</title> 10. 297</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βάν ῥ) ἴμεν ὥς τε λέοντε δύω διὰ νύκτα μέλαιναν</quote> (Odysseus and Diomedes): cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.548."> <title>Il.</title> 5. 548.</ref>So <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 938">Aesch. <title> Cho.</title> 938</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦς λέων</quote> (Orestes and Pylades): imitated by <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1401">Eur. <title> Or.</title> 1401</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέοντες Ἕλλανες δύο διδύμω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυλάσσετον_1436">. Since neither can prevail without the other, each has the other's welfare in his keeping. We can perceive that the poet's mind glances from the metaphor to the thought of a <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαιος κἀγαθὸς παραστάτης</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 671"> <title>Ant.</title> 671</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀσκληπιὸν_1437">. In the <title>Iliad</title> Asclepius is a mortal, an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμύμων ἰητήρ</quote> living in western Thessaly, whose two sons, the physicians Podaleirius and Machaon, go thence to Troy. But Sophocles here thinks of Asclepius as a god, whom Heracles is to send from heaven. Prosaic objections have been made to this passage, on the ground that in 1333 it was said that Ph. was to be healed by the Asclepiadae. But it might be replied that those were merely the words of Neoptolemus, who was giving his own interpretation to a vague prediction of Helenus that Ph. would be healed. Even, however, if the oracle itself spoke of the Asclepiadae, there is no poetical unfitness in this further promise—that the healing god himself should visit Troy, to direct and inspire their skill.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δεύτερον_1439">. The first occasion was when Heracles himself made war on Laomedon, and, after taking Troy, gave the king's daughter, Hesione, to Telamon ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1302"> <title>Ai.</title> 1302</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.638"> <title>Il.</title> 5. 638</ref> ff.). Cp. <ref n="Pind. I. 5"> Pind. <title>I.</title> 5. 36</ref>(the Aeacidae) <quote xml:lang="grc">δὶς πόλιν Τρώων πράθον, ἑσπόμενοι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλῆι πρότερον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σὺν Ἀτρείδαις</quote>. Propert. 3. 1. 32 <title>Troia bis Oetaei numine capta dei.</title>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴν_1439">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροίαν</quote>, implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἴλιον_1439">, which is regularly neuter (454) in post-homeric poetry. The exception in <ref n="Eur. Andr. 103">Eur. <title> Andr.</title> 103</ref> occurs in a quasi-epic hexameter: conversely, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴλιον αἰπὺ</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.71"> <title>Il.</title> 15. 71</ref> occurs in a suspected passage. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννοεῖθ_1440">, as a correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννοεῖσθ_1440">, is commended by the fact that, out of five other places where Soph. uses this compound, there are two ( <ref n="Soph. OT 559">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 559</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 61">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 61</ref>) in which the act. form is proved by metre; while in a third, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 578">Soph. <title>Tr.</title> 578</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννοήσασ’</quote> is not likely to have come from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννοηθεῖσ’</quote>. On the other hand, the midd. was not less Attic than the act.; and, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννοεῖσθ’</quote> here were not followed by an aspirate, it would have been rash to alter it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβεῖν_1440">. As the schol. observes, this warning derives force from the tradition that, after the fall of Troy, Neoptolemus ‘slew Priam, when he had taken refuge at the altar of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ἑρκεῖος</quote>.’ Neoptolemus himself was afterwards assassinated at Delphi: whence the proverb <quote xml:lang="grc">Νεοπτολέμειος τίσις</quote> ( <ref n="Paus. 4. 17. 4">Paus. 4. 17. 4</ref>), meaning, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ παθεῖν ὁποῖόν τις καὶ ἔδρασε</quote>. The outrage of Ajax Oileus on Cassandra, in the temple of Athena at Troy ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 70">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 70</ref>), was another instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσέβεια</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_πάντα_1442"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The ground for the precept (<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβεῖν</quote>) is given by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1442">,—viz., that Zeus deems ‘all other things,’—such as conquest, or glory—of secondary moment (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεύτερ_ἡγεῖται_1442">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title> 351</ref>). Then the sentence introduced by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_1442"> explains why Zeus so deems; viz., because the effect of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβεια</quote> does not cease with man's life on earth, but is imperishable. That is, it brings happiness to the <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβής</quote> in the life beyond the grave; and it is also of good example to the men who come after. Heracles can fitly say this; he is himself enjoying the reward of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβεια</quote>, and he comes from the presence of Zeus.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_1442">, for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_1442">, seems an almost certain one: but the case in favour of it has not yet, perhaps, been adequately stated. It is not merely, or even chiefly, a verbal question; we must consider the whole passage. If we retain the MS. reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_βροτοῖς_1442">, ‘piety dies with mortals,’ the meaning is, ‘piety <emph>passes</emph> with men <emph>into the other life</emph>,’ there to find a reward. (Cp. Rev. xiv. 13, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ γὰρ ἔργα αὐτῶν ἀκολουθεῖ μετ’ αὐτῶν</quote>.) Now, this narrows the scope of the thought in an arbitrary way: for then <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβεια</quote> is regarded only in its influence on the happiness of the departed. If, however, we read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_βροτοῖς_1442">, this allows us to think <emph>also</emph> of the abiding influence upon human conduct; and the more comprehensive view is certainly the more fitting one in an exposition of the reason why <title>Zeus</title> attributes a paramount importance to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβεια</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνθνῄσκει_1442">, which, though intelligible (in the light of v. 1444), would be forced. The regular meaning of <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκω</quote> and its compounds, when used figuratively, is ‘to become inoperative’ or ‘extinct,’ in contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. OC 611"> <title>O. C.</title> 611</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκει δὲ πίστις</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 846">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 846</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote>)...<quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκοντες μάτην</quote>. Eur. fr. 734 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετὴ δέ, κἂν θάνῃ τις, οὐκ ἀπόλλυται</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῇ δ’ οὐκέτ’ ὄντος σώματος· κακοῖσι δὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπαντα φροῦδα συνθανόνθ’ ὑπὸ χθονός</quote>: where it is immaterial that the reference is to fame living or perishing <emph>on earth:</emph> the point is that <quote xml:lang="grc">συνθανόνθ’</quote> is opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῇ</quote>. In <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 868">Ar. <title> Ran.</title> 868</ref>, too—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἡ ποίησις οὐχὶ συντέθνηκέ μοι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνῳ δὲ συντέθνηκεν</quote>—the jest turns on the fact that the verb would naturally mean, ‘has <emph> perished</emph>’ with the author.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_1442_2"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_1442_2">. (1) The position of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> is rightly so placed, because, as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄλλα_πάντα_1442"> indicates, there is an implied contrast between <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὐσέβεια</quote> and other things which <emph>do</emph> perish with men. Cp. Soph. fr. incert. 841 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοῖς ἀθύμοις ἡ τύχη ξυλλαμβάνει</quote> (though it <emph>does</emph> aid the brave). (2) The thought, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_συνθνῄσκει_1442">, is repeated by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἀπόλλυται_1442">. But v. 1444 is not a mere repetition; it is a re-statement in more forcible language, and, as such, it is rhetorically appropriate here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_1442"> was a gloss on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατήρ_1442">: (<emph>b</emph>) that some one had written vv. 1443 f. in the margin, the original form of 1443 having been, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ γὰρ εὐσέβεια συγγηρᾷ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">συνναίει</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖς</quote>: then a scribe evolved our text. This hypothesis is too complex: besides, the speech would not end well with v. 1442. One of Hermann's views (see cr. n.) was that v. 1444 only should be rejected (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> being read in 1443): but this, too, would be ineffective. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθεινὸν_ἐμοὶ_1445">: for the neglect of the usual caesura after the second foot, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1470">cp. 1470</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OC 1760"> <title>O. C.</title> 1760</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1771."> O. C. 1771.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμψας_1445">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 846">cp. 846</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνιος_1445"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ</quote>, ‘<emph>after</emph> a long time’ (as in <ref n="Soph. OC 441"> <title>O. C.</title> 441</ref>, n.): but in 1449 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνιοι</quote>=‘<emph>for</emph> long’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺν χρόνον</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_τίθεμαι_1448">, ‘give my voice in this sense’: <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι</quote> as in the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι ψῆφον</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 24.23">Lys. or. 24 § 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμῶς, ὦ βουλή, ταύτῃ θῆσθε τὴν ψῆφον</quote> </cit> (so Taylor: <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτῃ...τῇ ψήφῳ</quote> MSS.): Isae. or. 8 § 46 <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ δίκαιόν ἐστι, ταύτῃ τὴν ψῆφον τίθεσθε</quote> : <ref n="Hdt. 1. 120">Her. 1. 120</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτῃ πλεῖστος γνώμην εἰμί</quote>: id. 7. 143 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτῃ Θεμιστοκλέους ἀποφαινομένου</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμην</quote>).—The reading </gloss>

data.quote: μηδαμῶς, ὦ βουλή, ταύτῃ θῆσθε τὴν ψῆφον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_ταύτην_1448"> is also possible: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 674 A <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν τιθείμην ταύτην τὴν ψῆφον</quote>: Andoc. or. 3 § 21 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνα γνώμην ἔθεντο περὶ ὑμῶν</quote>...; In <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 658">Ar. <title> Eccl.</title> 658</ref> the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ ταύτην γνώμην ἐθέμην</quote>: but Toup conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτῃ</quote>, which Dindorf adopts.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_ταύτῃ_1448"> here is that it explains the alternative readings of the MSS., <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμῃ ταύτῃ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμην ταύτην</quote>. So in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 125"> <title>Ant.</title> 125</ref> f. the true reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπάλῳ...δράκοντος</quote> generated <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπάλῳ...δράκοντι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀντιπάλου...δράκοντος</quote>.—Those who read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμῃταύτῃ_τίθεμαι_1448"> suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆφον</quote> is understood with <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι</quote>, the dat. being modal. This is very awkward, and cannot be supported by any sound example. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλετε_πράσσειν_1449">: for the inf., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1627"> <title>O. C.</title> 1627</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μέλλομεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν</quote>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καιρὸς_πλοῦς_1449">: ‘occasion (the need of the hour) urges you on, and the fair wind yonder (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδ_1449">) at the ship's stern’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘it is time for you to sail, and the weather is fair.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 466">Cp. 466</ref> f. (n.). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_πρύμναν_1449">, cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 97">Thuc. 2. 97</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν ἀεὶ κατὰ πρύμναν ἱστῆται τὸ πνεῦμα</quote> : <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.6">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 11. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετόπισθε νεὸς κυανοπρώροιο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴκμενον οὖρον ἵει</quote>. It is best here to keep the ordinary Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">πρύμναν</quote>, which the MSS. give, since metre does not require <quote xml:lang="grc">πρύμνην</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 482">cp. 482</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1449"> as sixth word is noteworthy. Soph. does not elsewhere place it later than fourth word (as in v. 1268); and this was the ordinary classical limit. But the examples in Comedy of the 4th cent. B.C. show that the Attic ear tolerated a greater licence. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> stands as <emph> fifth</emph> word in Menander <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροφώνιος</quote> fr. 1. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">διαφέρει τῷ μαγείρῳ τοῦτο γάρ</quote>: as <emph> sixth</emph>, in Antiphanes <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἁλιευομένη</quote> 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τὸ τάριχός ἐστιν ὡρμηκυῖα γάρ</quote> (where Meineke needlessly writes <quote xml:lang="grc">τάριχος δ’ ἐστὶν ὡρμηκυἶ ἄφαρ</quote>): as <emph>seventh</emph>, in Athenion <quote xml:lang="grc">Σαμόθρᾳκες</quote> 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θηριώδους καὶ παρασπόνδου βίου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς γὰρ ἀπολύσασα</quote> etc. (But in Alexis fr. incert. 7. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ μήτε</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράττεται τέλος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν γὰρ ἡμᾶς</quote>, the emendation <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ἡμᾶς</quote> is clearly right.) The unusual position of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1449_2"> led to a point being placed in L after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδ_1449_2">, and has been one cause of doubt as to the construction of the whole passage (see cr. n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχων_1449"> shows, the movement is beginning.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλέσω_1449">, aor. subj., as usual with <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 300">cp. 300</ref> n. The sense is strictly, ‘invoke’ (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 737">cp. 737</ref>): the land is addressed as a divine power, to which he makes a prayer (1464). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαῖῤ_μέλαθρον_1453"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The eight verses which follow call up a picture of his past life in Lemnos,—the lonely cave,—the plain to the west of it,—the loud sea to the east,—the echoing cliffs to the north. Then, at v. 1461, his thoughts turn to the voyage that lies before him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύμφρουρον_1453">, the witness of his weary watching and waiting: the cave is personified, as in 1081 ff. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 142">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε φάραγγος σκοπέλοις ἐν ἄκροις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρὰν ἄζηλον ὀχήσω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νύμφαι_τ_1454"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Next to the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλαθρον</quote> itself, he naturally names the elemental deities of the region from which he obtained water, fuel, and the soothing <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλλον</quote> (292 ff., 649). Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.8"> <title>Il.</title> 20. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφάων, αἵ τ’ ἄλσεα καλὰ νέμονται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πηγὰς ποταμῶν καὶ πίσεα ποιήεντα</quote> (grassy water-meadows). <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 2.821">Ap. Rh. 2. 821</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφαι ἑλειονόμοι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρσην_1455">, of strong, deep sound: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 124">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 124</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κίθαρίν τε ματέρ’ ὕμνων</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρσενι βοᾷ δόκιμον</quote>. (In Soph. fr. 480 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρσενας χοὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἀχέροντος</quote> is explained by some as ‘deep-sounding waves.’) Conversely in <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.122">Hom. <title>Od.</title> 6. 122</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κουράων...θῆλυς ἀυτή</quote>. A difference between deeper and shriller tone was expressed by the terms <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλὸς ἀνδρήιος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> γυναικήιος</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 1. 17">Her. 1. 17</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβλής_1455"> (without </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_1455">) cannot be defended as an epithet of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτύπος_1455">,—‘a sound sent forth by the sea’ (as Seyffert takes it). We must read either (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβολῆς_1455"> with Hermann, or (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβλής_θ_1455"> with Musgrave and Schaefer. I prefer (1), because </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_1455_2"> is decidedly tame, whether </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόντου_1455"> be taken with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβλής_1455_2"> only, or (as seems needful) with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτύπος_1455_2"> also. Nor can it be questioned that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβολῆς_1455_2"> gives a much finer verse. It is true that we have had <quote xml:lang="grc">προβλῆτες</quote> in 936, whereas this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">προβολή</quote> recurs only in later Greek (Quintus Smyrn. 9. 378 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ προβολῇσι θαλάσσης</quote>). But, if such a use of <quote xml:lang="grc"> προβολή</quote> was actually a rare one, the presence of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντου</quote> would make it clear. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_1456"> seems to denote generally the <emph> region</emph> in which the cave was situated, —near, or perhaps upon, the <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντου προβολή</quote>. We can hardly refer it back to the word <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλαθρον</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλάκι_1456">, an epic and lyric form twice used in lyrics by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 227">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 227</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 131), but not elsewhere by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνδόμυχον_1456">, a poet. word (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδομάχης</quote>); but, in later Greek at least, the verb formed from it seems to have been common: thus the schol. on <title> Vesp.</title> 970 explains <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρός</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδομυχοῦντα</quote>. For the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδόμυχον</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτα</quote>, see note on <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχρυσον</quote> in 393. The cave was on the east coast (see 1459 n.), but its seaward mouth is imagined as having a s. or S. E. aspect, so that the blasts of the stormy <quote xml:lang="grc">νότος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 335">Soph. <title> Ant.</title> 335</ref> n.) could carry rain and spray into the inmost recesses.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πληγαῖσι_1456">: cp. <ref n="Lucr. 5.955">Lucr. 5. 955</ref> <emph>verbera ventorum.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑρμαῖον_ὄρος_1456"> is mentioned in only one other passage of classical literature,— <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 283">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 283</ref>, where the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμαῖον λέπας Αήμνου</quote> is the signalling station intermediate between Ida and Athos. It is doubtless the N. E. promontory of Lemnos, now Cape Plaka.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἕρμαιον_1456"> here, but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑρμαῖον_1456"> is right. Adjectives in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐αιος</quote>, of more than two syllables, were regularly properispomenon, like <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναῖος</quote>. Neuter substantives in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐αιον</quote> were proparoxytone; hence <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθήναια</quote>, as the name of the festival (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερά</quote>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρμαιον</quote>, a wind-fall. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίτυπον_1460">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 693">cp. 693</ref> f. (n.)— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειμαζομένῳ_1460">, fig., under stress of suffering; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1194">cp. 1194</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 562">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 562</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλινοῖς ἐν περίνοισιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμαζόμενον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λύκιόν_ποτόν_1461">. There can be no doubt that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λύκιον_1461"> is the true reading: the corruption </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλύκιον_1461">, facilitated by the use of a small <quote xml:lang="grc">λ</quote> as initial, may have been due simply to the fact that the Greeks, like ourselves, spoke of ‘fresh’ water as ‘sweet’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">γλυκύ</quote>, as dist. from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλμυρόν</quote>). The <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκιον ποτόν</quote> must be a spring, or fount, in Lemnos, so called after Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκιος</quote>. There was a more elaborate legend,—that this god, wishing to alleviate the sufferings of Philoctetes, had caused two fountains to arise in the island,—one of wine, and the other of honey. (Zenobius 4. 99, etc.: cp. Hermann's note here.) We do not know whether this <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκιον ποτόν</quote> had been mentioned by any poet before Sophocles, —by Lesches, for example: but the way in which the name is introduced favours that supposition. Perhaps, indeed, a ‘Lycian’ fount at Lemnos may have been well known to Athenians in the poet's day. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λείπομεν_ἤδη_1462">. Hermann's motive for converting v. 1463 into a paroemiac (see cr. n.) is that the emphasis seems to require such a pause. It would certainly be admissible; but it does not appear necessary. If v. 1463 remains an ordinary dimeter, then the final paroemiac (1468) is all the more effective. And the change is open to one decided objection. If </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤδη_1462"> is altered to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δὴ_1462">, the second </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λείπομεν_1462"> becomes weak. As to the sequence of dactyls in 1463, cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1361">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title> 1361</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσφορά μ’ αἴρετε, σύντονα δ’ ἕλκετε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιβάντες_1462">, ‘entered on’ that hope,—as upon ground which it was lawful to tread: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 189"> <title>O. C.</title> 189</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβίας ἐπιβαίνοντες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_πέμψον_1465">: for the modal dat., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 51"> <title>O. T.</title> 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀσφαλείᾳ τήνδ’ ἀνόρθωσον πόλιν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμέμπτως_1465">, ‘so that I shall have no cause to complain’: cp. <title>Suppl.</title> 269 <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξας ἀμέμπτως</quote>. (Others understand, ‘without complaint on <emph>thy</emph> part,’—because I leave thee, or because I have changed my resolve.)— Meineke objects that Ph. cannot properly ask the island for a good voyage. But just as Orestes prays Argos and her gods to welcome him ( <ref n="Soph. El. 67">Soph. <title>El.</title> 67</ref>), so Ph. here prays Lemnos to speed her parting guest. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 986">Cp. 986</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθ_1466">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε ὅποι</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. El. 1099"> <title>El.</title> 1099</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιποροῦμεν ἔνθα χρῄζομεν</quote>: similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε ὅπου</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 796"> <title>O. T.</title> 796</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφευγον, ἔνθα μήποτ’ ὀψοίμην.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μοῖρα_1466">: some write <quote xml:lang="grc"> μοῖρα</quote>, as in 331; but the epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μεγάλη</quote> seems here to imply definite personification.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλων_1466">: Heracles and Neoptolemus. —The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδαμάτωρ_δαίμων_1466"> is clearly Zeus, whose ordinances Heracles came to announce (1415). The epithet is fitting; for the stubborn purpose of Ph. has been overruled; and Troy is soon to fall. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 605">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 605</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νύμφαις_ἐπευξάμενοι_1470">. Ritter, who rejects vv. 1469—1471, argues that the nymphs had no power over the sea; that belonged to Poseidon and other gods. But this was not the old Greek conception. The sea-nymphs, properly so called, were the Nereids (for the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὠκεανῖναι</quote> were rather the nymphs of rivers and fountains). The list of the Nereids given by Hesiod ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 250"> <title>Th.</title> 250</ref> ff.) shows that they were imagined, not merely as representing, but as influencing, the various moods of the sea. Thus he says of the Nereid <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυμοδόκη</quote> that, with her sister <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυματολήγη</quote>, ‘she quickly calms waves on the gloomy deep, and the blasts of fierce winds.’ The good offices of the Nereids to mariners are expressed by such names as <quote xml:lang="grc">Φέρουσα, Ηοντοπόρεια</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐλιμένη</quote>. A voyager, then, might well pay his vows to them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόστου_1471">, said by the Chorus of sailors who had come from Troy, means ‘return,’ rather than merely ‘journey’ (as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 43">43</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωτῆρας_1471"> with fem. subst.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 81"> <title>O. T.</title> 81</ref> n.</gloss>
